Ask the Lord in prayer to give you a heart that treasures His Word above all else, for it is through the Scriptures that we are made wise for salvation (2 Timothy 3:15).
What is Shintoism?

Shintoism is the indigenous ethnic religion of Japan, centered on the worship of "kami," which are spirits or divine powers believed to inhabit nature, prominent landmarks, and ancestors. It is characterized by a focus on ritual purity, the maintenance of harmony between humanity and the natural world, and practices conducted at local shrines marked by distinctive "torii" gates.

In Shintoism, there is no formal founder or singular sacred text like the Bible. Instead, it emphasizes "makoto," or sincerity of heart, and the performance of "harae" (purification rituals) to wash away "kegare" (impurity or misfortune). While it encourages a deep respect for the beauty of the world, from a biblical perspective, Shintoism is a form of animism and polytheism. It directs worship toward the created order rather than the one true Creator who made all things. The Bible teaches that God's eternal power and divine nature are clearly seen in what He has made, so that people are without excuse when they worship the creature rather than the Creator (Romans 1:20-25).

You may wonder how a person can find true peace and internal purity without the constant performance of outward rituals. While Shintoism seeks cleansing through water and ceremonies, the Scriptures reveal that our deepest problem is not ritual impurity, but a heart separated from God by sin. Because God is holy and just, He cannot overlook our rebellion. However, in His immense love, He provided a way for us to be truly reconciled to Him.

The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. Through His sacrifice, we are offered a "clean heart" (Psalm 51:10) and a relationship with the living God that rituals could never achieve.

True harmony and forgiveness are found not in the veneration of spirits, but in a relationship with the Father through faith in His Son. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life.

As a practical step in your journey of understanding, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It describes Jesus as the "true light, which gives light to everyone" (John 1:9) and explains how He provides the "living water" that satisfies the soul's deepest thirst (John 4:10-14). What is it about the person of Jesus that stands out to you as you compare His claims to other paths?
Who is the man of lawlessness in 2 Thessalonians 2:1-12?

The man of lawlessness is a figure described by the Apostle Paul as the "son of destruction" who opposes and exalts himself above everything that is called God or is worshiped. 2 Thessalonians 2:3-4. He represents the ultimate height of human rebellion and defiance against the Creator, appearing before the second coming of our Lord Jesus Christ to deceive those who are perishing because they refused to love the truth. 2 Thessalonians 2:10.

Historically, faithful believers have held different views regarding his specific identity, as this is a secondary matter where interpretive emphasis varies. 

One common perspective, the futurist view, holds that the man of lawlessness is a literal individual, often referred to as the Antichrist, who will emerge at the very end of the age to lead a final revolt against God. 2 Thessalonians 2:9. Another historic position, often held by the Protestant Reformers and reflected in the 1689 London Baptist Confession, identifies this figure not as a single person but as a succession of individuals or an office, such as the Papacy, which they believed sat in the "temple of God" by claiming divine authority within the professing church. A third view, known as the preterist position, suggests that Paul was referring to a figure in the first century, such as a Roman emperor or a leader during the Jewish revolt, whose actions foreshadowed the final character of lawlessness.

While these interpretations differ, the heart of the passage is pastoral and clear. Paul writes to ensure that the church is not "quickly shaken in mind or alarmed" by rumors or false reports. 2 Thessalonians 2:2. The identity of this rebel is far less significant than the absolute sovereignty of the One who restrains him and will ultimately destroy him. 2 Thessalonians 2:6-8.

Our ultimate comfort is found in the Gospel. The Lord Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature to live a perfect life of obedience, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He has already triumphed over the powers of darkness, and His return will bring the final renewal of all things. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and those who belong to Him are kept secure by His power.

I encourage you to spend time today reading 2 Thessalonians 2:13-17. Focus your heart on the fact that God chose you for salvation through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in the truth. Ask the Lord in prayer to establish your heart in every good work and word, and to grant you a deeper love for the truth of His Word. You can turn to Jesus Christ today, repenting of sin and trusting in Him alone for forgiveness and the hope of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What are some commonalities in Eastern religions?

You may wonder how so many diverse cultures across the East have arrived at similar conclusions about the soul, the moral order of the universe, and the search for ultimate peace. This commonality often reflects a sincere human desire to make sense of the suffering and brokenness we see in the world.

Eastern religions, such as Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism, share several foundational concepts that distinguish them from the linear worldview of the West. Most prominently, they hold to a cyclical view of time and existence known as Samsara, where life is seen as a repeating cycle of birth, death, and rebirth. Closely tied to this is the principle of Karma, the law of moral cause and effect, which teaches that a person's intentional actions in this life determine their status and suffering in the next. Consequently, the ultimate goal in these traditions is liberation, often called Moksha or Nirvana, which is the release from this cycle of suffering and the attainment of a state of stillness or union with the ultimate reality.

Furthermore, many Eastern traditions lean toward monism or pantheism, the belief that the divine is an impersonal force inseparable from the universe itself, rather than a personal, transcendent Creator who exists apart from His creation. While these systems offer rigorous disciplines of meditation and ethical living to overcome ignorance or desire, the Holy Scriptures present a different and glorious reality.

The Bible teaches that time is linear, moving from a definite creation to a final judgment and the renewal of all things. We do not face a thousand lifetimes to get things right; rather, it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment, as stated in Hebrews 9:27. Our primary problem is not ignorance of our own divinity or the burden of past-life karma, but a personal rebellion against a holy and loving God, a condition the Bible calls sin. 

Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply overlook sin, yet because He is rich in mercy, He provided the solution we could never achieve through our own efforts. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, entered our linear history through the incarnation and virgin birth. He lived the perfect life we failed to live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over death and remains the historical fact upon which our hope rests.

While other paths suggest we must climb a ladder of self-improvement or enlightenment to reach the divine, the Gospel is the good news that God came down to us. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, as seen in Ephesians 2:8-9. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today? 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel according to John in the New Testament. It beautifully illustrates the person of Jesus as the Light of the World who gives life to all who believe. You might also consider visiting a local Bible-teaching church where you can hear the Word of God proclaimed and find a community of believers to walk alongside you. John 1:1-14; John 14:6; Acts 4:12; Romans 3:23-26.
What is the bottomless pit (Revelation 9:1-12)?

The bottomless pit, or the "Abyss" from the Greek word *abyssos*, is a place of confinement for fallen angels and demonic spirits who are under God's judicial restraint. It represents a realm of spiritual darkness and imprisonment, distinct from the final Lake of Fire, where these rebellious beings are held until their appointed time of judgment as seen in Revelation 9:1-11 and Revelation 20:1-3.

In the vision given to the Apostle John, the opening of this pit by a "star fallen from heaven" signifies a period of intense spiritual judgment. The smoke that rises and the swarm of locust-like creatures describe a release of demonic activity that brings profound torment to those who have rejected God. While the imagery is vivid and sobering, the theological center of this passage is the absolute sovereignty of God. Even the powers of darkness cannot move without His permission, for the key to the shaft is granted to the angelic being, and the demons are given strict boundaries regarding whom they may harm and for how long.

This passage highlights the reality of spiritual warfare and the gravity of God's wrath against sin. The demons themselves recognize the authority of Christ over this prison, as seen when they begged Jesus not to command them to go into the "abyss" in Luke 8:31. For the believer, this passage serves as a profound comfort rather than a source of terror. You may notice that those who belong to Christ are marked with the "seal of God on their foreheads" (Revelation 9:4), signifying that they are protected and owned by Him.

If you find your heart troubled by the reality of such spiritual forces, remember that the Lord is a shield for all who take refuge in Him. I encourage you to read through Psalm 91 today and meditate on the truth that God is your fortress. Let this lead you to pray, asking the Lord to deepen your trust in His protective hand and His victory over the powers of darkness.

You may wonder how one can be certain of this protection amidst such a fallen world. The Gospel is the only sure foundation. Because God is holy, He must judge sin, but because He is loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take that judgment upon Himself. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and the grave. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What is biblical theology?

Biblical theology is the study of the Bible as a unified, historical narrative that reveals God’s redemptive plan from Genesis to Revelation. It focuses on how God’s truth is unfolded over time, showing how every part of Scripture contributes to a single, grand story centered on the person and work of Jesus Christ.

While systematic theology organizes biblical truths into categories (such as the nature of God or the doctrine of salvation), biblical theology tracks the development of these truths through history. This approach recognizes what we call progressive revelation, the reality that God did not reveal His entire plan at once, but rather made it known in increasing clarity through successive covenants and historical events (Hebrews 1:1; Hebrews 1:2). 

At its heart, biblical theology is intensely Christ-centered. When our Lord walked with the disciples on the road to Emmaus, He explained to them the things concerning Himself in all the Scriptures, beginning with Moses and all the Prophets (Luke 24:27). To engage in biblical theology is to follow that same path, seeing how the shadows of the Old Testament find their substance and fulfillment in the light of the New Testament (Colossians 2:17). It teaches us that the Bible is not merely a collection of moralistic stories, but a record of God's sovereign acts to rescue a people for His own glory through the life, death, and resurrection of His Son.

Understanding this framework changes how you read your Bible. Instead of seeing the Old Testament as a separate or outdated book, you begin to see it as the necessary foundation for the Gospel. You see that the promises made to Abraham and the laws given to Moses are all pointing toward the one Mediator, Jesus Christ, who justifies us by grace alone through faith alone (Galatians 3:24).

As a practical next step in your discipleship, I encourage you to choose a theme, such as "the presence of God" or "the kingdom," and trace it from the Garden of Eden through the Tabernacle and Temple, until you see its ultimate fulfillment in Christ and the New Jerusalem. Reading a book of the Bible like Hebrews is an excellent way to see biblical theology in action, as it masterfully connects the Old Covenant rituals to the superior priesthood of Jesus. Set aside time this week to ask the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to the beauty of Christ on every page of His Word.
Can people in heaven look down and see those of us who are still on the earth?

Scripture does not explicitly state whether those who have passed into the presence of the Lord can look down and observe the specific daily activities of those still on earth. While the Bible provides glimpses into the heavenly realm, it focuses more on the glory of God and the rest of the saints than on their awareness of earthly events.

The most common passage cited regarding this question is Hebrews 12:1, which speaks of a great cloud of witnesses surrounding us. Many faithful Christians interpret this in one of two ways. Some believe it suggests that the saints in heaven are like spectators in a stadium, watching our progress in the Christian life. Others, however, observe the context of Hebrews 11 and conclude that these witnesses are not watching us, but rather their lives and testimonies bear witness to us of God’s faithfulness. In this view, their finished race encourages us to run our own with endurance.

There are, however, indications that those in heaven possess at least some awareness of significant spiritual events on earth. Jesus taught that there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner who repents in Luke 15:7; Luke 15:10. Additionally, in the book of Revelation, the martyrs in heaven are aware that justice has not yet been fully executed on the earth, crying out to God to ask how long it will be before He judges those who dwell there in Revelation 6:10. These passages suggest that those in the intermediate state, the period between death and the final resurrection, have a perspective focused on the progress of God’s kingdom and the vindication of His holiness.

It is helpful to remember that heaven is a place of perfect joy where God will wipe away every tear, as promised in Revelation 21:4. If the saints were constantly witnessing the suffering and sins of their loved ones without seeing the ultimate end of God's redemptive plan, it might seem to conflict with their perfect peace. However, we can be certain that because they are with Christ, they see all things through the lens of His sovereign victory and perfect love.

Whether or not they see us, we know they are in a state of far better joy, as Paul writes in Philippians 1:23. They are occupied with the beatific vision, seeing the Lord face to face, and they await the day when we will all be reunited at the resurrection.

If you are grieving or wondering about a loved one, a helpful next step in your discipleship is to turn your focus to the one whom the saints in heaven are currently worshiping. Take time today to read Hebrews 12:1-2 and ask God to help you fix your eyes on Jesus, the author and perfecter of our faith, just as those who went before us did.

If you are uncertain about your own place in that heavenly cloud of witnesses, know that the Bible is clear about how we may be reconciled to God. Because God is holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are naturally separated from Him. Yet, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who are the Free Will Baptists, and what do they believe?

Free Will Baptists are a family of evangelical Christian churches that belong to the Arminian tradition of the Baptist faith, tracing their organized history in America back to the early 1700s through the labors of men like Paul Palmer in the South and Benjamin Randall in the North. They are distinguished by their belief that God's saving grace is offered to all people and that individuals, through the influence of the Holy Spirit, possess the freedom of will to either accept or reject the Gospel call.

In matters of doctrine, Free Will Baptists hold firmly to the authority of Scripture and the necessity of the New Birth. Their theological distinctives are often categorized by the following points:

**General Atonement**
They believe that Jesus Christ died as a sacrifice for the sins of the entire world, not only for a specific group of people. They frequently point to the "whosoever" invitations of the Bible, believing that God sincerely desires all people to be saved and come to the knowledge of the truth.
Supporting passages: John 3:16; 1 Timothy 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9.

**Free Will and Prevenient Grace**
While they affirm that human nature is fallen and corrupted by sin, they believe in "prevenient grace," which is the grace that goes before salvation. This grace, provided by the Holy Spirit, enables a sinner to respond to God's call. They hold that while no one can seek God on their own power, God gives everyone enough light and grace to make a genuine choice to trust in Christ.
Supporting passages: John 1:9; Titus 2:11.

**Conditional Perseverance**
Unlike many other Baptist groups who hold to the "perseverance of the saints" (the belief that a true believer can never lose their salvation), Free Will Baptists believe that salvation is conditional upon continued faith. They teach that it is possible for a genuine believer to abandon their faith and, through persistent unbelief and unrepentant sin, "fall from grace" and be lost. They emphasize the need for believers to remain watchful and steadfast in their relationship with Christ.
Supporting passages: Matthew 24:13; Hebrews 6:4-6; Revelation 2:10.

**The Ordinances**
Like almost all Baptists, they practice believer's baptism by immersion and observe the Lord's Supper as a memorial of Christ's death. However, Free Will Baptists are unique in that many of them also practice the Washing of the Saints' Feet as a third ordinance. They view this as an act of humility and service commanded by Christ during the Last Supper.
Supporting passages: John 13:14-15; 1 Timothy 5:10.

**Open Communion**
While some Baptist groups practice "closed communion" (restricted to members of that specific local church or denomination), Free Will Baptists generally practice "open communion," inviting all believers who are in good standing with the Lord to participate in the Lord's Supper.

It is helpful to remember that while we may have significant theological discussions regarding the mechanics of how God's sovereignty and human will interact, Free Will Baptists are our brothers and sisters in Christ who love the Lord and the authority of His Word. If you are exploring these different views, a wonderful next step would be to read through the Gospel of John and the book of Romans, noting every time the Scripture speaks of God's choosing and every time it speaks of man's responsibility to believe. Ask the Holy Spirit to give you clarity and a heart that rests in the finished work of Christ.
What does it mean that you are the head and not the tail in Deuteronomy 28:13?

To be the head and not the tail means to occupy a position of leadership, dignity, and divine favor rather than being subservient, oppressed, or dependent on others. In the context of Deuteronomy 28:13, it is a metaphor for status and influence, the head leads and directs, while the tail is merely pulled along by external forces.

This promise was originally given to the nation of Israel as part of the Mosaic Covenant. God promised that if the people were diligent to obey His commandments, He would set them high above all the nations of the earth (Deuteronomy 28:1). Specifically, being the head meant they would be the lenders and not the borrowers, and they would be characterized by a steady upward trajectory in their national life (Deuteronomy 28:12; Deuteronomy 28:13).

However, it is vital to see that this blessing was strictly conditional upon perfect obedience. The text says these blessings come "if you listen to the commandments of the LORD your God" (Deuteronomy 28:13). Sadly, the history of Israel shows a consistent failure to keep this covenant, leading instead to the "curses" described later in the same chapter, where they became the "tail" through exile and subjugation (Deuteronomy 28:43; Deuteronomy 28:44).

For the believer today, we must understand this through the lens of the Gospel and the work of Jesus Christ. Because no human being could perfectly obey God's law, we all fell under the curse of disobedience. Jesus Christ, the true and faithful Israelite, lived the life of perfect obedience that we could not live. He earned the blessing of being the "Head" over all things (Ephesians 1:22).

When we are united to Christ by faith, we are no longer defined by our own failures but by His righteousness. Christ was treated as the "tail" on the cross, bearing our shame and the curse of the law, so that we might be brought into the family of God and share in His victory (Galatians 3:13). Our identity as "the head" is not necessarily a promise of worldly wealth or secular power, but a spiritual reality where we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us (Romans 8:37). We are given the dignity of being God’s children, set apart to lead others to the truth of the Gospel.

You may wonder if your current struggles mean God has abandoned this promise to you. Rest assured that your standing with God is not based on your daily performance or your bank account, but on the finished work of Jesus. He is the sovereign Head of the Church, and He is conforming you to His image (Colossians 1:18).

As an actionable step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read Deuteronomy 28 alongside Galatians 3 this week. Reflect on how Jesus Christ took the curse mentioned in Deuteronomy so that the blessings of Abraham might come to you through faith. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness and new life that only He can provide.

What do you find most challenging about trusting God's favor when life feels like you are "the tail"?
What is theistic agnosticism / agnostic theism?

Agnostic theism, or theistic agnosticism, is the philosophical position where an individual believes in the existence of a deity but maintains that the nature or the very existence of that deity is unknown or inherently unknowable. While the theist part of the term signifies a belief in God, the agnostic part reflects a claim that one lacks certain knowledge or proof to support that belief.

You may wonder if it is a sign of intellectual humility to admit that a finite human mind cannot fully grasp an infinite God. It is certainly true that we cannot know God exhaustively, but the Holy Scriptures teach that we can know Him truly because He has chosen to reveal Himself to us.

The Bible presents God not as a distant or hidden enigma, but as a Creator who has made His power and divine nature evident to all people. This is often called general revelation, as seen in Romans 1:19-20, which states, "Since what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood from His workmanship, so that men are without excuse."

Beyond the physical world, God has provided special revelation through His Word and, most perfectly, through His Son, Jesus Christ. While we are finite, God is personal and has spoken in history to ensure we are not left to mere guesswork or uncertain "theism." Hebrews 1:1-2 says, "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son, whom He appointed heir of all things, and through whom also He made the universe."

In Christian theology, we speak of the incomprehensibility of God, which means we can never know everything there is to know about Him. However, this is quite different from agnosticism. Because of Christ, we can have a settled, certain knowledge of God’s character, His will, and His love. Jesus said in John 17:3, "Now this is eternal life: that they may know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom You have sent."

The most profound way God has made Himself known is through the Gospel. Though we have all sinned and are separated from a holy God by our rebellion, God did not leave us in ignorance or judgment. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He was declared with power to be the Son of God, offering certain hope rather than vague belief.

You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness of sins and the gift of knowing Him personally. What is it that makes the idea of "knowing" God seem out of reach for you?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the New Testament. It was written specifically so that you might believe that Jesus is the Christ and that by believing you may have life in His name. Seek Him in prayer, asking Him to clear away the fog of uncertainty and show you the light of His truth.
The Fruit of the Holy Spirit – What is kindness?

Biblical kindness is a gracious, helpful, and tender disposition that seeks the well-being of others, modeled after the very character of God. It is a supernatural product of the Holy Spirit's work in a believer, often described by the theological term *chrēstotēs*, which refers to a goodness that is useful, mellow, and gentle in its expression.

Kindness is far more than mere social politeness or a naturally pleasant temperament. It is a divine attribute that flows from a heart transformed by the Gospel. In Galatians 5:22, kindness is listed as a fruit of the Spirit, meaning it is a quality grown in the life of a Christian by God Himself as we abide in Christ. This kindness is most vividly seen in how God has dealt with us. Though God is perfectly holy and we were once His enemies, separated from Him by our sin and rebellion, He reached out to us in mercy. Titus 3:4-5 declares, "But when the kindness and love of God our Savior appeared, He saved us, not because of righteous things we had done, but because of His mercy. He saved us through the washing of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit."

To truly practice biblical kindness, one must first be a recipient of God’s kindness through salvation. The Gospel is the message that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, ascending to the Father as Lord of all. Salvation is not earned by our own efforts but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God today, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting in His kindness toward you?

For those who follow Christ, kindness is an outworking of progressive sanctification. It is the Spirit enabling us to be "kind and compassionate to one another, forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). It involves a willingness to be "useful" to others, putting their needs above our own comfort, just as our Savior did for us.

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to look for one person this week who is often overlooked or perhaps difficult to be around. Purposefully seek a way to show them the kindness of Christ through a practical act of service or a word of sincere encouragement. As you do, pray and ask the Holy Spirit to continue softening your heart, making you a vessel of His grace to a world that is often harsh and unforgiving. Seek the strength to be kind from the Lord directly, for His yoke is easy and His burden is light (Matthew 11:30).
How should Christians view the idea of reparations for slavery?

The question of reparations for slavery is a matter that touches on the deep biblical themes of justice, restitution, and the lasting impact of sin. Christians should view this issue through the lens of Scripture, recognizing that while all believers agree on the gravity of the sin of slavery, they often differ on the specific application of justice in a contemporary political context.

At the heart of this discussion is the biblical concept of restitution, which is the act of restoring something to its proper owner or making amends for a wrong. In the Law of Moses, God established clear requirements for restitution when property was stolen or damage was done to another (Exodus 22:1; Leviticus 6:1-5). In the New Testament, we see the beautiful example of Zacchaeus, whose repentance was marked by a voluntary and fourfold restitution to those he had defrauded (Luke 19:8). These passages demonstrate that God values the tangible righting of wrongs.

Because this is a tertiary issue involving the application of biblical principles to complex historical and political systems, faithful Christians hold differing views.

One perspective emphasizes corporate responsibility and the enduring nature of systemic injustice. Those who hold this view point out that the institution of chattel slavery was a massive, state-sanctioned theft of labor, dignity, and life from millions made in the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27). They argue that because the economic and social advantages of slavery were passed down through generations, while the disadvantages were likewise inherited, modern society has a moral obligation to provide some form of corporate restitution to address these lingering inequities. This view often cites the biblical precedent of God dealing with nations or families as a whole for persistent sins (Daniel 9:4-19; 2 Samuel 21:1).

Another perspective emphasizes individual responsibility and the limits of civil restitution across generations. Those who hold this view often cite Ezekiel 18:20, which states that "The son will not bear the punishment for the father’s iniquity, nor will the father bear the punishment for the son’s iniquity." They argue that it is difficult to justly administer reparations when neither the perpetrators nor the direct victims are still living. From this viewpoint, the focus should be on ensuring equal justice under the law today and practicing personal charity rather than implementing state-mandated wealth redistribution for ancestral sins.

Regardless of where a Christian lands on the specific policy of reparations, we must all agree that slavery was a heinous sin that denied the Vera Deus (True God) by mistreating His image-bearers. We are called to "seek justice, encourage the oppressed" (Isaiah 1:17) and to "love your neighbor as yourself" (Matthew 22:39).

As a practical step, consider studying the history of your local community or church to see if there are specific, tangible ways you can serve and uplift those who still feel the effects of historical marginalization. This might include supporting educational initiatives, local missions, or community development.

Ultimately, we must remember that while human efforts at justice are necessary, they are often incomplete. True and lasting reconciliation between people is found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. It is through His sacrifice on the cross that the "wall of hostility" is broken down (Ephesians 2:14). We look forward to the day when Christ returns to judge the world in perfect righteousness and to wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4). If you find your heart burdened by the injustices of this world, know that there is hope in Jesus, who took the ultimate injustice upon Himself to bring us peace with God. You can turn to Him today, repenting of sin and trusting in His finished work for your salvation.
Who are we to pray to, the Father, the Son, or the Holy Spirit?

We primarily pray to God the Father, through the mediation of God the Son, and by the power of God the Holy Spirit. Because God eternally exists as one God in three persons, the Trinity, it is biblically appropriate to address any Person of the Godhead, though the most common biblical pattern is directed toward the Father.

Jesus Christ, the Son of God, established the fundamental model for our prayer life when He instructed His followers to pray, "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name" Matthew 6:9. This reveals the beautiful reality that, through Christ, we are brought into a family relationship with God. Our access to the Father is made possible only through the person and work of Jesus, who serves as our Advocate and Great High Priest. He told His disciples, "And I will do whatever you ask in My name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son" John 14:13. To pray in the name of Jesus is to rely on His merit and authority rather than our own.

The Holy Spirit also plays an essential role in our communion with God. He indwells every believer, illuminating the Scriptures and strengthening our souls. The Bible says, "In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know how we ought to pray, but the Spirit Himself intercedes for us with groans too deep for words" Romans 8:26. We can ask the Holy Spirit to help us pray according to the will of God when we are at a loss for words.

If you find yourself wondering how to approach such a holy God, remember that the Gospel is the ground of our confidence. We are all sinners separated from God by our rebellion, but God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ to live a perfect life, die on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave to defeat death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the Psalms this week. Notice how the psalmists cry out to God with honesty and reverence, and use their inspired words to shape your own conversations with the Lord.

What has your experience with prayer been like recently?
Why does God hate sin?

God hates sin because it is fundamentally contrary to His holy and righteous nature. As the source of all light, life, and goodness, God must oppose everything that is dark, destructive, and evil, for His eyes are too pure to look upon evil (Habakkuk 1:13).

To understand this more deeply, we must look at the character of God. God is infinitely holy, which means He is set apart and absolutely pure (Isaiah 6:3). Sin is lawlessness; it is a direct assault on His authority and a refusal to honor Him as the Creator (1 John 3:4; Romans 1:21). In theological terms, we speak of God’s "impeccability" and His "righteousness." Because He is the standard of all that is right, He cannot be indifferent toward what is wrong. His hatred of sin is not a reckless temper, but a settled, holy opposition to anything that contradicts His excellence.

Furthermore, God hates sin because of what it does to the creatures He loves. Sin is a parasite that distorts the "Imago Dei," the image of God, within us. It brings death, suffering, and brokenness into a world that He created to be "very good" (Genesis 1:31; Romans 5:12). Just as a loving father hates the disease that ravages his child, God hates the sin that enslaves and destroys human souls. Sin causes a separation between us and our Maker, cutting us off from the only source of true joy and life (Isaiah 59:2).

You may wonder how any of us can survive if God’s hatred of sin is so absolute. It is a terrifying thing to realize that our own hearts are prone to the very thing God abhors. However, the Gospel is the beautiful news of how God’s holy justice and His infinite love meet. Because God is just, sin must be punished; but because He is merciful, He provided the sacrifice.

God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a life of perfect obedience. On the cross, Jesus took the place of sinners, bearing the full weight of God’s righteous wrath against sin (2 Corinthians 5:21). He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, signifying His victory over death and the total satisfaction of God’s justice (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive complete forgiveness and a new heart. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to read through the book of 1 John. It beautifully describes the contrast between light and darkness and will help you understand how a believer can grow in "sanctification," which is the process of becoming more like Christ and learning to love what God loves. Seek out a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached, so you may grow in the company of other believers.
What will we look like in Heaven?

In eternity, we will possess physical, immortal, and glorified bodies that are modeled after the resurrected body of our Lord Jesus Christ. While we do not know every specific detail of our future appearance, the Scriptures assure us that the transformation will be both radical and wonderful, preserving our individual identities while removing every trace of the fall.

The Bible teaches that our current bodies are like seeds planted in the ground, which must perish to give way to the glorious life that follows. The Apostle Paul explains that the body is sown in corruption but raised in incorruption, sown in dishonor but raised in glory, and sown in weakness but raised in power as seen in 1 Corinthians 15:42-43. This means that every ailment, disability, and sign of aging that we endure in this life will be swallowed up by the vitality of the resurrection. Our lowly bodies will be transformed to be like His glorious body according to Philippians 3:21.

We can look to Jesus after His resurrection to understand what we might be like. He was recognizable to His disciples, He could eat, and He possessed a physical form that could be touched, yet He was no longer bound by the limitations of a fallen world as recorded in Luke 24:39-43 and John 20:27. This suggests a continuity of identity, you will still be you, but you will be the most vibrant, holy, and perfected version of yourself, entirely free from sin and its effects. We shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is according to 1 John 3:2.

For those who currently suffer from chronic pain, terminal illness, or the frailty of old age, this truth is a profound comfort. God does not merely intend to save your soul; He intends to redeem your entire person, body and spirit. In the New Heavens and the New Earth, God will wipe away every tear, and death and pain will be no more according to Revelation 21:4.

If you find yourself weary of the "earthly tent" of your current body, I encourage you to spend time this week reading 1 Corinthians 15. Let the promise of the resurrection steady your heart.

This glorious future is the inheritance of all who belong to Christ. It is a gift of grace for those who recognize their need for a Savior. We were created for God’s glory but separated from Him by our sin. However, God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death forever. Salvation is found through grace alone by faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive this hope of eternal life.

Have you ever considered what it means to be fully restored in the presence of God?
What is sola scriptura?

Sola scriptura is the conviction that the Bible is the only infallible and final authority for the faith, life, and doctrines of the Christian church. This principle declares that because the Scriptures are inspired by God, they hold a unique and supreme position that no human tradition, church decree, or personal experience can equal (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

This doctrine emphasizes the sufficiency of the Word. While God has provided the church with godly teachers and a rich history, these resources are always subordinate to the Bible. If any teaching or tradition contradicts the written Word, the Word must be obeyed, for it is the very breath of God (Matthew 15:3-9; Acts 17:11). Because the Scriptures are inerrant and authoritative, they serve as the final court of appeal for every spiritual matter.

The practical effect of this truth is that you can rest your soul on the promises of God without fear of being misled by human error. This is a great mercy, for the Bible is not merely a book of rules, but the revelation of God's plan to reconcile a fallen world to Himself. It is through these holy writings that we learn the Gospel: that God is holy, yet we are sinners separated from Him. In His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Scriptures, perhaps starting with the Gospel of John. Ask the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to the beauty of Christ as you read. It is also vital to seek out a local church that honors the Bible as its ultimate authority, where you can hear the Word preached and find fellowship with other believers.
What did Jesus mean when He said He would give us “whatever you ask in my name”?

When Jesus promised to grant whatever we ask in His name, He was teaching that our prayers should be offered in accordance with His character, His authority, and His sovereign will for the ultimate purpose of God’s glory. This is not a magic formula to secure our personal whims, but a call to deep spiritual alignment with our Savior.

In the biblical world, a person's name represented their entire character, reputation, and mission. Therefore, to ask "in His name" is to ask for what Jesus Himself would want. It is an exercise of our union with Christ, where our hearts become so intertwined with His that our desires begin to mirror His own. Jesus explains the purpose of this promise in John 14:13, saying, "And I will do whatever you ask in My name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son." The goal of answered prayer is always the manifestation of God's glory rather than the satisfaction of our selfish "passions" (James 4:3).

This promise is also inseparable from our "abiding" in Him. In John 15:7, Jesus says, "If you remain in Me and My words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you." As we saturate our minds with the Word of God, our prayers are increasingly shaped by Scripture. We find great confidence in knowing that "if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" (1 John 5:14).

It is a wonderful mercy that God does not treat this as a blank check for our every wandering thought. If He granted every request we made in our ignorance or selfishness, it would often lead to our harm. Instead, like a loving Father, He filters our requests through His perfect wisdom. As the prince of preachers, Charles Spurgeon, once noted, prayer is not a way to bend God's will to ours, but a way to bring our will into sunlight with His.

This access to the Father is only possible because of the finished work of Jesus Christ. Because we are sinners, we have no right to approach a holy God on our own merit. But Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now our Great High Priest who intercedes for us (Hebrews 4:14-16). 

If you find yourself wondering why your prayers seem unheard or how you can have this kind of relationship with God, remember that the greatest request anyone can make is for the forgiveness of sins. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

As a practical step in your prayer life, I encourage you to use the "Lord’s Prayer" in Matthew 6:9-13 as a structural guide. Before bringing your personal needs to God, spend time acknowledging His holiness and asking for His kingdom and His will to be done in your life and in the world around you. This simple rhythm helps align your heart with His "name" before you make your specific requests. What is one specific area of your life where you are currently struggling to submit your will to His?
What does the Bible say about witchcraft? Should a Christian fear witchcraft?

The Bible describes witchcraft as the practice of attempting to gain supernatural power, knowledge, or influence through occult means rather than through the Holy Spirit. Scripture consistently identifies it as an abomination to the Lord and a work of the flesh that stands in direct rebellion against God's authority. Deuteronomy 18:10-12; Galatians 5:19-21.

To understand the biblical perspective, we must see witchcraft for what it is: an attempt to be like God without submitting to God. In the Old Testament, God strictly forbade His people from engaging in sorcery, divination, or consulting mediums because these practices represent a lack of trust in His sovereign care and a turning toward "worthless idols." 1 Samuel 15:23; 2 Kings 21:6. Witchcraft seeks to manipulate the spiritual realm for personal ends, whereas the Christian life is defined by humble submission to the will of the Father.

Regarding fear, a believer in Jesus Christ has no reason to live in dread of witchcraft, curses, or demonic influence. While the Bible acknowledges the reality of spiritual forces of evil, it emphatically declares that they are defeated foes. At the cross, Jesus disarmed the powers and authorities, making a public spectacle of them. Colossians 2:15. If you belong to Christ, you are indwelt by the Holy Spirit, and the Word of God reminds us that "He who is in you is greater than he who is in the world." 1 John 4:4.

The "sovereignty" of God means that not a single sparrow falls to the ground apart from His will, and certainly, no dark power can bypass His protective hand over His children. Matthew 10:29-31. Rather than fearing the darkness, we are called to put on the full armor of God so that we may stand firm. Ephesians 6:11. Our safety is not found in superstitious rituals or "counter-spells," but in the finished work of Jesus Christ and our position in Him.

If you find yourself troubled by fear or a past involvement in the occult, remember that the Gospel is the power of God for salvation. God is perfectly holy, and our sin, including the sin of seeking power apart from Him, separates us from Him. However, Jesus Christ, the Vera Homo and Vera Deus (True Man and True God), lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin, death, and the devil. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes the idea of spiritual darkness feel so threatening to you today?

As a practical step for your discipleship, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Psalms, specifically Psalm 27 and Psalm 91, which highlight God’s role as our fortress and shield. Additionally, if you are experiencing a particular spiritual struggle, please reach out to the elders of a faithful, Bible-teaching local church for prayer and counsel. James 5:14.
Why am I here?

You are here because you were created by God, of His own good pleasure and for His eternal glory, to know Him and to enjoy Him forever. This is the foundational truth of our existence, that we are not accidents of nature or products of chance, but the deliberate handiwork of a loving and sovereign Creator.

Scripture teaches that God created mankind in His own image, the imago Dei, so that we might reflect His character and represent Him within His creation. Genesis 1:27; Isaiah 43:7. However, we often feel a sense of aimlessness because our relationship with God has been fractured by sin. When we attempt to find our purpose in work, relationships, or self-fulfillment rather than in our Creator, we find that our souls remain restless. Romans 3:23; Romans 1:25.

You may wonder if a life that feels broken or insignificant can truly have a divine purpose. The beautiful news of the Gospel is that God did not abandon us to our wandering. Because God is holy and just, He must punish sin, yet because He is merciful and gracious, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to restore us. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He has ascended to the right hand of the Father and will one day return to judge the world and make all things new. Colossians 1:16–20; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4.

Through Christ, your purpose is not merely to exist, but to be reconciled to God and transformed into the likeness of His Son. This salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we perform. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written so that you might believe that Jesus is the Christ and have life in His name. John 20:31. I also invite you to speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to you and to lead you to a faithful local church where His Word is preached.
Did Adam and Eve have belly buttons / navels?

The Bible does not explicitly state whether Adam and Eve had belly buttons, as the Holy Spirit focused the record of Genesis on the theological and historical significance of their creation rather than their specific anatomical details. Because this is a matter where the Scriptures are silent, it is considered a tertiary issue where faithful Christians may hold different views with charity and liberty (Romans 14:1-5).

One common perspective is that Adam and Eve did not have belly buttons. This view rests on the historical fact that Adam was formed directly by God from the dust of the ground (Genesis 2:7) and Eve was fashioned from Adam's rib (Genesis 2:21-22). Since a navel is a scar resulting from the detachment of the umbilical cord after birth, and since neither Adam nor Eve were born of a woman, many conclude they would have lacked this particular physical mark.

Another perspective suggests that God created Adam and Eve as "prototypical" humans with the full appearance of maturity. Just as the trees in the Garden of Eden were likely created already bearing fruit rather than starting as seeds, some believe God created the first couple with complete human anatomy, including navels, to serve as the perfect model for all human generations to follow.

While we may wonder about these physical details, the most important truth is that Adam and Eve were real, historical people created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27). This unique status gave them the capacity to know, love, and fellowship with their Creator. However, through their disobedience, sin entered the world, bringing death and separation from God to all their descendants (Romans 5:12).

The story of our first parents points us toward our need for a Savior. Just as the first Adam brought death, the "Last Adam," Jesus Christ, brings life (1 Corinthians 15:45). God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. Have you ever considered how the miraculous way God created us points to our need for Him?
What is an appropriate level of physical intimacy before marriage?

The Bible teaches that physical intimacy is a beautiful gift created by God, designed to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman. The appropriate level of physical intimacy before marriage is that which preserves the purity of both individuals and does not stir up desires that cannot be righteously fulfilled outside of the marriage bed. Hebrews 13:4; 1 Thessalonians 4:3–5.

In the Scriptures, we are commanded to flee from sexual immorality, or "porneia," which encompasses all sexual activity outside of marriage. 1 Corinthians 6:18. While the Bible does not provide a specific checklist of which touch is permitted, it calls us to a standard of holiness that seeks the neighbor's good and God's glory rather than the satisfaction of the flesh. We must remember that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, bought with the precious blood of Christ. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20. If an action rouses sexual passion or leads toward the "threshold" of the sexual act, it is a violation of the call to treat one another with absolute purity. 1 Timothy 5:2.

You may wonder if such a standard is possible or even necessary in a world that views physical freedom as a right. It is helpful to recognize that the desire for intimacy is a God-given reflection of our need for connection, but when we take that gift outside of His design, we often end up wounding our souls and the souls of those we care about. True love is not defined by how much it can take, but by how much it is willing to protect. 

For those who have already crossed these boundaries and feel the weight of guilt or shame, there is profound hope. We all fall short of God’s perfect holiness, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfectly pure life we could not live and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, including our sexual sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by our own efforts to be "clean enough." You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive total forgiveness and a new life. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust Him with your purity today?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to sit down with your partner and a mature couple from your local church to establish clear, written boundaries. Decide together that you will avoid secluded places and late-night settings that provide unnecessary temptation. Romans 13:14. Seek to build your relationship on the foundation of shared prayer, the study of the Word of God, and the pursuit of Christlikeness, trusting that God’s ways are always for your ultimate joy. 1 Peter 1:15–16.
What are the unfruitful works of darkness (Ephesians 5:11)?

The unfruitful works of darkness are those sinful behaviors, attitudes, and speech that characterize a life lived in rebellion against God, yielding no spiritual life but only corruption and death (Ephesians 5:11). These works are the direct opposite of the fruit of the Spirit, which consists of all goodness, righteousness, and truth (Ephesians 5:9).

In the immediate context of this passage, the Apostle Paul identifies these works specifically as sexual immorality, impurity, greed, filthiness, foolish talk, and crude joking (Ephesians 5:3; Ephesians 5:4). These are called "unfruitful" because, while they may provide a passing season of worldly pleasure, they produce nothing of eternal value and ultimately lead to the righteous judgment of God (Ephesians 5:6). They are "works of darkness" because they belong to the kingdom of this world and the power of Satan, seeking to hide from the piercing light of God’s holiness (John 3:19; John 3:20).

For the believer, these works are part of a former identity that has been put away. Through the process of progressive sanctification, the Holy Spirit enables us to walk as children of light (Ephesians 5:8). We are commanded not only to avoid participating in these sins but to expose them by living lives that reflect the purity and truth of Jesus Christ. True love does not coddle sin but shines the light of truth upon it so that the soul might be healed.

As an actionable step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray through Psalm 139:23 and Psalm 139:24. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any "unfruitful work" that may be lingering in your heart or habits, then take the step of confessing it to God and a trusted, mature believer in your local church to find strength and accountability.

You may wonder how anyone can stand before a God of such pure light when our own hearts feel so shadowed. The Gospel is the good news that although we have all walked in darkness and deserve God's wrath, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfectly sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death forever. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can an unmarried person relieve sexual tension in a non-sinful way?

The Bible teaches that sexual desire is a good gift from God, designed to be fulfilled exclusively within the covenant of marriage between a man and a woman (Hebrews 13:4). For the unmarried person, the "relief" of this tension is not found in seeking a physical outlet outside of marriage, but in the spiritual discipline of self-control and the stewardship of one's body as a temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19-20).

Scripture does not provide a "non-sinful" way to indulge sexual tension outside of marriage, as the physical act and the lustful thoughts that often accompany it are reserved for the marital union (Matthew 5:27-28). Instead, the believer is called to the process of sanctification, which involves "mortifying" or putting to death the earthly desires of the flesh (Colossians 3:5).

To navigate these natural desires in a way that honors Christ, consider these practical and pastoral steps:

1. **Physical Stewardship:** Since we are embodied souls, physical energy can be redirected. Engaging in regular, vigorous exercise is a helpful way to manage the body's natural vitality and stress. God has given us our bodies to use for His glory, and physical discipline can be a means of bringing our "body into subjection" (1 Corinthians 9:27).

2. **Mental Renewal:** Sexual tension often begins in the mind. Paul instructs us to be "transformed by the renewing of your mind" (Romans 12:2). This involves "taking every thought captive to obey Christ" (2 Corinthians 10:5). When feelings arise, consciously pivot your focus toward prayer or the study of God's Word.

3. **Community and Accountability:** Isolation is often where temptation grows strongest. Seek deep, transparent fellowship within your local church. Having a trusted, mature brother or sister in Christ to whom you can confess your struggles and who will pray for you is a vital means of grace (James 5:16).

4. **Devotion to Mission:** Use the unique season of singleness to serve the Lord with "undivided devotion" (1 Corinthians 7:35). Often, the intensity of sexual tension can be mitigated by being so consumed with the work of the Gospel and the service of others that your primary focus is shifted away from yourself.

If you find yourself overwhelmed, remember that Jesus Christ is our high priest who was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15). He offers grace and mercy in our time of need. You might begin by asking God in prayer for the gift of self-control, which is a fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23), and for a heart that finds its ultimate satisfaction in Him rather than in physical gratification.

For those who feel the weight of past failures in this area, the Gospel offers total forgiveness. Christ’s sacrificial death on the cross is sufficient to wash away every sin. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

Is there a specific area of your routine or thought life where you feel most vulnerable to this struggle?
What does it mean to be devout?

To be devout is to live with a heart sincerely consecrated to God, where one's thoughts, affections, and actions are habitually directed toward His glory and honor. In the biblical sense, it describes a person whose life is marked by a deep, reverent awe of God and a consistent commitment to His ways.

This quality is often referred to as piety, which is the fruit of the Holy Spirit’s work in a believer’s life, leading to a sincere desire for godliness and worship. Scripture highlights individuals like Simeon, who was described as righteous and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel (Luke 2:25). Similarly, Cornelius is identified as a devout man who feared God with all his household, gave alms generously, and prayed continually (Acts 10:2; Acts 10:7). True devotion is not a cold, external ritual, but an internal posture of love and loyalty to the Lord that overflows into every area of life.

You may wonder if your own devotion could ever be sufficient to satisfy a perfectly holy God. The reality is that our sin separates us from Him, and even our best religious efforts are stained by our fallen nature. No amount of human devotion or personal "goodness" can bridge the gap between a sinner and a holy Creator.

However, the beauty of the Gospel is that God provided what we could never achieve. Jesus Christ, who is eternally God, took on human flesh, lived a life of perfect devotion, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death and providing the only way for us to be reconciled to God. We are justified by grace alone through faith in Christ, not by our own acts of piety.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Bible and talking to God in prayer. You might start by reading one chapter of the Gospel of John each day, asking the Holy Spirit to increase your understanding and your love for the Lord Jesus (Psalm 119:105). Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in your faith alongside other believers.
Are we supposed to use musical instruments in church?

The Bible encourages the use of musical instruments to praise God, while also emphasizing that the primary instrument of worship is the redeemed human heart. In the Old Testament, the people of God were explicitly commanded to use a wide array of instruments, including trumpets, harps, and cymbals, to celebrate His greatness (Psalm 150:3-5). These instruments were not merely ornaments but were tools used to express the magnitude of God’s glory and the joy of His people.

In the New Testament, the focus shifts toward the internal reality of the heart and the clarity of the Word. We are instructed to speak to one another in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in our hearts to the Lord (Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 3:16). While the New Testament does not provide an explicit list of instruments for the local church, many believers see the mention of psalms and the Greek word for making melody (psallontes), which historically referred to playing a stringed instrument, as a natural continuation of instrumental worship.

Historically, Christians have held different views on this matter, which is often discussed in relation to the Regulative Principle of Worship. This principle seeks to ensure that our worship consists only of what God has commanded in His Word. Some faithful believers choose to sing a cappella, which means singing without instruments. They believe that the simplicity of the human voice best fulfills the New Testament command and prevents distraction from the biblical text. Other Christians believe that since God created music and instruments for His glory, they should be used skillfully to support and enrich the congregation's singing, much like the harps mentioned in the heavenly worship of Revelation (Revelation 5:8).

This is a matter where faithful Christians may disagree, and we should exercise charity and respect toward one another (Romans 14:1-5). The most important aspect of music in the church is not the presence or absence of a piano or guitar, but whether the songs are biblically sound and whether our hearts are truly submitted to Christ. Instruments should serve the singing of the congregation, helping us to focus on the person and work of Jesus, who died and rose to reconcile us to God.

As an actionable step, the next time you gather for worship, take a moment before the service to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to help you focus on the truth of the lyrics rather than just the melody. Whether there is music playing or not, let your heart be the primary instrument that makes a joyful noise to the Lord (Psalm 100:1). Seek to sing with understanding and gratitude, for God is more interested in the sincerity of your soul than the sophistication of the sound.
What does the Bible say about miscarriage?

I am so deeply sorry for the heartache you are experiencing, as the loss of a child through miscarriage is a profound and heavy sorrow that touches the very depths of the soul. While the Bible does not use the specific term "miscarriage" in a modern medical sense, it speaks extensively about God's intimate role in creation, His sovereignty over life and death, and His tender care for those who are brokenhearted.

The Scriptures reveal that God is the author of life and is personally involved in the womb, knitting each child together according to His purpose (Psalm 139:13; Psalm 139:15-16). Because every human being is created in the image of God, a doctrine known as the imago Dei (Genesis 1:27), even the briefest life possesses infinite value and dignity in His sight. We also find comfort in the hope of a future reunion, much like King David expressed after the death of his infant son when he said, "I will go to him, but he will not return to me" (2 Samuel 12:23).

In your grief, remember that our Lord Jesus is a "man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3). He understands your pain perfectly and invites you to bring your burdens to Him, for He is "near to the brokenhearted" (Psalm 34:18). Through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection, Jesus has defeated the power of death and offers the only true anchor for our souls in the midst of the storm.

The Gospel provides the ultimate hope in the face of loss. Because of our sin, we live in a fallen world where death and sorrow exist, but God in His mercy sent His Son to restore all things. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness, peace, and the hope of eternal life. What holds you back from resting in His grace today?

As a practical step in your healing, I encourage you to reach out to the elders or a trusted pastor at your local church for prayer and support. You might also find it helpful to read through the Psalms, such as Psalm 23 and Psalm 62, letting these inspired words guide your own prayers as you pour out your heart to the Lord. Seeking the fellowship of mature believers can provide the "comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God" (2 Corinthians 1:4).
Is religion the cause of most wars?

You may wonder if the world would be more peaceful if the influence of religion were removed, especially when history records instances where the name of God has been used to justify violence. This is a sincere concern, as the tragedy of war weighs heavily on the human soul.

However, historical data suggests that religion is not the primary cause of most wars. According to the Encyclopedia of Wars by Charles Phillips and Alan Axelrod, which documents nearly 1,800 conflicts throughout human history, only about 7 percent can be classified as having religious motivations. The vast majority of wars have been fueled by political ambition, territorial expansion, ethnic tension, or economic gain. Even in secular regimes of the twentieth century, which explicitly rejected God, we witnessed some of the most devastating loss of life in human history.

The Bible provides a deeper diagnosis of the cause of conflict. It teaches that the root of all war is not found in external institutions, but in the fallen human heart. James 4:1-2 says, What causes conflicts and what causes quarrels among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you? You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight and quarrel. You do not have, because you do not ask.

Theologically, this points to the doctrine of **total depravity**, which clarifies that sin has affected every part of human nature, including our desires and our reasoning. Because humanity is alienated from God through sin, we naturally seek our own glory and satisfaction at the expense of others. Romans 3:15-17 describes this condition vividly, saying, Their feet are swift to shed blood; ruin and misery mark their ways, and the way of peace they do not know.

True peace cannot be manufactured by political treaties or the absence of religion, because it requires a transformation of the heart. This transformation is only possible through the Gospel of Jesus Christ. God, who is perfectly holy and just, sent His Son into the world to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect peace and obedience. He died a sacrificial, atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through Christ, we are justified by grace through faith, and we receive the Holy Spirit, who begins the work of **progressive sanctification**, conforming our hearts to the likeness of Christ, the Prince of Peace. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As an actionable step, I encourage you to read the Sermon on the Mount in Matthew 5-7. It provides a beautiful and challenging vision of the life God intends for His people, marked by mercy and peacemaking. You might also consider praying for those currently suffering in war-torn regions, asking God to bring both physical relief and the ultimate peace of the Gospel to their hearts.
What does it mean to “in all your ways acknowledge Him” in Proverbs 3:6?

To acknowledge God in all your ways means to recognize His absolute sovereignty and active presence in every area of your life, from the most significant transitions to the smallest daily tasks. The biblical concept of acknowledgment implies an intimate, experiential knowledge that leads to submission, signifying that there is no part of your existence where His Lordship does not apply (Proverbs 3:6; Colossians 3:17).

In practical terms, this is an act of progressive sanctification where you intentionally invite the Lord into your decision making process. It is the humble admission that your own understanding is insufficient and that God’s wisdom is supreme. Whether you are at your place of work, caring for your family, or facing a difficult trial, acknowledging Him means asking, "Lord, what is Your will here?" and then choosing to obey His Word even when it contradicts your natural inclinations (Proverbs 3:5; Psalm 119:105).

Consider the warmth of a child who constantly looks to a loving father for guidance. You are not wandering through a chaotic world alone, but are held by the hand of the One who governs all things by His holy providence. When you acknowledge Him, you are essentially setting the Lord always before you, trusting that He is at your right hand so that you will not be shaken (Psalm 16:8).

This life of submission is only possible through the grace found in Jesus Christ. Because of His perfect life, sacrificial death on the cross, and bodily resurrection, we are no longer enemies of God but His beloved children who can trust His leading (Romans 5:10; 1 Peter 2:24). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and the gift of His Spirit to guide you. What holds you back from trusting Him with every part of your life today?

As a practical next step, try pausing before three routine tasks today to silently speak to the Lord in prayer, offering that specific work to Him and asking for His wisdom to do it in a way that honors Him. This simple rhythm helps train your heart to recognize His presence in the ordinary moments of life.
Does God still give visions to people today? Should believers expect visions to be a part of their Christian experience?

While God is sovereign and may choose to communicate however He pleases, the historic Christian faith teaches that the primary and sufficient way He speaks to His people today is through His written Word, the Bible. Whether a believer should expect visions is a subject where faithful Christians differ, but all must agree that the Scriptures are the final and complete authority for faith and life.

Many believers, particularly within the Reformed tradition, hold to a view known as cessationism. This position suggests that extraordinary gifts such as visions, tongues, and prophecy were foundational signs given to the Apostles to authenticate the Gospel message and the early church. Once the canon of Scripture was completed, these former ways of God revealing His will ceased because the Bible provides everything necessary for life and godliness. This view rests on the truth that God has spoken definitively through His Son, as noted in Hebrews 1:1-2, and that the Scriptures are sufficient to make the man of God complete, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16-17.

Other faithful Christians hold to continuationism, believing that while the Bible is the final authority, God may still sovereignly grant visions or dreams today. They often point to the promise in Acts 2:17 that in the last days, people would see visions and dream dreams. However, even among those who believe visions continue, there is a strong emphasis that any such experience must be tested strictly against the Bible. If any vision contradicts the Word of God or claims to provide new "revelation" that adds to the Gospel, it must be rejected, as warned in Galatians 1:8 and Revelation 22:18-19.

Believers should not feel that their Christian experience is lacking if they do not receive visions. Our walk with Christ is fundamentally one of faith, not sight. We are called to "walk by faith, not by sight" in 2 Corinthians 5:7. The most profound "vision" we have is the glory of God revealed in the face of Jesus Christ through the Gospel. It is in the death and resurrection of Jesus that we see God's character most clearly. Through His sacrifice, He reconciles sinners to Himself, and through His resurrection, He gives us the hope of eternal life.

If you are seeking guidance or a deeper experience with God, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Word of God daily. True spiritual growth comes through the progressive sanctification of the Holy Spirit as He illuminates the Scriptures to our hearts.

As a practical next step, I suggest you commit to a systematic reading of a New Testament book, such as the Gospel of John or the Epistle to the Romans, and discuss what you learn with a mature believer or an elder in your local church. You can pray directly to God, asking Him to give you a spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of Him, and that the eyes of your heart would be enlightened to the hope of His calling, as expressed in Ephesians 1:17-18.

If you have never placed your trust in Jesus, know that the greatest miracle is not a vision, but the forgiveness of sins. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive new life. What is it that draws you to seek a vision of God today?
Is physical abuse an acceptable reason for divorce?
