If you or someone you know is in immediate physical danger, please contact emergency services or a local domestic violence hotline immediately. Your safety and the safety of any children involved are of urgent importance, as God values the life and protection of the vulnerable.

Physical abuse is a grievous sin and a violation of the marriage covenant that provides biblical grounds for immediate separation and, as many faithful shepherds conclude, a basis for divorce under the principle of desertion. 

Marriage is a sacred institution ordained by God to be a lifelong union representing the relationship between Jesus Christ and His Church. Scripture teaches that a husband is to love his wife as his own body and as Christ loved the church (Ephesians 5:25; Ephesians 5:28). When a spouse uses violence against the other, they are flagrantly contradicting the very nature of the marriage bond and the commands of God, who hates violence (Psalm 11:5).

While the Bible explicitly names sexual immorality (Matthew 19:9) and the desertion of a believer by an unbeliever (1 Corinthians 7:15) as grounds for divorce, physical abuse is often understood within historic Christian teaching as a form of constructive desertion. This means that by becoming a threat to the life and safety of the spouse, the abuser has functionally abandoned the marriage covenant and their duty to provide and protect. In such cases, the abuser’s actions demonstrate a rejection of the marriage just as clearly as if they had physically moved out.

If you are facing this trial, please know that the Lord is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18). God does not demand that a spouse remain in a situation where their life or the lives of their children are being threatened. Your primary responsibility in a moment of danger is to seek safety.

As a practical and pastoral next step, I encourage you to contact the elders of a faithful local church. They are called to shepherd the flock and can provide the protection, wisdom, and accountability necessary to handle such a grave situation, including the implementation of church discipline for the offending party.

Matthew 19:9; 1 Corinthians 7:15; Ephesians 5:25; Ephesians 5:28; Psalm 11:5; Psalm 34:18
Why is “You shall not steal” in the Ten Commandments?

The commandment "You shall not steal" is included in the Ten Commandments because God, as the Creator and Sustainer of all things, establishes the right of ownership and the duty of stewardship among His people. It serves as a moral boundary that protects the livelihood of our neighbor and honors God’s sovereign distribution of resources. Exodus 20:15; Deuteronomy 5:19.

At its heart, this command reflects the holiness and justice of God. Because "the earth is the LORD’s, and everything in it" (Psalm 24:1), anything we possess is held in trust from Him. When we take what belongs to another, we are not merely violating a social contract; we are expressing a lack of trust in God’s **providence**. To steal is to suggest that God has been unwise or unkind in what He has provided for us, displaying a heart of discontentment rather than a heart of faith. The Law teaches us that loving our neighbor as ourselves involves respecting the means God has given them to provide for their families and contribute to the community. Romans 13:9.

While this commandment exposes our natural tendency toward greed and self-interest, it also points us to our need for a Savior. None of us have perfectly honored God or our neighbor in our thoughts and actions. Jesus Christ, however, lived a life of perfect **stewardship** and integrity. He did not come to take, but to give His life as a ransom for many. Through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection, those who trust in Him are not only forgiven for their sins but are also transformed by the Holy Spirit to become people of radical generosity.

A practical way to live out this truth is to move from being a taker to being a giver. If you find yourself struggling with envy or the temptation to obtain things dishonestly, I encourage you to bring this struggle to God in prayer. Ask Him to grant you a heart of contentment and to show you how you can use your work to bless others. As the Scripture says, "He who has been stealing must steal no longer, but must work, doing something useful with his own hands, that he may have something to share with those in need." Ephesians 4:28.

You may wonder how anyone can stand before a holy God after failing to keep His commands. The Gospel is the good news that Christ took the punishment for our law-breaking upon Himself. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that “His mercy endures forever” (Jeremiah 33:11 and Psalm 136)?

The declaration that his mercy endures forever means that God’s covenant love is unchangeable, eternal, and inexhaustible toward those who belong to Him. This truth assures us that God’s favor is not a flickering flame that dies when we fail, but an everlasting sun fueled by His own immutable character (Psalm 136:1; Jeremiah 33:11).

In the original Hebrew, the word often translated as mercy is *hesed*, which refers to a loyal, devoted, and reaching love that is bound by a covenant. When Psalm 136 repeats this phrase twenty six times, it is teaching us that every act of God, from the creation of the heavens to the deliverance of His people from their enemies, flows from this tireless spring of devotion. It means that God’s heart toward His children is not subject to mood or moments of regret. Because God is supreme and sovereign, His mercy cannot be overruled by any power in heaven or on earth (Psalm 136:2-4; Lamentations 3:22-23).

For the believer, this mercy finds its ultimate expression in the person and work of Jesus Christ. While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us, providing the perfect atonement for our transgressions (Romans 5:8). His mercy is not a license to continue in sin, but the very kindness that leads us to repentance. In Christ, we see the heart of a Father who does not treat us as our sins deserve but reaches out with the grace of justification, declaring us righteous through faith alone (Psalm 103:10; Romans 3:24).

If you are feeling weighed down by the guilt of your past or the struggles of your present, remember that you cannot exhaust a mercy that is infinite. You might find comfort in setting aside ten minutes today to read Psalm 136 slowly, replacing the historical events mentioned there with the ways God has personally provided for and sustained you in your own life.

This enduring mercy is available to all who call upon the name of the Lord. You may wonder how a holy God could truly accept someone with your history, but the Gospel reveals that Jesus Christ took the judgment we deserved so that we could receive the mercy He earned. God is holy and just, yet He is also the merciful Savior who invites you to come to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is the Church of God in Christ (COGIC) a good biblical church?

The Church of God in Christ, commonly known as COGIC, is a historic and orthodox Christian denomination that stands firmly upon the essential foundations of the faith. Since its founding in the late nineteenth century, it has grown to be the largest African American Pentecostal body in the world, maintaining a steadfast commitment to the authority of the Bible and the proclamation of the Gospel.

In terms of core doctrine, the Church of God in Christ affirms the Trinity, the deity of Jesus Christ, His virgin birth, His sinless life, His substitutionary atonement on the cross, His bodily resurrection, and His future return to judge the living and the dead. They believe that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ, and that the Scriptures are the inspired and infallible Word of God. On these central truths, they are in harmony with the historic Christian faith as expressed in the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19 and the defense of the faith in Jude 1:3.

There are, however, secondary doctrines where COGIC differs from other traditions, such as the Reformed Baptist or Presbyterian perspectives. These differences primarily concern the work of the Holy Spirit and the nature of sanctification.

1. The Baptism of the Holy Spirit: COGIC teaches that the baptism in the Holy Spirit is an experience distinct from and subsequent to the new birth, often evidenced by speaking in tongues as the Spirit gives utterance, as seen in Acts 2:4. While many other denominations believe the baptism of the Spirit occurs at the moment of conversion, this remains a secondary matter over which faithful Christians have differed for over a century.

2. Continuation of Miraculous Gifts: As a Pentecostal body, COGIC believes that the miraculous gifts of the Spirit, such as healing and prophecy, are still active in the church today. Other traditions hold to cessationism, the belief that these specific sign gifts ceased with the closing of the apostolic age. This is a matter of interpretation regarding passages like 1 Corinthians 13:8–10 and does not define whether a church is Christian, but rather how it practices its worship.

3. Holiness and Sanctification: Rooted in the Holiness movement, COGIC emphasizes a life of "entire sanctification" and outward holiness. They urge believers to live lives that are strictly set apart from the world in conduct and appearance, reflecting the command in Hebrews 12:14 to follow after the holiness without which no man shall see the Lord.

If you are considering a COGIC congregation, you will likely find a community that prizes fervent prayer, expressive worship, and a high view of God’s power to transform lives. The beauty of the body of Christ is that it is composed of many members with different emphases, yet we are all joined to the same Head, who is Christ.

Ultimately, the most important question for any church is whether it holds to the Gospel: that we are sinners separated from a holy God, and our only hope is the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. Through Christ alone, by grace alone, we are reconciled to God. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your soul and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the specific "Statement of Faith" for the local COGIC congregation you are interested in and compare it with the clear teachings of Scripture. You might also find it helpful to speak with the pastor to ask how they handle the exposition of the Word in their weekly services, for the steady preaching of the Bible is the primary way God nourishes His sheep. What is it specifically that draws you to this denomination?
What is the voice of one calling in the wilderness (Isaiah 40:3; John 1:23)?

The voice of one calling in the wilderness is John the Baptist, the divinely appointed herald who prepared the way for the ministry of Jesus Christ. This prophetic figure was foretold by the prophet Isaiah to announce the arrival of the Lord to His people (Isaiah 40:3).

When the religious leaders questioned John concerning his identity, he chose not to claim a title of worldly honor but instead identified himself through the words of Scripture. He declared, "I am the voice of one crying in the wilderness, 'Make straight the way of the Lord,' as the prophet Isaiah said" (John 1:23). John's primary mission was to serve as a forerunner, one who goes before a king to announce his arrival and ensure the path is ready.

In a spiritual sense, John prepared the way through the preaching of repentance. Just as an ancient herald would call for the physical repair of roads before a royal procession, John called for the moral and spiritual preparation of hearts. He urged the people to level the mountains of pride and fill the valleys of spiritual indifference so they might receive the Messiah. This preparation was essential because the One following John was not merely a man, but the incarnate Word of God.

The message of this voice was ultimately one of redirection. John pointed away from himself and toward Jesus, saying, "Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!" (John 1:29). This is the heart of the Gospel: that though we are separated from our holy and sovereign God by our sin and rebellion, God in His mercy sent His Son. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to secure eternal life for all who believe (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

As you reflect on the ministry of John the Baptist, consider how God might be calling you to prepare your own heart to follow Him more closely. A practical step you can take today is to read the first chapter of the Gospel of John and ask God to reveal any areas of your life where you need to "make the way straight" through repentance.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is neonomianism?

Neonomianism is the theological error that portrays the Gospel as a "new law" (from the Greek *neos*, meaning new, and *nomos*, meaning law) which God accepts in place of the perfect obedience required by His holy standard. It teaches that under the New Covenant, God has lowered the requirements for justification, accepting our sincere faith and imperfect obedience as the legal ground for our standing before Him, rather than the perfect righteousness of Jesus Christ alone.

This view is a dangerous departure from the core doctrine of justification by faith alone. While it may sound appealing to say God only requires "sincerity," this teaching actually turns the Gospel back into a system of works. The Bible is clear that we are not saved by a "softer" law that we must keep, but by the One who kept the Law perfectly on our behalf. Scripture states that "a man is not justified by works of the law, but by faith in Jesus Christ. So we, too, have believed in Christ Jesus, that we might be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, because by works of the law no flesh will be justified" Galatians 2:16.

Neonomianism fails to distinguish between the ground of our justification (Christ's work) and the fruit of our sanctification (our obedience). When we mix these two, we lose the comfort and certainty of the Gospel. If our peace with God depends even slightly on the quality of our own sincerity, we are left in a state of constant anxiety, for our sincerity is often flawed and our hearts are prone to wander. 

The true Gospel reveals that God is perfectly holy and His justice must be satisfied. Because we are all sinners and fall short of His glory, we cannot save ourselves by any law, old or new Romans 3:23. In His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature and lived a sinless life. He died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is secured not by our "new law" performance, but by Christ's historical work, received by grace alone through faith alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the book of Galatians and reflect on the absolute sufficiency of Christ. When you feel the weight of your own imperfection, look away from yourself and look to the Savior who said, "It is finished" John 19:30. What holds you back from resting completely in the finished work of Jesus?
What does it mean that neither height nor depth separates us from the love of God (Romans 8:39)?

In Romans 8:39, the phrase "neither height nor depth" serves as a cosmic declaration that there is no location in the entire universe, nor any spiritual power in the heavens or the abyss, that can act as a barrier between a believer and the love of God. These terms represent the extremes of space and existence, signaling that the reach of God's sovereign grace is infinite and His grip on His children is unbreakable.

This passage beautifully illustrates the doctrine of the perseverance of the saints. It teaches that those whom God has called and justified are kept by His power through faith for a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time (1 Peter 1:5). Because this love is anchored in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ rather than our own fluctuating merit, it remains a fixed reality that no created thing can disturb (Romans 8:38; Romans 8:39). This is the great "Hallelujah" of the Christian life, that our security rests in the immutable character of God (Malachi 3:6).

If you find yourself overwhelmed by a sense of distance or fear that your trials have separated you from God's favor, take heart. The Apostle Paul wrote these words to a church facing intense persecution and hardship, reminding them that God’s love is not a fair weather sentiment but a covenantal bond. As a practical step this week, I encourage you to read through the entirety of Romans 8 each morning, focusing specifically on the transition from "no condemnation" in verse 1 to "no separation" in verse 39.

You may wonder how such a perfect and holy God could offer this kind of unbreakable love to fallen human beings. The truth of the Gospel is that we were all separated from God by our sin, deserving of His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Yet, God demonstrated His love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a life of perfect obedience, and suffered the death we deserved on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and securing eternal life for all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the doctrine of preterition?

Preterition is the sovereign act of God in which He chooses to pass over those not elected to salvation, leaving them in their self-chosen sin and subject to His holy justice. This doctrine is the logical necessity of unconditional election, teaching that while God actively grants saving grace to some, He withholds that same grace from others according to His unsearchable counsel and good pleasure.

This concept is rooted in the biblical truth of God’s absolute sovereignty and the total depravity of the human race. Because every person is born in rebellion against God, no one is "entitled" to His mercy; therefore, when God chooses to save some, He is being infinitely merciful, and when He passes over others, He is being perfectly just. This is not a matter of God forcing innocent people into destruction, but rather God leaving guilty sinners to the path they have freely chosen. As the Scripture says, "So then He has mercy on whom He desires, and He hardens whom He desires" (Romans 9:18).

Theologically, preterition is often distinguished from the broader decree of reprobation. While reprobation includes the judicial act of God ordaining the non-elect to punishment for their sins, preterition specifically highlights the "passing over" or the sovereign decision not to rescue. This underscores that salvation is a work of monergistic grace, meaning it is God alone who initiates and completes the rescue of a soul. Without His intervention, no one would seek Him (Romans 3:10-12).

In the Gospel of Matthew, Jesus Himself acknowledges this sovereign discrimination, saying, "I praise You, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because You have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this was Your good pleasure" (Matthew 11:25-26). Furthermore, the Apostle Paul speaks of God enduring "with much patience the vessels of wrath prepared for destruction" (Romans 9:22).

Pastoral Application:
This doctrine, though heavy to the human mind, is intended to produce profound humility and fervent gratitude in the heart of the believer. It reminds us that our standing before God is not due to our own wisdom, strength, or "better" nature, but is entirely the result of His free and sovereign grace. If you find in your heart a desire to love Christ and follow Him, you should view that desire as a precious evidence of His special love toward you.

One actionable step you can take is to meditate on the mercy of God in your own life this week. In your private prayer time, specifically thank the Lord that He did not leave you to your own devices or the hardness of your own heart, but sovereignly drew you to Himself by His Spirit (John 6:44). Let this truth fuel your worship and your witness to others, knowing that God is the one who opens hearts to respond to the Gospel (Acts 16:14).
Are there supposed to be any rituals in Christianity?

Yes, Jesus Christ established two specific rituals for His followers, which are commonly referred to as ordinances: Baptism and the Lord's Supper. These are not merely human traditions but are commands from the Lord Himself to serve as visible signs of the invisible grace He works in the hearts of believers.

The first ordinance is Baptism. It is the solemn act of being immersed in water in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, as commanded in Matthew 28:19. Baptism serves as a public declaration of a believer’s union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection, symbolizing that the old self has died to sin and a new life has begun in Him (Romans 6:3–4).

The second ordinance is the Lord’s Supper, or Communion. This is a recurring meal where believers partake of bread and the fruit of the vine to remember Christ’s sacrificial death until He returns (1 Corinthians 11:23–26). In this ritual, we do not believe the elements literally become the body and blood of Jesus, but that Christ is spiritually present with His people, nourishing our souls as we reflect on His finished work.

You may wonder if performing these rituals is what God requires for us to be accepted by Him. It is vital to understand that while these ordinances are precious means of grace, they do not possess the power to save us or wash away our sins. True rituals in Christianity are meant to point us away from ourselves and toward the person and work of Jesus Christ.

The core of the Christian faith is that God is holy and we are sinners separated from Him by our rebellion. Because of His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious observances (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

If you have already trusted in Christ, a beautiful next step is to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can be baptized and regularly participate in the Lord’s Supper with other believers. What is your current understanding of how a person becomes right with God?
What is the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), and what do they believe?

The Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) is a conservative, evangelical Protestant denomination founded in 1973 by believers who sought to remain faithful to the historic Reformed faith and the Great Commission of Jesus Christ. It was established as a response to theological liberalism, with a firm commitment to the authority of the Bible and the spread of the Gospel throughout the world.

The beliefs of the PCA are rooted in the Reformed tradition and can be understood through several key pillars:

**The Authority and Inerrancy of Scripture**
The PCA holds that the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, serving as the only infallible rule for faith and practice. They believe that God has spoken clearly through His Word and that it remains the final authority over all human reason and tradition (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:20-21).

**Confessional Standards**
The denomination is "confessional," meaning it adheres to the Westminster Confession of Faith, along with the Westminster Larger and Shorter Catechisms. These documents provide a systematic summary of biblical teaching. Central to these standards is the doctrine of "God's sovereignty," which emphasizes that God is the supreme ruler over all creation, providence, and the salvation of His people.

**The Doctrines of Grace (Reformed Theology)**
The PCA affirms the "Five Points of Calvinism," emphasizing that salvation is entirely a work of God's grace. They teach that human beings are spiritually dead in sin and incapable of saving themselves, but God, in His infinite mercy, chose a people for Himself before the foundation of the world. Salvation is secured through the substitutionary atonement of Jesus Christ, who died to satisfy the justice of God for the sins of His people (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:23-26).

**Presbyterian Church Government**
The term "Presbyterian" refers to the way the church is governed. Rather than being ruled by a single leader or a pure democracy, the PCA is governed by "elders" (presbyters). Each local church is led by a "Session" of elders. These churches are grouped into regional "Presbyteries," and the denomination meets annually as a "General Assembly." This structure is intended to provide biblical accountability and connectional unity among the churches (Acts 20:28; Titus 1:5).

**Covenant Theology and the Sacraments**
The PCA views the Bible through the lens of "Covenant Theology," seeing God's relationship with humanity as a series of covenants. This shapes their view of the sacraments, baptism and the Lord's Supper. Unlike Baptists, the PCA practices "paedobaptism" (infant baptism), believing that the children of believers are members of the visible church and should receive the sign of the covenant, much like circumcision in the Old Testament (Genesis 17:7; Acts 2:38-39; Colossians 2:11-12). They also believe in the "spiritual presence" of Christ in the Lord's Supper, where believers are nourished by faith (1 Corinthians 10:16).

If you are interested in learning more about their specific teachings, a wonderful practical step would be to read the "Westminster Shorter Catechism." It provides clear, concise answers to many of the most important questions of the Christian life, starting with the famous reminder that "Man's chief end is to glorify God, and to enjoy Him forever."

As you explore these truths, remember that the heart of all faithful Christian teaching is the person and work of Jesus Christ. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and look to Jesus, who died and rose again to reconcile us to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What thoughts or questions come to your mind as you consider these biblical truths?
Where is God now? Where is God when it hurts?

God is omnipresent, which means He is present in all places at all times with His whole being. He is not limited by space or time, as He is the Creator of both, and the heaven of heavens cannot contain Him (1 Kings 8:27). While God is transcendent, ruling sovereignly from His throne in heaven (Psalm 103:19), He is also immanent, meaning He is personally present and active within His creation (Jeremiah 23:23-24).

When you experience deep pain, it can often feel as though God has hidden His face or traveled to a far country. However, the Scriptures provide the beautiful assurance that God is never closer to His children than when they are suffering. He is described as a "very present help in trouble" (Psalm 46:1). You may wonder how a loving and sovereign God could allow such heartache to touch your life. It is a heavy burden to carry, yet the Bible teaches that the Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit (Psalm 34:18).

God’s presence in our pain is most clearly seen in the person of Jesus Christ. Through the hypostatic union, Jesus is truly God and truly man. Because He took on human flesh, He is not a distant deity who is "unable to sympathize with our weaknesses," but one who has been "tempted in every way as we are, yet was without sin" (Hebrews 4:15). On the cross, Jesus endured the ultimate pain and the weight of our sins so that we might never have to face suffering alone or without hope.

If you are feeling the weight of the world today, I encourage you to pour out your heart to Him in prayer. A practical next step is to read through Psalm 23 and Psalm 139, speaking the words back to God as your own prayer. He invites you to cast all your cares on Him because He cares for you (1 Peter 5:7).

Ultimately, our greatest need in both joy and sorrow is to be reconciled to God. Though our sins have separated us from Him, God in His holiness and love sent His Son to bridge that gap. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him with your life today?
What is the just war theory?

Just war theory is a theological and ethical framework used to determine when a nation may morally engage in armed conflict and how that conflict must be conducted to remain remains within the bounds of justice. It rests on the biblical conviction that while peace is the ultimate desire of God's heart, He has providently ordained civil authorities to restrain evil and protect the innocent in this fallen world, as seen in Romans 13:4.

The historic Christian view, shaped by thinkers such as Augustine and Thomas Aquinas, divides this framework into two primary categories. The first is the right to go to war, often called *jus ad bellum*. This requires a just cause, such as defending the weak from an aggressor, as commanded in Psalm 82:4. It also requires that the war be declared by a competent, lawful authority and held as a last resort after all peaceful means of resolution have been exhausted. The intention must always be the restoration of a just peace, never fueled by a desire for vengeance or territorial gain.

The second category is the right conduct within war, or *jus in bello*. This demands proportionality, meaning the force used must not exceed what is necessary to achieve the just objective. It also mandates discrimination, which is the moral obligation to protect non combatants and innocent lives from intentional harm. Even in the midst of the storm of battle, the Christian is called to remember that every person is an image bearer of God, as noted in Genesis 1:27.

While many believers throughout history have found this framework a necessary tool for justice, there are other faithful Christians who hold to the view of pacifism. They believe the call of Jesus to "turn the other cheek" in Matthew 5:39 precludes any participation in violence. Both views seek to honor the Lord, though they differ on how the believer interacts with the state's use of force. This is a matter where Christians may disagree, yet we must all remain committed to the pursuit of peace and the protection of the vulnerable, following the principle of liberty in non essentials.

If you find your heart heavy with the news of conflict and the reality of a broken world, I encourage you to set aside a specific time this week to pray for those in positions of leadership. Ask the Lord to grant them wisdom, a hunger for justice, and a heart for mercy, as we are instructed in 1 Timothy 2:1-2.

You may wonder how a God of love can allow the horrors of war to persist. The Bible teaches that war is a tragic result of human sin and our separation from God. However, the Gospel offers the only true hope for lasting peace. Jesus Christ, the Prince of Peace, took the violence of our sin upon Himself on the cross, dying and rising again to reconcile us to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and the hope of a world where one day He will make all things new. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that women should have a gentle and quiet spirit (1 Peter 3:4)?

In 1 Peter 3:4, a gentle and quiet spirit refers to an inner disposition of the heart that finds its rest and security in God rather than in outward appearance or assertive self-promotion. It describes a soul that is tranquil and humble, trusting in God’s sovereignty even in difficult or provocative circumstances.

This "gentle" quality is the same word often translated as "meek," which does not mean weakness but rather strength under the control of the Holy Spirit. It is a Christ-like attribute, as Jesus described Himself as "gentle and humble in heart" in Matthew 11:29. A "quiet" spirit is one that is peaceful and free from the internal clamor of anxiety, bitterness, or the need to demand one's own way. 

Theologically, Peter is emphasizing the beauty of the hidden person of the heart, which he calls "imperishable." While external fashions and physical beauty eventually fade, a character shaped by the grace of God has a value that is "very precious in the sight of God" in 1 Peter 3:4. This internal ornament is not about a specific personality type—whether one is naturally extroverted or introverted—but about a heart that is stilled by the Gospel. It is the fruit of progressive sanctification, where a woman grows to rely more on the character of God than on her own ability to control her surroundings or her husband.

This beauty is rooted in hope. Peter points to the "holy women who hoped in God" as examples in 1 Peter 3:5. Their tranquility did not come from a lack of trouble, but from the firm conviction that God is a faithful Judge and Provider.

As a practical step in cultivating this spirit, I encourage you to spend time each day in the Psalms, specifically looking for descriptions of God’s sovereignty and care. When you feel the impulse to be agitated or to take matters into your own hands, stop and offer a short prayer, asking the Lord to be your peace and to help you trust His timing. Bringing your anxieties to Him in prayer is the primary way the heart remains quiet in a noisy world.
What does it mean to be free from sin?

To be free from sin means that through faith in Jesus Christ, you are released from the legal guilt and eternal condemnation of your transgressions, and you are no longer a slave to the power of sin in your daily life. This freedom is a factual reality for every person who belongs to Christ, rooted in what He accomplished through His historical death and bodily resurrection.

This freedom has two primary dimensions that describe the believer's relationship to God and to sin. The first is justification, which is the judicial act of God where He declares a sinner righteous. This is not because of anything the person has done, but because the perfect obedience of Jesus is credited to them and their sins were laid upon Him at the cross (Romans 5:1; 2 Corinthians 5:21). In justification, you are free from the penalty of sin, which is spiritual death and separation from God (Romans 8:1).

The second dimension is sanctification, the ongoing process by which the Holy Spirit works within a believer to break the dominion of sin. While Christians still struggle with the presence of sin in this life, they are no longer under its mastery or command (Romans 6:14; Romans 6:22). To be free from sin is to be given a new heart with new desires, enabling you to serve God out of love rather than being driven by sinful impulses (Ezekiel 36:26; Romans 6:17-18).

You may wonder how a person who feels deeply stained by their past or trapped by current failures can ever truly experience such liberty. The Gospel is the glorious announcement that although God is perfectly holy and our sin separates us from Him, He has provided the way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose again, demonstrating His victory over death and the grave (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read Romans chapter 6 slowly this week. As you read, notice how the Apostle Paul describes your old self being crucified with Christ so that you might walk in newness of life. I also urge you to find a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of the Lord alongside other believers.
Is God man-made? Did man create the idea of God?

It is a common and understandable observation to look at the vast array of human religions and wonder if "God" is merely a projection of human hopes, fears, or a way to explain the mysteries of the universe. However, according to the Scriptures, God is not a product of human imagination but is the eternal, self-existent Creator who brought all of humanity into being. 

The Bible presents God as the one who existed before all things and through whom all things were made. Genesis 1:1; Psalm 90:2. He does not depend on us for His existence, His power, or His identity. This is known as the "aseity" of God, meaning He possesses life in and of Himself. Rather than man creating the idea of God, the Bible teaches that God created man in His own image, planting a sense of His existence within every human heart. Genesis 1:27; Romans 1:19-20. As a great preacher once noted, there is a "God-shaped vacuum" in every person that only the Creator can fill.

Because God is our Creator, He has a rightful claim over our lives and our worship. Yet, every one of us has turned away from His good and perfect design to follow our own paths, which the Bible calls sin. Romans 3:23; Isaiah 53:6. This sin separates us from the holy God who made us. However, God in His great mercy did not abandon us to our own inventions. He entered into human history in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus is truly God and truly man, born of a virgin, who lived the perfect life we could not live. Matthew 1:23; Hebrews 4:15.

The heart of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ died on a cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the judgment we deserved, and was buried. 1 Peter 2:24. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming that He is exactly who He claimed to be. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4. Through His sacrifice, the way to a restored relationship with our Creator is open to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most challenging about the idea of a Creator who exists independently of human thought?

As a next step in your journey, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It specifically highlights how the eternal Word of God became flesh to dwell among us so that we might know the Father. John 1:1-14.
What is the significance of the statement “God is within her; she will not fall” in Psalm 46:5?

Psalm 46:5 refers to the city of God, Zion, declaring that the community of God's people remains unshakable because the Almighty dwells in her midst. While this verse is frequently applied to individual circumstances today, its primary historical and biblical significance is that the security of God’s people depends entirely on His immediate presence rather than their own defenses.

The context of this Psalm describes a world in total upheaval, with mountains falling into the heart of the sea and kingdoms in an uproar as seen in Psalm 46:2; Psalm 46:6. In the midst of this chaos, the city of God is depicted as a place of peace and stillness because of the divine immanence of the Creator. The city does not fall because God is "within her," signifying that He is not a distant observer but a present help in trouble as stated in Psalm 46:1. 

The promise that "God will help her at break of day" points to His perfect timing. Just as the morning light dispels the terrors of the night, God’s deliverance arrives at the precisely appointed moment when His people need it most. For the believer today, this truth is beautifully realized through the Church, which is the dwelling place of God by His Spirit as described in Ephesians 2:22. Because Christ has promised to be with His people always, the Church can face the fiercest opposition with the same confidence found in Zion.

You may wonder how such ancient promises apply to your own life when the storms of life feel overwhelming. It is a natural human concern to feel fragile in a world that is constantly shifting. The comfort of Psalm 46:5 is found not in looking at our own resilience, but in looking at the King who stands in the center of the city. We are made secure not by our own resolve, but by the grace of God that keeps us.

To experience this unshakable peace, one must first be a citizen of this heavenly city. We enter this state of security through the Gospel of Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory as seen in Romans 3:23. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read the entirety of Psalm 46 this week. When you pray, specifically ask the Lord to help you recognize His presence in your current trials, and thank Him that your stability rests in His character rather than your circumstances. Seek to gather with a local body of believers where the Word is preached, for God dwells uniquely among His gathered people.
What does the Bible say about worship?

Worship is the act of ascribing ultimate worth to God, recognizing His holiness, sovereignty, and glory as the Creator and Redeemer of the world. It is the rightful and joyful response of the creature to the Creator, involving both a heart of deep affection and a life of obedience.

The Bible teaches that worship must be centered on God alone, for He is worthy to receive glory and honor and power (Revelation 4:11). Jesus clarified the nature of true worship when He stated that the Father is seeking those who will worship Him in spirit and in truth (John 4:23-24). To worship in spirit means our devotion must come from a heart renewed by the Holy Spirit, while worshipping in truth means our worship must be regulated by and aligned with the Word of God rather than human invention or mere emotion.

While we often associate worship with singing, the Scriptures present it as a comprehensive lifestyle. We are commanded to present our bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God, which is described as our spiritual act of worship (Romans 12:1). This means that every area of life, including our work, our relationships, and our private thoughts, should be directed toward the honor of Christ. Whether we eat or drink, or whatever we do, we are called to do it all for the glory of God (1 Corinthians 10:31).

In the gathered life of the church, worship involves specific "means of grace" that God has provided for our edification. This includes the preaching of the Word, fervent prayer, the administration of baptism and the Lord's Supper, and the singing of psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs (Colossians 3:16; Acts 2:42). Corporate worship is not a performance to be watched, but a collective sacrifice of praise where believers encourage one another and exalt the Lord together (Hebrews 10:24-25).

You may wonder why a sovereign God desires the worship of human beings. It is not because He lacks anything, but because He is the only object worthy of our highest affection, and we are most satisfied when He is most glorified in us. We were all created to be worshippers, but because of sin, we often trade the glory of the immortal God for images made to look like mortal man or animals (Romans 1:23). This misplaced worship leads to brokenness and judgment.

The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, became man to perfectly worship the Father on our behalf. He died on the cross to pay the penalty for our idolatry and rose bodily from the grave to give us new life. Through faith in Him, our hearts are changed so that we can truly know and love God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a restored relationship with your Maker. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to begin each day this week by reading a "Psalm of Praise," such as Psalm 100 or Psalm 145. Use the words of the Psalmist to guide your own prayers, specifically naming attributes of God, such as His faithfulness or His mercy, that you are thankful for that day. Finding a local, Bible-believing church where the Word is faithfully preached is also essential for your growth in corporate worship.
What is more important, the death of Christ or His resurrection?

In the economy of salvation, the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ are inseparable and equally essential, forming two parts of a single, finished work of redemption. One cannot be "more important" than the other because, without both, there is no Gospel and no hope for the sinner.

The death of Christ is the moment of penal substitution, where the Lord Jesus, acting as our Great High Priest and sacrifice, took the full weight of the Father’s righteous wrath against our sin. By His blood, He made propitiation, which means He satisfied the justice of God on our behalf. As the Scripture says, God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:21). Without His death, our debt remains unpaid and we remain under condemnation.

The resurrection is the vital confirmation and vindication of that death. It is the Father’s "Amen" to the Son’s "It is finished." If Christ remained in the grave, it would signify that death and sin still held dominion, and we would have no assurance that His sacrifice was sufficient. The Apostle Paul is very clear on this point, stating that if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins (1 Corinthians 15:17). The resurrection provides our justification, proving that the debt was fully paid and that Christ has conquered the power of the grave (Romans 4:25).

Therefore, we do not choose between the cross and the empty tomb. We boast in a Savior who was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification (Romans 4:25). The death provides the payment, and the resurrection provides the proof and the power of new life.

Because of our rebellion against a holy and sovereign God, we are all naturally separated from Him and deserve His judgment. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. By His bodily resurrection, Jesus was declared with power to be the Son of God and the Lord over all (Romans 1:4). Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from resting in the finished work of Jesus today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through 1 Corinthians 15 this week. Meditate on how the historical reality of both the death and resurrection provides a concrete foundation for your hope, and thank God in prayer for the completeness of the salvation He has provided in His Son.
What is the emerging / emergent church movement?

The emerging church movement is a diverse and decentralized trend that arose in the late twentieth century, seeking to adapt Christian practice and expression to the shift from a modern to a postmodern culture. It often emphasizes community, social justice, and "conversation" over traditional denominational structures and systematic theology.

You may wonder if the traditional church has lost its heartbeat for the contemporary world, leading to a search for something more authentic and engaged with the culture. This desire to reach the lost is commendable, for our Lord Himself moved among the broken and the searching. However, we must be careful that in our attempt to be relevant, we do not sacrifice the very Truth that sets people free.

In its earlier days, the movement was often categorized into two streams. One stream was "missional," seeking to change the methods of the church to reach people while remaining faithful to the historic Gospel. The other stream focused on "theological deconstruction," a process of questioning and often rejecting established doctrines such as the absolute authority of Scripture, the reality of God's wrath, and the necessity of the substitutionary atonement of Christ.

As Christians, we believe that while our methods of reaching people can and should change, the Message remains unchangeable. The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17). We are called to contend earnestly for the faith that was once for all entrusted to the saints (Jude 1:3). Truth is not something we create in conversation; it is something God has revealed in His Word.

The movement often prioritized orthopraxy (right practice) over orthodoxy (right belief). While it is true that a faith without works is dead, our works must be fueled by a right understanding of who God is and what He has done. We cannot have a "Christianity" that discards the core doctrines of the faith, for a gospel without the cross is no gospel at all.

The heart of our message is this: God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or cultural relevance (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to find a local, Bible-believing church that is committed to the expositional preaching of Scripture. Grounding yourself in a community that loves the Word will provide the stability and fellowship needed to navigate the complexities of our modern world while remaining anchored in the ancient Truth. Seek to spend time daily in the Word, perhaps starting in the Gospel of John, and ask the Holy Spirit to lead you into all truth (John 16:13).
What does the Bible say about underage drinking?

The Bible teaches that believers must obey the laws of their government and maintain a life of sobriety and self control. While Scripture does not specify a legal age for drinking, it commands us to submit to the authorities God has placed over us, which includes adhering to age-related laws regarding alcohol.

First, we must consider the principle of submission. God has instituted civil government for the good of society, and as Christians, we are called to honor these laws as an act of obedience to Him. Romans 13:1 says, "Everyone must submit himself to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which is from God; those that exist have been instituted by God." Consequently, when a person drinks under the legal age, they are not merely breaking a social rule; they are committing a sin of disobedience against God’s established order. 1 Peter 2:13-14 further instructs us to "Submit yourselves for the Lord’s sake to every human institution."

Second, the Bible warns against the dangers of alcohol and strictly forbids drunkenness. Proverbs 20:1 declares, "Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by them is not wise." The goal of a believer is to be governed by the Holy Spirit rather than by substances that impair judgment. Ephesians 5:18 commands, "And do not get drunk on wine, which leads to debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit." Underage drinking often occurs in environments where the temptation to lose self control is high, which stands in direct opposition to the Christian call to sober-mindedness and the fruit of the Spirit which is temperance.

If you are a young person living at home, you also have the biblical responsibility to honor and obey your parents. Exodus 20:12 and Ephesians 6:1 make it clear that honoring your parents is a primary duty. If they have set boundaries regarding alcohol, those boundaries must be respected as part of your stewardship of your life and body.

If you have struggled with this or feel pressured by peers, remember that the grace of God is available to help you stand firm. I encourage you to find a mature Christian mentor or a leader in your local church to whom you can be accountable. Ask them to pray with you for the strength to honor God with your body and your obedience to the law. True freedom is not found in breaking rules, but in the joyful service of the Lord Jesus Christ.
Will there be a great apostasy / falling away during the end times?

Yes, the Bible explicitly teaches that a great falling away, or apostasy, will occur before the return of Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul warns that the Day of the Lord will not come until the rebellion, or the apostasy, takes place first. 2 Thessalonians 2:3.

This period is marked by a widespread departure from the core doctrines of the Gospel and a turning toward false teachers and worldly ideologies. Jesus cautioned His disciples that because lawlessness will multiply, the love of many will grow cold. Matthew 24:12. The Holy Spirit also clearly states that in later times some will abandon the faith to follow deceitful spirits and things taught by demons. 1 Timothy 4:1. This apostasy is not merely a lack of interest in religion, but a specific rejection of the truth of God’s Word by those who once associated themselves with the church.

From a pastoral perspective, it is important to understand the distinction between the visible church (those who profess to follow Christ) and the invisible church (those who are truly born again). While many who profess faith may fall away, we hold fast to the doctrine of the perseverance of the saints. Those who are truly united to Christ by the Holy Spirit are kept by God's sovereign power and will not finally fall away. John 10:27-29; Philippians 1:6. As the Apostle John explained, those who depart from the fellowship of the believers often do so because they were never truly "of us" in a saving sense. 1 John 2:19.

If the thought of a great falling away causes you to worry about your own standing, remember that our security rests not in our own strength, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him. In His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

**Actionable Step:**
To guard your soul during times of spiritual confusion, commit to a daily rhythm of reading the Bible, starting with the Gospel of John, and involve yourself deeply in a local church that faithfully preaches the Word of God. Ask the Lord in prayer to grant you a discerning heart and to root you firmly in the truth of the Gospel. What is it about the idea of a "falling away" that most concerns you?
Should all pronouns referring to God be capitalized?

The question of whether to capitalize pronouns referring to God is a matter of English literary tradition and personal conscience rather than a direct biblical command. In the original languages of Scripture, Hebrew and Greek, there was no such distinction between capitalized and lowercase pronouns used for the Divine.

This is a tertiary issue where faithful Christians may disagree, as the Bible does not explicitly instruct us on modern grammatical rules.

Many believers choose to use "reverential capitalization" (He, Him, His) as a way to set God apart and acknowledge His supreme holiness and sovereignty. This practice serves as a constant, visible reminder that He is the Creator and we are the creatures. Those who follow this tradition often feel it is a small but meaningful way to honor the Lord in their writing and to avoid any ambiguity about whom they are referring to.

On the other hand, many other Christians and standard Bible translations, such as the King James Version or the Berean Standard Bible, use lowercase pronouns. They do this to remain faithful to the original text's simplicity and to follow standard English grammar. For these believers, the honor given to God is found in the weight of the words themselves and the underlying posture of the heart, rather than in typographical additions.

The Apostle Paul reminds us in Romans 14:5 that "each one should be fully convinced in his own mind," and in 1 Corinthians 10:31 that "whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God." Whether you choose to capitalize these pronouns or not, the vital matter is that you approach God with the awe and reverence He deserves.

As a practical step, I encourage you to focus less on the mechanics of the pen and more on the devotion of the soul. When you read the Word or write about the Lord, take a moment to pause and thank Him for His grace. Your primary concern should be walking in humble obedience and growing in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ. 2 Peter 3:18.
What is Kabbalah?

Kabbalah is a system of Jewish mysticism that seeks to explore the hidden or esoteric nature of God and the universe. While it claims ancient roots, its foundational text, the Zohar, appeared in the late thirteenth century, presenting a framework where the "Ein Sof" (the Infinite) reveals Himself through ten divine emanations or attributes known as the "Sephirot."

As believers, we must understand that Kabbalah differs fundamentally from the Christian faith regarding how we know God and how we are saved. Christianity rests upon the public revelation of God's Word rather than secret or mystical knowledge. The Bible teaches the sufficiency of Scripture, meaning that God has already given us everything we need for life and godliness through His written Word, as seen in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. We do not need to climb a mystical ladder of emanations to reach God because God has come down to us in the person of Jesus Christ.

You may wonder if there is a deeper spiritual reality that can only be found through such ancient mysteries. However, the Bible warns us to "see to it that no one takes you captive through philosophy and empty deceit, which are based on human tradition and the spiritual forces of the world rather than on Christ," according to Colossians 2:8. In Jesus Christ, "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge," as stated in Colossians 2:3. True spiritual depth is found in knowing Christ, not in seeking "hidden" codes or spiritual hierarchies.

The central truth of the Gospel is that we are reconciled to a holy God not through our own enlightenment or mystical efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone. Because of our sin, we are separated from God, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is the only mediator between God and man, as recorded in 1 Timothy 2:5.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Book of Colossians in the Bible. It was written specifically to show that Christ is supreme and sufficient, and that we do not need worldly philosophies or secret traditions to be complete in Him.

What is it about Kabbalah that has been on your mind?
Should a Christian be a prepper or in any way involved with doomsday prepping?

The question of whether a Christian should be a prepper is a matter of Christian liberty, where faithful believers may arrive at different conclusions based on their conscience, circumstances, and interpretation of biblical wisdom. Scripture does not command or forbid the storage of resources for future trials, but it provides clear principles regarding our motivations and our ultimate source of security.

The Bible frequently commends the virtue of foresight and the wise management of resources. This is often described as stewardship, which is the responsible use of what God has entrusted to us. Proverbs 22:3 states, "The prudent see danger and take refuge, but the simple keep going and pay the penalty." Furthermore, the account of Joseph in Genesis 41:47-49 demonstrates that God used the strategic storage of grain to preserve lives during a severe seven year famine. In the New Testament, we are also reminded that "if anyone does not provide for his own, and especially his own household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever" (1 Timothy 5:8). From this perspective, some Christians believe that moderate preparation for natural disasters, economic instability, or social unrest is a practical way to fulfill their duty to care for their families and to be in a position to help neighbors in need.

However, other believers emphasize the call to radical reliance on God's daily provision. They point to the words of Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount, where He commands His followers not to be anxious about tomorrow, saying, "Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself" (Matthew 6:34). For these Christians, an excessive focus on prepping can become an idol of self reliance, reflecting a "spirit of fear" rather than the "spirit of power, love, and self discipline" that God has given us (2 Timothy 1:7). They argue that our greatest witness is often found in our vulnerability and our manifest trust in God's divine providence, even when resources are scarce.

The danger in doomsday prepping often lies in the heart's inclination toward fear or isolationism. A Christian must ensure that their preparation is not an attempt to find security apart from Christ. We must remember that "unless the Lord builds the house, its builders labor in vain; unless the Lord protects the city, its watchmen stand guard in vain" (Psalm 127:1). If preparation leads a person to hoard resources while ignoring the immediate needs of the poor, or if it causes them to retreat from their mission to be salt and light in the world, it has become a spiritual snare.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to bring this matter before the Lord in prayer. Evaluate your motivations by asking whether your desire to prepare is rooted in a loving wish to serve others or in a fearful attempt to control an uncertain future. Consider reading through Matthew 6:25-34 and Proverbs 6:6-11 to see how the Bible balances the call to trust God with the call to be diligent. If you choose to prepare, do so with a heart that is ready to share your resources generously, mirroring the sacrificial love of Jesus Christ who gave everything for us.

Ultimately, whether we have much or little, our hope is anchored in the Gospel. Jesus Christ died for our sins and rose again to give us an eternal inheritance that can never perish, spoil, or fade (1 Peter 1:3-4). If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of everlasting life. What is it that truly gives you peace when you think about the future?
What is the church?

The church is the assembly of all true believers who have been redeemed by the blood of Jesus Christ and called out of the world to belong to God. Historically and biblically, the church is referred to as the *ecclesia*, a term meaning those who are called out for a specific purpose, namely to worship God and testify to His truth (Matthew 16:18; 1 Peter 2:9).

While we often think of a building when we hear the word church, the Bible describes it as a living organism, the Body of Christ, of which Jesus Himself is the supreme Head (Ephesians 1:22–23; Colossians 1:18). Scripture speaks of the church in two primary ways. First, there is the universal church, which consists of every person across all history and geography who has been truly united to Christ by faith. Second, there is the local church, which is a visible, organized gathering of believers in a specific location who meet regularly for worship, prayer, and the ministry of the Word (Acts 14:23; Hebrews 10:24–25).

The church is the "pillar and foundation of the truth," serving as the primary place where God provides the "means of grace" for our growth in holiness, a process called sanctification (1 Timothy 3:15; Acts 2:42). In the local church, believers are built up through the preaching of the Gospel, the administration of baptism and the Lord’s Supper, and the exercise of spiritual gifts for the mutual edification of the family of God (Ephesians 4:11–16).

The existence of the church is rooted entirely in the saving work of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we have all sinned, we were once separated from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating death and securing eternal life for all who believe (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 5:8). It is by grace alone, through faith alone in Christ alone, that we are justified and adopted into this heavenly family (Ephesians 2:8–9).

If you are a follower of Christ, a vital next step in your spiritual journey is to commit yourself to a local, Bible-believing church where you can submit to godly leadership and serve your brothers and sisters in love. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to become part of His redeemed people. What is it that might be holding you back from fully trusting Him today?
Does a cashless society have anything to do with the end times?

It is understandable to feel a sense of concern as you watch the rapid shift toward digital commerce and away from physical currency. Many believers look at these global changes and wonder how they might align with the prophetic descriptions of the end times found in the Word of God.

The Bible does not use the specific term "cashless society," but it does describe a future period of unprecedented economic control. In the book of Revelation, we are told of a coming global system led by a figure often called the Antichrist. The Scripture says, "He also causes all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and slave, to receive a mark on their right hand or on their foreheads, and that no one may buy or sell except one who has the mark or the name of the beast, or the number of his name" Revelation 13:16-17.

A cashless society provides the technological infrastructure that would make such a total restriction on buying and selling possible. While a digital economy is not inherently evil, it creates a mechanism where a centralized authority could, in theory, "turn off" a person's ability to participate in society. This mirrors the biblical warning of a day when economic participation is tied to spiritual allegiance.

Among faithful Christians, there are different perspectives on how to interpret these events. This falls under the category of eschatology, or the study of last things, where there is room for charitable disagreement on the exact timing and nature of these developments. Some see the move toward digital currency as a direct preparation for the events in Revelation. Others view it more cautiously as a tool that a future wicked regime could exploit, while emphasizing that the "mark" involves a conscious rejection of God and a pledge of loyalty to a false savior.

Regardless of the specific technology used, the heart of the matter is the sovereignty of God and the security of those who belong to Him. We must remember that history is not spiraling out of control but is moving toward the certain return of Jesus Christ. As our Lord told His disciples when speaking of the end of the age, "See that you are not troubled; for all these things must come to pass, but the end is not yet" Matthew 24:6.

If these thoughts cause you anxiety, I encourage you to turn your focus from the headlines to the Shepherd of your soul. A practical next step is to ground yourself daily in the promises of Scripture, perhaps by reading through the Gospel of John or the Psalms, and to remain active in a local church where the Word is preached faithfully. Trusting in Christ’s care is the best antidote to fear.

The most important preparation for the future is not financial or logistical, but spiritual. The Bible teaches that God is holy and that our sin has separated us from Him. Yet, out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What is it about the idea of a cashless society that concerns you the most?
I am a former Catholic. Should I continue to go to Catholic Church so I can reach people for Christ?

It is a noble and Christ like burden to desire the salvation of those within the Roman Catholic Church, yet your primary responsibility is to worship God in truth and to nourish your own soul in a local body that faithfully proclaims the Gospel. While you may feel that staying allows you to be a light, the Bible calls believers to be part of a church where the Word of God is the supreme authority and where the Gospel of justification by grace alone through faith alone is clearly taught.

The doctrine of justification is the hinge upon which the door of salvation turns. Historic Roman Catholic teaching, particularly from the Council of Trent, maintains that justification is a progressive process involving human cooperation and the sacraments, whereas the Bible teaches that it is a legal declaration by God where the righteousness of Christ is imputed to the believer the moment they trust in Him. Romans 5:1 states, Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. Furthermore, the Book of Hebrews emphasizes the sufficiency and finality of Christ's work, noting that He offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, which stands in contrast to the repetitive nature of the Mass. Hebrews 10:12-14 says, But when this priest had offered for all time one sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God. . . For by one offering he has made perfect forever those who are being sanctified.

Continuing to participate in a system that officially denies these central truths can confuse your testimony and hinder your own sanctification. It is difficult to call others out of a system of works-righteousness while you yourself are still formally identified with it. True fellowship requires a shared foundation of truth. 2 Corinthians 6:14-17 encourages believers not to be unequally yoked and to come out from among those who do not hold to the purity of the Gospel.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to join a faithful, Bible preaching local church where you can be discipled and strengthened in your faith. Your most effective witness to your Catholic friends and family will likely be your clear departure based on the finished work of Christ and the joy you find in the assurance of His grace. You can reach them far more effectively by inviting them to coffee or into your home to share how the Scriptures opened your eyes to the sufficiency of Jesus.

The Gospel we share is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory. Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is not something we earn through rituals or merit; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Is there anything specific about the Gospel that you find difficult to explain to your Catholic friends?
What does the Bible say about world missions?

The Bible presents world missions not as a mere human program, but as the unfolding of God’s eternal plan to redeem a people for His own glory from every nation, tribe, and tongue. From the beginning of Scripture to its final pages, God reveals Himself as a missionary God who pursues rebellious humanity with the message of reconciliation.

The foundation of missions is established in the Old Testament when God promised Abraham that in his seed, all the families of the earth would be blessed in Genesis 12:3. This global vision continues through the Psalms, where the people of God pray for His way to be known on earth and His saving power among all nations in Psalm 67:2. It is further echoed in the prophets, who foretold that the Messiah would be a light to the nations so that God's salvation might reach the ends of the earth in Isaiah 49:6.

The mission finds its central authority in the person and work of Jesus Christ. After His resurrection, Jesus issued what we call the Great Commission, commanding His followers to make disciples of all nations, baptizing them and teaching them to obey everything He commanded in Matthew 28:18-20. This is not a suggestion for a few, but a mandate for the whole Church. The book of Acts illustrates the early believers being empowered by the Holy Spirit to be witnesses in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth in Acts 1:8.

Theologically, missions is necessary because of the universal fallenness of man and the exclusive sufficiency of Christ. Because all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and because salvation is found in no one else, the Gospel must be preached so that people may hear, believe, and be saved in Romans 3:23; Acts 4:12; Romans 10:14-15. Christ’s substitutionary death on the cross is the only means by which God’s justice is satisfied and sinners are brought into fellowship with Him. This work of missions is the primary "means of grace" by which God gathers His people.

The ultimate goal of missions is the worship of the Lamb. In the end, we see the glorious fulfillment of this mission as a great multitude that no one can count, from every nation, tribe, people, and language, stands before the throne crying out that salvation belongs to our God in Revelation 7:9-10.

If you have not yet trusted in Jesus, please know that He is the only Savior who can forgive your sins and grant you peace with God. God is holy and just, but in His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life, die in the place of sinners, and rise again on the third day to secure victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in following this biblical mandate, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray specifically for one "unreached" people group. You might use a resource like the Joshua Project or your local church’s missionary updates to guide your intercession for those who have yet to hear the name of Jesus. Missions begins on our knees as we ask the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into His harvest in Matthew 9:38.
How should we live our lives in light of our identity in Christ?

To live in light of our identity in Christ is to live from a place of settled peace rather than a pursuit of performance. Because you have been joined to Jesus through faith, your life is no longer defined by your past failures or your present struggles, but by His perfect righteousness and His finished work on the cross.

The cornerstone of this new life is the doctrine of our union with Christ. This means that when God looks at you, He sees the merit and holiness of His Son (2 Corinthians 5:21). Because you are now a child of God, your conduct is not an attempt to get God to love you, but a joyful response to the fact that He already does (Ephesians 1:3-6).

Here is how this identity shapes your daily walk:

First, we live in a state of progressive sanctification, which is the lifelong process of becoming in practice what God has already declared us to be in position. Since you are "dead to sin" in Christ, you are now empowered to say "no" to the desires of the flesh and "yes" to the things of the Spirit (Romans 6:11-14). You do not fight for victory; you fight from the victory that Jesus has already won (1 Corinthians 15:57).

Second, our identity changes how we view our purpose. You are a "new creation" (2 Corinthians 5:17), which means you are now an ambassador for Christ. Your work, your relationships, and your private thoughts are all opportunities to reflect the character of the One who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light (1 Peter 2:9).
