Third, this identity provides an unshakable security. Because your salvation is held by the power of God and not by your own grip, you can live with boldness. You do not have to fear the judgment of the world or the accusations of the enemy, for there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1).

A practical next step for you this week is to choose one specific truth about who you are in Christ, such as being "completely forgiven" or "dearly loved," and meditate on it every morning. Ask the Lord in prayer to help you believe this truth more deeply than you believe your own feelings. As you go about your day, when you feel the weight of guilt or the pressure to prove yourself, remind your soul that your life is hidden with Christ in God (Colossians 3:3).

If you find yourself still searching for this peace or wondering how to begin this journey, remember that the door is open. The Gospel is the good news that Christ died for our sins and rose again to give us new life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a brand new identity. What is it that makes it difficult for you to fully rest in what Christ has done for you?
How do I know the Bible is not just mythology?

The Bible distinguishes itself from mythology through its verifiable historical grounding, the specific testimony of eyewitnesses, and its remarkable internal consistency across centuries. While myths often describe events in a vague, "long ago" primordial past, the biblical authors meticulously place their accounts within the context of known world history, naming specific rulers, cities, and dates that archaeology continues to confirm.

You may wonder if these ancient accounts are merely poetic inventions meant to explain the world, much like the legends of Greece or Rome. It is a sincere concern to want to know if the foundation of your life is built on reality or a fable. However, the biblical writers themselves were acutely aware of the difference between myth and history. The apostle Peter wrote that we did not follow cleverly devised stories when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of His majesty (2 Peter 1:16). Similarly, the Gospel of Luke begins by stating that the account was compiled from the testimony of those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word, so that the reader might know the certainty of the things they were taught (Luke 1:1-4).

The historical reliability of the Bible is most profoundly seen in the resurrection of Jesus Christ. This was not a private "spiritual" vision, but a physical event witnessed by more than five hundred people at one time, many of whom were still alive and able to be questioned when the New Testament was being written (1 Corinthians 15:6). Furthermore, the Bible contains hundreds of specific prophecies, such as the exact birthplace of the Messiah or the details of His suffering, which were recorded centuries before they were fulfilled in the life of Jesus (Micah 5:2; Isaiah 53:3-12). No collection of myths possesses this kind of prophetic precision or historical scrutiny.

Ultimately, the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, revealing the historical reality of our condition and God's solution. It teaches that God is holy and that we are separated from Him by our sin. Out of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature through the virgin birth and lived a sinless life. He died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord of all. This is not a legend; it is the central fact of history. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John. As you read, notice how Jesus speaks as one with divine authority and how His disciples respond to the reality of His presence. You might also speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to give you a heart that perceives the truth of His Word as you seek Him.
What does it mean that the Bible is infallible? What is biblical infallibility?

Biblical infallibility is the conviction that the Holy Scriptures are incapable of failing or leading us into error because they are the very breath of God. It signifies that the Bible is an entirely reliable and trustworthy guide in everything it addresses, serving as the final authority for our faith, our conduct, and our understanding of salvation.

This doctrine is rooted deeply in the character of God Himself. Because God is the author of truth and cannot lie, His Word must necessarily be free from falsehood or failure (Titus 1:2; Hebrews 6:18). We believe in what is called "verbal plenary inspiration," which means that the Holy Spirit so moved the human authors that the very words they wrote were exactly what God intended, without overriding their individual personalities (2 Peter 1:21; 2 Timothy 3:16). While the term "inerrancy" focuses on the Bible being free from factual error in the original manuscripts, "infallibility" highlights the Bible's enduring power and the fact that it will never deceive those who trust in it.

The practical beauty of this truth is that you can rest your soul upon the promises of Scripture with absolute certainty. When the Bible speaks of your justification by grace through faith in Christ alone, it is not offering a helpful suggestion or a religious theory, but an infallible reality (Romans 5:1; Ephesians 2:8-9). As the prophet Isaiah reminds us, "The grass withers and the flowers fall, but the word of our God stands forever" (Isaiah 40:8). It is the firm ground upon which we stand when the shifting sands of culture and personal feelings attempt to pull us away from Christ.

If you are looking for a place to begin experiencing the reliability of God's Word, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John. As you do, approach the text not merely as a historical record, but as the living and abiding Word of God that has the power to transform your heart (John 20:31). Consider setting aside fifteen minutes each morning to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to the wonderful truths found in His infallible Law (Psalm 119:18).
What does the Bible mean when it says something is unclean?

In the Bible, being "unclean" refers to a state of ritual or moral impurity that disqualified a person from entering the sanctuary or participating in the community's worship of God. It was a category used to illustrate the vast distinction between the common, fallen world and the perfect holiness of the Creator (Leviticus 10:10; Leviticus 11:44).

Uncleanness was often ceremonial, involving things like touching a dead body, eating forbidden foods, or suffering from certain illnesses (Numbers 19:11; Leviticus 11:4-8). These laws were not always meant to suggest the person had committed a specific sin, but they served as a constant, daily reminder that because of the Fall, the world is broken and unfit for God's immediate presence without a mediator. To be "clean" was to be ritually prepared to meet with God, while to be "unclean" was to be excluded until a process of washing or sacrifice was completed (Leviticus 15:31).

The Old Testament system was a shadow of a greater reality. It taught the people that holiness is not our natural state. We see this most clearly in the moral uncleanness of the heart, which no amount of physical washing can cure (Jeremiah 17:9; Isaiah 64:6).

This is why the ministry of Jesus Christ is so beautiful and significant. When Jesus encountered the "unclean," He did not shrink back in fear of contamination. Instead, He touched the leper and the outcast, and His own perfect purity healed and cleansed them (Matthew 8:2-3). By His sacrificial death on the cross, Jesus took our moral filth upon Himself as our substitute to satisfy the righteous justice of God. He provides the definitive cleansing and justification that the ancient washings could only point toward (Hebrews 9:13-14).

Today, for those who trust in Christ, the category of "unclean" has been transformed. We are declared holy and clean, not because of our own efforts at ritual purity, but because we have been washed by His grace and clothed in His righteousness (1 Corinthians 6:11; Revelation 7:14).

If you find yourself struggling with a sense of shame or uncleanness due to past or present sin, I encourage you to meditate on the promise of God's complete forgiveness. Read Psalm 51 slowly as a personal prayer, acknowledging your need for Him. Bring your specific burdens to the Lord in prayer today, trusting that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9).
What is the meaning of life?

The meaning of life is to know God, to be reconciled to Him through Jesus Christ, and to enjoy Him by reflecting His glory in everything we do. We were created specifically for His pleasure and for the purpose of walking in fellowship with our Creator (Isaiah 43:7; Revelation 4:11).

You may wonder why, if we have such a high purpose, life often feels aimless, heavy, or fragmented. It is a deeply human experience to feel a void that no earthly achievement or relationship can seem to fill. This restlessness exists because we were made in the Imago Dei (the image of God), yet our fellowship with Him was broken by sin. This separation leaves us searching for meaning in things that were never meant to sustain the human soul.

The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, and our sin has created a chasm between us and Him. However, because God is also rich in mercy, He provided the way for us to fulfill our true purpose. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day serves as the definitive victory over death and the guarantee of new life for all who believe. Through Christ, we experience justification (being declared righteous before God) and begin the journey of sanctification (becoming more like Him).

Ultimately, our chief end is to glorify God and enjoy Him forever (Romans 11:36; 1 Corinthians 10:31). Meaning is not something we invent for ourselves, but something we receive from the One who fashioned us. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and an eternal purpose. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31). Speak to God directly in your own words, asking Him to reveal His truth to you as you read.
How can there be subordination in the Trinity?

In the Holy Trinity, there is an eternal equality of essence alongside a voluntary subordination of role for the sake of our redemption. To understand this, we must distinguish between the "Ontological Trinity," which refers to who God is in His eternal nature, and the "Economic Trinity," which refers to how the three Persons relate to one another in the work of creation and salvation.

The Ontological Trinity describes the three Persons—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—as being "homoousios," a theological term meaning they are of the very same substance. There is no hierarchy of being or deity; the Son is not "less God" than the Father, and the Spirit is not "less God" than the Son. They are co-equal in power, glory, and eternity. Scripture affirms this unity of essence when Jesus declares, "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30) and "Anyone who has seen Me has seen the Father" (John 14:9).

The Economic Trinity describes the distinct roles each Person takes in the plan of God. In this "economy" of salvation, the Son voluntarily subordinates Himself to the Father's will. This is not a subordination of nature, but of office and function. Just as a king's son might serve as an ambassador—equal to the king in royal blood but subordinate in authority for a specific mission—so the Son of God took on human nature to accomplish our rescue. Jesus said, "For I have come down from heaven, not to do My own will, but the will of Him who sent Me" (John 6:38).

This distinction is vital for a right understanding of the Gospel. If Jesus were not truly God, His sacrifice would not have the infinite value required to pay for our sins. If He were not truly subordinate in His mission, He could not have been the perfect, obedient Servant who suffered in our place. In His humiliation, Jesus "emptied Himself by taking the form of a servant" (Philippians 2:7), yet He remained "the radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of His nature" (Hebrews 1:3).

This beautiful harmony reveals that subordination is not a sign of inferiority, but can be a profound expression of love and order. The Son delights to do the Father's will, and the Father delights to exalt the Son.

If you are wrestling with these truths, remember that the goal of theology is not merely to win an argument but to know the living God. Because the Son submitted to the Father's will, even unto death on a cross, the way to eternal life is open to you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John this week. Pay close attention to how Jesus speaks of His relationship with the Father, noting both His claims to deity and His joyful obedience. Ask the Lord in prayer to deepen your wonder at His perfect unity and His sacrificial love. What part of Jesus' submission to the Father is most difficult for you to understand?
Why are there so many religions? Do all religions lead to God?

The reason for the vast number of religions in the world is that every human being is created in the image of God with an inherent longing for Him, yet our fallen nature leads us to suppress the truth and construct various systems to reach the Divine on our own terms. While these religions often reflect a sincere search for meaning or morality, the Bible teaches that they do not all lead to God, as Jesus Christ is the only way to the Father (John 14:6).

You may wonder how it could be fair for there to be only one way to God when so many people across the globe are deeply sincere in their different traditions. This concern often arises from a compassionate desire for justice and a respect for the diversity of human experience. However, the Bible explains that the multiplication of religions is actually a result of humanity turning away from the true God. Romans 1:19–23 describes how God has made His power and divine nature plain to all through creation, but people have exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images and ideas of their own making. Religion, in a general sense, is often man's attempt to reach up to God through effort, ritual, or merit, but the Gospel is the account of God reaching down to man because we could never reach Him.

Scripture is clear that there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). This exclusivity is not based on human pride, but on the unique nature of who Jesus is and what He has accomplished. Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man, the only one capable of reconciling a holy God with sinful humanity.

The central message of the Christian faith is that God is perfectly holy, and our sin has created a separation that no amount of religious observance or good works can bridge. Because God is also perfectly loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience, was crucified to pay the debt for our sins as a substitute, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the dead, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted at the right hand of the Father as Lord of all.

Salvation is not earned by following a religious code, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you find yourself seeking to know Him more, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character of Jesus for yourself. It is also vital to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find a community of believers to support you in your journey.
Do Mormons believe in the Trinity?

No, Mormons do not believe in the historic, biblical doctrine of the Trinity. While they use the term "Godhead," they define it as three separate and distinct Gods who are one in purpose and will, but not one in essence or substance.

In biblical Christianity, we believe in one God who eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). This doctrine teaches that there is only one divine Being or essence (Deuteronomy 6:4; Isaiah 44:6). In contrast, Mormonism teaches that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three different beings altogether. Furthermore, they believe that God the Father and Jesus Christ possess physical bodies of flesh and bone, whereas the Bible clearly teaches that God is Spirit (John 4:24) and that a spirit does not have flesh and bones (Luke 24:39).

It is vital to understand these distinctions because the identity of Jesus Christ is central to our salvation. To lose the biblical view of the Trinity is to lose the true nature of God Himself. If Jesus is not truly God in the absolute, monotheistic sense, His work on the cross would not have the infinite value required to reconcile us to a holy God. 

The Scriptures tell us that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just, our sin carries a penalty we cannot pay on our own. However, in His great mercy, God the Son took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, not by any works or religious systems we might perform (Ephesians 2:8-9). 

You may wonder if these theological differences really matter as long as someone "follows Jesus." However, a "Jesus" who is a separate god from the Father is not the Jesus of the Bible. I invite you to turn to the true Lord Jesus Christ, repenting of sin and trusting in Him alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What do you find most confusing or perhaps most comforting about the idea that God is three persons in one essence?

As a practical next step in your study, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the Gospel of John. It provides a profound and clear foundation for the deity of Jesus Christ and His eternal relationship with the Father (John 1:1-14).
Where is the moral law summarily comprehended?

The moral law is summarily comprehended in the Ten Commandments, which were delivered by the voice of God and written with His own finger on tables of stone. These ten words, often called the Decalogue, provide the unchanging standard of God's holiness and our moral obligation to Him and to our fellow man (Exodus 20:1-17; Deuteronomy 10:4).

While the Ten Commandments contain the breadth of our duty, Jesus Christ further summarized them into two great requirements: to love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and mind, and to love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:37-40). This moral law is not a list of suggestions but a reflection of the very character of God. It is holy, righteous, and good, reaching beyond outward actions to the secret thoughts and intentions of the heart (Romans 7:12; Matthew 5:21-28).

You may wonder why God would give a law that is so difficult to follow perfectly. It is important to see that the law acts as a schoolmaster to lead us to Christ. By looking into the mirror of the law, we see our own sinfulness and our inability to save ourselves. The Gospel is the glorious news that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect obedience to this law on our behalf. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our law breaking, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. 

Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the Ten Commandments in Exodus 20:1-17 this week. As you do, pray and ask the Holy Spirit to show you how Christ has fulfilled the law for you and how He is now at work in your sanctification to help you walk in obedience out of love for Him.
What does it mean to be contrite? What is contrition?

To be contrite is to possess a heart that is crushed or broken by the weight of one's own sin against a holy God. Contrition is the sincere, godly sorrow that recognizes the depth of our rebellion and leads us to seek His mercy and restoration rather than merely fearing the punishment we deserve.

The word itself comes from a root meaning to be bruised or ground into powder. In the eyes of our Heavenly Father, this is not a state of hopeless despair, but the necessary beginning of true repentance. When we are contrite, we stop making excuses for our failings and agree with God's assessment of our spiritual condition. The Scripture assures us that the High and Lofty One who inhabits eternity dwells with the one who has a contrite and lowly spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly and to revive the heart of the contrite ones (Isaiah 57:15).

True contrition is a gift of God's grace that prepares the soul for the comfort of the Gospel. It is distinguished from worldly sorrow, which often mourns the loss of reputation or the onset of consequences, whereas godly sorrow produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret (2 Corinthians 7:10). We see this most clearly in the life of David, who cried out that the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit, and that a broken and contrite heart God will not despise (Psalm 51:17). This inward brokenness is the fertile soil where the Holy Spirit works to produce the fruit of a changed life.

If you feel the heavy weight of your sin today, do not run from it, for that very heaviness is often God's kindness leading you to seek His face. You may wonder how a holy God could ever accept someone who has failed so deeply. The wonderful truth is that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took the crushing weight of our sin upon Himself at the cross. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, rising bodily on the third day to prove His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him with your heart today?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to set aside quiet time this evening to read through Psalm 51. As you read, use the words of the Psalmist to guide your own direct conversation with God, confessing specific areas where you have wandered and asking Him to create in you a clean heart. Seek out a local, Bible teaching church where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ alongside other believers.
What does it mean that “at your right hand are pleasures forevermore” (Psalm 16:11)?

This beautiful promise in Psalm 16:11 means that the ultimate end of human existence is to find total satisfaction and unending joy in the immediate presence of God. When David speaks of being at God's right hand, he is describing a place of supreme honor, security, and intimacy where the soul finally finds what it was created to desire.

In the ancient world, the right hand was the position of highest favor and authority. To be at God's right hand is to be brought into His inner circle of fellowship. The pleasures mentioned here are not the fleeting, shallow gratifications of this world, which often leave us empty, but are the substantial and eternal delights that flow from God's own character. This is often referred to by theologians as the beatific vision, the direct and transformative sight of God's glory that constitutes the highest heaven for the believer (1 Corinthians 13:12; 1 John 3:2).

While David wrote these words as a personal expression of trust, the New Testament reveals that they are preeminently fulfilled in Jesus Christ. The Apostle Peter, preaching on the day of Pentecost, explained that David was looking forward to the resurrection of the Messiah (Acts 2:25-28). Because Jesus was not abandoned to the grave but was raised and exalted to the right hand of the Father, He has opened the way for all who trust in Him to share in this eternal joy (Hebrews 12:2). 

Our hearts were made for God, and they will remain restless until they find their rest in Him. The world offers many broken cisterns that cannot hold water, but God offers Himself as the fountain of living waters (Jeremiah 2:13). To have "pleasures forevermore" is to experience a state of glorification where sin, sorrow, and death are replaced by the radiant light of God's countenance (Revelation 21:3-4).

If you find yourself searching for a happiness that does not fade, I invite you to look to the One who sat down at the right hand of Majesty. The Gospel tells us that though we are separated from God by our sin, Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to reconcile us to the Father. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What is it that prevents you from resting your hope entirely on Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to set aside time this week to meditate specifically on the attributes of God, such as His holiness and His love. Practice the discipline of being still in His presence through prayer, asking Him to give you a greater distaste for the "passing pleasures of sin" (Hebrews 11:25) and a deeper hunger for the lasting joy found in His Word.
What is divine providence?

Divine providence is God's sovereign preservation and governance of all things, from the vastness of the heavens to the smallest detail of your life, according to His holy will and for His glory. It is the active and constant exercise of His power by which He upholds His creation and directs every event to its appointed end (Psalm 103:19; Hebrews 1:3).

My friend, this means that you do not live in a world of chaotic chance or cold fate. Rather, you are held in the hands of a God who is both infinitely powerful and tenderly personal. Scripture teaches that God’s providence is so comprehensive that not even a sparrow falls to the ground apart from the will of your Father (Matthew 10:29-31). He works through all things, including the natural laws of the universe and the choices of human beings, to accomplish His perfect purposes. While humans are responsible for their actions, God remains the supreme governor who guides even the difficult moments of life for the ultimate good of those who love Him (Romans 8:28).

We see the beauty of providence most clearly in the cross of Jesus Christ. While men acted out of their own sinful desires to crucify the Lord of glory, God was sovereignly using those very actions to provide the sacrifice necessary for our salvation (Acts 2:23; Acts 4:27-28). What man intended for evil, God intended for good (Genesis 50:20).

This same God, who governs the stars, invites you into a relationship with Himself. God in His providence sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all creation. Salvation is found in Him alone, received by grace alone through faith alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step for your soul, I encourage you to read through Psalm 121 this evening. Let it remind you that your help comes from the Lord, the Maker of heaven and earth, who neither slumbers nor sleeps. When you pray, specifically thank Him for three "unseen" ways He has protected or provided for you today.

What aspect of God’s control over your circumstances do you find most difficult to trust Him with today?
In what ways is the Christian life like the Olympics?

The Christian life is frequently compared to an athletic competition in Scripture to illustrate the necessity of discipline, unwavering endurance, and a single-minded focus on the eternal prize found in Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul often used the imagery of the Greek games to show that the life of faith is not one of passivity, but of purposeful, spiritual exertion.

One primary way the Christian life resembles the Olympics is through the necessity of discipline and self control. Just as an Olympic athlete must forgo certain comforts and follow a strict regimen to be at their best, the believer is called to the process of progressive sanctification. In 1 Corinthians 9:25, it says, "Everyone who competes in the games exercises self control in all things. They then do it to receive a perishable wreath, but we an imperishable one." This discipline involves putting off the old self and training the soul through the means of grace, such as the study of the Word, prayer, and fellowship with the local church.

A second comparison is found in the requirement of endurance. An Olympic marathoner must push through "the wall" of exhaustion to reach the finish line. Similarly, the Christian life is a long distance race that requires the perseverance of the saints. We are encouraged in Hebrews 12:1 to "lay aside every weight, and the sin which so easily ensnares us, and let us run with endurance the race that is set before us." This endurance is sustained by "looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith" (Hebrews 12:2). When the trials of life make the race feel impossible, we remember that Christ has already run the perfect race on our behalf and provides the strength we need to continue.

Thirdly, both the athlete and the believer must compete according to the rules. In 2 Timothy 2:5, the Bible states, "And if anyone competes as an athlete, he is not crowned unless he competes according to the rules." For the Christian, this means living in obedience to God’s revealed will in Scripture. We do not set our own course or define our own truth, but we follow the path marked out for us by our Sovereign Lord.

While an Olympian runs for a gold medal that will eventually fade or be forgotten, the Christian runs for an incorruptible crown of righteousness. Paul writes in 2 Timothy 4:7-8, "I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith. From now on there is laid up for me the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will award to me on that day."

The most beautiful distinction, however, is that while Olympic prizes are earned strictly by human merit, our prize is a gift of grace. You may sometimes feel as though you have tripped or been disqualified by your failures, but the Gospel brings hope to the weary runner. Jesus Christ lived the only life of perfect discipline and died to pay for our sins, rising again to secure our victory. Salvation is not a trophy for the fastest, but a gift for the repentant who trust in Him. You can turn to God even now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive the gift of eternal life.

As a practical step in your spiritual training, I encourage you to establish a daily rhythm of reading the Bible, perhaps starting with the Gospel of John, and to join a faithful local church where other runners can encourage you in the race. What is one specific area of your life where you feel you need the Holy Spirit's strength to persevere right now?
What should be the Christian perspective on nudity in art?

The Christian perspective on nudity in art begins with the foundational truth that the human body is a masterpiece of God's creation, designed by Him and declared very good in its original state. However, because we live in a fallen world, a faithful view must also account for the reality of sin, the necessity of modesty, and the biblical command to guard the heart against lust.

Scripture teaches that God created man and woman in His own image, and in the beginning, they were naked and unashamed, Genesis 1:27; Genesis 2:25. The human form reflects the wisdom and beauty of the Creator, and there is a legitimate sense in which art can celebrate the "Imago Dei" (Image of God) by depicting the anatomy God designed, Psalm 139:14. Yet, after the Fall, the entrance of sin brought a sense of shame and a divine provision for clothing, Genesis 3:7; Genesis 3:21. This indicates that in our current state, nakedness is generally reserved for the privacy and sanctity of marriage, Hebrews 13:4.

When evaluating nudity in art, Christians often find themselves navigating three primary considerations:

1. **Intent and Context:** There is a profound moral difference between art that seeks to capture the dignity and grace of the human form and material created to incite illicit desire. While pornography is designed to exploit the body for selfish gratification, classical art often aims at anatomical study or historical storytelling. However, even with "artistic" intent, the Christian must be honest about the effects of the work on the viewer.

2. **The Guarding of the Heart:** Our Lord Jesus taught that to look at another with lustful intent is to commit adultery in the heart, Matthew 5:28. Because the human heart is prone to sin, what one person views as a harmless sculpture, another may find to be a significant temptation. Christians are called to walk in holiness and to avoid providing "provisions for the flesh," Romans 13:14.

3. **Christian Liberty and Conscience:** This is often treated as a matter of Christian liberty where faithful believers may reach different conclusions based on their own conscience and susceptibility to temptation. Some may feel a total avoidance of such art is necessary for their sanctification, while others may appreciate the artistic merit without stumbling. We are commanded not to judge one another in these non-essential matters, but also to ensure our liberty does not become a stumbling block to others, Romans 14:13; 1 Corinthians 8:9.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to practice a "theology of the eyes" by asking yourself whether a particular piece of art leads you to worship the Creator for His handiwork or draws your heart toward sinful thoughts. If you find your conscience is uneasy or your thoughts are wandering toward lust, it is an act of love for God and yourself to turn away. 

Remember that God is the author of all beauty, and He desires for us to enjoy His creation in a way that preserves our purity and honors His holiness. You might find it helpful to pray and ask for wisdom, James 1:5, as you discern how to engage with culture while keeping your gaze fixed on Christ.
Should we allow false teachers into our home?

The Bible provides clear instruction regarding the reception of false teachers, most notably in the second epistle of John. We are commanded not to receive into our homes anyone who comes to us while departing from the essential teachings of Christ, particularly His deity and humanity.

The Apostle John writes, "If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into your house or greet him. For whoever greets him shares in his wicked works" (2 John 1:10-11).

In the context of the early church, receiving someone into one's home often implied providing a base of operations for their ministry and granting them the "right hand of fellowship." To welcome a false teacher in this manner is to offer a platform for the spread of "heresy," which is a soul-destroying error. By providing such hospitality, a believer inadvertently gives the impression that the false teacher's message is valid, thereby becoming a participant in the subversion of the truth.

This warning specifically targets those who actively propagate a "false gospel" or deny the "hypostatic union," which is the reality that Jesus is fully God and fully man. Protecting the purity of the church and the spiritual safety of your own household is a profound act of love for God and His people.

However, we must distinguish between an itinerant false teacher seeking a platform and a lost neighbor or friend who is simply confused. We are called to be "gentle to all, able to teach, patient" (2 Timothy 2:24) when engaging those who are in error. We should show kindness to the lost, but we must never provide the sanctuary of our homes to the promotion of doctrines that lead people away from the only Savior.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the study of the New Testament epistles, such as Galatians and 2 Peter, which define the boundaries of the Gospel. Compare every teaching you hear against the "infallible" Word of God. If you are unsure about a particular teacher or group, please reach out to the elders of your local church for guidance and protection.

The heart of our faith is the finished work of Jesus Christ, who died for our sins and rose bodily from the grave. If you have not yet placed your full trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be preventing you from resting entirely in His grace today?
How is God’s Word a lamp to my feet (Psalm 119:105)?

God’s Word serves as a lamp by providing the spiritual illumination necessary to see the truth of your circumstances and the path of obedience in a world often clouded by the darkness of sin. It acts as a practical guide that reveals both the obstacles to avoid and the righteous direction to follow, ensuring that your life is built on the firm foundation of God’s truth rather than the shifting sands of human opinion.

This divine guidance is rooted in the doctrine of the sufficiency of Scripture, which teaches that the Bible contains everything we need for life and godliness. While the world is often a place of confusion and moral ambiguity, the Word of God is a "light shining in a dark place" until the day dawns (2 Peter 1:19). It does not always reveal the entire map of your life years in advance, but like a lamp held in the hand, it provides enough light for the very next step you must take. This requires you to walk in constant, humble dependence on the Lord, returning to His Word daily to keep your path visible.

The Holy Spirit performs a work called illumination, opening your heart to perceive the beauty and requirements of God’s Law (Psalm 119:18). Without this light, we are prone to stumble over the roots of temptation or wander into the ravines of error. As the Proverbs remind us, "For the commandment is a lamp, and the teaching is a light; and the reproofs of discipline are the way to life" (Proverbs 6:23). When you submit your decisions, relationships, and even your private thoughts to the light of the Bible, you are protected from the "stumbling blocks" that lead to spiritual ruin.

To experience this light in your daily life, I encourage you to begin a "Lamp-Lighting" rhythm. Each morning this week, read one chapter of the Gospel of John or the Psalms and ask the Lord in prayer to show you one specific command to obey or one promise to trust for that day. By fixing your eyes on Jesus, who is the Light of the World (John 8:12), you will find that His Word never fails to guide you home.
Why were the people guilty for a priest’s sin?

In the Old Testament, the high priest was not merely a private citizen but the official representative of the entire nation before God. When the priest sinned, he brought guilt upon the people because he acted as their representative head, meaning his standing before God directly affected the spiritual standing of the community he served (Leviticus 4:3).

Because the priest was responsible for maintaining the holiness of the sanctuary and mediating the relationship between the Lord and the assembly, his sin polluted the very channel through which the people approached God. This principle of corporate responsibility shows that in God's eyes, the leader and the people are linked together. If the mediator who is supposed to offer sacrifices for sin becomes a sinner himself, the entire system of worship is compromised until atonement is made (Leviticus 4:3; Hebrews 5:1–3). 

This reality highlights our desperate need for a perfect representative. Human priests, being frail and sinful, could never provide a permanent solution for our guilt. This is why the Gospel is such glorious news. While we were once represented by Adam, whose sin brought death to all, believers are now represented by Jesus Christ, the Great High Priest. Jesus took on human nature, lived a perfectly sinless life, and offered Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried, rose bodily on the third day, and is now exalted as Lord over all.

You may wonder how it could be just for the actions of one person to affect so many others. While it seems difficult, this is the very mechanism God uses to save us. Just as one man's sin brought guilt, one Man’s righteousness brings life. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive total forgiveness and a new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to pray regularly for your church leaders. Ask the Lord to protect their hearts and keep them blameless, for their spiritual health is deeply tied to the health of the congregation (1 Timothy 2:1–2).
What is spiritual mapping?

Spiritual mapping is a practice within certain modern Christian circles that involves researching the history of a city or region to identify perceived demonic influences or "territorial spirits" that may be hindering the Gospel. Proponents of this method believe that by uncovering historical sins, such as ancient idolatry or past injustices, they can engage in "strategic level spiritual warfare" to "bind" these spirits and clear the way for spiritual revival.

While the Bible is clear that we are engaged in a spiritual battle, it is important to distinguish between the reality of spiritual warfare and the specific methodology of spiritual mapping. The Scripture teaches that our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world and against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms (Ephesians 6:12). However, when we look at the ministry of the apostles, we find a consistent focus on the proclamation of the Gospel rather than the mapping of demonic hierarchies.

When the Apostle Paul entered Athens, a city full of idols, he did not spend his time researching the names of the specific demons behind those idols to perform a ritual of "mapping." Instead, his spirit was provoked by the idolatry he saw, and he went directly to the marketplace and the Areopagus to preach the resurrection of Jesus Christ (Acts 17:16; Acts 17:22-31). The "strongholds" the New Testament speaks of are often described as arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, which are destroyed by the power of the Word (2 Corinthians 10:4-5).

As a believer, your primary weapon in the face of spiritual darkness is the Gospel itself, which is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes (Romans 1:16). While it is helpful to understand the culture and history of your community to love your neighbors better, your confidence must remain in the finished work of Christ and the authority of His Word. God is sovereign over every square inch of this earth, and He does not require us to uncover hidden demonic names before He can save a soul.

If you feel a burden for your city or a sense of spiritual opposition, the most faithful path is to put on the full armor of God (Ephesians 6:13-18). This includes grounding yourself in truth, righteousness, the Gospel of peace, faith, the assurance of your salvation, and the diligent use of the Bible and prayer. You might consider gathering with other believers to pray specifically for the hearts of those in your community to be opened to the light of Christ.

If you are seeking peace or victory over the darkness in your own life, remember that Jesus Christ has already triumphed over every power and authority through His death and resurrection (Colossians 2:15). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from fully resting in His victory today?
What is God’s design for marriage?

God’s design for marriage is a sacred, lifelong covenant between one man and one woman, established by Him at the beginning of creation for His glory and our good. This union is the foundational building block of human society and a profound earthly picture of the eternal relationship between Jesus Christ and His people.

In the beginning, God declared that it was not good for man to be alone, and He created a helper suitable for him (Genesis 2:18). He did not create another man or a group of people, but one woman, Eve, and brought her to the man. Scripture records that a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh (Genesis 2:24). This one flesh union involves a physical, emotional, and spiritual bonding that is meant to be exclusive and indissoluble.

Jesus Christ confirmed this original design when He was questioned about divorce, stating that what God has joined together, no human being should separate (Matthew 19:4-6). This highlights that marriage is a divine work, not merely a human arrangement. Within this design, husbands and wives are called to reflect the Gospel through their love and service to one another. The husband is called to lead with sacrificial, Christlike love, while the wife is called to support and respect her husband, just as the church submits to Christ (Ephesians 5:22; Ephesians 5:23; Ephesians 5:24; Ephesians 5:25; Ephesians 5:26; Ephesians 5:27; Ephesians 5:28; Ephesians 5:29; Ephesians 5:30; Ephesians 5:31; Ephesians 5:32; Ephesians 5:33). This relationship is a mystery because its ultimate purpose is to point the world toward the love of Jesus for His bride, the Church.

If you are married, a beautiful next step in honoring this design is to set aside a specific time this week to pray together with your spouse, asking the Holy Spirit to strengthen your union and help you reflect His love. If you are not married, you can use this time to reflect on how God’s faithfulness in the Gospel is the true foundation for all human relationships.

This beautiful design reminds us that we all fall short of God’s perfect standard and need His grace. We often fail to love as we should, but the ultimate marriage is the one between Christ and His people. This is made possible because Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to give us victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your understanding of how Christ’s love for the church helps us understand the purpose of marriage?
What is the value of group Bible study?

Group Bible study is the communal practice of believers gathering to read, interpret, and apply the Holy Scriptures together under the guidance of the Holy Spirit. This shared discipline is essential because it fosters mutual edification, protects the individual from isolated misinterpretations, and serves as a vital means of grace for progressive sanctification.

In the early church, the believers "devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer" (Acts 2:42). This fellowship, or *koinonia*, is not merely social, it is a spiritual participation in the life of Christ through His Word. When we study together, we allow the "word of Christ to dwell in you richly as you teach and admonish one another with all wisdom" (Colossians 3:16). 

The value of this corporate study is seen in several specific ways. First, it provides a healthy environment for "iron to sharpen iron" (Proverbs 27:17). Our brothers and sisters often perceive truths or applications in a text that our own personal biases or experiences might overlook. Second, it provides a hedge of protection against doctrinal error. While the Bible is our ultimate authority, the collective wisdom of the local church helps us stay tethered to the "faith that was once for all entrusted to the saints" (Jude 1:3).

Furthermore, group study moves us toward practical obedience. It is one thing to read a command alone, but it is quite another to discuss how to live out that command with others who can hold us accountable. We are called to "consider how to stir up one another to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another" (Hebrews 10:24-25). 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to contact the elders or leaders of your local church this week and ask to join a small group or a mid-week Bible study. If your church does not currently have one, perhaps you could invite one or two fellow believers to meet regularly to read through a book of the Bible together, such as the Gospel of John, using a simple method of observation, interpretation, and application. Walking together in the Word is a necessary rhythm for every believer who desires to grow in the knowledge and grace of our Lord Jesus Christ.
How can I overcome a feeling of spiritual emptiness?

Spiritual emptiness is a profound longing that serves as a reminder that your soul was created for fellowship with the living God. It is a hunger that no earthly success, relationship, or possession can ever satisfy, because God has set eternity in the human heart (Ecclesiastes 3:11).

You may feel as though you are wandering in a dry and weary land where there is no water (Psalm 63:1). This ache is often the very means God uses to draw you away from broken cisterns that cannot hold water and back to Himself, the fountain of living waters (Jeremiah 2:13).

To overcome this emptiness, you must first recognize that true life is found only in a right relationship with God through Jesus Christ. We are all separated from God's holiness by our sin, which leaves us spiritually dead and hollow. However, God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers us not just an improved life, but new life. Our justification, being declared righteous before God, comes by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you have never placed your trust in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For those who already belong to Christ but feel a season of spiritual dryness, the remedy is to return to the means of grace that God has provided for our sanctification. Our union with Christ is secure, but our communion with Him requires diligent pursuit. Here are practical steps to seek Him:

First, immerse yourself in the Word of God. Do not merely read it for information, but feast upon it for spiritual life. Jesus said, "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst" (John 6:35). 

Second, prioritize honest, persistent prayer. Tell the Lord exactly how you feel, for He is a sympathetic High Priest who invites us to draw near to the throne of grace (Hebrews 4:15-16). 

Third, reconnect with the local church. We are not meant to walk this path in isolation. The fellowship of the saints and the preaching of the Word are vital for stirring up love and good works (Hebrews 10:24-25).

As a concrete next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, perhaps one chapter each morning. Before you read, ask the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to see the beauty and sufficiency of Jesus, and then talk to Him about what you find. Seek out a mature believer in your local church this week to share your struggle and ask them to pray with you, for the prayer of a righteous person has great power (James 5:16).
How can I, a Muslim, become assured of paradise?

You may wonder if your good deeds will ever be enough to outweigh your sins on the day of judgment, and it is a heavy burden to live without knowing for certain if God has accepted you. Biblical assurance of paradise is not found in the uncertainty of our own efforts, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ, who provides a certain hope that no human striving can achieve.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, and His standard for entering His presence is nothing less than moral perfection (Matthew 5:48; Habakkuk 1:13). Because every human being has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God, none of us can earn our way into heaven through religious rituals or moral scales (Romans 3:23). Our best works are unable to bridge the gap created by our rebellion against a holy Creator (Isaiah 64:6).

True assurance comes when we stop looking at our own performance and look instead to Jesus Christ. Jesus is truly God and truly man, the eternal Word who took on human flesh to save us (John 1:1; John 1:14). He lived the life of perfect obedience that we failed to live and then willingly died on the cross as a substitute for sinners. On that cross, He bore the wrath of God that we deserved, paying the debt of our sin in full (1 Peter 2:24; Romans 5:8). His bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day is the historical proof that His sacrifice was accepted and that death has been defeated (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9). When you trust in Him, His righteousness is credited to you, and your sins are forgiven forever. This is why the Apostle John wrote that we may "know" that we have eternal life (1 John 5:13).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today? As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel according to John in the Bible, which was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ and have life in His name (John 20:31).
What is the spiritual gift of miracles?

The gift of miracles is a supernatural empowerment given by the Holy Spirit that allows a believer to perform acts that override the natural order of the world. These acts serve as signs to authenticate the truth of God’s Word and the authority of His messengers (Hebrews 2:3–4; 1 Corinthians 12:10).

In the New Testament, these "sign gifts" were prominently used by Jesus and the Apostles to prove that their message was truly from God. For example, the Apostle Paul spoke of the "signs of a true apostle" being performed among the believers in Corinth (2 Corinthians 12:12). These miracles were never intended to be mere spectacles, but were pointers to the reality of the Kingdom of God and the divinity of Jesus Christ.

Within the global church, there are different historic views regarding how this gift functions today. Many believers hold to "cessationism," the view that these specific sign gifts were foundational and ceased once the New Testament was completed and the Apostolic office passed away. Others hold to "continuationism," believing that the Holy Spirit continues to distribute the gift of miracles to believers today for the edification of the church. Regardless of which view one holds, all orthodox Christians agree that God is sovereign and still performs miracles today in response to the faithful prayers of His people.

While we may marvel at physical wonders, we must remember that the greatest miracle of all is the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we were without hope. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This miracle provides the only way for us to be reconciled to God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in your study, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John. Pay close attention to the seven specific miracles, or "signs," that Jesus performs, and notice how each one is intended to lead the observer to believe that He is the Christ, the Son of God (John 20:30–31). Seeking a mature believer in your local church to discuss these passages with can also provide great clarity and encouragement.
What does it mean that God is merciful?

God is merciful means that He is compassionate and kind toward His creatures, particularly by withholding the punishment that our sins deserve and offering help in our distress. It is the divine disposition to be moved by the misery of others and to act for their relief, rooted in His very nature as the one who is "gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love" (Exodus 34:6; Psalm 103:8).

In the context of our salvation, mercy is often distinguished from grace. While grace is God giving us the favor and eternal life we do not deserve, mercy is God not giving us the judgment and condemnation we do deserve. This divine compassion is not a sign of weakness or an overlooking of sin, but is perfectly satisfied through the work of Jesus Christ. In His mercy, God provided a substitute to bear the penalty of our transgressions so that His justice would be upheld while His compassion reached the sinner (Romans 3:25-26; Titus 3:5).

This mercy is most clearly seen in the propitiation made by Christ on the cross. Propitiation refers to the turning away of God's righteous wrath through a sacrifice. Because Jesus took the punishment for sin upon Himself, God is able to be "rich in mercy" toward us, making us alive in Christ even when we were dead in our sins (Ephesians 2:4-5). For the believer, this means that when we stumble, we do not approach a cold judge, but a merciful High Priest who has been tempted in every way as we are, yet was without sin (Hebrews 4:15-16).

If you are currently feeling the heavy weight of guilt or the sorrow of your own failures, you may wonder how a holy God could ever look upon you with kindness. It is a natural human concern to fear judgment when we know we have fallen short. However, the Bible teaches that God's mercies are new every morning and His faithfulness is great (Lamentations 3:22-23). He does not treat us as our sins deserve but invites the weary and heavy-laden to find rest in Him.

The heart of the Gospel is this display of mercy: God is holy and we are sinful, separated from Him by our rebellion. Yet, because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise bodily on the third day. Through His victory over death, salvation is offered as a free gift. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive this wonderful mercy. What holds you back from trusting in His mercy today?

As a practical step in experiencing this truth, I encourage you to read through Psalm 103 today. As you read, take those specific burdens or sins that are weighing on your conscience and speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to help you rest in the mercy He has provided through Jesus. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word preached and find fellowship with others who live by this same mercy.
Who is the head of the church, biblically speaking?

Jesus Christ is the sole and supreme head of the church, which is His body. Scripture declares that God the Father placed all things under Christ's feet and appointed Him to be head over everything for the church (Ephesians 1:22; Colossians 1:18).

This truth establishes the Lordship of Christ as the only authority that governs the life, worship, and doctrine of His people. Because He is the head, the church does not belong to any person, denomination, or government, but to Him alone by virtue of His finished work on the cross. While He graciously appoints elders to lead and care for local congregations, these leaders serve as under-shepherds who must give an account to the Chief Shepherd (1 Peter 5:4; Hebrews 13:17). A healthy church is one that looks constantly to Christ, relying on His Spirit and His Word for every direction and decision (Ephesians 5:23; Ephesians 5:24).

Practically, this means that our first loyalty in all matters of conscience and faith belongs to Jesus. As a next step, I encourage you to pray for the leaders of your local church, asking that God would give them the humility to follow Christ's lead and the wisdom to teach His Word faithfully. Since Christ is your head, you can rest in His perfect care, knowing He provides everything the body needs to grow in grace.
Who is the ancient serpent of Revelation 12:9?

The ancient serpent of Revelation 12:9 is the devil, also known as Satan, the spiritual being who leads the entire world into deception. This title links him directly to the account of the fall of man in the Garden of Eden, where he appeared as a serpent to tempt our first parents into sin and rebellion against God.

The Apostle John records this identification with great clarity, stating that "the great dragon was hurled down, that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray" in Revelation 12:9. We see this title repeated again in Revelation 20:2, which refers to "the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan." These passages reveal that the enemy of our souls is not a mere metaphor or a myth, but a real, personal adversary who has been a murderer and a liar from the beginning, as Jesus describes in John 8:44.

While the serpent’s deception brought sin and death into the world, the Scriptures provide us with the glorious hope of the Gospel. God promised from the very beginning that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent’s head, a prophecy fulfilled by Jesus Christ through His perfect life, sacrificial death on the cross, and bodily resurrection (Genesis 3:15; Colossians 2:15). By His victory, Christ has disarmed the powers of darkness and secured the salvation of all who trust in Him.

You may wonder how a holy and sovereign God could allow such an ancient enemy to continue his work of deception for a season. It is an understandable concern, yet we rest in the truth that Satan’s time is short and his judgment is certain. If you find yourself burdened by the weight of sin or the deceptions of this world, know that there is total forgiveness and safety in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to put on the full armor of God described in Ephesians 6:10-18, specifically immersing yourself in the "sword of the Spirit," which is the Word of God, to recognize and resist the schemes of the devil. What holds you back from fully trusting in Christ's victory today?
Book of 1 John - Bible Survey

The First Epistle of John is a pastoral letter written by the Apostle John to provide believers with the absolute certainty of their salvation while defending the orthodox faith against those who deny the incarnation of Jesus Christ. It functions as a spiritual compass, guiding the church into deeper fellowship with God through the essential themes of truth, holiness, and brotherly love.

The primary purpose of this letter is stated clearly toward its conclusion, which is that you may know that you have eternal life as you believe in the name of the Son of God 1 John 5:13. John writes with the heart of a father, addressing his readers as little children, and he provides several tests by which a person can examine the reality of their faith. These are not tests to earn salvation, but rather the evidence that a miraculous work of grace has already occurred in the heart.

First, John emphasizes the moral test. He declares that God is light, and in Him there is no darkness at all 1 John 1:5. Therefore, those who claim to have fellowship with Him but continue to walk in habitual, unrepentant darkness are deceiving themselves. True believers will confess their sins and seek to walk in obedience to His commands 1 John 1:9; 1 John 2:3-4.

Second, there is the social test of love. John insists that because God is love, those who are born of God must love one another 1 John 4:7-8. To hate a brother while claiming to love God is presented as a spiritual impossibility. This love is not a mere feeling but a sacrificial action modeled after Christ, who laid down His life for us 1 John 3:16.

Third, John presents the doctrinal test. During this time, false teachers were beginning to suggest that Jesus did not actually come in the flesh. John vigorously defends the hypostatic union, the truth that Jesus is truly God and truly man. Every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God 1 John 4:2.

At the heart of this letter is the glorious Gospel. You may wonder how a holy God could ever welcome sinners into such intimate fellowship. The answer is found in the propitiation of Jesus Christ. Because we have all sinned and fallen short, Christ came as the perfect sacrifice to satisfy the righteous wrath of God on our behalf 1 John 2:2; 1 John 4:10. Through His death and bodily resurrection, the way to the Father is opened.

If you find yourself doubting your standing before God or feel the weight of your transgressions, remember that your assurance does not rest on the strength of your grip on Christ, but on the strength of His grip on you. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the five chapters of 1 John in a single sitting this week. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to highlight where you can grow in practical love for the people in your local church. What holds you back from fully trusting in the finished work of Christ today?
What happens at the final judgment?

The final judgment is the climactic and public event where Jesus Christ, as the appointed Judge of the living and the dead, will bring all human history to its conclusion by rendering a final, righteous verdict upon every individual (Acts 17:31; 2 Timothy 4:1). It is the moment when the perfect justice of God and His infinite mercy are fully and finally displayed to all creation.

During this judgment, every person who has ever lived will stand before the great white throne of God (Revelation 20:11-12). The Bible describes the opening of books that record every deed, word, and hidden motive of the heart (Matthew 12:36; Ecclesiastes 12:14). For those who stand before God on the basis of their own works, the standard is nothing less than the perfect holiness of God. Because all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, no one can be justified by their own merit (Romans 3:20; Romans 3:23).

However, there is another book mentioned: the Lamb's Book of Life (Revelation 21:27). This book contains the names of all who have been redeemed by the blood of Christ. For the believer, the final judgment is not a day of terror or condemnation, because Jesus Christ has already endured the wrath of God in their place through His penal substitutionary atonement (Romans 8:1; Galatians 3:13). While believers will give an account for their lives and receive rewards for their faithfulness, their entrance into eternal life is secured entirely by the grace of God through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; 2 Corinthians 5:10).

The ultimate outcome of the final judgment is the eternal separation of humanity. Those whose names are not found in the Book of Life will face eternal punishment, while the righteous, clothed in the righteousness of Christ, will enter into eternal life in the new heavens and the new earth (Matthew 25:46; Revelation 21:1-4).

It is understandable to feel a sense of dread when considering the holiness of God and the certainty of judgment. We all possess a conscience that whispers of our inadequacy. Yet, this very realization is meant to drive us to the only One who can save us. The Gospel is the good news that although we are more sinful than we ever dared believe, we are more loved in Christ than we ever dared hope. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a sinless life, died for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to give us victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You do not have to face the final judgment alone or in your own strength. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and the gift of His own righteousness. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, specifically chapter 3 and chapter 5, to see the authority of Jesus and the promise of life He offers to all who believe. Seeking fellowship with a local, Bible-believing church can also provide you with a community of believers to walk alongside you as you explore these truths.
In what ways is becoming a Christian becoming an entirely new man / woman?

To become a Christian is to undergo a miraculous transformation called regeneration, where the Holy Spirit imparts spiritual life to a soul that was previously dead in sin. The Bible describes this not merely as a moral improvement or a change of opinion, but as being a new creation in Christ Jesus, as stated in 2 Corinthians 5:17.

This transformation affects a person in several profound ways. First, there is a change in identity and standing before God. Through the work of Jesus Christ, a person is moved from a state of condemnation under God's righteous wrath to a state of justification, where they are declared righteous because the perfect obedience of Christ is credited to them, as seen in Romans 5:1. You are no longer defined by your past failures or your own efforts, but by your union with the Son of God.

Second, becoming a new person involves a change of heart and affections. God promises in Ezekiel 36:26 to remove the heart of stone and give a heart of flesh. This means that the things you once loved, such as autonomy from God and the pleasures of sin, lose their appeal, while the things you once ignored, such as the glory of God and the holiness of His Word, become your greatest desires. This is the beginning of sanctification, the lifelong process where the Holy Spirit conforms you more and more into the image of Jesus.

Third, there is a change in purpose and destiny. A Christian is no longer living for the fleeting treasures of this world, but for the glory of the King who bought them with His own blood. As Galatians 2:20 explains, the old self has been crucified with Christ, and it is now Christ who lives within the believer. This new life is characterized by a hope that transcends death, grounded in the historical fact of the resurrection of Jesus.

At the heart of this new life is the Gospel. The holy and righteous God created us for His glory, but we have all rebelled and fallen short of His standard, which is called sin. Because God is just, He must punish sin. However, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in this new life, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. It is a wonderful place to see the heart of Jesus and to understand more deeply what it means to believe in Him. Additionally, seek out a local church where the Bible is faithfully preached, as fellowship with other believers is essential for growing as a new man or woman in Christ.
What are the Pauline epistles?

The Pauline epistles are the thirteen letters in the New Testament written by the Apostle Paul to various churches and individuals to provide theological instruction, pastoral correction, and practical guidance. These inspired writings consist of Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon.

These letters are of immense importance because they explain the profound mystery of the Gospel and how the finished work of Jesus Christ applies to the life of the believer. Paul was chosen by God to articulate the doctrine of justification, which is the legal declaration by God that a sinner is righteous in His sight, not based on our own works, but through faith alone in the person and work of Christ (Romans 3:21-26; Galatians 2:16). In these writings, we see the beautiful harmony of God's sovereign grace and the believer’s responsibility to live a life of holiness.

Paul wrote these epistles under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, meaning that every word carries the authority of God Himself (2 Timothy 3:16). Whether he was writing to the church in Rome to explain the depths of God’s plan for salvation or writing a personal note to Philemon regarding a runaway slave, Paul’s constant aim was to exalt Jesus Christ as Lord and to build up the church in love (Colossians 1:15-20; Ephesians 4:11-16).

The Pauline epistles remain the primary source for understanding how to live as a Christian in a fallen world. They teach us how to worship, how to structure the local church, and how to find peace in the midst of trials. A wonderful way to begin studying these letters is to read the book of Philippians. It is a short, joyful letter that will teach you how to find your ultimate satisfaction in Christ, regardless of your circumstances (Philippians 4:4-7; Philippians 4:13). As you read, I encourage you to pray that the Holy Spirit would open your eyes to see the beauty of the Savior on every page.
How can I be sure I am praying according to the will of God?

To pray according to the will of God is to align your heart’s desires with the truths He has revealed in the Holy Scriptures. It is the humble act of submitting our finite requests to His infinite wisdom, trusting that His purposes are always good, holy, and perfect.

Beloved, the desire to pray rightly is a gracious evidence of the Holy Spirit’s work of sanctification in your life. We can have great confidence when our prayers are rooted in the character of God. The first way to be sure you are praying according to His will is to pray for what He has explicitly commanded or promised in His Word. When you ask for the strength to forgive, for the growth of the local church, or for the wisdom to navigate a trial, you are standing on solid ground. 1 John 5:14-15 tells us, "This is the confidence we have before Him: If we ask anything according to His will, He hears us. And if we know that He hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we already possess what we have asked of Him."

However, we often face situations where God’s specific plan is not immediately clear, such as decisions regarding a career, a move, or the timing of a blessing. In these instances, praying according to His will means asking with a hand that is open to His sovereign providence. We follow the example of our Lord Jesus, who expressed His honest desire in the garden but concluded with, "Yet not My will, but Yours be done" in Luke 22:42. True prayer is not an exercise in bending God’s will to match ours, but in conforming our will to match His.

We must also examine the motives of our hearts. James 4:3 warns us, "When you ask, you do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, that you may spend what you get on your pleasures." To pray in God's will is to pray for His glory and the advancement of His kingdom rather than for selfish gain. You may find comfort in knowing that you are not left to your own strength in this duty, for the Holy Spirit helps us in our weakness and intercedes for the saints according to the will of God as promised in Romans 8:26-27.

As a practical next step to grow in this area, I encourage you to begin "praying the Bible." Choose a passage of Scripture, such as the Lord’s Prayer in Matthew 6:9-13 or the apostle Paul’s prayer in Ephesians 1:15-23, and use the biblical text as the vocabulary for your own petitions. By speaking God's words back to Him, you ensure that your requests remain anchored in His revealed truth. Spend some time in a quiet place today, open your Bible to a Psalm, and turn its verses into your own personal conversation with the Father. What specific promise from Scripture can you turn into a prayer today?
What does the Bible say about busyness / being too busy?

The Bible presents busyness not merely as a matter of a full calendar, but as a condition of the soul that can either be a fruit of diligent stewardship or a thicket of worldly distraction.
