Regardless of the specific theological framework, the Bible makes it clear that we are not machines; we are frail creatures who need regular intervals to cease from labor, gather for corporate worship, and refocus our hearts on our Creator.

This rhythm of rest serves our sanctification, which is the ongoing process of being made more like Christ. When we intentionally stop our work, we declare our trust in God's sovereignty rather than our own productivity. We acknowledge that it is God who sustains the world and provides for our needs, not our constant striving (Psalm 127:2).

On a pastoral level, you might find it helpful to look at your weekly calendar and prayerfully designate a consistent time to set aside your ordinary secular employments. Use this time not merely for physical sleep, but for the "means of grace," such as reading the Scriptures, engaging in private and family prayer, and participating in the life of your local church (Hebrews 10:24-25).

Ultimately, our physical rest points us toward a deeper spiritual reality. We all carry the heavy burden of sin and the exhaustion of trying to prove our worth through our own efforts. Jesus offers a rest that no calendar day can provide, saying, "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This rest is found through the Gospel: the truth that Jesus lived a perfect life, died for our sins, and rose again so that we might be reconciled to God by grace through faith alone. 

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive this eternal rest. What is it that makes it difficult for you to truly rest in His finished work?
How can I be a good Christian?

The journey of a Christian does not begin with our own goodness, but with the goodness of God bestowed upon us through Jesus Christ. To be a "good" Christian is to live a life rooted in the grace of God, being daily transformed into the image of Jesus by the power of the Holy Spirit.

You may wonder if you must achieve a certain level of moral perfection before God accepts you. However, the Bible teaches that none of us are good by God’s perfect standard. Romans 3:23 states that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. Through His substitutionary atonement on the cross and His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus defeated sin and death.

A person becomes a Christian not by works, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. This is known as justification, where God declares a sinner righteous because of the merits of Jesus. Ephesians 2:8-9 says, "For it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this not from yourselves; it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast."

If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?

For those who have trusted in Christ, being a "good" Christian is about the process of sanctification, which is the lifelong work of the Holy Spirit making us more like Jesus. This is not about earning God’s love, but responding to the love He has already given. Here are several essential ways to grow in your faith:

First, abide in the Word of God. Just as our bodies need food, our souls need the Bible. It is our ultimate authority for faith and practice. Jesus said in John 15:5, "I am the vine; you are the branches. If a man remains in Me and I in him, he will bear much fruit; apart from Me you can do nothing."

Second, cultivate a life of prayer. Prayer is your direct line of communication with your Heavenly Father. Speak to Him honestly about your struggles, thank Him for His blessings, and ask for the strength to obey Him. Philippians 4:6-7 encourages us to present our requests to God in everything by prayer and petition.

Third, commit to a local church. Christianity was never meant to be lived in isolation. We need the fellowship, accountability, and teaching found in a body of believers. Hebrews 10:24-25 urges us not to give up meeting together, but to encourage one another.

Fourth, practice repentant obedience. When you fail and sin, do not run away from God. Instead, run to Him. 1 John 1:9 promises that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of Mark in the Bible this week to see the life and ministry of Jesus clearly. Find a Bible-believing local church where the Word of God is preached, and seek out a mature believer or an elder there who can pray with you and help disciple you in these early steps of your walk with Christ.
What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?

The Bible teaches that while civil authorities are established by God to maintain order, Christians should prioritize reconciliation and internal church mediation over secular lawsuits, especially when the dispute is with a fellow believer. Taking a brother or sister in Christ to court before unbelievers is described by the Apostle Paul as a spiritual failure that damages the reputation of the Gospel.

In 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, the Scripture argues that since the saints will one day judge the world and even angels, they should be capable of settling smaller cases within the body of Christ. Paul asks with a heavy heart if there is no one wise enough among the believers to decide between his brothers, as seen in 1 Corinthians 6:5. He suggests that it is actually better to be wronged or cheated than to bring reproach upon the name of Jesus through public legal battles before those who do not share the faith. This reflects the priority of the unity of the Spirit and the pursuit of corporate sanctification over personal vindication.

For disputes involving those outside the church or in matters of criminal law, Scripture recognizes that God has ordained the state to execute justice and "bear the sword" as His servant for our good, according to Romans 13:1-4. However, even in these instances, the believer is called to a heart of peace. In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus teaches a radical posture of grace, saying that if someone wants to sue you and take your tunic, let him have your cloak as well, as noted in Matthew 5:40. This does not mean a Christian can never seek legal protection, but it does mean our primary motive must be the glory of God rather than worldly gain or revenge.

As an actionable next step, if you are currently facing a legal dispute with another Christian, I encourage you to pause and request a meeting with your local church elders. They are called to provide shepherdly oversight and can help facilitate a biblical mediation process that seeks a God honoring resolution without the public shame of a secular courtroom. Direct your heart to God in prayer, asking Him to give you a spirit of humility and to show you if there is any way you can demonstrate the sacrificial love of Christ in this situation.
What is kingdom now teaching?

Kingdom Now theology is a belief system, primarily found within certain charismatic and restorationist movements, which teaches that God lost control of the world to Satan at the Fall and that the Church’s primary mission is to reclaim this dominion through social, political, and spiritual influence. Proponents of this view believe that Jesus Christ cannot or will not return to the earth until the Church has successfully established His Kingdom across all spheres of human society, often referred to as the seven mountains of culture.

This teaching relies heavily on a specific interpretation of Dominion Theology, suggesting that believers must take back the earth for God. While it is true that Christians are called to be salt and light (Matthew 5:13-16), Kingdom Now teaching often minimizes the necessity of Christ’s personal, bodily return to sovereignly set all things right. It tends to place the burden of establishing the Kingdom upon human effort and organizational success rather than upon the sovereign power of God and the return of the King.

Scripture teaches that while the Kingdom of God has arrived spiritually in the hearts of those who believe (Luke 17:21), its full, physical manifestation awaits the return of the Lord Jesus Christ. Jesus told Pilate, "My kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36). We do not usher in the Kingdom by our own strength or political power; rather, we faithfully witness to the Gospel while we wait for the day when Jesus returns to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new (Revelation 21:1-5).

It is deeply understandable to long for justice and the end of suffering in our broken world. However, the Bible reminds us that the Father has set the times and seasons by His own authority (Acts 1:7). Our hope is not found in a perfected society created by human hands, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ. True transformation begins not with institutional control, but with the regeneration of the human heart through the Holy Spirit.

The central message of the Kingdom is the Gospel itself. We are all born in sin and separated from our holy Creator, but God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our works or our ability to reform the world.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. As you look toward the future, I encourage you to fix your eyes on the promise of His return, resting in His sovereignty rather than the weight of trying to save the world yourself. What thoughts or concerns do you have about how the Church should engage with the world today?
Does God have favorites?

God does not show partiality or favoritism based on worldly status, merit, or outward appearance, for He judges according to truth (Romans 2:11; Acts 10:34). Yet, in His sovereign grace, He does choose to set a special, covenantal love upon His people, not because of anything in them, but according to the good pleasure of His will (Ephesians 1:4-6).

The theological term for this is sovereign election. While God provides common grace to all, such as the sun and the rain, He provides saving grace specifically to those He has adopted as His children (Matthew 5:45; Romans 8:15-17). This is not favoritism in the human sense, which is often biased or unfair, but is the manifestation of His mercy. If God were only "fair," we would all receive judgment, for "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). His special love for His children is an act of pure, unmerited favor.

Beloved, if you feel that you are not enough for God, or that you are overlooked by the world, remember that God does not look at the things man looks at. Man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart (1 Samuel 16:7). His love is not a reward for the worthy, but a rescue for the broken.

You may wonder how one enters into this special relationship with God. The Bible teaches that because of our sin, we are naturally separated from our holy Creator. However, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or status.

One practical step you can take today is to read the first chapter of the book of Ephesians and meditate on what it means to be chosen and adopted by God. I encourage you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive His forgiveness and the peace of being His child. What holds you back from trusting in His grace today?
How can I live a holy life?

To live a holy life is to be set apart by God for His specific purposes and to grow in conformity to the character of Jesus Christ through the power of the Holy Spirit. This process is called progressive sanctification, and it is the necessary result of being justified, which is when God declares a sinner righteous based solely on the imputed righteousness of Christ received through faith.

You must first understand that holiness is not a prerequisite to come to God, but a fruit of belonging to Him. We cannot produce holiness in our own strength any more than a dead man can give himself life. True holiness begins with the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, and His wrath against sin is real (Isaiah 6:3; Romans 1:18). Because we are all sinners by nature and choice, we are separated from Him. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life of obedience we could never live and died a sacrificial, substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death. Salvation is a gift of grace, not earned by our works, but received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Once you have trusted in Christ, living a holy life involves several vital elements:

First, you must saturate your mind with the Word of God. Scripture is the primary tool the Holy Spirit uses to transform our hearts and renew our minds (John 17:17; Romans 12:2). As you read, the Bible acts as a mirror, showing you where you fall short and pointing you to the beauty of Christ (James 1:23-25).

Second, you must practice the mortification of sin. This means actively identifying and "putting to death" the sinful desires and habits of your old nature by the power of the Spirit (Romans 8:13; Colossians 3:5). Holiness is not just the absence of bad behavior, but the presence of Christlike love, joy, and purity.

Third, you must lean upon the means of grace, which include regular prayer and fellowship within a local church. We were never meant to walk this path alone. God uses the encouragement, accountability, and preaching found in the local body of believers to strengthen us (Hebrews 10:24-25).

A practical next step for you is to establish a daily rhythm of reading one chapter of the New Testament, perhaps starting in the Gospel of John, and spending time in prayer asking God to give you a greater hatred for sin and a deeper love for Jesus.

What is the primary motivation in your heart as you seek to live a holy life?
What is the sinner's prayer?

The sinner's prayer is an appeal to God for mercy, where an individual acknowledges their sinfulness and expresses trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ for their salvation. While there is no single mandated script in the Bible, it represents the outward expression of an inward heart change toward repentance and faith.

In the Scriptures, we find the spirit of this prayer in the tax collector who humbled himself, saying, "God, have mercy on me, a sinner" (Luke 18:13). It is essential to recognize that the power to save does not reside in the repetition of a formula, but in the person and work of Jesus Christ. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith, not a result of human works or the performance of a ritual (Ephesians 2:8-9). When a person truly believes, God grants them justification, which is His legal declaration that the sinner is now righteous in His sight because the perfect righteousness of Christ has been credited to them (Romans 5:1).

The Gospel is the glorious truth that God, who is holy and just, sent His Son to be the Savior of the world. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the righteous judgment of God against our sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all creation.

You may wonder if your past prevents you from coming to Him, but the heart of the Gospel is that Christ came to save sinners. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Scripture promises that if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved (Romans 10:9).

I encourage you to begin speaking honestly with God in your own words, confessing your need for Him and asking Him to help you follow Him. As a practical next step, please consider reading the Gospel of John to learn more about who Jesus is, and reach out to a local, Bible-teaching church where you can find support and fellowship. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?

A Christian avoids being a doormat by recognizing that biblical humility is not a lack of dignity, but rather a proper submission to the lordship of Jesus Christ. True meekness is power under control, not the absence of a backbone.

To understand this correctly, we must begin with the doctrine of the Imago Dei, which teaches that every human being is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27). Because you bear God's image, you have an inherent dignity that you are called to steward. Being a doormat often stems from a fear of man, where the desire for human approval outweighs the desire to please God (Proverbs 29:25). When we allow others to mistreat or exploit us without cause, we are often failing in our stewardship of the life, time, and emotional health that God has entrusted to us.

Consider the example of the Lord Jesus. He was the perfect servant, yet He was never a doormat. He spoke the truth with boldness, confronted the religious hypocrites (Matthew 23:13-33), and even physically cleared the temple when God’s honor was at stake (John 2:13-17). He laid down His life voluntarily; it was not taken from Him against His will (John 10:18). Similarly, the Apostle Paul asserted his legal rights as a Roman citizen when it served the cause of the Gospel and his own protection (Acts 22:25-29).

Biblical love does not mean enabling the sin of another person. If you allow someone to continue in selfishness, manipulation, or abuse without setting a boundary, you are not actually loving them in a Christlike way. True love seeks the ultimate good of the other person, which often involves a gentle but firm rebuke or the refusal to participate in their sin (Galatians 6:1; Ephesians 5:11).

In your daily life, this involves the discipline of progressive sanctification, where the Holy Spirit clarifies your judgment. You are called to be a servant of all, but a slave to none except Christ (1 Corinthians 7:22-23). 

A practical next step is to evaluate your current relationships through the lens of stewardship. Ask yourself: "Does saying 'yes' to this person's demand prevent me from saying 'yes' to God’s priorities for my life?" Practice letting your "Yes" be "Yes" and your "No" be "No," without the need for excessive apologies or manipulation (Matthew 5:37). If you find it difficult to set these boundaries, bring this specifically to your local church elders or a mature mentor who can provide accountability and help you discern the difference between sacrificial service and harmful passivity. 

Turn to the Lord in prayer, asking Him to replace the fear of man with a robust fear of God, and trust that His grace is sufficient to help you stand firm in the freedom Christ has given you (Galatians 5:1).
What does it mean that the workers are few (Matthew 9:37)?

In Matthew 9:37, Jesus is highlighting the disparity between the vast number of people who need the Gospel and the small number of faithful believers actively sharing it and shepherding souls. He looks upon the crowds with deep compassion, seeing them as people who are harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd (Matthew 9:36).

This statement serves as a call to recognize the spiritual urgency of the world around us. The harvest represents those who are ready to hear the message of salvation, while the workers are the disciples who proclaim the Word and care for the flock. By saying the workers are few, Jesus emphasizes that the mission is far greater than the current number of people engaged in it. It is a reminder that every believer is called to have a heart for the lost and to participate in the mission of God through prayer, support, and active witness.

You may wonder why an all powerful God would require human workers to accomplish His purposes. A sincere concern here is whether God’s plan depends on our fragile efforts. However, the Bible teaches that while God is the sovereign Lord of the harvest who alone grants life, He has chosen to use the proclamation of the Gospel as the means by which He calls His people to Himself (Romans 10:14; Romans 10:15). This is the beauty of His grace, that He invites us to be instruments in His hands.

The message we are called to carry is the good news of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. In His mercy, Jesus Christ took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God’s justice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, defeating death and securing eternal life for all who believe (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to identify one person in your life who does not know Christ and commit to praying for them daily this week. Ask the Lord of the harvest to open a door for a Gospel conversation and for the courage to be a faithful worker in His field.
What was the purpose of the Levitical Law?

The Levitical Law was established by God to reveal His absolute holiness, to set the nation of Israel apart from the surrounding pagan cultures, and to provide a temporary system of worship that pointed toward the coming Savior. It served as a mirror to show humanity its sinfulness and a shadow of the perfect sacrifice that Jesus Christ would eventually provide.

First, the Law revealed the character of God. In Leviticus 11:44, the Lord says, "For I am the LORD your God; consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy." Because God is perfectly righteous and just, His people were called to a standard of living that reflected His nature. This included moral laws, civil regulations, and ceremonial requirements that touched every aspect of daily life.

Second, the Law functioned to expose human sin and the inability of man to save himself through works. Romans 3:20 teaches that "through the law comes the knowledge of sin." By providing a standard that no fallen human could perfectly keep, the Law demonstrated that righteousness must come from God as a gift rather than being earned through human effort. Galatians 3:24 explains that the Law was our "tutor" or "guardian" to lead us to Christ, so that we might be justified by faith.

Third, the sacrificial system within the Levitical Law established the necessity of substitutionary atonement. Leviticus 17:11 states, "For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you to make atonement for your souls upon the altar; for it is the blood that makes atonement for the soul." These animal sacrifices could never fully take away sins, but they were a "shadow of the good things to come" (Hebrews 10:1), looking forward to Jesus Christ, the Lamb of God, who would take away the sin of the world (John 1:29).

You may wonder why God would give so many complex rules if they were never intended to be the permanent way to salvation. The beauty of the Gospel is that what the Law could not do because of our weakened human nature, God did by sending His own Son. Jesus lived the perfect life of obedience that the Levitical Law demanded, and then He died on the cross to pay the penalty for our law-breaking. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Because of Christ's work, we are no longer under the tutor of the ceremonial law, but we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step in your study, I encourage you to read the book of Hebrews in the New Testament. It serves as a divinely inspired commentary on how Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of every aspect of the Levitical system, from the priesthood to the sacrifices. What holds you back from trusting in the One who fulfilled the Law perfectly on your behalf?
What does the Bible say about confession of sin to a priest?

The Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is the sole High Priest and the only Mediator between God and man, providing every believer with direct access to the Father. While the Old Testament established a levitical priesthood to offer sacrifices and intercede for the people, the New Testament declares that Christ has fulfilled these roles perfectly and permanently through His own blood.

Scripture emphasizes that when we sin, our primary confession must be directed to God Himself. We are promised that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness, as stated in 1 John 1:9. This direct access is possible because Jesus, our Great High Priest, has passed through the heavens and now sympathizes with our weaknesses, inviting us to draw near to the throne of grace with confidence to find mercy, as described in Hebrews 4:14-16.

The New Testament does not establish a special class of human priests to mediate forgiveness. Instead, it describes all believers as a royal priesthood in 1 Peter 2:9. However, the Bible does encourage believers to confess their sins to one another for the purpose of mutual accountability, prayer, and healing. James 5:16 instructs us to confess our sins to each other and pray for each other so that we may be healed. This practice within the local church is not for the sake of obtaining judicial forgiveness from God, which Christ has already secured, but for the sake of relational restoration and spiritual growth.

You may wonder how a person can be truly certain their sins are forgiven without a formal declaration from a human authority. This certainty rests entirely on the finished work of Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, and because we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are separated from Him. Yet, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or human rituals.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive full and free forgiveness. What is it that might be causing you to hesitate in resting fully in Christ’s completed work for your salvation?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time this week reading the Book of Hebrews, specifically chapters 7 through 10, which beautifully explain how Christ is superior to any earthly priest. Additionally, seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can find a mature brother or sister in Christ to walk with you in biblical accountability and fellowship.
Should Christians go to nightclubs? Is clubbing a sin?

While the Bible does not mention nightclubs by name, it provides clear and authoritative principles regarding the environments we choose and the company we keep. For the believer, the primary question is not merely "Is this a sin?" but "Does this honor Christ and promote my progressive sanctification?"

The modern nightclub is typically an environment defined by what the Apostle John calls worldliness, characterized by the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life (1 John 2:15-16). Scripture commands us to walk properly as in the daytime, not in carousing and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and sensuality (Romans 13:13). Because nightclubs are frequently centered around the consumption of alcohol to the point of intoxication and the promotion of immodesty, they often serve as "unfruitful works of darkness" that Christians are told to expose rather than participate in (Ephesians 5:11).

Furthermore, as a believer, your body is the temple of the Holy Spirit, and you have been bought with a price (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). This reality calls for a lifestyle of stewardship where we avoid placing ourselves in situations that provoke temptation or hinder our witness to a watching world. If an activity or environment dims your desire for God or leads others to stumble, it is not a path of wisdom or love (Romans 14:13).

You may wonder if you can go to such places simply to be a "light" to others. While we are called to be in the world, we are not to be of it (John 17:14-16). There is a significant difference between meeting a lost friend for coffee to share the Gospel and joining them in an atmosphere designed to celebrate the very sins Christ died to forgive.

The central truth of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ, the holy Son of God, took on human nature and lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. He suffered the wrath of God on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, triumphed over death and sin. He did this to reconcile us to God and to purify for Himself a people who are zealous for good works (Titus 2:14). Salvation is a gift of grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and a new heart with new desires.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to sit down with a pastor or a mature believer in your local church to discuss how you can find joy and fellowship in environments that build up your faith rather than tear it down. What is it about the nightclub scene that you find appealing, and how might those desires be met in a way that honors Christ?
Does the statue outside the United Nations have any connection to the beast of the end times?

The "Guardian for International Peace and Security" statue recently displayed at the United Nations has drawn significant attention because its hybrid appearance of a lion and an eagle mirrors descriptions of symbolic "beasts" found in biblical prophecy. Specifically, the features of this statue bear a striking resemblance to the first beast described in Daniel 7:4 and the composite beast mentioned in Revelation 13:2.

In the study of eschatology (the doctrine of last things), these biblical beasts represent worldly kingdoms or systems of power that rise in opposition to the kingdom of God. The Bible describes the end times as a period when humanity will seek global "peace and security" through its own strength and institutions rather than through the Lord. The Apostle Paul warns in 1 Thessalonians 5:3 that "While people are saying, 'Peace and security,' destruction will come upon them suddenly." 

Whether this specific statue is a deliberate reference or a coincidental resemblance to Mexican folk art (an alebrije), it serves as a vivid reminder for the believer to remain vigilant. Christians hold different views on how literally to interpret modern symbols in relation to prophecy. Some see such displays as significant "signs of the times" that point to the gathering of nations against the Lord, while others view them as cultural artifacts that merely illustrate the recurring patterns of human pride described in Scripture. Regardless of one's specific interpretative view, the priority for the believer is to remain anchored in the truth that God is supreme and sovereign over every nation and every ruler.

It is easy to feel a sense of unease or concern when the world seems to be aligning with the warnings of Scripture. However, our hope is not found in deciphering every world event, but in the person of Jesus Christ. I encourage you to focus your study on the book of Revelation, specifically looking for the "Lamb who was slain," for He is the one who has already won the victory (Revelation 5:5; Revelation 5:12).

You may wonder how a person can be ready for the return of Christ or the judgment that the Bible describes. The Gospel is the only source of true peace. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him, leaving us deserving of His just wrath. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your greatest concern when you think about the end of the world? 

As a practical step, I suggest setting aside time this week to read the first five chapters of Revelation and then speaking to God directly in prayer, asking Him to replace any fear you feel with the peace that comes from trusting in His power. Seeking fellowship with a local, Bible teaching church is also a vital way to find encouragement among other believers.
What is the sign of Jonah?

The sign of Jonah is the three days and three nights that the prophet Jonah spent in the belly of a great fish, which Jesus Christ identified as a prophetic type of His own death, burial, and bodily resurrection. It serves as the definitive proof of His divine authority and the sufficiency of His work for the salvation of sinners (Matthew 12:38–40).

In the biblical history, Jonah was swallowed by a great fish after fleeing from God’s command, yet he was miraculously preserved and delivered from the depths on the third day (Jonah 1:17; Jonah 2:10). When the religious leaders of Jesus’ day demanded a miraculous sign to prove He was the Messiah, Jesus pointed them to this event. He declared that just as Jonah was in the belly of the fish, the Son of Man would be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:40). This sign is the cornerstone of the Christian faith, confirming that Jesus is truly God and truly man, and that His victory over the grave is a historical reality.

The theological significance of this sign is found in the doctrine of justification. Because Jesus Christ was raised from the dead, we have the assurance that His sacrificial death was an acceptable and perfect atonement for sin (Romans 4:25). Just as Jonah emerged from the fish to preach a message of repentance to the Ninevites, the risen Christ commands all people everywhere to repent and believe the Gospel, for He has been appointed as the Judge of the living and the dead (Acts 17:30–31).

You may wonder why Jesus would choose such an extraordinary event from the Old Testament to validate His ministry. It is because the resurrection is not merely a religious story, but the pivot point of all human history. God is holy and just, and our sin has separated us from Him, leaving us under His righteous wrath. However, in His mercy, God sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. By His bodily resurrection, Jesus proved that the debt of sin was paid in full and that death has been defeated.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior?

As a practical next step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to read the book of Jonah and then read the account of the resurrection in the Gospel of Matthew 28. As you read, talk to God in prayer, asking Him to give you a heart that trusts in the finished work of Jesus Christ alone. Seek out a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached, so that you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
What does it mean to walk worthy?

To walk worthy means to live in a way that corresponds to the weight, value, and character of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. The word "worthy" in the original language of the New Testament carries the image of a balance scale. On one side of the scale is the glorious reality of your calling in Christ, and on the other side is your daily conduct. To walk worthy is the lifelong pursuit of bringing your behavior into alignment with your high identity as a child of God.

The Apostle Paul provides the foundational command for this in Ephesians 4:1, where he says, "I, therefore, the prisoner in the Lord, urge you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called." This calling is the sovereign work of God in which He has rescued you from the kingdom of darkness and brought you into the kingdom of His beloved Son. Walking worthy is not an attempt to earn God's favor or merit salvation, for we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Instead, it is the grateful response of a redeemed heart. It is the process of progressive sanctification, where the Holy Spirit works within you to make your life look more like the Lord Jesus.

In practical terms, walking worthy involves several specific characteristics described in Scripture:

First, it is marked by a Christlike character. Paul follows his command in Ephesians 4:2 by listing humility, gentleness, and patience, bearing with one another in love. A worthy walk is not defined by outward religious performance but by a heart that reflects the meekness and love of Christ.

Second, it is a life of fruitfulness and growth. In Colossians 1:10, the Bible describes walking worthy of the Lord as "bearing fruit in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God." Your life should show the evidence of the Spirit's work, resulting in tangible good deeds and a deepening intimacy with your Creator.

Third, it is a life of integrity before the world. In 1 Thessalonians 2:12, Paul reminds believers to "walk worthy of God, who calls you into His own kingdom and glory." This means representing the King well in your workplace, your home, and your private thoughts, ensuring that your testimony does not bring reproach upon the name of Christ.

If you find yourself struggling to live up to this high calling, remember that the power to walk worthy does not come from your own willpower, but from the indwelling Holy Spirit. We must constantly return to the grace of the Gospel, for it is God who works in you both to will and to act according to His good purpose (Philippians 2:13).

As a practical next step, I encourage you to choose one specific area of your life this week, such as your speech or your attitude toward a difficult person, and prayerfully ask the Lord to help you align that area with the beauty of the Gospel. If you have never begun this walk by trusting in Christ, I invite you to turn from your sin and believe in Jesus Christ, who died for your sins and rose again, so that you might have new life and the power to live for His glory.

What is one area of your life where you feel it is currently most difficult to walk in a way that reflects the value of the Gospel?
What is a divine appointment?

A divine appointment is an event orchestrated by the sovereign hand of God, where He brings specific people or circumstances together at a precise moment to fulfill His holy purposes. In the life of a believer, these are not mere coincidences or chance encounters, but rather the unfolding of God’s gracious providence.

The Scriptures show us that God is the Master Architect of every encounter. We see this clearly when the Holy Spirit led Philip to a desert road to meet an Ethiopian official who was reading the prophet Isaiah, leading to the man’s salvation in Acts 8:26-39. We also see it when Jesus had to pass through Samaria to meet a broken woman at a well, offering her the living water that satisfies the soul in John 4:4-26. These moments reveal that God is intimately involved in the details of our lives, directing our steps for His glory and our good, as stated in Proverbs 16:9 and Psalm 37:23.

To recognize a divine appointment, we must walk in fellowship with the Lord, remaining sensitive to the promptings of the Holy Spirit. Often, these moments require us to set aside our own schedules to serve another or to share the hope of the Gospel. If you find yourself in an unexpected conversation or a sudden interruption, consider that God may be inviting you to be His instrument in that very moment.

One practical step you can take today is to begin your morning by asking the Lord to open your eyes to the opportunities He has prepared for you. You might pray and ask the Lord to help you see the people He puts in your path as He sees them, and to give you the courage to speak His truth in love.

The greatest divine appointment in history was when God the Son took on human flesh to seek and save the lost. Because of our sin, we were separated from a holy God, but in His great mercy, Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died on the cross for our sins, and rose again on the third day to give us new life and victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and eternal hope. Have you ever considered that your reading of these words today might be a divine appointment intended to draw you closer to Him?
What is the Qur’an?

The Qur’an is the central religious text of Islam, which Muslims believe to be the literal word of God revealed to the prophet Muhammad over a period of about twenty-three years. It consists of 114 chapters, known as surahs, and serves as the primary authority for Islamic theology, law, and daily conduct.

You may wonder how a person can discern the truth among various claims of divine revelation when seeking to know the Creator. It is a deeply human and vital concern to desire a sure word from God to guide your soul and provide hope for eternity.

While the Qur’an is the foundation of the Islamic faith, the Bible teaches that God's ultimate and perfect revelation is found in the person of Jesus Christ. Hebrews 1:1-2 says, "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom also he made the universe." We believe the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, which reveals that Jesus is truly God and truly man. John 1:1; John 1:14. Unlike any other religious figure, Jesus Christ confirmed His authority by His historical and bodily resurrection, proving His victory over sin and death. Romans 1:4; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

This leads us to the heart of the Gospel. The Bible tells us that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and are separated from Him. Romans 3:23. In His great mercy, God sent Jesus to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating that the debt has been paid in full. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious devotion. Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see how Jesus describes Himself as the only way to the Father. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What would have happened “if the Lord had not been on our side” (Psalm 124:2)?

If the Lord had not been on our side, we would have been utterly consumed and destroyed by the overwhelming power of our enemies and the consequences of our own sin. The Psalmist describes this alternate reality as being swallowed alive in a moment of burning anger and being swept away by a raging, uncontrollable torrent (Psalm 124:3–5).

This passage serves as a powerful reminder of God’s **Providence**, the truth that He actively sustains and protects His people through every storm. It teaches us that our survival is never a matter of luck or human merit, but is entirely due to the faithful character of a God who refuses to give us up as prey to the teeth of the wicked (Psalm 124:6). Without His sovereign intervention, the "snare" of death would have remained closed around us forever.

The ultimate fulfillment of this promise is found in Jesus Christ. While we were helpless under the flood of our own transgressions and the righteous judgment of God, Christ stepped into the waters for us. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, securing eternal victory for all who trust in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

If you are feeling overwhelmed by life’s pressures today, I encourage you to set aside ten minutes this evening to write down three specific moments in your past where it is clear that God rescued you when you could not rescue yourself. Use these memories to anchor your heart in the truth that your help comes from the Lord, the Maker of heaven and earth (Psalm 124:8). What is one specific worry you can hand over to His sovereign care right now?
What did Jesus mean when He said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to get into heaven?

Jesus used this striking illustration to show that entering the kingdom of God is humanly impossible for those who trust in their own resources. By comparing the largest animal in the region with the smallest common opening, He highlighted the utter helplessness of man to save himself through wealth, status, or moral effort (Matthew 19:24; Mark 10:25).

This teaching followed His interaction with the rich young ruler, who walked away in sorrow because his heart was tied to his possessions. Wealth often creates a false sense of self-sufficiency, making it difficult for a person to recognize their spiritual bankruptcy before a holy God. Jesus was not saying that no rich person can be saved, but rather that no person can save themselves. When the disciples asked in astonishment who then could be saved, Jesus pointed them to the only hope, stating that while it is impossible for man, all things are possible with God (Matthew 19:26; Luke 18:27).

The "eye of the needle" refers to a literal sewing needle. Jesus was emphasizing that salvation is a miracle of sovereign grace. Because God is holy and we are all separated from Him by our sin, no amount of human riches or effort can bridge the gap. However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of Mark chapter 10 and spend time in prayer, asking the Lord to help you see Christ as your greatest treasure, far above any earthly wealth. Seeking the counsel of a local, Bible-teaching church can also provide a wonderful community to help you grow in this understanding.
How do we decide which books belong in the Bible since the Bible does not say which books belong in the Bible?

You may wonder how we can be certain about the boundaries of the Bible when there is no internal list provided by God to serve as a table of contents. This is a significant question that touches on the very foundation of how we hear our Shepherd’s voice.

The collection of books that make up the Bible, often called the canon, was recognized by the early church through a process of spiritual discernment, focusing on which writings bore the inherent authority of God. Christians believe that the church did not "create" the Bible or "grant" authority to certain books, but rather humbly recognized and received the books that God had already inspired through His Spirit.

For the Old Testament, the early church followed the lead of Jesus Himself, who frequently quoted the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms as the authoritative Word of God in Luke 24:44. For the New Testament, the early believers looked for three primary markers:

1.  Apostolicity: Was the book written by an apostle or a close associate of an apostle?
2.  Orthodoxy: Did the teaching align with the established "rule of faith" and the person and work of Jesus Christ?
3.  Catholicity: Was the book being used and recognized by the church at large as being uniquely powerful for teaching and worship?

This recognition was an act of divine providence, where God ensured that His people would not be left without His sure and steady Word. The Bible describes itself as "breathed out by God," meaning its ultimate source is the Creator Himself, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. Even within the New Testament, we see the apostles beginning to recognize each other's writings as Scripture, such as when Peter refers to the letters of Paul as having the same status as the "other Scriptures" in 2 Peter 3:15-16.

The authority of the Bible does not rest on a human vote, but on the fact that these books are the very breath of God. Just as a child recognizes the voice of their mother without needing a certificate of identification, the sheep of Christ recognize the voice of their Shepherd in these sixty-six books, as Jesus promised in John 10:27.

Because the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, it is our final authority for everything we believe and do. If you find yourself questioning its origins, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John. As you read, ask the Lord to show you the truth of His Word and the glory of His Son, Jesus Christ. 

Trusting in the Bible is ultimately an act of trusting in the God who wrote it and promised to preserve it for His children. You can turn to Him today in prayer, asking Him to give you a heart that loves His Word and eyes that see the beauty of the Savior revealed within its pages. For "the word of the Lord remains forever" in 1 Peter 1:25.
What does it mean to walk by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16)?

Walking by the Spirit is the habitual, active reliance on the Holy Spirit to govern your conduct, desires, and decisions, resulting in a life that resists the sinful nature and produces the fruit of righteousness. It is the practical outworking of progressive sanctification, where the believer lives in a state of continuous dependence on God's power rather than their own moral effort.

The Apostle Paul presents this as both a command and a promise in Galatians 5:16: "So I say, walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh." To walk implies a steady, daily progress—a step by step journey rather than a single, finished experience. This process involves the mortification of sin, which is the intentional putting to death of sinful habits through the power of the Spirit, and the vivification of the soul, which is being made increasingly alive to the things of God. 

This spiritual walk is not a path of legalistic rule following, but a relationship of yielding your will to the Spirit’s leading as He speaks through His Word. Because the flesh and the Spirit are in constant conflict, walking by the Spirit requires a vigilant heart. Paul describes this harmony as "keeping step" with the Spirit in Galatians 5:25. It is like a soldier following the cadence of a leader or a dancer following the rhythm of the music. As you depend on Him, He produces in you a character that you could never manufacture on your own: "But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control" (Galatians 5:22–23).

A practical next step in this walk is to saturate your mind with Scripture each morning. Since the Holy Spirit is the author of the Bible, He leads us primarily through the truths found in its pages. Before you begin your day, ask God in prayer to make you sensitive to the Spirit's convictions and to grant you the specific strength to choose obedience in the moments of temptation you will face today.

If you find yourself struggling to find the power to change, remember that this new life begins with a new birth. We cannot walk with God until we have been reconciled to Him through His Son. Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved for our sins, rising bodily on the third day to defeat death forever. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the indwelling Spirit who empowers this walk. What is it that makes walking by your own strength feel so heavy today?
What does the Bible mean when it says "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'"?

When the Scripture declares that "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God,'" it is not making a statement about a person's intellectual capacity or IQ, but rather about their moral and spiritual condition. In the biblical context, specifically in the Hebrew language, the word for fool is nabal, which describes someone who is morally deficient, callous, and dismissive of God's righteous standards (Psalm 14:1; Psalm 53:1).

This rejection of God happens "in the heart," which the Bible identifies as the seat of the will, the emotions, and the core of a person's being. This is a deliberate choice to suppress the truth that God has made evident to all people through creation and conscience (Romans 1:18-20). It is not a lack of evidence that leads to this conclusion, but a desire for autonomy. By denying the existence of a Sovereign Judge, a person seeks to free themselves from accountability for their actions. The text immediately links this inward denial to outward behavior, noting that those who live this way are corrupt and do "vile deeds" (Psalm 14:1).

This condition of moral rebellion is not unique to a few, for the Bible teaches that apart from God's grace, we all naturally wander away from Him. Romans 3:10-12 reminds us that "there is no one righteous, not even one," and that we have all turned aside from the path of wisdom.

You may wonder how a loving God could use such a strong term for someone who has honest doubts or questions. It is helpful to see this not as a name-calling exercise, but as a shepherd's warning. Just as a father warns a child that it is foolish to run into a busy street, God warns us that it is spiritually ruinous to live as if our Creator does not exist.

The beauty of the Gospel is that God did not leave us in our foolishness and rebellion. God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him, but He is also rich in mercy. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a life of perfect wisdom on our behalf. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all, and He offers reconciliation to everyone who believes.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the book of Romans this week and ask God to speak to your heart as you consider the evidence of His handiwork in the world around you.

What is it that makes the idea of a Sovereign Creator difficult for you to embrace at this time?
What is onanism? Is onanism a sin?

Onanism is a term derived from the account of Onan in the book of Genesis, historically referring to the practice of withdrawing during intercourse to prevent conception (Genesis 38:9). In modern usage, however, the term is frequently employed as a synonym for masturbation.

To understand if this is a sin, we must look at both the historical context of Onan and the broader biblical principles regarding sexual purity. The sin of Onan was not merely the physical act he performed, but his rebellious heart and his refusal to fulfill his legal and familial duty to provide an heir for his deceased brother. He sought the pleasure of the act while selfishly avoiding the responsibility and the covenantal purpose of the union. For this act of greed and disobedience, the Lord took his life (Genesis 38:10).

Regarding masturbation, while the Bible does not explicitly use that word, it provides clear boundaries for sexual behavior. God designed sex as a gift to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman (Genesis 2:24; Hebrews 13:4). Anything that seeks sexual gratification outside of that union deviates from His holy design. Furthermore, Jesus taught that the root of sexual sin is found in the heart, stating that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart (Matthew 5:28). 

Because masturbation almost always involves lustful thoughts and treats the body as an instrument for self-gratification rather than a temple of the Holy Spirit, it is a sin. We are commanded to flee from sexual immorality and to glorify God with our bodies, which were bought at a high price (1 Corinthians 6:18-20). The struggle with such desires is a matter of the "flesh," and believers are called to walk by the Spirit so they will not gratify the desires of the flesh (Galatians 5:16).

If you find yourself struggling with guilt or a lack of self-control in this area, know that God is a merciful Father who offers restoration. True hope is not found in "trying harder," but in the power of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfectly pure life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers us forgiveness and the power for sanctification, which is the process of being made more like Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from fully trusting Him with your life and your purity today?

As an actionable step toward walking in purity, I encourage you to establish a rhythm of daily Scripture reading and find a trusted, mature believer in your local church to whom you can be honest and accountable (James 5:16). Bringing these struggles into the light is a vital step toward healing and victory.
Is Israel and the church the same thing? Does God still have a plan for Israel?

The relationship between Israel and the church is a profound theme in the Bible, revealing how God unfolds His redemptive purposes throughout history. While they are distinct in their historical origin and specific roles, they are united in God's ultimate plan to gather a people for His own glory through Jesus Christ.

To answer directly, the Bible does not present Israel and the church as identical, yet they are not entirely separate entities either. The church consists of both Jews and Gentiles who have been reconciled to God through faith in Christ. The Apostle Paul describes this beautifully in Romans 11:17-24, where he uses the illustration of an olive tree. National Israel is the natural branch, and the church, including Gentile believers, is grafted into the same nourishing root of God's promises.

There are two primary ways faithful Christians have understood this relationship. One view, often called Covenantalism, emphasizes the continuity of the people of God, seeing the church as the fulfillment and expansion of Israel, where the promises made to Abraham are realized in Christ (Galatians 3:29). Another view, known as Dispensationalism, maintains a clearer distinction between the two, suggesting that God has a specific, earthly program for national Israel and a distinct, spiritual program for the church.

God most certainly still has a plan for Israel. The Scriptures make it clear that God has not rejected His people whom He foreknew (Romans 11:1-2). Paul teaches that a partial hardening has come upon Israel until the full number of the Gentiles has come in, and that "all Israel will be saved" (Romans 11:25-26). This does not mean every individual ethnic Jew is automatically saved, but rather that God intends a future, large-scale turning of the Jewish people to their Messiah, Jesus Christ.

The core of God's plan for both Israel and the church is the Gospel. Because of our sin, every human being is separated from a holy and just God. In His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who was born of a virgin, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What is your current understanding of how someone is made right with God?

As a practical next step in your study, I encourage you to read through Romans 9, 10, and 11. These chapters provide the most detailed biblical explanation of God's sovereign choice, the current state of Israel, and His faithful plan for their future. As you read, pray that the Holy Spirit would grant you wisdom to see the depths of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God (Romans 11:33).
Are Catholic beliefs and practices biblical?

To understand if Roman Catholic beliefs and practices are biblical, we must look to the Holy Scriptures as our final and perfect anchor. It is helpful to recognize that Roman Catholicism and historic Protestantism share deep common ground on several core doctrines of the faith. Both affirm the Holy Trinity, the deity of Jesus Christ, His virgin birth, His bodily resurrection, and His future return to judge the living and the dead. These are essential truths found in Matthew 28:19, John 1:1, and 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

However, when we compare specific Catholic dogmas to the plain teaching of the Bible, significant departures appear, particularly regarding how God reveals truth and how a person is made right with Him.

First, there is the question of authority. The Bible teaches that the Scriptures are the unique, breathed out Word of God and are sufficient for faith and life in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. Roman Catholicism, however, places Sacred Tradition and the teaching authority of the Church (the Magisterium) on an equal level with Scripture. Jesus warned against Elevating human tradition to the level of divine command in Mark 7:7-9, noting that it can lead us to set aside the word of God.

Second, we must consider the central question of justification. The Bible teaches that a person is justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Romans 3:28 states that a man is justified by faith apart from the works of the law, and Ephesians 2:8-9 reminds us that salvation is a gift of God, not of works, so that no one can boast. In contrast, Catholic teaching view justification as a long process that involves the merit of good works and the reception of sacraments to maintain a state of grace. To add human effort to the finished work of Christ is to obscure the glory of His cross.

Third, the Bible presents Jesus Christ as the only mediator between God and man in 1 Timothy 2:5. While Catholic practice often encourages seeking the intercession of Mary or the saints, the Scriptures invite us to draw near to the throne of grace directly through our Great High Priest, Jesus, who always lives to make intercession for us in Hebrews 4:14-16 and Hebrews 7:25.

You may wonder if these differences truly matter for your daily walk with God or your eternal security. They matter because the purity of the Gospel is at stake. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory in Romans 3:23. The good news is that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose again to conquer death. Salvation is not a ladder we climb through rituals, but a rescue we receive by trusting in the Savior.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Book of Romans and the Book of Galatians. Compare what you read there about faith and grace with any human religious system. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from resting entirely in the finished work of Christ today?
What should we learn from the tribe of Simeon?

The tribe of Simeon serves as a historical testimony to the sobering reality of divine discipline and the long term consequences of unchecked anger and impulsive sin. From their origins as the second son of Jacob and Leah to their eventual absorption into the tribe of Judah, their history illustrates that while God is merciful, He also upholds the integrity of His word regarding the fruit of our actions.

The most prominent lesson from Simeon is the danger of a heart ruled by "fierce anger" and "cruel wrath." Along with his brother Levi, Simeon responded to the mistreatment of his sister Dinah with a deceptive and bloody slaughter of the men of Shechem in Genesis 34:25-26. Decades later, on his deathbed, Jacob prophesied that because of this violence, the descendants of Simeon would be "divided in Jacob and scattered in Israel" in Genesis 49:5-7. Unlike the tribe of Levi, who later redeemed their status through their zeal for the Lord at Mount Sinai, the tribe of Simeon appears to have continued in a pattern of spiritual decline.

This decline is most visible during the wilderness wanderings. In the first census of Israel, Simeon was a large tribe of 59,300 men, but by the second census, their numbers had plummeted to only 22,200 in Numbers 1:23 and Numbers 26:14. This massive loss is widely understood to be connected to the plague at Baal-Peor, where a Simeonite leader named Zimri openly defied God by bringing a Midianite woman into the camp in Numbers 25:14. Consequently, when the land of Canaan was divided, Simeon did not receive its own distinct territory but was granted an inheritance within the borders of Judah in Joshua 19:1.

Theologically, we see here the principle of "consequential discipline." While Simeon remained part of the covenant people, their influence and strength were diminished because they failed to repent of the violence and idolatry that characterized their early history. It reminds us that our choices matter and that God takes the holiness of His people seriously.

For the believer today, the history of Simeon is a call to examine our own hearts for "roots of bitterness" or "unbridled passions" that might hinder our walk with Christ. Yet, we must also see the mercy of God in this story. Despite their failings, the Simeonites were not cast out of the nation; they were tucked into the safety of Judah, the royal tribe from which the Messiah would eventually come.

If you find yourself struggling with a cycle of anger or the weight of past failures, remember that Jesus Christ came to break the power of sin and to offer us a new inheritance. He took the "scattering" and the curse we deserved upon Himself so that we could be gathered into His family. I encourage you to bring these struggles to the Lord in prayer today, specifically asking Him to replace a spirit of anger with the "gentleness and self control" produced by the Holy Spirit in Galatians 5:22-23. Consider finding a trusted brother or sister in your local church to walk with you in accountability as you seek to honor God with your temperament and your choices.
What is the hypostatic union?

The hypostatic union is the joining of two distinct natures, divine and human, in the one person of Jesus Christ. This means that Jesus is fully God and fully man, possessing two natures that are united without being mixed, changed, or divided.

This doctrine is an essential foundation of the Christian faith because our salvation depends entirely upon who Jesus is. To be our Mediator, Jesus had to be man so that He could represent us and die in our place, yet He had to be God so that His sacrifice would have infinite value and the power to overcome death. John 1:1 states that "the Word was God," and John 1:14 explains that "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us." He is truly God and truly man, a mystery that reveals the depths of God's love for His people.

In His humanity, Jesus experienced hunger, weariness, and the weight of human sorrow. In His divinity, He performed miracles, forgave sins, and rose from the dead by His own power. This union is not a temporary arrangement, for Jesus remains the God-man forever, interceding for His people before the Father as seen in 1 Timothy 2:5.

You may wonder why it was necessary for God to become a man to save us. The answer lies in God's perfect holiness and our deep need. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His just judgment. However, out of great love, the Father sent the Son to take on our likeness. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, offering Himself as a penal substitution for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His total victory over sin and the grave. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins. What holds you back from trusting Him?

Because Jesus is fully man, He is a sympathetic High Priest who understands your every struggle, according to Hebrews 4:15. Because He is fully God, He is an almighty Savior who is able to sustain you through every trial. As a practical next step, take your specific burdens to Him in prayer today, specifically acknowledging that He understands your human frailty because He has walked this earth and suffered in your place.
Doubting God — how can I overcome doubt in my relationship with God?

It is a common experience for a believer to walk through seasons where the fog of doubt seems to obscure the face of God, yet Scripture assures us that our security rests in His grip on us rather than our grip on Him. Doubt is not necessarily the absence of faith, but often a struggle within faith as we navigate the gap between what we believe and what we feel or experience.

You may wonder if your questions and uncertainties mean that you have lost your way or that God has turned His back on you. Please know that even the most stalwarts of the faith have cried out in times of spiritual darkness. To overcome doubt, we must move our focus from our shifting emotions to the unchangeable character of God revealed in His Word.

**Look to the Object of Your Faith**
Assurance does not come by looking inward at the strength of your own belief, but by looking outward at the perfection of Jesus Christ. Our justification before God is based entirely on the finished work of Christ—His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection which proved His victory over death. Romans 5:1 states, "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." When you doubt, remind your soul that Christ’s performance, not yours, is the ground of your acceptance.

**Utilize the Means of Grace**
God has provided specific tools, often called the means of grace, to strengthen a flickering faith.
1. **The Word of God:** Faith is nourished by hearing the promises of Scripture. Romans 10:17 teaches, "Consequently, faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word about Christ."
2. **Prayer:** Bring your doubts directly to God. The father of the child in the Gospel of Mark offered a beautiful and honest prayer: "I do believe; help me overcome my unbelief!" in Mark 9:24.
3. **Fellowship:** We were never meant to walk alone. The local church provides elders and mature believers who can carry you when your own strength fails. Galatians 6:2 encourages us to "Carry each other’s burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ."

**Actionable Step**
I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading one of the Psalms, such as Psalm 13 or Psalm 23, and then speaking those truths back to God in prayer. Tell Him exactly where you feel weak, and ask Him to grant you the gift of assurance through the power of the Holy Spirit.

If you find that your doubt stems from a lack of certainty about your standing before God, remember that salvation is a free gift of grace received through faith alone. The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ took the judgment we deserved so that we could receive the mercy He earned. You can turn to Him today, repent of any known sin, and trust in His sacrifice and resurrection as your only hope for forgiveness and eternal life. 1 John 5:13 says, "I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know that you have eternal life."

What specifically has been triggering these doubts in your heart lately?
Did God create other people in addition to Adam and Eve?

The Bible teaches that God created Adam and Eve as the first and only original human beings from whom the entire human race is descended. Scripture identifies Adam as the first man in 1 Corinthians 15:45, and Genesis 3:20 explicitly calls Eve the mother of all living.

The historical account in Genesis describes a unique act of creation where God formed Adam from the dust of the ground and subsequently fashioned Eve from Adam's side (Genesis 2:7; Genesis 2:21-22). This establishes a fundamental biological and spiritual unity for all people. The Apostle Paul confirms this in the Book of Acts, declaring that God made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth (Acts 17:26).

It is a natural and sincere curiosity to wonder how the population grew if there were only two original parents. The answer is found in the longevity of the early patriarchs. Adam lived for 930 years and had many other sons and daughters who are not named individually in the text (Genesis 5:4). In those earliest generations, the marriage of close relatives was a necessity for the expansion of the race and was not yet prohibited by the laws God later gave to Moses.

This doctrine of our common ancestry is not merely a matter of history, it is central to the Gospel through the concept of federal headship. Just as we are all physically descended from Adam, we are all spiritually represented by him. When Adam disobeyed God, sin entered the world, and death passed to all of us because we are his descendants (Romans 5:12).

You may wonder how a person can escape this heritage of sin and death. Because God is holy and just, He cannot overlook our rebellion, yet in His great love, He provided a way of rescue. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, became a man to serve as the last Adam. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is not earned by our efforts but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in your journey, I encourage you to read the first three chapters of the Book of Genesis. Ask God to show you the beauty of His design and the depth of His grace from the very beginning. 

What holds you back from trusting in the provision God has made through Jesus Christ?
What does the Bible say about self-discipline?

The Bible presents self-discipline, often referred to as self-control, as a vital fruit of the Holy Spirit and a necessary component of the Christian life. It is the God-given ability to govern one's desires, emotions, and actions in obedience to Christ rather than being a slave to impulsive whims or sinful passions.

Scripture teaches that self-discipline is not merely a matter of human willpower but is a gift from God. In 2 Timothy 1:7, we are told that "God has not given us a spirit of cowardice, but of power, and of love, and of self-control." Without this restraint, a person is described as being dangerously vulnerable, like "a city broken into and left without walls" in Proverbs 25:28.

This discipline is central to the process of sanctification, which is the lifelong work of the Holy Spirit in a believer to make them more like Jesus. The Apostle Paul illustrates this by comparing the Christian life to an athlete training for a race, noting that "everyone who competes in the games exercises self-control in all things" so that they might receive a crown that lasts forever in 1 Corinthians 9:24–27. This training is not fueled by a desire to earn God's favor, but is a response to His grace. As Titus 2:11–12 explains, "For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all men. It instructs us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live sensible, upright, and godly lives in the present age."

True self-discipline is always listed as a "fruit of the Spirit" in Galatians 5:22–23, meaning it grows as we walk in fellowship with God. It is the outward evidence of an inward transformation.

If you find yourself struggling to maintain discipline in your walk with Christ, remember that the goal is not perfection through your own strength, but faithfulness through His. I encourage you to choose one specific area of your daily routine—perhaps your time in prayer or your use of speech—and ask the Holy Spirit each morning to grant you the specific strength to honor the Lord in that area. Discipline is a muscle that the Spirit strengthens through consistent, humble practice.
