You may wonder if your failures in self-control disqualify you from God's love. Rest assured that while our discipline often wavers, God’s faithfulness does not. If you have never done so, I invite you to turn from your own efforts to save yourself, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone. He lived the perfectly disciplined life we could not and died to pay the penalty for our failures, rising again to give us new life and the power of His Spirit. What is the biggest hurdle you face right now in seeking to live a more disciplined life?
What is original sin?

Original sin is the historical and spiritual reality that all humanity inherits a corrupted nature and legal guilt due to Adam's first act of rebellion against God. Because of this fallen state, every person is born spiritually dead and naturally inclined toward sin rather than toward their Creator.

The Bible teaches that Adam acted as the representative, or federal head, of the entire human race. When he fell, the entire human family fell with him. As a result, sin is not merely something we do, it is a condition of who we are from the moment of conception. Romans 5:12 explains, "Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, so also death was passed on to all men, because all sinned." This inherited corruption is often called total depravity, meaning that every part of the human person, the mind, the will, and the emotions, has been affected by sin (Ephesians 2:3; Psalm 51:5).

You may wonder how it could be just for us to be held responsible for a sin committed at the beginning of time. While we inherit Adam's nature, we also daily confirm that rebellion through our own thoughts, words, and deeds. Our need for a Savior is absolute because we are incapable of changing our own hearts or making ourselves acceptable to a holy and just God.

This "bad news" of original sin is the necessary foundation for the "good news" of the Gospel. Just as we were made sinners through the disobedience of the first Adam, we are made righteous through the obedience of the "Last Adam," Jesus Christ (Romans 5:18; Romans 5:19). Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers reconciliation to all who believe.

Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in understanding this truth, I encourage you to read through the book of Romans, specifically chapters one through five. As you read, ask God to show you both the depth of your need and the even greater depth of His mercy in Christ. Seek out a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached, so you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
Are intrusive thoughts sin? Are spontaneous violent, sexual, or blasphemous thoughts sin?

Intrusive thoughts are not in themselves sin when they are involuntary, unbidden, and rejected by your heart. These spontaneous flashes of violence, immorality, or blasphemy are often a source of great distress to the believer, but because they occur without your consent and are contrary to your actual desires, they are a form of temptation or mental affliction rather than a transgression of God's law.

Scripture teaches that even our Lord Jesus Christ was tempted in every way as we are, yet He remained without sin (Hebrews 4:15). If the mere presence of a wicked suggestion or a blasphemous idea were a sin, our Savior could not have been sinless. Sin involves the exercise of the will and the consent of the heart, where a person welcomes, dwells upon, or acts on a suggestion (James 1:14-15). The Apostle Paul describes a similar inward warfare in Romans 7:15-20, where he notes that the "flesh" can produce impulses that the "inner man" truly hates.

If these thoughts cause you grief, it is actually evidence of the Holy Spirit's work of sanctification within you. A heart that is dead in sin does not recoil at blasphemy; it embraces it. Your desire to be free from these thoughts proves that you love God and desire to honor Him. You must remember that for those who are in Christ, there is no condemnation (Romans 8:1). Your standing before God is based on the perfect righteousness of Jesus, not the perfection of your wandering mind. This is the comfort of justification, where we are declared righteous by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you find yourself crushed by the guilt of these thoughts or wondering if God can truly accept you, look to the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). However, in His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature through the virgin birth and lived a perfectly sinless life. Jesus died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for sinners, bearing the wrath we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord over all. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the total forgiveness of all your sins. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step for your daily walk, do not try to argue with these intrusive thoughts or suppress them with your own strength, as this often makes them more frequent. Instead, practice taking every thought captive to the obedience of Christ (2 Corinthians 10:5) by immediately pivoting your focus away from the thought and toward the finished work of Jesus. If these thoughts become a heavy burden that disrupts your life, I encourage you to seek guidance from a local pastor or a certified biblical counselor who can help you navigate what may be a struggle with scrupulosity or anxiety.
Book of 1 Corinthians - Bible Survey

The Book of 1 Corinthians is a pastoral letter written by the Apostle Paul to the church he planted in Corinth, addressing specific reports of disorder and answering questions sent to him by the believers there. It serves as a foundational guide for how the Gospel of Jesus Christ should practically transform the behavior, unity, and worship of a local congregation.

Paul wrote this letter around 54 AD from Ephesus to a church struggling with its surrounding pagan culture. Corinth was a major cosmopolitan center known for its wealth, intellectual pride, and deep seated immorality. The believers there were bringing worldly values into the church, leading to significant internal conflict.

**Structural Overview of the Letter**

The letter is organized around several key issues that Paul addresses with the authority of an apostle and the heart of a father.

First, Paul tackles the problem of divisions (1 Corinthians 1:1 through 1 Corinthians 4:21). The Corinthians were forming factions based on their favorite teachers. Paul corrects this by pointing to the "theology of the cross," explaining that God’s wisdom is found in the crucified Christ, which shames human pride and unites all believers under one Lord (1 Corinthians 1:18; 1 Corinthians 1:30 through 1 Corinthians 1:31).

Second, he addresses moral failures and lawsuits (1 Corinthians 5:1 through 1 Corinthians 6:20). He speaks with clarity regarding sexual immorality, commanding the church to exercise discipline for the sake of the offender’s soul and the church's purity. He reminds them that their bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, bought with a price, and must be used to glorify God (1 Corinthians 6:19 through 1 Corinthians 6:20).

Third, he provides instructions on marriage, singleness, and Christian liberty (1 Corinthians 7:1 through 1 Corinthians 10:33). Paul teaches that while believers have freedom in Christ, that freedom must be governed by love for others. He famously argues that we should limit our own rights if exercising them would cause a brother or sister to stumble (1 Corinthians 8:9 through 1 Corinthians 8:13).

Fourth, he brings order to corporate worship (1 Corinthians 11:1 through 1 Corinthians 14:40). This section includes the proper observation of the Lord’s Supper and the use of spiritual gifts. While there are varying views among faithful Christians regarding the continuation of miraculous gifts today, Paul’s primary emphasis is that all things must be done for the edification of the body and in an orderly manner (1 Corinthians 14:26; 1 Corinthians 14:40). At the center of this instruction is the "way of love," without which even the most impressive spiritual gifts are meaningless (1 Corinthians 13:1 through 1 Corinthians 13:3).

Finally, Paul defends the doctrine of the bodily resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:1 through 1 Corinthians 15:58). He affirms that Christ’s resurrection is a historical fact and the essential foundation of the Christian faith. Without the resurrection, our preaching is in vain and we are still in our sins.

**Theological Significance**

A central theme of 1 Corinthians is progressive sanctification, the process by which the Holy Spirit conforms the believer into the image of Christ. Paul does not merely tell the Corinthians to "be better," he reminds them of who they are in Christ. He calls them "sanctified in Christ Jesus" and "called to be saints" (1 Corinthians 1:2) even while correcting their many errors. This illustrates that our standing before God is based on Christ’s finished work, which then becomes the motivation for holy living.

**Practical Application**

A concrete step for applying this book today is to evaluate your own contribution to the unity of your local church. You might ask yourself if your words and attitudes build up the body of Christ or contribute to unnecessary division. Consider committing to a specific act of service this week that prioritizes the needs of others above your own preferences, reflecting the sacrificial love described in 1 Corinthians 13.

**The Hope of the Gospel**

At the heart of this letter is the message that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that He was buried, and that He was raised on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3 through 1 Corinthians 15:4). Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from a holy God, but through faith in Jesus Christ, we receive the forgiveness of sins and the promise of eternal life. 

If you find yourself struggling with guilt or searching for purpose, know that God’s grace is sufficient for you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How do I identify my spiritual gift?

Spiritual gifts are divine enablements sovereignly distributed by the Holy Spirit to every believer for the purpose of edifying the Church and glorifying God. These gifts are not merely natural talents, though God often uses our natural bents, but are manifestations of the Spirit given for the common good (1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Corinthians 12:11).

Identifying your gift is less about taking a personality test and more about active stewardship within the body of Christ. You may begin by looking at your heart's inclinations, for God often gives a burden for a specific area of ministry alongside the ability to fulfill it (Romans 12:6-8). However, subjective desire must be met with objective fruit. You should look for where you see God's grace effectively reaching others through your words or actions, as a gift is recognized by its impact on the community of faith.

The most reliable way to confirm your gifting is through the local church. Just as a hand cannot function apart from the body, we often cannot see our own gifts clearly without the perspective of our brothers and sisters. Seek the counsel of your elders and mature believers who can observe your life and service. They are often the first to recognize the grace given to you (Ephesians 4:7).

I encourage you to take this practical next step: approach a leader in your local church this week and ask where there is a pressing need you can fill. Whether the task involves teaching, serving, or showing mercy, volunteer with a willing heart. As you serve, you will naturally discover where God has uniquely equipped you and where your efforts result in the spiritual growth of others (1 Peter 4:10-11).

In your private devotions, pray and ask the Lord for clarity and a humble heart to serve wherever He places you. He is faithful to provide wisdom to those who ask Him (James 1:5). Trust that as you seek to love His people, He will make your path and your specific role in the body clear.
What is lacking in Christ’s afflictions (Colossians 1:24)?

In Colossians 1:24, the phrase what is lacking does not refer to any deficiency in the merit, power, or sufficiency of the atoning sacrifice of Jesus Christ. The work of redemption is perfectly finished, and it requires no supplement from human effort or suffering to reconcile a sinner to God.

When Jesus died on the cross, He cried out, It is finished (John 19:30). This declaration confirms that the debt of sin was paid in full. Scripture further teaches that Christ appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself (Hebrews 9:26). Therefore, the lack Paul speaks of is not a lack of value in the blood of Christ, but a lack of visibility.

What was lacking was the personal, physical presentation of those finished afflictions to the people Paul was reaching. Paul understood that the world cannot see the scars of the risen Christ directly, so he became a living vessel through which the reality of Christ’s suffering was made manifest. By enduring trials, Paul was filling up the delivery of the Gospel message. He was extending the reach of Christ’s love through his own body so that the Church might see the cost of their salvation displayed in the life of their apostle.

This is a beautiful and sobering truth for every believer. While we can never add to the justification Christ purchased for us, we are called to a life of service that often involves hardship. Our sufferings for the sake of the Gospel serve as a bridge, carrying the message of the cross to a world that has not yet heard or seen its power.

As you reflect on this, consider the trials you may currently face. You might find it helpful to view your own patience in suffering not as a sign of God’s absence, but as an opportunity to demonstrate the sufficiency of His grace to those around you.

I encourage you to take this to the Lord in prayer today. Ask Him to help you see your current circumstances through the lens of His Great Commission. Ask that He would use your life, even the difficult parts, to make the beauty of Christ’s finished work known to someone who is hurting or lost. What specific hardship in your life right now feels most difficult to offer up for the sake of others seeing Christ?
How can I get my prayers answered by God?

Prayer is the humble cry of a child to a heavenly Father, and it is heard not because of our eloquence or our merit, but because of the perfect mediation of Jesus Christ. To have your prayers answered, you must understand that prayer is not a means of bending God's will to yours, but a way for your heart to be aligned with His sovereign purposes.

The first and most essential foundation for answered prayer is coming to God through the Lord Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we are by nature sinful, our sins create a separation that we cannot bridge on our own. Jesus said, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" in John 14:6. When we pray "in Jesus' name," we are not using a magical formula, but we are asking based on His authority and for His glory. Jesus promised, "And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son" in John 14:13.

Second, our requests must be aligned with the will of God as revealed in His Word. The Apostle John writes, "This is the confidence we have in approaching God: that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us" in 1 John 5:14. If we ask for things that satisfy selfish desires or contradict Scripture, we should not expect a "yes." James warns, "When you ask, you do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, that you may spend what you get on your pleasures" in James 4:3.

Third, we must approach God with a heart that is repentant and full of faith. Unconfessed sin acts as a barrier to our fellowship with God. The Psalmist noted, "If I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened" in Psalm 66:18. Faith is also vital, for "without faith it is impossible to please God, because anyone who comes to him must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who earnestly seek him" in Hebrews 11:6.

If you find yourself wondering if God hears you because you feel distant from Him, please know that the greatest answer to prayer is the gift of reconciliation with God Himself. God is perfectly holy and just, and our sin deserves His righteous judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die on the cross as a substitute for sinners. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For a practical next step in your prayer life, I encourage you to begin a "Scripture-saturated" prayer rhythm. Take a Psalm, such as Psalm 23 or Psalm 103, read it slowly, and turn each verse into a prayer back to God. This helps ensure your heart is focused on His character and His promises rather than just your own immediate needs. Pray directly to God throughout your day, and consider asking a mature believer at a local church to pray with you as well.
What is the more excellent way (1 Corinthians 12:31)?

The more excellent way is the way of love, which the Apostle Paul describes as the essential motive and governing principle for every Christian life. While the preceding verses in 1 Corinthians 12 discuss various spiritual gifts given for the building up of the church, Paul explains in 1 Corinthians 13 that these gifts are hollow and profit nothing if they are not exercised in love.

This love is not a mere feeling or a fleeting emotion, but a purposeful, sacrificial commitment to the well being of others, modeled perfectly by Jesus Christ. Paul clarifies that even if one possesses the most spectacular gifts, such as speaking in the tongues of angels, prophetic powers, or a faith that can move mountains, that person is "nothing" without love (1 Corinthians 13:1-3). Love is superior to spiritual gifts because gifts are temporary tools for the present age, whereas love is an eternal attribute of God's own character that will never fail (1 Corinthians 13:8).

In the economy of God's kingdom, the "way" we do things matters just as much as the things we do. You may be serving in your local church or using your talents in the workplace, but the "more excellent way" calls you to check your heart's motivation. This involves the pursuit of progressive sanctification, where the Holy Spirit conforms your character to be patient, kind, and not easily provoked (1 Corinthians 13:4-5).

The ultimate expression of this more excellent way is found in the Gospel. We were sinners, separated from a holy God and deserving of His just wrath, yet God showed His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and suffered the penal substitutionary death we deserved so that we might be reconciled to the Father. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering death and securing eternal life for all who believe.

If you find yourself striving to be "good enough" or relying on your own abilities to find favor with God, realize that only Christ’s perfect love can save you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be preventing you from resting in His love today?

As a practical step in following this more excellent way, I encourage you to read through 1 Corinthians 13:4-7 this week and identify one specific quality of love, such as patience or not being arrogant, that you find difficult to practice. Ask the Lord in prayer to grant you the grace to exhibit that quality toward someone in your life who is difficult to love, and look for one concrete opportunity to serve that person without expecting anything in return.
What is the open heaven concept?

The concept of an open heaven refers to those biblical instances where the veil between the earthly realm and the divine realm is drawn back, allowing human beings to witness God's glory, hear His voice, or receive His direct intervention and provision. This theme runs through the Scriptures as a sign of God's favor, revelation, and the establishing of His kingdom among men.

In the Old Testament, the patriarch Jacob experienced this at Bethel when he saw a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending, signifying that God was present and communicative with His people (Genesis 28:12). Later, the prophet Isaiah cried out for God to rend the heavens and come down, expressing the deep human longing for God to break through the silence caused by our sins (Isaiah 64:1).

The ultimate and permanent opening of heaven occurred through the person and work of Jesus Christ. At His baptism, the heavens were opened, and the Holy Spirit descended like a dove, marking the beginning of His public ministry (Matthew 3:16). Jesus later told His disciples that they would see heaven open and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man (John 1:51). By this, He identified Himself as the true Mediator, the only bridge between a holy God and sinful humanity.

Because of the fall, our sin created a barrier that no human effort could penetrate. However, through Christ’s substitutionary atonement on the cross, the curtain of the temple was torn from top to bottom (Matthew 27:51). This historical event signifies that for those who are in Christ, heaven is no longer shut. We now have bold access to the throne of grace through His blood (Hebrews 10:19–22).

While some modern teachers use the term open heaven to describe a specific atmosphere of emotional intensity or financial breakthrough, the biblical emphasis is far more profound. It focuses on our justification and the restored fellowship we have with the Father. To live under an open heaven is to live in the reality of being reconciled to God, where His Word is clear and His Spirit is active in our sanctification.

If you feel distant from God or as though the heavens are like brass, remember that the way has already been opened by Jesus. You do not need to perform rituals to open a portal to God, for Christ is the door. You may wonder if your past prevents this access, but the Gospel declares that Christ’s perfect life and sacrificial death are sufficient to clothe you in His righteousness. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life and immediate access to the Father. What is it that holds you back from resting in His finished work today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to spend time this week reading the book of Hebrews, specifically chapters 4 through 10. These passages beautifully explain how Jesus serves as our Great High Priest, ensuring that the way to God remains open for all who believe. Direct your prayers to Him with the confidence that He hears you and welcomes you because of His grace.
What is the origin of baptism?

Christian baptism was instituted by the Lord Jesus Christ as a command for His Church, though it finds its historical roots in the ceremonial washings of the Old Testament and the preparatory ministry of John the Baptist. It is an outward sign of an inward reality, signifying a believer's union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection.

The origins of baptism can be traced through three primary stages in redemptive history:

First, the Old Testament established various ceremonial washings, known in Greek as "baptisms," which God required for ritual purification. These washings, described in Hebrews 9:10, were applied to the priests and to those who had become ceremonially unclean according to the Law, as seen in Leviticus 14:8 and Numbers 19:7. While these rituals could not cleanse the conscience, they pointed forward to the need for a deeper, spiritual cleansing that only the Messiah could provide.

Second, in the period immediately preceding the ministry of Jesus, John the Baptist began a unique ministry in the wilderness. He called the people of Israel to a "baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins," as recorded in Mark 1:4 and Luke 3:3. John's baptism was a bridge between the old and new covenants, preparing a people for the arrival of the King by symbolizing a heart turned away from sin and toward the coming Savior.

Third, the formal origin of Christian baptism as we practice it today is found in the command of Jesus Christ. Following His resurrection and just before His ascension, Jesus gave the Great Commission to His disciples, saying, "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit," in Matthew 28:19. This ordinance was further clarified by the Apostles as a means of identifying with Christ. The Apostle Paul explains in Romans 6:3-4 that "all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into His death," and that we were buried with Him so that "just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may walk in newness of life."

Baptism is a beautiful picture of the Gospel. It declares that we are sinners who have been washed clean by the blood of Christ and granted new life by His Spirit. If you have placed your trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins but have not yet been baptized, a wonderful next step would be to speak with a pastor at a local, Bible-believing church about making this public profession of your faith. 

Has there been a time in your life when you turned from your sin to trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ?
What is historical criticism?

Historical criticism is a method of academic study that seeks to understand the historical origins, authorship, and original context of ancient texts, specifically the Bible. It focuses on investigating "the world behind the text" to determine how, when, and why these books were written, often using tools such as archaeology and comparative linguistics.

While it can be helpful to understand the cultural setting of the ancient world, we must be very careful with this approach. Many forms of historical criticism, often called higher criticism, begin with the assumption of naturalism, which is the belief that only natural laws and forces operate in the world. This often leads scholars to treat the Bible as a purely human product, questioning the reality of miracles, the predictive nature of prophecy, or the historical facts of the faith.

As Christians, we must never allow human intuition or academic theories to sit in judgment over the Word of God. The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority for all of life (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20–21). When we study history, we do so to see the hand of God more clearly, not to explain away His divine intervention. We believe, for instance, that the resurrection of Jesus Christ is not a "theological perspective" or a "religious story," but a literal, physical, historical fact (1 Corinthians 15:14–20).

I encourage you to approach the Scriptures with a heart of humble submission, recognizing that the Holy Spirit who inspired the text is the same One who helps us understand it today. A practical next step for your growth is to read the Bible within the community of your local church, perhaps joining a small group or a Bible study where the absolute authority of God's Word is cherished and taught. As you read, you might pray and ask God to give you wisdom and a deeper love for the truth that He has revealed to us.
What does the Bible say about paying taxes?

The Bible teaches that Christians are to pay taxes faithfully as an act of submission to the governing authorities appointed by God. This obligation is not merely a social contract but a matter of conscience and obedience to the Lord, who establishes all earthly power (Romans 13:1-7; Matthew 22:17-21).

In the Gospels, when Jesus was asked about the legality of paying taxes to a pagan Roman government, He looked at a denarius and declared, "Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s, and to God the things that are God’s" (Matthew 22:21). By this, our Savior affirmed that we live under two spheres of authority. While our souls and ultimate worship belong to God alone, we owe a debt of honor, taxes, and revenue to the civil magistrate because God uses human government to maintain order and restrain evil.

The Apostle Paul further clarifies this in his letter to the Romans, stating that we should "pay to all what is owed to them: taxes to whom taxes are owed, revenue to whom revenue is owed, respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed" (Romans 13:7). This teaching rests upon the sovereignty of God, who is the source of all legitimate authority. Even when we disagree with how funds are used, our primary duty is to represent Christ through our integrity and honesty, recognizing that "there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God" (Romans 13:1).

As a practical step of discipleship, I encourage you to approach your financial obligations to the state with a heart of integrity. When you prepare your taxes, do so honestly as if you were serving the Lord directly. Additionally, set aside a specific time to pray for your government leaders (1 Timothy 2:1-2), asking that God would grant them wisdom to use the revenue for the justice and well-being of all people. In doing so, you demonstrate that your ultimate hope is in the King of Kings while remaining a faithful citizen of your earthly country.
Should Christian women wear pants?

The Bible does not provide an explicit list of prohibited or required garments for women; instead, it establishes timeless principles regarding modesty, the heart, and the distinction between the sexes. This is a matter of tertiary doctrine where faithful Christians, seeking to honor God, have arrived at different convictions.

The primary scriptural foundation for this discussion is found in the Law, which states, "A woman shall not wear anything that pertains to a man, nor shall a man put on a woman’s garment, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD your God" Deuteronomy 22:5. In the New Testament, the emphasis shifts toward the internal state of the heart and the outward expression of modesty. The Apostle Paul writes that women should "adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control" 1 Timothy 2:9; 1 Timothy 2:10.

Within the global church, there are two primary historic views on this topic:

First, some believers hold a traditional view, interpreting Deuteronomy 22:5 as a permanent moral command that requires a clear, visible distinction in dress. They argue that because pants were historically associated with men in many cultures, women should wear skirts or dresses to honor God's distinct design for the sexes. For these Christians, maintaining this distinction is a matter of joyful obedience and public testimony to God’s created order.

Second, many other believers hold to a view of Christian liberty. They argue that what constitutes "men's" or "women's" clothing is often defined by cultural norms. In this view, as long as a woman wears pants designed for women and maintains modesty, she is not violating the principle of Deuteronomy 22:5. They believe the heart of the command is to avoid cross-dressing or the intentional blurring of sexual identity. These believers focus on the New Testament priority of inner beauty, as Peter encourages that a woman's "adorning should not be external," but rather "the hidden person of the heart with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit" 1 Peter 3:3; 1 Peter 3:4.

Because the New Testament does not give a specific dress code, this falls under the principle of conscience. Scripture teaches that "each one should be fully convinced in his own mind" Romans 14:5. Whether you choose to wear pants or dresses, the standard for every believer is to dress in a way that is modest, honors your God-given identity, and does not cause a brother or sister to stumble.

As you seek to honor the Lord, I encourage you to examine your own heart and motivations. Is your desire to reflect the holiness and beauty of Christ? Consider the context of your local church and the conscience of those around you. A helpful next step would be to pray for wisdom and speak with your local church elders or a mature woman in your congregation who can offer guidance specific to your community. Seek to adorn the Gospel in all things, for the Lord looks primarily at the heart.
Why wasn't Jesus named Immanuel?

The name Jesus was His personal name given to identify His mission of salvation, while Immanuel is a descriptive title that reveals His divine nature. In the biblical context, saying someone will be called by a name often refers to their character, office, or the reality of their presence rather than their literal birth name.

When the angel spoke to Joseph, he commanded that the child be named Jesus because He would save His people from their sins, Matthew 1:21. The name Jesus is the Greek form of the Hebrew name Yeshua, which means Yahweh is Salvation. This name specifically addresses the work Christ came to do as our Mediator and Savior.

The prophecy in Isaiah 7:14 states that He would be called Immanuel, which means God with us, Matthew 1:23. This title highlights the miracle of the Incarnation. It tells us who Jesus is in His very essence, He is the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh to dwell among us. Therefore, Jesus did not need to have Immanuel as His literal first name to fulfill the prophecy. Every time He healed the sick, taught the crowds, or showed mercy to the brokenhearted, He was proving that God was truly with His people.

If you are wondering about the identity of Jesus, it is the most important question you will ever face. The Bible teaches that we are all separated from a holy God because of our sin. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son to bridge that gap. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the Gospel of Matthew and the first chapter of the Gospel of John. These passages beautifully explain how Jesus is both the Savior who died for us and the God who is with us. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How is choosing a good name better than choosing riches (Proverbs 22:1)?

Choosing a good name is superior to great riches because a godly reputation represents a heart of integrity and the enduring fruit of sanctification, whereas material wealth is temporary and cannot secure true favor with God or man. While silver and gold can purchase physical comforts, they are fleeting and easily lost, but a character built on the truth of God’s Word yields a legacy that honors the Lord and serves as a powerful witness to the Gospel.

In the wisdom of Scripture, a "name" refers to much more than a label; it signifies a person’s character and the weight of their testimony. Riches are often "uncertain" (1 Timothy 6:17) and can even become a snare that leads to pride or spiritual wandering. In contrast, a good name is a "fragrant oil" (Ecclesiastes 7:1) that provides a believer with the spiritual influence needed to point others toward the hope of Christ. When we prioritize our character over our bank accounts, we demonstrate that we value the honor of God’s name above our own personal gain.

The ultimate reason a good name is better than riches is that it reflects the nature of our Savior. Jesus Christ, though He was rich, for our sakes became poor so that we might become rich in Him (2 Corinthians 8:9). He maintained a perfect, sinless name even unto death, and God has now highly exalted Him, giving Him the name that is above every name (Philippians 2:9). As followers of Christ, we are stewards of His reputation. When we walk in honesty and kindness, we are not just protecting our own status; we are showing the world the transformative power of God’s grace.

As a practical step in pursuing a good name, I encourage you to identify one specific relationship or commitment this week where you can practice radical integrity, even if it costs you a personal advantage or financial gain. Take time to pray and ask the Lord to reveal any area where the pursuit of "gold" has outpaced your pursuit of "godliness," and ask Him for the strength to value His approval above all earthly treasure.

Proverbs 22:1; 1 Timothy 6:17; Ecclesiastes 7:1; 2 Corinthians 8:9; Philippians 2:9
What is the lust of the eyes?

The lust of the eyes is the sinful craving of the heart for things that are seen, where the visual perception of the world arouses a desire to possess what God has forbidden or withheld. It is the restless wandering of the eyes that seeks satisfaction in the creation rather than the Creator (1 John 2:16).

This temptation is not merely the act of seeing, but the heart’s response to what is seen. It is a form of covetousness where the eye acts as a scout for an idol making heart. When we fix our gaze on wealth, physical beauty, or the possessions of others with a heart that says, "I must have that to be happy," we are succumbing to this lust. It is the deceptive belief that if we could only possess what we see, our souls would be satisfied.

We see the destructive power of this sin throughout the historical accounts of Scripture. In the Garden of Eden, the fall of humanity began when Eve saw that the forbidden fruit was "a delight to the eyes" (Genesis 3:6). King David, a man after God’s own heart, fell into adultery and murder after he lingered in his gaze upon a woman bathing (2 Samuel 11:2). Even Achan brought judgment upon himself and Israel because he saw a beautiful robe and silver and "coveted them and took them" (Joshua 7:21).

Theologically, the lust of the eyes reveals a lack of contentment in the sovereignty and goodness of God. It is a failure to believe that God has provided exactly what we need for His glory and our good. Because our hearts are naturally prone to wander and seek fulfillment in temporal things, we all find ourselves ensnared by this struggle. Our sin separates us from a holy God who sees not just our outward actions, but the very secrets of our hearts.

There is, however, great hope found in the Gospel. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh and lived a life of perfect purity, never once succumbing to the lust of the eyes or any other temptation. He suffered the penalty we deserved, dying on the cross to pay for our covetousness and idolatry. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death, and He is now the exalted Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts to remain pure. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to make a habit of praying the words of the Psalmist: "Turn my eyes from looking at worthless things; give me life in Your ways" (Psalm 119:37). In moments of visual temptation, whether through media or materialism, practice "the bounce," which is the immediate decision to look away and redirect your thoughts to the beauty of Christ. 

What is one specific area of your life where you find it most difficult to keep your eyes from wandering toward things that do not honor God?
What does the Bible say about Voodoo? How should a Christian view Voodoo?

The Bible teaches that Voodoo is a practice that combines elements of ancestral worship, spiritism, and magic, all of which are strictly forbidden as forms of idolatry and sorcery. Scripture is clear that we are to worship and serve the one true God alone, seeking no other spiritual power or mediation (Exodus 20:3; Deuteronomy 18:10-12).

A Christian should view Voodoo as a spiritual deception that is incompatible with the Gospel of Jesus Christ. While the cultural roots of such practices are often tied to a desire for protection, healing, or connection to one's heritage, the Bible reveals that the spirits sought in these rituals are not neutral forces or ancestors, but demonic entities (1 Corinthians 10:20). To engage in Voodoo is to seek help from the kingdom of darkness rather than trusting in the sovereign care of our Heavenly Father.

The precision of Truth reminds us that there is only one Mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5). Any system that requires rituals, sacrifices, or the appeasement of spirits to find favor or safety denies the sufficiency of Christ’s finished work on the cross. Jesus has already triumphed over every principality and power, disarming them and putting them to open shame (Colossians 2:15).

You may wonder how a person can find safety in a world that feels spiritually heavy or frightening. It is important to remember that God is perfectly holy and supreme, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him (Romans 3:23). In His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature and live the perfect life we could not live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation and protection are found by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For those who have been involved in Voodoo or similar practices, a concrete and pastoral next step is to remove any objects, charms, or idols associated with these rituals from your home, just as the early believers did in the book of Acts (Acts 19:19). I encourage you to seek out a faithful, Bible teaching local church where you can find community and be discipled in the freedom that is found in Christ. You might also find it helpful to read through the Gospel of John to see the power and love of Jesus clearly displayed.
What is the meaning of Lent?

Lent is a period of forty days, excluding Sundays, that leads up to the celebration of Easter and is traditionally used by many Christians as a season of fasting, prayer, and repentance. It is modeled after the forty days Jesus Christ spent fasting in the wilderness before beginning His public ministry, as recorded in Matthew 4:1-2.

While Lent is a widespread tradition, it is considered a tertiary matter within the Christian faith, meaning it is not a requirement for salvation or a command found in the Bible. Faithful believers hold different views on its observance. Some follow the liturgical calendar closely, finding that the focused season helps them practice the mortification of sin, which is the intentional putting to death of sinful habits through the power of the Holy Spirit. Others, particularly in the Reformed and Baptist traditions, emphasize Christian liberty, noting that while the practice can be a helpful tool for devotion, we are not bound by man made holy days, as Christ is the fulfillment of all shadows and types according to Colossians 2:16-17.

The heart behind observing such a season should always be to fix our gaze more intently upon the person and work of Jesus Christ. It serves as a somber reminder of the weight of our sin and the immense cost of our redemption. We see the holiness of God and our own spiritual bankruptcy apart from His grace. This recognition is meant to lead us to a deeper joy when we celebrate the resurrection, knowing that Christ has conquered sin and death on our behalf.

If you find yourself burdened by guilt or seeking a deeper meaning in these traditions, remember that the ultimate purpose of any spiritual discipline is to lead us to the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and that our sin has separated us from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious observances. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step for this season, whether you choose to fast from a specific food or activity, I encourage you to replace that time with a focused reading of the Gospel of Mark. Aim to read one or two chapters a day, asking God to show you the beauty and authority of His Son. You might also consider setting aside ten minutes each morning to pray specifically for a heart that is more sensitive to the leading of the Holy Spirit, as promised in Ezekiel 36:26-27.

What thoughts or questions come to your mind as you consider the sacrifice of Jesus?
What doth every sin deserve?

Every sin, being a transgression against the law of an infinitely holy and sovereign God, deserves His righteous wrath and curse, both in this life and in the eternity to come. The Bible teaches that the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23), and that anyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law is under a curse (Galatians 3:10).

Because God is perfectly just, He cannot ignore rebellion or treat evil as if it were insignificant. Sin is not merely a lapse in judgment, but a rejection of the glory and authority of the Creator who gives us breath. This divine justice requires a penalty that matches the infinite dignity of the One who has been offended. Consequently, those who remain in their sins face eternal separation from the presence of God in a state of conscious punishment (Matthew 25:46).

You may wonder how a God of love could decree such a heavy penalty for sin. It is because His love is inseparable from His holiness, and He would not be good if He were not also just. However, the same God who is a consuming fire is also the God of all grace. Though we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), God showed His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8).

Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a life of perfect obedience, and suffered the substitutionary death we deserved. On the cross, He endured the righteous wrath of the Father in the place of His people. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, triumphing over sin and death forever. This salvation is not earned by our works, but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to spend time today reading Romans chapter 5 and Romans chapter 8. Reflect on the great exchange where our sin was placed on Christ and His righteousness is given to those who believe. Allow this truth to move you toward a heart of deep gratitude and worship in your daily prayer.
What does the Bible say about diligence?

The Bible presents diligence as the steady, earnest, and energetic application of one's strength and mind to the tasks God has assigned. It is the antithesis of sloth, which the Scriptures warn leads to decay and poverty, as seen in Proverbs 10:4 and Proverbs 12:24.

From a biblical perspective, diligence is rooted in the doctrine of stewardship. We recognize that our time, talents, and energy are gifts from a sovereign Creator, and we are called to manage them for His renown. This applies to our vocation, a term that reminds us that every legitimate job is a calling from God. When we are diligent, we reflect the character of God Himself, who is tirelessly active in sustaining His creation, as Jesus noted in John 5:17.

Diligence is also a vital component of our sanctification, which is the process of being made more like Christ. While we are justified by grace alone through faith alone, the Holy Spirit produces in us a desire to grow in holiness. We are commanded to make every effort to supplement our faith with virtue and godliness in 2 Peter 1:5. This spiritual industry is not an attempt to earn God's favor, but a grateful response to the love He has already lavished upon us through the Gospel.

The Apostle Paul provides a beautiful summary of this mindset when he instructs believers to work with their whole heart as if they are serving the Lord Christ Himself, rather than merely seeking to please men, in Colossians 3:23 and Colossians 3:24. Whether you are sweeping a floor, raising children, or leading a company, your diligence is a form of worship that testifies to the reality of your faith.

To practically apply this, I encourage you to begin your next period of work or service with a brief moment of dedication. Ask the Lord to help you see your tasks through the lens of His glory, and choose one specific responsibility that you have been tempted to neglect. Commit to completing that task with excellence this week as a tangible offering of thanks to God.
What is agnosticism?

Agnosticism is the philosophical position that the existence of God, the divine, or the supernatural is unknown or inherently unknowable. The term is derived from the Greek word agnostos, which means unknown, and it suggests that human reason and evidence are insufficient to provide certain knowledge regarding the existence of a higher power.

While agnosticism often presents itself as a humble admission of human limitation, the Bible teaches that God has not left Himself without a witness. Through what theologians call general revelation, God has clearly displayed His eternal power and divine nature through the things He has made, so that all people are without excuse, as seen in Romans 1:19-20. Furthermore, God has provided special revelation through His Word and, most perfectly, through the person of Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh, as described in John 1:1; John 1:14.

You may wonder how a person can be truly certain of a God who is Spirit and remains invisible to the physical eye. This is a sincere concern, yet the Christian faith is not a leap into the dark, but a response to the light God has already shone into the world.

The central message of the Bible is that although God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, He has made Himself known to us in love. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was bodily raised from the dead on the third day. Through His resurrection, Jesus was declared with power to be the Son of God, offering us a certain hope rather than an abstract theory, as noted in Romans 1:4; 1 Peter 1:3.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or intellectual reaching. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What is it that makes the idea of God's knowability seem difficult to embrace? 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name, according to John 20:31. As you read, ask God to reveal the truth of His character to your heart.
What does it mean to “be angry and do not sin” (Psalm 4:4)?

To "be angry and do not sin" means to acknowledge the rising tide of emotion in response to injustice or personal offense without allowing that emotion to drive you into disobedience against God. In the context of Psalm 4:4, David is calling for a holy restraint, urging his readers to tremble in awe of God rather than being consumed by their own passions.

Anger is a powerful, God given capacity to react against what is wrong, often referred to as righteous indignation when it mirrors God's own hatred of sin. However, because of our fallen nature, human anger is rarely pure and often becomes a foothold for the enemy. To "not sin" in your anger involves guarding your heart against the desire for personal vengeance, malice, or the use of destructive words. The Apostle Paul carries this command into the New Testament, reminding believers that the sun should not go down on their anger, emphasizing that unresolved or uncontrolled anger gives an opportunity to the devil, as seen in Ephesians 4:26-27.

This process is a vital part of progressive sanctification, where the Holy Spirit works within the believer to produce self-control, which is a fruit of the Spirit, as noted in Galatians 5:22-23. True spiritual maturity is not the absence of feeling, but the submission of our feelings to the Lordship of Christ. We are called to be quick to hear, slow to speak, and slow to anger, for the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God, according to James 1:19-20.

As a practical step in your walk with Christ, the next time you feel the heat of anger rising, follow the counsel of the very next phrase in Psalm 4:4: "meditate in your heart upon your bed and be still." Take a moment of intentional silence to pray, asking the Lord to reveal whether your anger is for His glory or your own ego, and seek His peace to govern your response. Through the grace of the Gospel, we are forgiven for our outbursts and empowered to reflect the gentleness of our Savior, who, when He was reviled, did not revile in return, as recorded in 1 Peter 2:23.
How will the covenant with death be annulled (Isaiah 28:18)?

The covenant with death in Isaiah 28:18 is annulled by God through the sweeping away of false human security and the establishment of a sure foundation in Jesus Christ. Historically, the leaders of Jerusalem believed they had secured their safety through political alliances or spiritual compromises, effectively making a pact to avoid the reach of death and the grave. God declares that He will cancel this futile agreement by bringing the very judgment they feared, proving that no human scheme can provide a refuge from His righteous standards (Isaiah 28:15; Isaiah 28:17; Isaiah 28:18).

In the immediate context, God warns that when the overwhelming scourge of judgment passes through, these false refuges will be trampled. However, in the midst of this warning, God provides the only true alternative to this covenant with death. He says, "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation" (Isaiah 28:16). The New Testament identifies this cornerstone as Jesus Christ, who provides the only security that stands against the judgment of God and the power of the grave (Romans 9:33; 1 Peter 2:6).

Theologically, this passage underscores the reality of substitutionary atonement. While humanity often attempts to "deal" with death through self-effort, philosophy, or false religion, these are but "refuges of lies." Our true "covenant with death" is the result of our sin, for the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23). This debt is not settled by our negotiations but was canceled by Christ on the cross. He took the overwhelming scourge of God's wrath upon Himself so that those who trust in Him would never be put to shame.

You may wonder how a person can find peace when faced with the certainty of death and judgment. It is a natural human concern to seek some form of protection against the unknown. Yet, the Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and our sin has separated us from Him, leaving us under a sentence of death that we cannot escape on our own. 

The good news of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, became man, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9; Acts 4:12; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from placing your trust in this sure foundation today?

As a practical next step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read Isaiah 28 alongside 1 Peter 2. Meditate on what it means for Jesus to be the cornerstone of your life, and spend time in prayer today, asking the Lord to show you any "false refuges" you might be leaning on instead of Him. Seek out the fellowship of a faithful local church where the Word of God is honored and the Gospel is preached clearly.
What is the Hindu caste system?

The Hindu caste system is an ancient social hierarchy that categorizes people into hereditary groups based on birth, occupation, and perceived spiritual purity. It is primarily structured around four main classes known as varnas, which are further divided into thousands of localized sub-groups called jatis.

The four varnas consist of the Brahmins (priests and scholars), the Kshatriyas (rulers and warriors), the Vaishyas (merchants and landowners), and the Shudras (laborers and servants). Outside of this formal structure are the Dalits, historically referred to as untouchables, who have traditionally performed tasks considered spiritually polluting and have faced severe social exclusion. This system is traditionally linked to the concepts of karma and reincarnation, suggesting that one's current social position is a result of actions in a previous life.

From a biblical perspective, the caste system stands in contrast to the fundamental truth of the Imago Dei, or the image of God. The Bible teaches that God created man in His own image, in the image of God He created him, male and female He created them (Genesis 1:27). This means that every human being, regardless of their birth, family lineage, or occupation, possesses equal and infinite worth before their Creator. There is no hierarchy of human value in the eyes of God, as He shows no partiality (Acts 10:34).

The Gospel of Jesus Christ further dismantles such divisions by offering salvation and a new identity to all people through faith alone. In the church, there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus (Galatians 3:28). While the world may seek to rank individuals by their social standing, the Gospel reveals that we are all equally fallen and in need of the grace found in Christ's sacrificial death and resurrection.

If you are searching for a sense of belonging or worth that the world has failed to provide, I encourage you to look to Jesus. He is the one who welcomes the weary and the heavy laden (Matthew 11:28). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is your understanding of how a person finds true value and identity?
What does the Bible say about rest and relaxation?

The Bible teaches that rest is not a luxury or a sign of weakness, but a divine gift and a command rooted in the very character of God. From the beginning, God established a rhythm of work and rest, sanctifying the seventh day because He Himself rested from His creative labors (Genesis 2:2–3).

True biblical rest involves more than the mere cessation of physical activity; it is an act of trust in God’s **providence**. When we pause from our work, we acknowledge that the world continues to turn under His sovereign care and that we are not the sustainers of our own lives (Psalm 127:1–2). This practice preserves our finite bodies and reorients our hearts toward our Creator. Even Jesus, in His perfect humanity, modeled the necessity of pulling away from the crowds to rest and pray, specifically inviting His disciples to "come away by yourselves to a desolate place and rest a while" (Mark 6:31).

However, the physical rest we enjoy is a shadow of a deeper, spiritual reality. Our ultimate rest is found in Jesus Christ, who provides the only true relief for the soul burdened by the weight of sin and the impossible task of self-justification. He graciously invites us, saying, "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This rest is secured through His perfect life, His sacrificial death on the cross for our sins, and His victorious resurrection from the grave. By resting in His finished work, we are reconciled to God and freed from the need to earn His favor through our own efforts.

For the believer, relaxation is also an expression of Christian liberty, provided it is done to the glory of God and does not lead to the sin of slothfulness. We are called to be good stewards of our strength so that we may serve Him with joy.

As a practical step this week, I encourage you to set aside a specific block of time for "Sabbath rest"—intentional time where you put away your work and technology to focus on God’s Word, enjoy His creation, and delight in the fellowship of His people.

If you find your heart is restless and heavy today, remember that God is holy and our sin separates us from Him, but in His great love, He sent His Son to be our substitute. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find the eternal rest your soul craves. What is it that makes it difficult for you to truly rest in Him today?
What is deism? What do deists believe?

Deism is the belief in a Creator who brought the universe into existence but does not intervene in its operation or the lives of people. Deists typically believe that God is like a master clockmaker who wound up the world and then stepped back to let it run according to natural laws, leaving human reason as the only reliable guide for life.

Those who hold to deism generally believe that God’s existence can be proven through nature and logic, a concept known as general revelation, but they reject special revelation, which is God’s specific communication to humanity through the Bible. Because they view God as distant and uninvolved, deists do not believe in miracles, the inspiration of Scripture, the Trinity, or the divinity of Jesus Christ. For the deist, God is a First Cause to be acknowledged, rather than a Father to be worshipped or a Savior to be known.

You may wonder how an orderly universe could exist without a Master Designer, and deism rightly acknowledges that the beauty and complexity of creation point to a Creator. As the Scriptures say, the heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky proclaims the work of His hands (Psalm 19:1). However, the Bible teaches us that God is not a cold, distant architect. He is the God of Providence who actively sustains all things by the word of His power (Hebrews 1:3). He is so near to us that in Him we live and move and have our being (Acts 17:27; Acts 17:28).

The great tragedy of deism is that it leaves us alone in our guilt and searching for meaning in a silent universe. But the Gospel tells us that God has spoken. In the past, God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son (Hebrews 1:1; Hebrews 1:2). 

The Truth is that God is perfectly holy, and we have all rebelled against Him, falling short of His glory. Because of His love, He did not remain distant. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came into the world to live the perfect life we could not live and to die on a cross as a sacrifice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, defeating death and opening the way for us to be reconciled to God. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own reason or works.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

A wonderful next step would be to read the Gospel of John in the Bible, which shows how the Creator became flesh to dwell among us so that we might know Him personally. What prevents you from believing that God wants to be known by you?
Is the distinction between clergy and laity biblical?

The Bible teaches that while there is an essential spiritual equality among all believers, there is also a clear distinction in office and function within the local church. Every person who has been redeemed by the blood of Christ belongs to a "royal priesthood," yet God has specifically appointed some to lead, teach, and shepherd the flock. 1 Peter 2:9; Ephesians 4:11-12.

To understand this correctly, we must look at two biblical pillars that stand side by side.

First, the New Testament establishes the priesthood of all believers. This means that you do not need a human mediator to approach God, for there is only one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. 1 Timothy 2:5. Every believer has direct access to the throne of grace and is called to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God. 1 Peter 2:5. In this sense, there is no "spiritual class" system; the person in the pew and the man in the pulpit are ontologically equal before the Lord.

Second, the Scriptures explicitly command the appointment of specific offices. The New Testament identifies the office of elder, also called overseer or pastor, and the office of deacon. Acts 14:23; 1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9. These men are given a particular authority and responsibility to watch over souls, and the congregation is called to honor and submit to their leadership in the Lord. Hebrews 13:17; 1 Thessalonians 5:12-13.

The term "clergy" comes from the Greek word *kleros*, meaning "inheritance" or "lot," while "laity" comes from *laos*, meaning "people." In the biblical sense, the entire church is God’s inheritance and His people. However, the distinction in role is vital for the health of the body. The purpose of the "clergy" (the elders and pastors) is not to do the ministry *instead* of the people, but to equip the saints for the work of ministry. Ephesians 4:12.

In a Reformed Baptist understanding, this looks like a church led by a plurality of godly elders who teach the Word and provide oversight, while the congregation as a whole remains responsible for maintaining the purity of the Gospel and the membership of the church. Matthew 18:15-17.

If you are currently part of a local church, a wonderful next step would be to study 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1 this week. As you do, pray specifically for your pastors and elders by name, asking God to give them wisdom and holiness as they shepherd His people. At the same time, ask the Lord to show you how you can use your unique spiritual gifts to build up the body, remembering that you are a vital member of His royal priesthood. Romans 12:4-8.
What does it mean to have a wilderness experience?

A wilderness experience is a season of spiritual trial, isolation, or profound difficulty where a believer’s earthly comforts and sense of God’s immediate presence are stripped away, leaving them to depend solely on the sustaining grace of God. While these times often feel like a desert of abandonment, they are purposeful appointments in God’s providence designed to humble the soul, test the heart, and produce deeper sanctification (Deuteronomy 8:2; James 1:2–4).

In the Scriptures, the wilderness is a place of both preparation and revelation. Consider the historical account of the Israelites, whom God led into the desert for forty years. This was not to destroy them, but to teach them that man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD (Deuteronomy 8:3). Similarly, our Savior, Jesus Christ, was led by the Holy Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil (Matthew 4:1). Where Israel failed their test through grumbling and unbelief, Christ remained perfectly faithful, demonstrating His righteousness and His victory over the enemy on behalf of His people.

If you find yourself in such a season, you may feel as though God is silent or that your prayers are bouncing off a brass heaven. It is important to realize that the Great Physician often uses the lonely place to perform His most delicate work. It is in the dry places that our idols are exposed and our thirst for the living God is intensified (Psalm 63:1). This experience is not a sign of God’s rejection, but is often a mark of His fatherly discipline and love, intended to wean us from the world and tether us more closely to Himself (Hebrews 12:6).

A practical next step for you is to establish a daily rhythm of praying through the "Lament Psalms," such as Psalm 13 or Psalm 142. These inspired prayers give you a biblical vocabulary to express your pain to God while simultaneously anchoring your soul in His character and past faithfulness.

If your wilderness is marked by a sense of guilt or a realization that you have been wandering far from God, remember the hope of the Gospel. We all, like sheep, have gone astray, but the Lord laid on Jesus the iniquity of us all (Isaiah 53:6). Jesus endured the ultimate wilderness of the cross—tasting the darkness of separation from the Father—so that His children would never truly be forsaken. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What is the heaviest burden or the greatest "thirst" you are currently feeling in this season of your life?
What does Malachi 2:16 mean when God says, ‘I hate divorce’?

In Malachi 2:16, God expresses His deep opposition to the breaking of the marriage covenant because it represents a betrayal of the faithfulness He intends for His people. Malachi 2:14; Malachi 2:15. This statement is a powerful reminder that marriage is not merely a legal contract but a sacred bond established by God Himself.

The historical context of this passage involves the men of Israel who were acting treacherously toward the "wife of their youth." They were discarding their wives, often to pursue relationships with those who worshipped false gods, thereby violating their promises and wounding their families. When God says He hates divorce, He is expressing His holy grief over the violence and pain that the rupture of a covenant causes to individuals, children, and the community of faith. Marriage is intended to be a living picture of the relationship between Christ and His Church, characterized by sacrificial love and enduring loyalty. Ephesians 5:31-32.

It is vital to distinguish between God's hatred for the act of divorce and His love for the people involved. While God upholds the permanence of marriage, He remains a refuge for the brokenhearted. Psalm 34:18. In a fallen world, Jesus acknowledged that divorce sometimes occurs because of human "hardheartedness," yet the original design remains one of lifelong unity. Matthew 19:8.

If you are walking through the shadows of a difficult marriage or the aftermath of a divorce, do not carry this burden alone. An actionable step for you is to seek the support of your local church elders, who can provide biblical guidance and prayerful care. If you find yourself in a place of guilt or sorrow, turn to the Lord in prayer, confessing your needs to Him and resting in the truth that there is no sin too great for the blood of Christ to cover for those who trust in Him. 1 John 1:9.
How transparent should church finances be?

Church finances should be characterized by a high degree of transparency and integrity, reflecting the holiness of the God we serve. Because every gift given to the church is a sacred trust from the people of God for the work of God, leadership must be beyond reproach in how those funds are managed.

The Bible teaches that we are to handle financial matters with great care, not only in the sight of the Lord but also in the sight of men. The Apostle Paul provided a clear model for this when he spoke about the collection for the saints in 2 Corinthians 8:20-21, saying, "We are taking this precaution so that no one can find fault with our administration of this generous gift. For we are taking pains to do what is right, not only in the eyes of the Lord but also in the eyes of men."

In a healthy church, this transparency usually includes regular financial reports provided to the congregation, an annual budget approved by the members, and clear internal controls handled by qualified individuals such as deacons or a finance committee. Within a Reformed Baptist framework, this often involves elder-led oversight with congregational accountability. Such measures serve as a guard against the sin of greed and protect the ministers from unfounded accusations, as those who lead must be "not lovers of money" (1 Timothy 3:3; Titus 1:7).

True stewardship recognizes that we own nothing; we are merely managers of God's resources. When a church is open about its needs and its spending, it fosters trust and encourages the body of Christ to give cheerfully, knowing their offerings are being used for the advancement of the Gospel and the care of the needy. Transparency ensures that the "way of truth" is not slandered by the world (2 Peter 2:2).

As a practical next step, if you have questions about your own church's finances, I encourage you to attend the next members' business meeting or kindly ask an elder for a copy of the most recent financial statement. You should also pray that God would grant your leaders wisdom and unwavering integrity as they manage the resources He has entrusted to them (Hebrews 13:17).
Is intinction the correct way to do communion?

Intinction is the practice of dipping the bread into the wine or juice so that both elements are received at the same time, and while it is used in some Christian traditions, the historical and biblical pattern generally emphasizes partaking of the bread and the cup as two distinct actions. In the institution of the Lord’s Supper, Jesus took the bread and broke it, and afterward He took the cup, giving thanks for each individually as recorded in Matthew 26:26-28 and 1 Corinthians 11:23-25.

Whether intinction is considered the "correct" way often depends on how a church views the specific commands of Christ and the symbolism involved. There are two primary perspectives on this matter within the body of Christ.

First, many believers, particularly in Reformed and Baptist traditions, hold that we should maintain the distinction between eating and drinking. This view suggests that because Jesus explicitly commanded His disciples to "eat" and to "drink," we honor His institution best by performing these as separate acts of obedience. Symbolically, the separation of the bread (His body) and the wine (His blood) points to the reality of His death, where His blood was poured out from His body as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins as noted in 1 Corinthians 11:26.

Second, other traditions, such as some Anglican, Lutheran, or Eastern Orthodox churches, practice intinction for various reasons. Some do so out of a desire for liturgical flow, while others adopt it for practical or hygienic concerns, especially when using a common cup. Those who practice intinction would argue that the essence of the sacrament is receiving the elements of Christ’s sacrifice, and that receiving them together does not diminish the grace offered through the ordinance.

Since the Bible does not explicitly forbid intinction but provides a clear pattern of separate partaking, this is generally viewed as a tertiary issue or a matter of church order. Faithful Christians may disagree on the method of administration while remaining united in the core truth that the Lord’s Supper is a visible word that proclaims the Lord's death until He comes.

As a practical step, I encourage you to speak with the elders or pastors of your local church to understand why they practice communion in their specific way. Following the leadership of your local congregation in these matters promotes the unity and peace of the body. When you next partake of the elements, whether separately or by intinction, set your heart fully on the finished work of Jesus Christ, whose body was broken and whose blood was shed to reconcile you to God.
What is the church suffering?

The suffering of the church refers to the various trials, persecutions, and afflictions that believers endure because of their union with Jesus Christ and their residence in a fallen world. Far from being a sign of God's abandonment, the Bible teaches that suffering is a standard part of the Christian life, used by God for our sanctification and His ultimate glory.

Our Lord Jesus was very plain with His disciples when He told them, "In the world you will have tribulation. But take courage; I have overcome the world" (John 16:33). We see this suffering manifest in several distinct ways.

First, there is the suffering of persecution. Because the church represents the kingdom of God in a world that often loves darkness rather than light, believers frequently face opposition. This can range from social exclusion and mockery to physical harm and martyrdom. Scripture reminds us that "all who desire to live godly in Christ Jesus will be persecuted" (2 Timothy 3:12). In these moments, the church participates in the "fellowship of His sufferings" (Philippians 3:10), standing firm in the truth even when the cost is high.

Second, there is the suffering of refinement. God, in His providence, uses the common trials of life—sickness, loss, and hardship—to prune His children and produce the "peaceable fruit of righteousness" (Hebrews 12:11). Just as gold is purified by the fire, our faith is strengthened and our character is shaped through the difficulties we face (1 Peter 1:6-7). This is a vital part of our progressive sanctification, stripping away our self-reliance and teaching us to trust more deeply in the sufficiency of Christ.
