Third, there is the internal suffering of the "groaning" church. We live in the tension of the "already and the not yet." While we are already saved and justified by grace through faith, we still live in mortal bodies subject to decay and in a world corrupted by sin. We groan inwardly as we wait for the redemption of our bodies and the final renewal of all things (Romans 8:23).

If you are currently walking through a season of deep pain or opposition, please know that your sorrow is not overlooked by your Heavenly Father. He is the "Father of mercies and God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3). Your suffering is never meaningless in His hands; He is working all things together for the good of those who love Him (Romans 8:28).

I encourage you to take this practical step: Draw near to your local church family this week. Share your burdens with a trusted elder or a mature believer, for we are called to "bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ" (Galatians 6:2). Do not suffer in isolation, but find strength in the means of grace—the Word, prayer, and the fellowship of the saints.

Ultimately, all human suffering finds its answer in the cross of Jesus Christ. He is the "Man of Sorrows" who took upon Himself the wrath we deserved for our sins so that we might be reconciled to God. By His historical death and bodily resurrection, He defeated sin and death forever. You can turn to Him today, repenting of your sin and trusting in Him alone for forgiveness. In Christ, your present sufferings are not worthy to be compared with the glory that will be revealed in us (Romans 8:18). What is it that prevents you from casting your cares upon Him today?
What does it mean that a person will not inherit the kingdom of God (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)?

To not inherit the kingdom of God means to be eternally excluded from the presence, blessings, and reign of God, remaining instead under His holy and just judgment. In 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, the Apostle Paul explains that those whose lives are defined by persistent, unrepentant rebellion against God’s moral law have no share in the eternal life He promises to His children.

This passage is a serious warning against self-deception. Paul lists several categories of sin, including sexual immorality, idolatry, theft, and greed, to show that those who make these things their practice are living as enemies of God. The "unrighteous" mentioned in 1 Corinthians 6:9 are those who remain in their natural state of sin, separated from the life of God. This inheritance is not something we earn by our own effort, but it is a gift given to those who are members of God's family.

The weight of this warning is matched by the immense hope of the Gospel found in verse 11: "And such were some of you." This phrase indicates that the individuals in the Corinthian church had once been defined by the very sins Paul listed, but they were transformed by the grace of God. This transformation involves three vital theological realities:

1. You were washed: This is the spiritual cleansing of the soul from the defilement of sin (Titus 3:5).
2. You were sanctified: This means being set apart by God for His holy purposes, beginning a new life of growth in holiness.
3. You were justified: This is a legal declaration where God counts a sinner as righteous because the perfect righteousness of Jesus Christ has been credited to them (Romans 5:1).

The reason anyone can inherit the kingdom is not because they have never sinned, but because they have been reconciled to God through Jesus Christ. We have all fallen short of God’s glory and deserve His wrath (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a sinless life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers full forgiveness to all who turn to Him.

You may feel the burden of your past or the struggle of your present, but the Gospel declares that your identity does not have to be defined by your sin. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, receiving the gift of eternal life and a place in His kingdom.

As an actionable step, I encourage you to spend time in prayer today, honestly confessing your needs to God. Ask Him to help you understand the depth of His love revealed in the cross. If you are not already part of a faithful local church, seek out a community of believers where the Word of God is preached and where you can find support in your walk with Christ. What is it that makes the promise of being "washed and justified" feel most urgent to you today?
Is it wise for Christian teens to be dating?

Whether it is wise for a Christian teenager to date depends heavily on their spiritual maturity, their reasons for dating, and their readiness to move toward the lifelong commitment of marriage. While the Bible does not explicitly mention dating, it provides clear principles for how we are to steward our affections and treat one another in holiness and honor (1 Thessalonians 4:3-5).

In the economy of God, romantic intimacy is a beautiful gift intended to be enjoyed within the covenant of marriage (Genesis 2:24; Hebrews 13:4). Because dating in our modern context is the primary path toward marriage, it is often unwise for a young person to enter a private, romantic relationship when marriage is a distant or impossible prospect. Engaging the heart's deepest affections without the ability to fulfill them in marriage can lead to unnecessary heartache and intense temptation (Song of Solomon 2:7).

Wisdom suggests that these years are a season for a different kind of growth. Scripture encourages the young to flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace, along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart (2 Timothy 2:22). This is a time to focus on your sanctification, which is your growth in holiness, and to learn how to serve the Lord without distraction (1 Corinthians 7:32-35).

If you are considering dating, reflect on these biblical guideposts:

1. The Purpose of Marriage: Since the biblical end of romance is marriage, ask yourself if you are at a stage in life where you can reasonably seek a spouse. If marriage is many years away, consider if a committed dating relationship is the best use of your time and emotional energy.

2. The Command for Purity: Christians are called to a standard of absolute purity. Our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). Intimate dating environments often make it difficult to maintain the "holiness and honor" required by God.

3. The Priority of the Local Church: Focus on building deep, godly friendships within the body of Christ. High-school and college years are wonderful opportunities to learn how to treat "younger women as sisters, in all purity" and "younger men as brothers" (1 Timothy 5:1-2).

If you feel a particular interest in someone, a wise next step is to speak with your parents or a trusted elder in your local church. Ask them to help you evaluate your heart and your readiness for such a responsibility. They can provide the accountability and perspective needed to ensure that Christ remains the center of your life and your relationships. 

Above all, remember that your ultimate identity and satisfaction are found in Jesus Christ alone. He is the one who truly knows you and loves you perfectly. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and look to Jesus, who died and rose again to reconcile you to God. In Him, you will find a love that surpasses any earthly romance. What is it that makes the idea of dating so important to you right now?
Why does God allow us to go through trials and tribulations?

A sincere concern here is how a loving, all-powerful Father could permit deep pain and struggle in the lives of those He calls His own. It is a question that touches the very heart of our relationship with God, and it is one the Bible addresses with both honesty and hope.

God allows trials because He uses them as a sovereign tool to refine our faith, produce spiritual endurance, and ultimately conform us to the image of His Son, Jesus Christ. Romans 8:28-29. In His perfect wisdom, God never wastes a tear or a season of difficulty, but employs them for our ultimate good and His eternal glory.

There are several biblical reasons why the Lord leads His children through the furnace of affliction:

First, trials test and prove the genuineness of our faith. Just as gold is purified by fire to remove dross, our faith is strengthened and shown to be real when it survives the heat of life's challenges. 1 Peter 1:6-7. When we continue to trust God despite our circumstances, it demonstrates that our hope is rooted in Him, not in our comfort.

Second, tribulations produce perseverance and maturity. James 1:2-4. Without resistance, we do not grow strong. God uses the weight of trials to build spiritual muscle, teaching us to depend more fully on His grace rather than our own strength. 2 Corinthians 12:9-10.

Third, suffering allows us to experience the comfort of God in a way that prosperity never could. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4. When we are brokenhearted, He draws near to us, and our experience of His mercy then equips us to comfort others who are walking through similar valleys.

Most importantly, our trials point us to the reality of the Gospel. We live in a world broken by sin, a reality that brings death, disease, and heartache. However, God, who is perfectly holy and just, did not leave us in our brokenness. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human flesh and suffered the ultimate trial. Jesus lived a perfect life, yet He took our sin upon Himself, dying an atoning death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, victorious over sin and death.

Through Christ's sacrifice, salvation is offered by grace alone through faith alone. This means that while we face trials now, they are "light and momentary" compared to the eternal weight of glory awaiting those who belong to Him. 2 Corinthians 4:17. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the peace that surpasses understanding.

What is the specific trial you are facing today that makes it hard to see God's hand?

As a practical step, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Psalms, such as Psalm 23 or Psalm 34. These passages show that God's people have always cried out to Him in distress, and they provide a language for your prayers. Take these verses to the Lord in prayer, asking Him not only for relief but for the grace to see what He is teaching you in the midst of the storm. Seek out a mature believer or an elder in your local church to pray with you, for we are called to bear one another's burdens. Galatians 6:2.
What does Deuteronomy 10:12 tell us God requires of us?

Deuteronomy 10:12 teaches that God requires His people to fear the LORD, to walk in all His ways, to love Him, and to serve Him with all their heart and soul. This verse serves as a beautiful summary of the heart of true worship and covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 10:12; Deuteronomy 10:13).

To fear the LORD is to have a holy, trembling awe of His majesty and a deep reverence for His holiness (Proverbs 1:7). Walking in His ways refers to the process of sanctification, where a believer aligns their daily conduct with God’s revealed character and commands (Micah 6:8). Love is the primary motive that fuels our obedience, ensuring our actions are not merely cold duty but a response to His grace (Mark 12:30). Finally, serving Him with all the heart and soul implies a total surrender of our will and affections to His supreme Lordship (Romans 12:1).

While these requirements are righteous and good, we must humbly acknowledge that in our own strength, we all fall short of this perfect standard (Romans 3:23). This is why the Gospel is such glorious news. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature through the virgin birth and lived a life of perfect obedience, fulfilling every requirement of the Law on our behalf. He loved the Father perfectly and served Him even unto His sacrificial death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God (Philippians 2:8).

You may wonder how anyone can satisfy such a high calling from a holy God. The truth is that salvation is not secured by our ability to keep these requirements, but by Christ’s historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection on the third day. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Through His victory over sin and death, God provides us with a new heart that desires to follow Him.

I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to choose one of the requirements mentioned in Deuteronomy 10:12 to focus on in prayer this week. Ask the Holy Spirit to increase your love for God, and seek out a local Bible-believing church where you can grow in fellowship and serve others in His name.
Is it okay to get tattoos if they are of a Christian nature?

The Bible does not provide a direct command that forbids or permits tattoos for believers today, which places this issue in the category of Christian liberty and personal conscience. While some point to the Old Testament law as a prohibition, others see the freedom we have in Christ to use art as a means of expressing faith.

In the Old Testament, Leviticus 19:28 says, "You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves: I am the LORD." Within its historical context, this command was given to set Israel apart from neighboring pagan nations who marked their bodies as part of idolatrous mourning rites or to show devotion to false gods. As believers under the New Covenant, we are no longer bound by the ceremonial laws of the Mosaic code, yet we still look to the underlying principles of holiness and devotion to God.

When considering a tattoo, even one with Christian themes, there are three primary biblical principles to weigh in your heart.

First, consider your motive and the glory of God. The Apostle Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 10:31, "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God." Ask yourself if the tattoo is truly intended to honor the Lord or if it stems from a desire for worldly attention or rebellion.

Second, remember the stewardship of your body. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 reminds us, "Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit, who is in you, whom you have received from God? You are not your own; you were bought at a price. Therefore honor God with your body." This principle of sanctification calls us to treat our physical selves with respect as a gift from our Creator.

Third, examine your conscience and the impact on others. Romans 14:23 warns that "everything that does not come from faith is sin." If you feel any hesitation or lack of peace from the Holy Spirit, it is better to wait. Additionally, consider whether your choice might cause a brother or sister in your local church to stumble or be unnecessarily offended, as we are called to walk in love toward one another.

A helpful next step would be to set aside a season of time to pray specifically about your reasons for wanting a tattoo and to seek the wisdom of a mature believer or an elder in your local church who knows you well. Reflect on whether this permanent mark will serve as a lasting testimony to the grace of Christ in your life or if your resources and body might be used for His kingdom in another way.
What day is the Sabbath, Saturday or Sunday?

In the Old Testament, the Sabbath is the seventh day of the week, which corresponds to Saturday. Under the New Covenant, Christians gather for worship and rest on the first day of the week, Sunday, which the Scriptures refer to as the Lord's Day.

The Sabbath was established by God at creation when He rested on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2-3), and it was later commanded to Israel as a holy day of rest (Exodus 20:8-11). However, because Jesus Christ rose from the grave on the first day of the week (Matthew 28:1; Mark 16:2), the early church began meeting on Sundays to celebrate His resurrection and the new creation He inaugurated (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2).

Within historic Christianity, there are different perspectives on how these days relate. Many believers, following the Reformed tradition, view Sunday as the "Christian Sabbath," believing the moral principle of one day in seven remains while the specific day shifted from the last to the first to honor Christ’s victory. Others see the Old Testament Sabbath as a ceremonial shadow that pointed to the spiritual rest we now have in Christ (Colossians 2:16-17; Hebrews 4:9-10). There are also faithful believers who continue to observe the seventh-day Sabbath.

Regardless of the specific day observed, the heart of the matter is that Jesus is our true Sabbath rest. He invites all who are weary to come to Him for the rest that our souls truly need (Matthew 11:28-30). We are encouraged to hold our convictions with charity toward others, as the Apostle Paul notes that one person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike (Romans 14:5-6).

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to commit yourself to a local, Bible-believing church where you can gather regularly for corporate worship on the Lord's Day. Use this time not merely as a break from labor, but as a deliberate rhythm to refocus your heart on the grace of God and the fellowship of the saints.
Why did God punish Pharaoh for Abram’s lie (Genesis 12:17)?

God intervened by striking Pharaoh’s household with plagues to protect the sanctity of marriage and the integrity of His covenant promises to Abram. While it may seem troubling that Pharaoh suffered for a situation he did not fully understand, we must look at this event through the lens of God's sovereign providence and His commitment to His Word.

You may wonder how it can be just for someone to suffer when they were the ones being deceived. This is a natural concern for fairness, but in the biblical narrative, these plagues were not necessarily a final judgment on Pharaoh’s character so much as a divine "stop" to a situation that would have derailed the history of redemption.

First, we see the priority of the Covenant. God had just promised Abram that he would become a great nation and that through him all the families of the earth would be blessed in Genesis 12:1 through Genesis 12:3. If Sarai, Abram's wife, had remained in Pharaoh's house, the purity of the family line through which the promised Savior would eventually come was at risk. God’s intervention was a shield over Sarai and a fulfillment of His promise to protect Abram, even when Abram’s own faith faltered.

Second, we see the reality of objective holiness. Marriage is a sacred institution established by God, and the taking of another man’s wife, even in ignorance, is an objective violation of that order. By striking Pharaoh’s house, God communicated the gravity of the situation. This act actually served as a mercy to Pharaoh in a hidden way, for it prevented him from committing a further sin that would have brought even greater judgment. We see a similar theme of God restraining someone from sin in the story of Abimelech in Genesis 20:6.

Finally, this account highlights the grace of God toward His frail children. Abram’s lie was a sin of cowardice and a failure to trust God’s protection. However, God is a faithful Shepherd who does not abandon His people when they stumble. As the Scripture says, if we are faithless, He remains faithful, for He cannot deny Himself in 2 Timothy 2:13. God did not bless Abram’s lie, but He did sovereignly overrule Abram’s sin to ensure His purposes were accomplished.

In your own life, you may feel that your past failures or lapses in faith have disqualified you from God’s care. Take heart, for the same God who protected Sarai and preserved Abram is the God who holds you fast today. He is the God of all grace who justifies the ungodly through faith in Jesus Christ alone, not based on our perfect performance but on His perfect faithfulness.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the life of Abram in the coming week. Note how often he fails and how consistently God restores him. Let this lead you into a time of prayer where you thank the Lord for His "restraining grace," the many times He has likely protected you from the full consequences of your own poor choices or the sins of others. Trust in Him, for His promises never fail.
Christmas Story — Day 4 — Do Christians worship Mary?

No, Christians do not worship Mary. According to the Holy Scriptures, worship is reserved for God alone, existing eternally as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

You may wonder why Mary receives so much attention during the Christmas season or why some traditions seem to hold her in such high esteem. It is a natural human response to want to honor those whom God has used in a significant way. However, the Bible is clear that while we honor her as a faithful servant, we do not accord her the divine adoration that belongs only to the Creator.

Jesus Himself commanded, "Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only" (Matthew 4:10). When an angel appeared to the Apostle John and John fell down to worship him, the angel quickly corrected him, saying, "Do not do that! I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers the prophets... Worship God!" (Revelation 22:9). If even the glorious angels refuse worship, Mary, a humble human being, would certainly do the same.

Mary is rightly called "blessed among women" (Luke 1:42) because she was sovereignly chosen by God to be the mother of the Lord Jesus Christ in His incarnation. She was the "theotokos," the one who carried the Son of God in His human nature. Yet Mary herself recognized her own need for a Savior, exclaiming, "My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior" (Luke 1:46-47). Only a sinner needs a Savior, and by calling God her Savior, Mary acknowledged her own dependence on the grace of God through the work of her Son.

In the history of the church, a distinction is often made between "latria," which is the supreme worship offered to God alone, and "dulia," which is a form of honor or veneration given to saints. However, from a biblical and Reformed perspective, we must be careful not to let honor slide into a religious devotion that belongs to Christ. There is only "one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5).

Mary’s own words provide the best guidance for how we should view her. At the wedding in Cana, she pointed the servants away from herself and toward Jesus, saying, "Do whatever He tells you" (John 2:5). To truly honor Mary is to follow her example of humble submission to the Lordship of Jesus Christ.

If you find yourself looking for comfort or an advocate, remember that Jesus Christ is our perfect High Priest who "is able to save completely those who come to God through Him, since He always lives to intercede for them" (Hebrews 7:25).

As a practical next step in your study of the Christmas story, I encourage you to read Mary's song of praise, often called the Magnificat, in Luke 1:46-55. Notice how she focuses entirely on the character, mercy, and power of God rather than on herself.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from placing your full trust in Jesus as your only Savior and Mediator?
What is Kemetic spirituality / Kemetism?

Kemetic spirituality, or Kemetism, is a contemporary revival of the ancient Egyptian religion that seeks to reconstruct its rituals, beliefs, and worship in the modern world. It centers on the veneration of the Netjeru (deities such as Osiris, Isis, or Ra) and the pursuit of Ma’at, a concept representing cosmic balance, truth, and justice.

While the desire for order and historical connection is understandable, the Bible teaches that there is only one true and living God who created the heavens and the earth (Genesis 1:1). Scripture identifies the worship of other deities as idolatry, noting that while human hands may craft or revive these systems, they lack the power to save or provide eternal life (Psalm 96:5; Isaiah 45:20).

You may wonder if connecting with an ancient heritage or a structured moral code can provide the peace and purpose your soul craves. It is a deeply human longing to find a sense of belonging and a way to live rightly. However, the truest balance and the only perfect justice are found in the character of the God of the Bible.

The Gospel reveals that God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are all separated from Him (Romans 3:23). No amount of human effort or ritual can restore this broken relationship. Because God is merciful, He stepped into history in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus is truly God and truly man, and He lived the perfect life of Ma’at (truth and justice) that we could never achieve. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Jesus Christ is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. Compare the historical person of Jesus, who entered time and space to seek and save the lost, with the reconstructed myths of ancient systems. Reach out to a local, Bible teaching church to discuss these truths with a pastor who can walk alongside you.
Should Christians be concerned about the idea of Sharia Law?

It is natural for a believer to feel a sense of concern when considering any system of governance that claims total authority over both the public square and the private conscience, especially when that system is rooted in a worldview that denies the Lordship of Jesus Christ. You may wonder how a society can remain truly free or how the Gospel can be unhindered when a legal framework like Sharia is introduced, which seeks to regulate religious practice through civil penalty.

From a biblical and historical perspective, Christians should be concerned about Sharia Law for several significant reasons. First, there is a fundamental theological conflict regarding the nature of salvation and the law. Sharia is a comprehensive legal system based on the Quran and Hadith that emphasizes outward submission to a set of divine laws to earn favor with God. In contrast, the Bible teaches that "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes" (Romans 10:4). We are not justified by our adherence to a legal code, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). To elevate any other religious law as a standard for society is to promote a system that lacks the power to change the human heart, which only the Gospel of Jesus Christ can do.

Second, there is a vital concern regarding religious liberty and the "freedom of conscience." Historically, followers of the Reformed Baptist tradition have championed the idea that God alone is Lord of the conscience and has left it free from the doctrines and commandments of men which are in any way contrary to His Word. Sharia Law often mandates civil punishments for what it deems as blasphemy or apostasy, which directly contradicts the biblical principle that faith cannot be coerced by the state. Jesus Himself declared, "My kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36), indicating that His followers do not use the sword of the state to enforce spiritual devotion. While we recognize that "the authorities that exist have been established by God" (Romans 13:1) for the punishment of evildoers and the praise of those who do good, we also believe that the state's jurisdiction does not extend to the internal worship of the soul.

Practically, the introduction of Sharia principles into a legal system often results in the marginalization of Christians and other non-Muslims, making it difficult to fulfill the Great Commission and live "peaceful and quiet lives in all godliness and holiness" (1 Timothy 2:2). However, our concern should never turn into a spirit of fear or a heart of hostility. We remember that our ultimate citizenship is in heaven, and no earthly legal system can thwart the sovereign purposes of God.

As a pastoral next step, I encourage you to commit to praying regularly for your Muslim neighbors and for the leaders of your nation. Ask God for the wisdom to engage in the public square with both "truth and grace," and for the courage to stand firm in your convictions while showing the love of Christ to those who hold different views.

If you are troubled by the shifting shadows of this world’s laws, I invite you to look to the One whose law is perfect and whose yoke is easy. We have all fallen short of God’s holy standard, but Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. He rose again in victory to offer us eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that most concerns you about the future of your community?
What is the meaning of the foxes in Song of Solomon 2:15?

In Song of Solomon 2:15, the foxes represent the subtle threats, hidden sins, or small distractions that undermine the flourishing of a godly relationship or the believer’s spiritual health. These "little foxes" are the seemingly insignificant compromises that, if left unchecked, can destroy the "vineyard" of love and devotion during its most tender season of growth.

Theologically, this imagery serves as a vital lesson for our sanctification. While we may be vigilant against obvious or "large" sins, we are often less guarded against the "little foxes" such as pride, a complaining spirit, or the neglect of private prayer. The text calls us to "catch" these intruders, which reflects the biblical necessity of the mortification of sin, the practice of putting to death the sinful habits of the heart by the power of the Holy Spirit (Romans 8:13; Colossians 3:5). Just as a vineyard in bloom is fragile and requires protection to produce a harvest, our intimacy with Christ and our harmony in marriage require constant vigilance against small resentments or worldly preoccupations that can sour the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22–23).

Beloved, do not be deceived by the size of these intruders. It is rarely a single, massive blow that ruins a spiritual life or a marriage, but rather the cumulative effect of many "little foxes" that are allowed to roam free. To "catch" them means to identify them through the light of Scripture and deal with them through honest confession and repentance before they take deep root.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to take a "vineyard inventory" during your time with the Lord today. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any small area of compromise or a "little" sin you have been excusing. When He brings something to mind, do not minimize it, but bring it directly to Christ in prayer, asking for His grace to remove it and for His strength to guard your heart.

Our hope in this struggle is not found in our own strength, but in the faithfulness of Jesus Christ, the Great Shepherd. He is the one who began a good work in you and is faithful to bring it to completion (Philippians 1:6). If you feel overwhelmed by the "foxes" in your life, remember that Christ has already won the ultimate victory over sin and death through His resurrection. You can trust in Him alone for the power to live a life that is fruitful and pleasing to God (John 15:4–5; Song of Solomon 2:15).
What is the false cause fallacy?

The false cause fallacy is a logical error where one incorrectly identifies the cause of an event, often by assuming that because two things happen together or in sequence, one must have caused the other. This mistake is frequently seen in the "post hoc" error, where someone believes that because event B followed event A, event A must be the reason B occurred.

In our walk of faith, we must be careful not to apply this fallacy to our understanding of God's providence, which is His purposeful and sovereign guidance of all things in the universe. We often fall into this trap when we assume a specific trial is a direct punishment for a particular sin, or that a worldly success is a definitive sign of God's approval of our own merit. Job’s friends famously committed this error, wrongly concluding that Job’s immense suffering was proof of hidden wickedness (Job 4:7-8).

Jesus specifically corrected this line of thinking when His disciples encountered a man born blind. They asked, "Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?" (John 9:2). The disciples assumed a false cause, but Jesus answered, "Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him" (John 9:3).

While it is true that God is the primary cause behind all things, we must exercise humility and discernment before claiming to know His specific reasons for every circumstance. Instead of seeking to decode the "why" of every event through human logic, we are called to trust in the character of God, knowing that He works all things together for the good of those who love Him and are called according to His purpose (Romans 8:28).

As an actionable step, when you face an unexpected trial or see a brother or sister struggling, refrain from immediate speculation about the cause. Instead, go to God in prayer, asking for the wisdom to respond with faithfulness and the grace to support others without passing uninformed judgment (James 1:5; Romans 12:15).
Is it a sin to share copyrighted material on the internet?

Sharing copyrighted material without the legal right or the owner's permission is a sin because it constitutes theft and a refusal to submit to the authorities God has placed over us. The Bible teaches that we must respect the property of others and obey the laws of the land.

The eighth commandment states, "You shall not steal" (Exodus 20:15). This moral law protects not only physical possessions but also the fruit of a person's labor and creativity, often called intellectual property. When you distribute material that belongs to someone else without their consent, you are taking what is not yours and potentially depriving the creator of their rightful compensation. Furthermore, Scripture commands every person to "be subject to the governing authorities" (Romans 13:1). Since copyright laws are the established legal standard, a Christian's violation of these laws is a direct act of disobedience against God's ordained order (Romans 13:2; 1 Peter 2:13-14).

In your walk of progressive sanctification, the Holy Spirit works to bring every area of your life, including your digital habits, into subjection to Christ. It is easy to view digital sharing as a victimless or minor issue because it is so common, but God calls His children to a life of total integrity. We are told to "renounce disgraceful, underhanded ways" (2 Corinthians 4:2) and to "lead a peaceful and quiet life, godly and dignified in every way" (1 Timothy 2:2). True love for your neighbor involves honoring their hard work and their right to control how their creations are used.

As a practical step of repentance, I encourage you to audit your digital devices and remove any material that was obtained or shared illegally. If you have benefited from someone's work without paying for it, consider making restitution by purchasing a legal copy. When you pray, you might ask the Lord to sharpen your conscience and to help you find satisfaction in being a good steward of the resources He has legally provided for you.
What does the Bible say about Christian fathers?

The Bible presents the Christian father as the spiritual head of his household, called to represent the character of God the Father through sacrificial love, diligent instruction, and compassionate discipline. This role is a sacred stewardship where a man is tasked with pointing his children toward the gospel of Jesus Christ.

Scripture places a primary emphasis on the father's duty to provide spiritual leadership. In the Old Testament, fathers were commanded to keep the words of God in their hearts and to teach them diligently to their children at all times, whether sitting at home or walking along the road (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). This duty continues in the New Testament, where the Apostle Paul instructs fathers not to provoke their children to anger but to bring them up in the training and instruction of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4). This requires a father to exercise spiritual headship, which is the humble, Christlike responsibility to lead his family in holiness and the knowledge of the Truth.

A Christian father is also called to mirror the tender compassion of God. Just as a father has compassion on his children, so the Lord has compassion on those who fear Him (Psalm 103:13). While discipline is a necessary part of love, as a father corrects the son in whom he delights (Proverbs 3:11-12), it must be administered with a gentle spirit. Paul warns fathers not to embitter or discourage their children, which can happen through harshness or unrealistic expectations (Colossians 3:21).

In his practical life, a father is responsible for the physical and moral well-being of his home. A man who does not provide for his own household is described as having denied the faith (1 Timothy 5:8). However, the ultimate provision he offers is a godly example. By living a life of integrity, a father provides a secure foundation for his children (Proverbs 20:7).

If you are a father feeling the weight of this calling, remember that you are not expected to be perfect, but to be faithful. Your children do not need a flawless father as much as they need a father who points them to a flawless Savior. One actionable step you can take today is to establish a simple rhythm of family worship. Set aside ten minutes this evening to read a short passage of Scripture with your family and pray specifically for each of your children, asking God to give them a heart that loves Him. Trust that God, who is the perfect Father, will supply the grace and wisdom you need as you seek to honor Him in your home (James 1:5).
What was the process of deciding on the Old Testament canon?

The Old Testament canon was recognized by the community of faith as God progressively revealed His Word through His prophets and leaders. It was not a process of human beings granting authority to certain books, but rather the faithful acknowledgment of the inherent authority and divine inspiration already present in the texts God had provided.

The formation of the canon was guided by the providence of God through the history of Israel. The books were generally categorized into three sections known as the Tanakh: the Law (Torah), the Prophets (Nevi’im), and the Writings (Ketuvim). These writings were accepted as they were written because the authors were known to be spokesmen for God, such as Moses or the later prophets. By the time of the second century before Christ, the Hebrew Scriptures were widely accepted in the form we have them today.

The most significant confirmation of the Old Testament canon for the Christian is the testimony of Jesus Christ Himself. In His teachings, Jesus frequently referred to the Scriptures as the final and authoritative Word of God. He specifically alluded to the entire scope of the Hebrew canon when He spoke of the martyrs from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, which spans from the first book (Genesis) to the last book in the Hebrew arrangement (2 Chronicles) as recorded in Luke 11:51 and Matthew 23:35.

Jesus also explicitly affirmed the three-fold division of the Old Testament when He told His disciples that everything written about Him in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms must be fulfilled as noted in Luke 24:44. Because our Lord Jesus treated these specific books as the infallible and authoritative Word of God, we receive them with the same humble submission.

The Old Testament is not merely a collection of ancient national literature; it is the breath of God (theopneustos) given to prepare the way for the coming of the Messiah. It reveals God's holiness, our desperate need for a Savior due to our sin, and the unfolding promise of redemption through Jesus Christ.

As you consider the wonder of how God has preserved His Word for you, I encourage you to begin a consistent reading plan that takes you through both the Old and New Testaments. Seeing the unity of the Bible will deepen your trust in God's sovereign care over history and His personal care for your soul. You might consider starting with the Psalms to see how the Old Testament saints poured out their hearts to God in every season of life as described in Psalm 1:1–3 and Psalm 119:105.
Is cyber-sex / phone sex a sin?

Yes, cyber-sex and phone sex are sinful because they violate God's design for sexual intimacy and involve the cultivation of lust, which the Lord Jesus taught is a violation of the heart.

God created sex as a good and holy gift to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman, as seen in Genesis 2:24 and Hebrews 13:4. Any sexual activity outside of this sacred bond, whether physical or digital, falls under the biblical category of sexual immorality, or "porneia." The Lord Jesus raised the standard of holiness by explaining that sexual sin is not merely an outward act of the body but a condition of the heart. He stated, "But I tell you that anyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart" in Matthew 5:28. Cyber-sex and phone sex rely on the stimulation of illicit desires and the use of another person as an object for self-gratification, which contradicts the call for believers to "flee from sexual immorality" in 1 Corinthians 6:18.

You may wonder if these acts are "lesser" sins because they do not involve physical touch, yet the Bible teaches that our bodies and minds belong to God. To engage in these activities is to provide a "hint of sexual immorality," which Ephesians 5:3 warns should not even be named among the saints. This struggle often stems from a deep human longing for intimacy and pleasure, but these digital substitutes are "broken cisterns" that cannot hold the water of true satisfaction found in Christ alone, according to Jeremiah 2:13.

The weight of this truth can feel heavy, but there is immense hope in the Gospel. God is holy and His standards are perfect, and we have all failed to meet them, falling into various forms of sin and rebellion. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Through His sacrifice, you can be justified, declared righteous before God, not by your own works but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of this sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for complete forgiveness and a new life.

As a practical step in your walk of sanctification, I encourage you to bring this struggle into the light by confessing it to a trusted elder or a mature believer in your local church. Bringing sin into the light breaks its power, and having an accountability partner can help you "make no provision for the flesh, to fulfill its lusts" as exhorted in Romans 13:14. What is it that makes it difficult for you to let go of this particular habit and trust God's design instead?
Do unbelievers immediately go to hell when they die?

The Bible teaches that when an unbeliever dies, their soul immediately enters a state of conscious separation from God and undergoes a particular judgment. Scripture indicates that there is no second chance for salvation after death, as Hebrews 9:27 declares that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment. 

In the account of the rich man and Lazarus, Jesus describes an unbeliever who died and was immediately "in torment" in Hades, which is the intermediate state of the dead (Luke 16:22-23). While this soul is experiencing the weight of God's righteous displeasure, this is distinct from the final, eternal state. At the end of the age, there will be a bodily resurrection of both the just and the unjust. Those who rejected Christ will stand before the Great White Throne to be judged according to their deeds, and because their names are not found in the Book of Life, they will be cast into the Lake of Fire (Revelation 20:11-15).

This is a sobering reality that reflects the absolute holiness of God and the gravity of our sin. God is perfectly just and must punish rebellion, yet He is also rich in mercy. We are all separated from God by our sin and deserve His judgment, but God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to save us. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our own efforts but is a gift of God's grace received through faith in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today? As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the heart of Christ for sinners, and consider visiting a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word of God preached.
How should Christians react to persecution against the LGBTQ community?

Christians should respond to persecution against any group, including the LGBTQ community, with a firm commitment to the inherent dignity of every human being as an image-bearer of God and a steadfast witness to the truth of the Gospel. Because every person is created by God in His own image, any act of unjust violence, malice, or dehumanization is a sin against the Creator Himself (Genesis 1:27; James 3:9).

You may wonder how a believer can remain faithful to biblical standards of morality while also advocating for the safety and protection of those living in opposition to those standards. The heart of the Christian response is found in the command to love our neighbors as ourselves (Matthew 22:39). This love does not mean we affirm or celebrate behaviors that the Bible clearly identifies as sinful, such as same-sex sexual relations (Romans 1:26-27; 1 Corinthians 6:9-10). Rather, true love seeks the physical well-being of our neighbors and, most importantly, their eternal reconciliation with God.

We must remember that we were all once alienated from God by our own sin, yet He showed His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Therefore, the Christian cannot participate in or ignore the mistreatment of others. We are called to "do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God" (Micah 6:8). We oppose cruelty and advocate for the protection of all people from harm, following the example of Christ who was full of both grace and truth (John 1:14).

The Gospel offers the only true hope and transformation for every human heart. We believe that God is holy and just, and that our sin separates us from Him. However, out of His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers forgiveness and a new identity to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of everlasting life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to pray for the courage to stand against injustice in your community while also seeking opportunities to share the hope of the Gospel with gentleness and respect (1 Peter 3:15). If you are struggling with how to navigate these conversations, consider seeking counsel from a faithful elder in a local, Bible-believing church.
How did Satan and other angels sin if heaven is sinless?

Sin originated as a voluntary act of rebellion within the hearts of certain angels who were created holy but were capable of falling. While heaven is the dwelling place of God’s holiness, these celestial beings were placed in a state of voluntary obedience, and through pride, they chose to reject their Creator’s authority.

You may wonder how a perfect environment could ever give rise to such darkness and rebellion. It is a profound mystery that has weighed on the hearts of many who seek to understand the nature of God’s holiness and the origin of evil.

The Scripture teaches that sin began in the heart of a high ranking celestial being, often identified as Satan, who was created perfect in beauty and wisdom. Although he dwelt in the presence of God, he was not satisfied with being a creature, he desired the glory and authority belonging only to the Creator. This is described in Isaiah 14:13–14, where he says in his heart, "I will ascend to heaven; I will raise my throne above the stars of God; I will sit on the mount of assembly, in the remote parts of the north. I will ascend above the tops of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High." This internal turning from God to self is the root of all sin.

Other angels followed him in this rebellion, failing to keep their original positions of authority and instead abandoning their proper dwelling (Jude 1:6). God did not spare these angels when they sinned but cast them into darkness (2 Peter 2:4). This rebellion did not occur because of a flaw in God or a stain in heaven itself, rather, it demonstrates that these angels were moral agents who, in their initial state of probation, possessed the capacity to choose between humble submission and proud autonomy. Those who fell did so by a deliberate exercise of their own will, while the "elect angels" were preserved by God’s grace to remain in their holy state (1 Timothy 5:21).

This rebellion in the heavenly realm eventually reached our world, leading to the fall of humanity and the brokenness we experience today. Yet, we are not without hope. The same God who is holy is also infinitely merciful. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to undo the works of the devil and reconcile sinners to Himself. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His total victory over the powers of darkness.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you reflect on these deep truths, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the book of Colossians. It beautifully describes the supremacy of Christ over all things, both in heaven and on earth, and His work in making peace through His blood shed on the cross (Colossians 1:15–20).
What is the greatest sin?

The greatest sin is the failure to love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, as this is the fulfillment of the first and greatest commandment. Matthew 22:37-38. At its root, this sin is expressed through unbelief, which is the willful rejection of the glory, authority, and grace of God.

While we often measure the weight of sin by its visible impact on society, the Bible views sin primarily as a vertical rebellion against a holy and sovereign Creator. To not love God is to commit the ultimate idolatry, placing the creature or the self above the Creator who is blessed forever. Romans 1:25. This state of unbelief is particularly grave because it rejects the only remedy God has provided for our salvation. Jesus taught that the Holy Spirit would convict the world of sin because they do not believe in Him. John 16:8-9. Without faith in Christ, a person remains in their sins, separated from the life and light of God.

You may feel the heavy burden of your own failures or wonder if some specific action has forever closed the door to God's mercy. It is a common and heavy concern to fear that one's rebellion is too deep for God to reach. However, the Scripture reminds us that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more. Romans 5:20. God is perfectly just and must punish sin, yet He is also profoundly merciful. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, truly God and truly man, to bear the penalty for our law-breaking through His substitutionary death on the cross. By His bodily resurrection on the third day, He triumphed over death, offering reconciliation to all who believe.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the total forgiveness of your debts and the gift of new life. Romans 10:13. To follow Him, begin by speaking honestly to Him in prayer about your need for His grace and start reading the Gospel of John to see the beauty of the Savior. What is it that holds you back from resting in His finished work today?
What does the Bible say about sedition?

The Bible identifies sedition as the stirring up of rebellion, discord, or factions against established authority, categorizing it as a work of the flesh that is contrary to the Spirit of God. In Galatians 5:19-21, the Apostle Paul includes dissensions and factions, often translated as seditions, in a list of behaviors that characterize a life apart from Christ, warning that those who practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God.

This biblical stance is rooted in the doctrine of God's sovereignty. Scripture teaches that all legitimate authority is ultimately derived from God, who is the author of peace and not of confusion (1 Corinthians 14:33). Romans 13:1-2 explains that everyone must submit to governing authorities because they have been instituted by God. Therefore, to engage in sedition without a clear biblical mandate to obey God over man (Acts 5:29) is to resist the very order God has established for the restraint of evil and the promotion of the common good.

From a pastoral perspective, it is important to examine whether a desire for rebellion is fueled by a genuine love for justice or a spirit of pride and self-will. Believers are called to lead a peaceful and quiet life in all godliness and holiness (1 Timothy 2:2). As an actionable step, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray specifically for those in authority over you, as commanded in 1 Timothy 2:1-2, asking God to give them wisdom and to grant you a heart that trusts in His providential rule over all human affairs.

Ultimately, we must recognize that every human heart has a natural tendency toward sedition against our Creator. We have all at times desired to be our own masters, breaking God's holy law and seeking our own way. Yet, Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a life of perfect submission to the Father and died on a cross to pay the penalty for our rebellion. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, victorious over sin and death, and He now reigns as the King of Kings. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?
What is the International Christian Church (ICC), and what do they believe?

The International Christian Church (ICC), often called the SoldOut Discipling Movement, is a religious organization founded in 2006 by Kip McKean after his departure from the International Churches of Christ (ICOC). It is characterized by a "restorationist" view, claiming to be the only true movement of God and the only legitimate recovery of the New Testament church in the modern era.

While the ICC uses many biblical terms, several of their core teachings and practices deviate significantly from historic, orthodox Christianity.

**Baptismal Regeneration and Salvation**
The ICC teaches that water baptism by immersion is the precise moment an individual receives the forgiveness of sins and the Holy Spirit. They believe that without baptism in their specific context, one is not saved. This view conflicts with the biblical doctrine of **justification** by grace alone through faith alone. The Bible teaches that we are made right with God through faith in Christ before any ritual act (Romans 3:28; Romans 4:4–5; Ephesians 2:8–9). Baptism is a beautiful outward sign of an inward reality, but it is not the cause of our salvation.

**Exclusivism**
The movement holds to a strict exclusivism, teaching that they are the only "true" church. They often require individuals who were previously baptized in other Christian denominations to be "rebaptized" into the ICC to be considered truly saved. This denies the biblical reality of the universal church, which consists of all people across time and geography who have truly repented and placed their faith in Jesus Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12–13; Revelation 7:9).

**Authoritarian Discipling**
A central feature of the ICC is their "one over one" discipling hierarchy. Every member is assigned a "discipler" who often exerts significant control over the member's personal life, including finances, dating, and daily schedules. While the Bible encourages elder leadership and mutual accountability, it also warns against lording authority over the flock and protects **Christian liberty** (Matthew 20:25–26; 1 Peter 5:2–3; Galatians 5:1). True discipleship should point a person to the Lordship of Christ, not the lordship of a human mentor.

**The Hope of the Gospel**
It is understandable to be drawn to a group that calls for total commitment and radical devotion, as many are weary of lukewarm spirituality. However, our security must rest in the finished work of Jesus Christ, not in our membership in a specific organization or our performance of "disciple" works. 

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift received by grace through faith in Him alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in the sufficiency of His grace today?

**A Pastoral Next Step**
If you are involved with or considering the ICC, I encourage you to spend time reading the Book of Galatians. Notice how the Apostle Paul warns against any "gospel" that adds human requirements to the grace of God. I also suggest seeking out a local church that faithfully preaches the Bible and emphasizes the sufficiency of Christ's work rather than the authority of a human hierarchy. You may wish to speak with a mature, biblically grounded pastor outside of the ICC to discuss these distinctions further.
Do some Christmas traditions have pagan origins?

It is historically true that several traditions we associate with Christmas have roots in or parallels to ancient pagan festivals. For example, the choice of December 25 was likely influenced by the Roman festival of Sol Invictus, which marked the birth of the unconquered sun. Likewise, the use of evergreen greenery was common in various ancient northern European cultures to symbolize life during the cold winter solstice.

However, for the believer, these historical connections do not change the glorious reality of the Incarnation. We celebrate the historical fact that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, was born of the Virgin Mary to save His people from their sins (Matthew 1:21; John 1:14). In the early centuries of the church, Christians often reclaimed these cultural dates and symbols to testify to the supremacy of Christ. Where the world saw the return of the physical sun, the church proclaimed the arrival of the Sun of Righteousness (Malachi 4:2).

The question of whether to participate in these traditions falls under the category of Christian liberty. Within historic Christianity, faithful believers hold different views. Some choose to avoid these customs altogether because of their non-Christian origins, desiring to keep their worship as pure as possible. Others choose to use these traditions as a joyful means to celebrate the birth of the Savior, believing that the original pagan meanings have been stripped away and replaced by the truth of the Gospel. Scripture teaches that one person considers one day more sacred than another, while another considers every day alike, and each one should be fully convinced in his own mind (Romans 14:5).

What matters most is not the origin of a tree or a date, but whether your heart has been reconciled to God through His Son. The core of the Gospel is that we are all sinners separated from a holy God, but in His great love, God sent Jesus Christ to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 5:8). Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the first two chapters of the Gospel of Luke this week. Let the focus of your heart be on the wonder of God's grace in sending His Son, and seek to honor Him in all your celebrations (Colossians 3:17). What holds you back from fully trusting in Him today?
Where does the Old Testament predict the coming of Christ?

The Old Testament predicts the coming of Christ through hundreds of specific prophecies and historical patterns that establish His lineage, birth, ministry, death, and resurrection. These promises began immediately after the Fall in the Garden of Eden and continued through the prophets until the close of the Old Testament canon.

In Genesis 3:15, God provides the first glimpse of the Gospel, often called the Protoevangelium, by promising that the offspring of the woman would crush the head of the serpent. This points directly to Christ's ultimate victory over Satan through His work on the cross. Later, the prophet Isaiah provides remarkable detail regarding the nature of the Messiah. Isaiah 7:14 foretells that the Lord would give a sign where a virgin would conceive and bear a son named Immanuel, which means God with us. This prophecy confirms the miracle of the incarnation where Jesus, being truly God and truly man, entered our world to save His people.

The specifics of His earthly origin are found in Micah 5:2, which identifies the small town of Bethlehem as the birthplace of the One whose origins are from of old, from ancient times. Regarding His mission, Isaiah 53:5-6 describes the heart of the Gospel through penal substitution, stating that He was pierced for our transgressions and crushed for our iniquities. This passage clarifies that the Messiah would not merely be a political deliverer but a suffering servant who would take the divine wrath we deserved upon Himself to reconcile us to God.

Even the manner of His death was described centuries before the practice of crucifixion was common. Psalm 22:16-18 speaks of His hands and feet being pierced and soldiers casting lots for His clothing. These are historical facts recorded by God so that when Christ arrived, His identity would be unmistakable to all who seek the truth.

Jesus Himself taught that all the Scriptures point to Him in Luke 24:27. This reveals that the Old Testament is the foundation of a single, consistent story of redemption. If you are seeking to understand how these ancient promises find their fulfillment in Jesus, a wonderful next step is to read the Gospel of Matthew alongside the book of Isaiah. As you see the promises meet their historical fulfillment, you might consider what holds you back from trusting in the One who was promised from the beginning.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.
What is spiritual adultery?

Spiritual adultery is the betrayal of our sacred covenant with God by placing our ultimate affection, trust, or worship in anything other than Him. Just as physical adultery breaks the marriage vow, spiritual adultery occurs when the heart wanders away from its true Husband to pursue the idols or "lovers" of this world.

Throughout the Scriptures, God uses the beautiful and intimate imagery of marriage to describe His relationship with His people. In the Old Testament, He was a husband to Israel, and in the New Testament, the Church is described as the Bride of Christ. Because God is holy and His love is exclusive, He is rightly "jealous" for the undivided devotion of His children. When we allow money, career, relationships, or even our own self-will to take the place that belongs only to God, we commit this grave sin of unfaithfulness.

The Apostle James speaks with startling clarity on this matter, saying, "You adulteresses! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore, whoever chooses to be a friend of the world renders himself an enemy of God" James 4:4. This "friendship with the world" does not mean being kind to neighbors, but rather adopting the world’s values, seeking its approval, and finding our satisfaction in its fleeting pleasures rather than in the living God.

The prophet Hosea provides a profound and heartbreaking illustration of this truth. God commanded Hosea to marry an unfaithful woman named Gomer to display how Israel had "played the harlot" by departing from the LORD Hosea 1:2. Yet, even in the face of such betrayal, the central message of Hosea is God’s pursuing, redeeming love. He calls His wayward people to return, promising to heal their waywardness and love them freely Hosea 14:4.

If you find that your heart has grown cold toward Christ and warm toward the world, do not despair, for our God is rich in mercy. True repentance involves acknowledging that your wandering heart has grieved a loving Savior and turning back to Him with a sincere desire for restoration.

As a practical step, I encourage you to set aside time this week to read through the book of Hosea. As you read, reflect on the depths of God's patience and the height of His grace. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any "functional idols" in your life—things you feel you must have to be happy or secure. 

Take these matters directly to God in prayer. You do not need a formula; simply speak to Him as a child speaks to a Father, or a spouse speaks to a beloved, confessing your wandering and asking Him to tether your heart to His truth. You may also find it helpful to seek out a mature believer or an elder in your local church to pray with you, for "the prayer of a righteous person has great power" James 5:16.

The Gospel is the only cure for a wandering heart. Remember that Jesus Christ, the perfect Husband, gave His very life to wash His Bride and make her holy Ephesians 5:25-27. His faithfulness never wavers, even when ours does, and His arms remain open to all who come to Him in faith.
How can we rely on the power of God?

To rely on the power of God is to acknowledge our own human frailty while resting entirely upon His sovereign strength and the grace provided through Jesus Christ. This reliance is an active, daily trust in the Holy Spirit to accomplish in and through us what we are utterly incapable of doing for ourselves (Zechariah 4:6; Philippians 2:13).

The first step in relying on God's power is the humble recognition of our own weakness. The Apostle Paul learned that God's power is actually brought to completion in our infirmity, leading him to boast in his weaknesses so that the power of Christ might rest upon him (2 Corinthians 12:9). When we stop trying to manufacture spiritual results through our own intellect or willpower, we create the necessary space for God to work. This is the heart of the Christian life, recognizing that apart from Christ, we can do nothing (John 15:5).

We connect to this divine power through what are often called the means of grace, which are the tools God has provided to strengthen His children. These include:

1. The Word of God: Scripture is not merely information but is living and active, serving as the primary way God speaks to and directs His people (Hebrews 4:12).
2. Prayer: Through prayer, we express our dependence on God and lay hold of His promises. It is the sincere cry of a heart that knows it cannot survive the day without its Shepherd (Hebrews 4:16).
3. The Local Church: God often manifests His power through the encouragement, accountability, and preaching found within the body of believers (Hebrews 10:24–25).

Ultimately, the greatest demonstration of God's power is found in the Gospel. We were once dead in our sins, separated from a holy and just God who cannot overlook transgression. However, out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was bodily raised from the dead on the third day. Through this victory, He conquered sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8–9).

If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. It is in this initial act of faith that we receive the Holy Spirit, who provides the power for progressive sanctification, the ongoing process of being made more like Jesus.

As a practical step this week, I encourage you to begin each morning by reading one Psalm and specifically asking God in prayer to provide the strength you need for the tasks ahead. What is one specific area of your life right now where you feel your own strength is failing?
What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled the law, but did not abolish it?

To fulfill the law means that Jesus Christ perfectly met every righteous demand of God’s commands through His life and bore the full penalty for our failure to keep them through His death. When Jesus said He did not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets, He meant He did not come to set them aside as irrelevant or wrong, but rather to bring them to their intended goal and completion, as recorded in Matthew 5:17 through Matthew 5:18.

There are three primary ways we see this fulfillment in the person of Jesus:

First, Jesus fulfilled the law through His active obedience. While every other human being has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God, as stated in Romans 3:23, Jesus lived a life of flawless moral perfection. He loved God with all His heart and His neighbor as Himself, successfully keeping the law in every thought, word, and deed. Because He did this, His righteousness can be credited to those who trust in Him, as explained in Romans 8:3 through Romans 8:4.

Second, Jesus fulfilled the law through His passive obedience, which refers to His willingness to suffer the law’s penalty. The law rightfully demands death for sin, but Christ took that curse upon Himself on the cross. He satisfied the justice of God by acting as a substitute for sinners. This is what the Apostle Paul means in Galatians 3:13 when he says that Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us.

Third, Jesus fulfilled the ceremonial aspects of the law. The Old Testament system of animal sacrifices, temple rituals, and dietary laws were "shadows" intended to point toward a greater reality. Jesus is the final Lamb of God and the true Temple. Because the substance has arrived in Christ, the shadows are no longer observed by believers, yet they are fulfilled rather than discarded, as described in Hebrews 10:1 and Colossians 2:16 through Colossians 2:17.

The law remains a reflection of God’s holy character. While it can no longer condemn the believer, it continues to guide us in how to love God and others. You may wonder if you can ever be "good enough" to please a holy God, but the Gospel is the good news that you do not have to be. We are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the book of Romans, specifically Romans 3 through Romans 5, and ask God in prayer to help you understand how Christ’s righteousness is given to us by faith alone. Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

The Major Prophets and Minor Prophets are the two primary groupings of prophetic books in the Old Testament, distinguished by the length of the books rather than the significance of the prophets or the importance of their messages. The Major Prophets consist of five books: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel. These writings are generally longer and provide a sweeping, detailed view of God’s judgment, His sovereignty over the nations, and His glorious promises of future restoration.

The Minor Prophets, often referred to in the Hebrew tradition as "The Twelve," comprise twelve shorter books: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. While their writings are brief, they are packed with the same divine authority and spiritual weight as the longer books. They frequently focus on specific issues of social justice, spiritual adultery, and the coming "Day of the Lord."

In both groups, we see the unfolding of God’s redemptive history. These men were moved by the Holy Spirit to speak God’s truth to a wayward people, as Peter writes, "For no prophecy was ever borne of human impulse; rather, men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit" (2 Peter 1:21). Their ultimate purpose was to call the people to repentance and to point forward to the person and work of Jesus Christ, who is the fulfillment of all prophetic revelation. The author of Hebrews explains, "On many occasions and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers through the prophets, but in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son" (Hebrews 1:1-2).

Every prophet, whether "major" or "minor" in length, highlights the holiness of God and the necessity of reconciliation with Him. To begin seeing the heart of God for the lost, I encourage you to read the book of Jonah this week. It is a short but powerful account showing that God’s mercy reaches even to those we might consider beyond hope.

This same mercy is offered to you today through the Gospel. The prophets spoke of a Savior who would bear the iniquities of His people (Isaiah 53:5-6), and Jesus Christ fulfilled these prophecies through His perfect life, sacrificial death on the cross, and bodily resurrection. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why believe in Jesus?

We believe in Jesus because He is the eternal Son of God who became man to save us from our sins and reconcile us to the Father through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection. He alone provides the definitive answer to the human condition of guilt and our universal longing for hope and eternal life.

You may wonder why a single historical figure from two thousand years ago should be the object of your total trust, especially when so many voices claim to have the truth. It is a significant thing to consider where one places their soul's security, and it is natural to desire a foundation that is historically firm and spiritually sufficient.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, the Creator of all things (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). However, we have all rebelled against Him, falling short of His glory and earning the penalty of spiritual and physical death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because God is both just and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to do what we could never do for ourselves. 

Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience, and then, in an act of penal substitutionary atonement, He took the wrath of God that we deserved upon Himself on the cross (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). His bodily resurrection on the third day is a historical fact that confirms His victory over sin and death and proves that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4).

Believing in Jesus is not merely about trying harder to be a good person. It is about justification by faith, where we are declared righteous before God, not because of our own works, but because of the merit of Christ credited to us (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 5:1). When you trust in Him, you are not just adopting a new religion, you are entering into a living relationship with the Creator who loves you and has redeemed you.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John. As you read, ask God to show you the truth of who Jesus is. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is a Bible Baptist Church, and what do Bible Baptists believe?

A Bible Baptist Church is a local congregation of believers who hold the Holy Scriptures as the final, absolute authority for all matters of faith and practice. While the name is used by various independent congregations, it generally signifies a commitment to the historic Baptist faith and a rejection of any human tradition or modern philosophy that contradicts the Word of God.

At the heart of their identity is the conviction that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. 2 Timothy 3:16.
