Bible Baptists believe in the one true God who eternally exists in three Persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Matthew 28:19. They teach that Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man, born of a virgin, and lived a sinless life. His death on the cross was a substitutionary sacrifice, meaning He died in the place of sinners to satisfy the righteous wrath of God against sin. 1 Peter 3:18.

Regarding salvation, Bible Baptists believe that all people are born in sin and are separated from God. Salvation is a work of God's grace and is received through faith alone in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ, not by any religious works or human efforts. Ephesians 2:8-9. They hold that once a person is truly saved, they are kept by the power of God and can never lose their salvation, a doctrine often called the perseverance of the saints. John 10:28.

Distinctively, they practice believer's baptism by immersion. This is an outward sign of an inward grace, symbolizing the believer's identification with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection. Romans 6:3-4. It is reserved for those who have first made a personal profession of faith in Jesus. Most Bible Baptist churches are congregational in governance and independent, meaning they are self governing and not under the authority of a denominational hierarchy, though they may associate with other like minded churches for missions and fellowship.

If you are considering what this means for your own soul, please understand the gravity of the Gospel message. God is holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him. Yet, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to visit a local Bible Baptist church or a faithful Gospel preaching church in your area. Ask the pastor for a copy of their "Statement of Faith" or "Confession of Faith." This document will give you a detailed look at how they interpret the Scriptures on specific doctrines. Most importantly, bring your own Bible and compare everything you hear taught with the written Word of God. Acts 17:11.
What is sexual ethics?

Sexual ethics is the study and application of moral principles regarding human sexuality, which, for the Christian, finds its ultimate and unchanging authority in the Holy Scriptures. It is the pursuit of understanding God’s design for our bodies and our desires so that we might honor Him in our conduct and our thoughts.

The foundation of biblical sexual ethics is established in the creation account, where God defines marriage as a covenantal union between one man and one woman (Genesis 2:24). Jesus Christ affirmed this original design, teaching that what God has joined together, no human should separate (Matthew 19:4-6). Consequently, the Bible teaches that sexual intimacy is a sacred gift intended exclusively for the marriage bed (Hebrews 13:4). Any sexual activity outside of this boundary—including premarital sex, adultery, or homosexual behavior—is identified as sexual immorality or "porneia," which falls short of God’s holy standard (1 Corinthians 6:9-10; 1 Thessalonians 4:3-5).

This ethical framework is not merely a list of prohibitions but a call to reflect the character of God. Our bodies are not our own; they are the temple of the Holy Spirit, bought with the precious blood of Christ (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). Therefore, sexual purity is an act of worship and a vital part of progressive sanctification, where we are being shaped into the image of Jesus.

In our modern world, these truths can often feel restrictive or out of step with cultural trends. It is understandable to feel the tension between personal desires and biblical commands. However, God’s laws are not meant to steal our joy but to protect us and lead us toward the flourishing He intended for humanity.

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of sexual sin or the brokenness of past experiences, please know there is profound hope and healing available. We all fall short of God’s holiness, and our sin separates us from Him. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life on our behalf. He died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. Through Christ, you can find complete justification and the power to live a transformed life.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the heart of Christ for those who struggle, and consider reaching out to a faithful local church where you can find a community of believers to walk alongside you in grace and accountability. What is your current understanding of God's grace in the midst of human struggle?
What is spiritual sickness? What does it mean to be spiritually sick?

Spiritual sickness is a condition of the soul where an individual is either fundamentally separated from God through sin or, in the case of a believer, experiencing a season of weakened fellowship and joy due to neglected communion with Christ. It manifests as a lack of desire for God's Word, a persistent struggle with unconfessed sin, or a coldness of heart toward the things of the Spirit.

In the broadest biblical sense, every human being is born with a terminal spiritual sickness called total depravity. This does not mean that every person is as wicked as they could possibly be, but rather that every part of our nature, our mind, will, and emotions, is corrupted by sin and turned away from God. Scripture teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, as noted in Romans 3:23. Without the intervening grace of God, we are not merely sick but spiritually dead in our trespasses, according to Ephesians 2:1.

For the person who has already trusted in Christ, spiritual sickness often feels like a heavy weight or a loss of the "joy of salvation" that King David prayed to have restored in Psalm 51:12. It can be caused by harboring secret sin, which affects even the physical body and the peace of the mind. David described this experience by saying that when he kept silent about his sin, his bones wasted away and God's hand was heavy upon him, in Psalm 32:3-4. It can also stem from lukewarmness, a state where a believer becomes self-sufficient and loses their fervent first love for Jesus, as warned in Revelation 3:15-17.

The beauty of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ identifies Himself as the Great Physician. He said that those who are healthy have no need of a physician, but those who are sick; He came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance, in Mark 2:17. To be spiritually sick is to be in the exact position to receive the healing mercy of God.

If you feel the weight of your sins or a distance from God, please know that Jesus Christ took the punishment for our spiritual rebellion upon Himself. Through His perfect life, His sacrificial death on the cross, and His bodily resurrection on the third day, He conquered sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone. When we trust in Him, He does not just provide a temporary remedy; He gives us a new heart and begins the lifelong process of sanctification, where the Holy Spirit gradually heals our character and conforms us to the image of Jesus.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

A practical next step for you would be to set aside time today to read Psalm 32 and Psalm 51, using these passages as a guide to talk honestly with God about the state of your heart. I also encourage you to reach out to a faithful, Bible teaching local church where you can find fellowship and pastoral care. What is it that feels most heavy on your heart today?
What is fellowship with God?

Fellowship with God is the intimate, shared life and spiritual communion that a believer enjoys with the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit through the person and work of Jesus Christ. It is a relationship of deep harmony and friendship where we walk in the light of His presence, characterized by mutual love, communication, and a shared purpose (1 John 1:3).

This fellowship was the original design for humanity in the Garden of Eden, but it was severed by our sin and rebellion (Genesis 3:8; Isaiah 59:2). Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin created a barrier that we could never cross on our own. However, out of His immense love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to restore what was lost. Jesus took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died on the cross as a sacrificial substitute to satisfy God’s righteous wrath against our sin. He was buried and then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Peter 3:18; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

True fellowship is not merely an emotional feeling or an intellectual agreement with facts about God. It is a vital, living connection rooted in our justification, which is being declared righteous before God by grace alone through faith alone. This leads to our adoption into His family, where we can truly call Him Father (Romans 5:1; Galatians 4:4-7). We experience this communion through the means of grace, which are the tools God has given us to grow in our relationship with Him. This includes reading and meditating on the Word of God, engaging in persistent prayer, and participating in the life of a local church (Acts 2:42; Hebrews 10:24-25).

You may wonder how an imperfect human being can truly relate to a holy and infinite God. The answer is found only in the Gospel. Fellowship is not earned by our efforts but is a gift received through Christ. I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the beginning of a new, eternal life in Him. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step to nurture this fellowship, I encourage you to set aside time each morning to read the Gospel of John and speak to God in prayer about what you find there, asking Him to help you know Him more deeply. Seeking out a local, Bible-teaching church will also provide you with the fellowship of other believers, which is essential for your spiritual growth (1 John 1:7).
What role does Iran play in the end times?

In the Holy Scriptures, Iran is primarily identified by its ancient names, Persia and Elam. Its most prominent role in end times prophecy is found in the book of Ezekiel, where Persia is listed as a key member of a vast coalition that gathers to oppose God's people in the latter years. Ezekiel 38:5 specifically names Persia alongside other nations that assemble under the leadership of a figure known as Gog.

Additionally, the prophet Jeremiah spoke concerning Elam, which comprises the western portion of modern day Iran. In Jeremiah 49:34-39, the Lord declares that He will bring judgment upon Elam but also promises that He will restore their fortunes in the days to come. These passages remind us of the absolute sovereignty of God over the rise and fall of every earthly power.

The interpretation of these events is a matter of eschatology, a secondary area of doctrine where faithful Christians often hold different perspectives. Many believers understand these prophecies as describing a literal, future military conflict involving modern nations. Others view these descriptions more symbolically, representing the ongoing spiritual and physical opposition that the world system brings against the kingdom of God throughout history. Regardless of the specific interpretive framework one adopts, we are called to handle these differences with charity and patience, acknowledging our shared hope in the return of Christ as noted in Romans 14:1-13; Romans 14:19.

You may wonder if the world is spinning toward an inevitable catastrophe as you see tensions rise among nations. It is true that the Bible describes a world fractured by rebellion and a creation that groans for redemption. However, the heart of the matter is not merely geopolitical, but spiritual. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him, leaving us under a rightful judgment. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, victorious over death. He is now the exalted Lord who will one day return to judge the living and the dead and to renew all things. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As you reflect on these truths, a helpful next step is to pray for the people of Iran. Despite political tensions, the underground church in Iran is currently one of the fastest growing Christian communities in the world. You might pray that the Lord would strengthen those believers, protect them in the face of persecution, and continue to use them to share the peace of Christ with their neighbors (1 Timothy 2:1-4). What holds you back from fully trusting in God's sovereign care for the future?
What does Jesus mean when He says, “Verily, verily” or “Truly, truly,” in the gospels?

"Verily, verily" or "Truly, truly" is a translation of the Greek words *Amen, amen*, which Jesus used to emphasize the absolute certainty, solemnity, and divine authority of the words He was about to speak. While the people of Israel traditionally used "Amen" at the conclusion of a prayer or a legal oath to signal agreement, Jesus uniquely placed it at the beginning of His statements to demonstrate that His words carry inherent authority and do not require human validation.

This expression is a transliteration of the Hebrew word *amen*, which is rooted in the idea of firmness, reliability, and truth. In the Gospel of John, Jesus uses this double "Amen" twenty five times to introduce profound spiritual revelations. When He speaks this way, He is acting as the faithful and true witness to the character and will of God. He is not merely sharing a perspective; He is declaring the objective reality of heaven. Revelation 3:14.

For instance, Jesus uses this phrase when speaking of the necessity of spiritual rebirth, saying, "Truly, truly, I tell you, no one can see the kingdom of God unless he is born again" John 3:3. He also uses it to provide the ultimate assurance of salvation, stating, "Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever hears My word and believes Him who sent Me has eternal life and will not come under judgment, but has crossed over from death to life" John 5:24.

You may wonder if there is anything in this shifting world that is genuinely solid and reliable. The human heart often searches for a truth that will not fail when life becomes difficult or when we face the reality of our own sin. Jesus Christ is that unchanging foundation. Because He is the eternal Son of God who lived a perfect life, died as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave, His words are the only ones that offer certain hope. He is the way, the truth, and the life. John 14:6.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from resting your soul upon His "Truly, truly" today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John and underline every instance where Jesus says, "Truly, truly." Take a moment to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to help you believe and apply those specific truths to your life, for God's Word is the ultimate authority for our faith and practice.
What should we learn from the tribe of Asher?

The tribe of Asher teaches us that God is a lavish provider who grants His people both the "bread" of earthly sustenance and the "oil" of spiritual favor, yet such blessings require a heart that remains vigilant against the ease of the world. Asher, whose name means "happy" or "blessed," was the eighth son of Jacob, and his tribal history provides a striking portrait of how God’s goodness should lead us to greater faithfulness rather than comfortable compromise.

One of the primary lessons from Asher is the reality of God's abundant providence. When Jacob blessed his sons, he prophesied that Asher’s food would be rich and that he would provide delicacies fit for a king (Genesis 49:20). Moses later added to this, praying that Asher would be the most blessed of sons and "dip his foot in oil" (Deuteronomy 33:24). Their territory along the Mediterranean coast was exceptionally fertile, famous for its olive groves and mineral wealth. This reminds us that every good gift comes from the Father of lights, who delights in providing for the needs of His children.

However, the history of Asher also serves as a pastoral warning against spiritual complacency. In the book of Judges, we find that the tribe of Asher did not drive out the Canaanites from the land God had given them; instead, the Asherites "lived among the Canaanite inhabitants of the land" (Judges 1:31-32). Because their land was rich and their bellies were full, they chose peaceful coexistence with pagan culture rather than the difficult work of obedience and spiritual warfare. We must be careful that the very blessings God gives us do not become cushions that put our souls to sleep. True prosperity is found in walking closely with Christ, not merely in the abundance of our possessions.

We also find a beautiful promise of God’s sustaining grace in the blessing given to Asher. Moses declared, "The bolts of your gates will be iron and bronze, and your strength will equal your days" (Deuteronomy 33:25). This is a profound comfort for the believer today. It teaches us that God does not give us a lifetime of strength all at once, but He provides the exact measure of grace we need for each individual day. Whether you are facing a season of peace or a day of deep trial, you can trust that His supply will not run dry before the sun sets.

Finally, the tribe of Asher offers us a testimony of God’s enduring faithfulness through the person of Anna the prophetess. Long after the ten northern tribes had been scattered, this woman from the tribe of Asher remained devoted to God, fasting and praying in the temple until she saw the infant Jesus (Luke 2:36-38). Her life proves that no matter our background or the failures of our ancestors, God preserves a remnant who hope in Him. She recognized that the "rich food" promised to Asher found its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the true Bread of Life.

As an actionable step in your own walk, I encourage you to spend time in prayer this week specifically thanking God for His daily provision. As you acknowledge His hand in your "daily bread," ask Him to reveal any areas where physical comfort has led you to compromise your spiritual devotion. Trust Him that as your days are, so shall your strength be.
Why does the Bible speak against braided hair?

The Bible addresses braided hair not to forbid a specific hairstyle, but to warn against vanity, extravagance, and an obsession with outward appearance that eclipses the beauty of a godly character. In the historical context of the New Testament, elaborate hairstyles often involved weaving gold, pearls, and expensive gems into the hair to signal high social standing or to draw undue attention to oneself (1 Timothy 2:9).

The Apostle Paul and the Apostle Peter were calling the church to the virtue of modesty. Modesty, in the biblical sense, is the self-control that refuses to use one's appearance to incite envy, promote pride, or distract others from the worship of God (1 Peter 3:3–4). When we prioritize the unfading beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, we reflect the character of Christ, who, though He was rich, for our sakes became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9).

This teaching focuses on the heart’s orientation toward sanctification, which is the process of being made more like Jesus in our desires and conduct. The concern is that a preoccupation with "costly attire" or "outward adornment" can become an idol that replaces our devotion to the Lord. Scripture reminds us that while man looks at the outward appearance, the Lord looks at the heart (1 Samuel 16:7).

As a practical step, you might consider asking yourself when preparing for the day: "Does my appearance aim to point others toward my own status, or does it reflect a heart that is primarily occupied with the glory of God?" True adornment is found in good works and a spirit that rests in the approval of the Father rather than the applause of the world (1 Timothy 2:10). May you find great joy in knowing that you are deeply loved by God for who you are in Christ, far beyond any outward ornament.
What is the difference between a covenant and a contract?

A contract is a legal agreement between parties based on an exchange of goods or services, whereas a biblical covenant is a solemn, life-binding relationship initiated by God that involves a commitment of persons. In a contract, the parties remain separate and the agreement is often conditional upon mutual performance, but in a covenant, God binds Himself to His people by His own promise and character.

Theological precision is vital here. Contracts are transactional, seeking to protect individual interests through limited liability. Covenants, however, are relational and comprehensive. In the Old Testament, we see God "cutting" a covenant with Abraham, where God alone passed through the pieces of the animals, signifying that He would bear the penalty if the promise were broken Genesis 15:17. This demonstrates sovereign initiation, where the Almighty reaches down to sinful man to establish a bond that man could never merit.

The most beautiful expression of this is the New Covenant. While we failed to keep our obligations under the law, Jesus Christ acted as our Mediator. He fulfilled every requirement of righteousness and took the curse of our disobedience upon Himself. Through His blood, He established an everlasting bond that cannot be broken Hebrews 8:6; Hebrews 13:20.

You may wonder if your own failures can dissolve your standing with God. A contract might be voided by your mistakes, but the Covenant of Grace rests on the finished work of Christ. Because He was faithful, those who trust in Him are secure.

To experience the depth of this truth, I encourage you to spend time this week reading through Hebrews 8 and Hebrews 9. As you read, pray that the Lord would help you rest in the security of being His child, not because of your performance, but because of His promise.

If you have never entered into this relationship, know that the Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a perfect life, died on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to reconcile us to God. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
If Jesus was God, why did He say "No one is good but God alone"?

Jesus was not disclaiming His own divinity in this passage, but was instead challenging the young man’s shallow understanding of goodness and the identity of the person standing before him. By asking this question, Jesus was forcing the ruler to consider the logical conclusion of his own words: if he truly recognized Jesus as "good" in the ultimate sense, he must also recognize Him as God.

In this encounter, recorded in Mark 10:18 and Luke 18:19, the rich young ruler approached Jesus with a title of respect, "Good Teacher," yet he likely viewed Jesus only as a highly respected human rabbi. Jesus, who is the second person of the Trinity and possesses the full divine nature, sought to move the man beyond mere politeness to a realization of His deity. If God alone is the absolute standard of goodness, and if Jesus is indeed good, then Jesus is God.

This interaction also highlights the core truth that no human being is inherently good enough to earn salvation by their own merit. The Bible teaches that "there is no one righteous, not even one" in Romans 3:10 and "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" in Romans 3:23. By pointing to God as the only one who is truly good, Jesus was preparing the man to see his own sinfulness and his desperate need for a Savior.

Jesus lived a life of perfect, sinless obedience, which qualified Him to be the perfect sacrifice for our sins. Because of the hypostatic union, where Jesus is truly God and truly man, His death on the cross has infinite value to reconcile us to the Father. He did not merely teach the way to God; He is the Way, the Truth, and the Life as stated in John 14:6.

It is a beautiful and sobering reality that while we are not good, God is rich in mercy. He sent His Son to take the punishment we deserve so that we might receive the righteousness of Christ through faith alone. The Gospel is the good news that Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and His identity as the Lord of all in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

You may wonder how a holy God could ever accept someone who is not "good" by His perfect standard. The answer is found entirely in the grace of Jesus Christ. I invite you to turn from your own efforts to be "good enough" and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical next step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the Gospel of John. Reflect on how "the Word was God" and "the Word became flesh" to reveal God's glory to us in John 1:1; John 1:14. Spend time in prayer today, asking the Lord to deepen your understanding of His perfect goodness and His great love for you. What is it that makes it difficult for you to see Jesus as more than just a good teacher?
What does it mean that God will reveal the hidden things (Daniel 2:22 and 28)?

To say that God reveals hidden things means that He possesses absolute knowledge of all secrets, whether they are the private thoughts of a human heart or the future events of world history. This attribute is rooted in His divine omniscience, which signifies that nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight, but everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of Him to whom we must give account (Hebrews 4:13).

In the context of the book of Daniel, King Nebuchadnezzar was troubled by a dream that his own wise men could not even describe, let alone interpret. Daniel 2:22 declares that God reveals deep and hidden things, showing that God is the sovereign Designer of history. Because He ordains what will come to pass, He alone has the authority to disclose those plans to His servants. Daniel 2:28 emphasizes that there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, contrasting the true God with the powerless idols and sorcerers of Babylon. This truth provides great comfort, for it means that even when the world seems chaotic or the future feels dark, God is in perfect control and nothing surprises Him.

Theologically, this points us to the providential sovereignty of God. He is the Father of lights, and in Him there is no darkness at all (James 1:17; 1 John 1:5). While we are limited by our human frailty and ignorance, God graciously chooses to reveal what is necessary for our salvation and His glory. The ultimate "hidden thing" that God has revealed is the mystery of the Gospel. For ages, the full plan of redemption was shadowed in types and prophecies, but it has now been made manifest through the appearing of our Savior, Jesus Christ (2 Timothy 1:10).

The dream Daniel interpreted was about a great stone, cut out by no human hand, that would strike the kingdoms of the world and become a mountain that fills the earth (Daniel 2:34-35). This is the Lord Jesus Christ. Though many in the world do not see Him for who He is, God has revealed Him to be the King of kings. Christ’s sacrificial death on the cross and His bodily resurrection are the historical facts that secure the victory over sin and death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to bring your hidden anxieties and uncertainties to Him in prayer this week. Like Daniel, who sought God’s mercies when faced with an impossible situation, you can ask the Lord for wisdom and trust Him to lead you through things you do not yet understand (Daniel 2:18; James 1:5). Is there a particular area of your life where you find it difficult to trust God's secret purposes?
What items should be included in a church constitution?

A church constitution is a foundational document that establishes the governing principles and doctrinal standards of a local congregation to ensure that all things are conducted decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40). It serves as a guard for the purity of the church and a guide for its peace and unity.

The following items should typically be included in a well ordered church constitution:

1. **Name and Purpose:** This section identifies the legal name of the church and its primary mission. The purpose should center on the glory of God through the worship of His name, the edification of the saints, and the proclamation of the Gospel to all nations (Matthew 28:18-20).

2. **Statement of Faith:** This is the doctrinal anchor of the congregation. it outlines the church's commitment to the core doctrines of the faith, such as the Trinity, the full deity and humanity of Jesus Christ, the substitutionary atonement, and the absolute authority of the inspired, inerrant Word of God. Many churches adopt a historic confession, such as the 1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith, to provide a precise theological framework.

3. **Membership:** This section defines the qualifications for membership, typically requiring a credible profession of faith in Christ and believer's baptism. It should also outline the responsibilities of members and the process for church discipline. Discipline is not a tool for cruelty but a biblical means of maintaining the holiness of the body and seeking the restoration of a wandering brother or sister (Matthew 18:15-17; Galatians 6:1).

4. **Leadership and Officers:** This details the biblical offices of the church, primarily elders (also called pastors or overseers) and deacons. The constitution should list the biblical qualifications for these roles and the process for their recognition and appointment (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9). While some churches utilize a modified congregationalism or an elder led structure, the document must clearly define where authority and accountability reside.

5. **The Ordinances:** The constitution should define the church’s practice of the two ordinances commanded by Christ: Baptism and the Lord’s Supper. This ensures they are administered according to the pattern of Scripture as visible signs of the New Covenant.

6. **Meetings and Governance:** This section provides the logistical framework for congregational business meetings, including how often they occur, what constitutes a quorum, and the procedures for voting on significant matters like calling a pastor or approving a budget.

7. **Amendments and Dissolution:** Because human documents are subordinate to the Word of God, there must be a clear process for making changes when the congregation discerns a need for greater biblical clarity. It should also include a plan for the distribution of assets should the church ever close, ensuring they continue to be used for the advancement of Christ's kingdom.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to gather with your current leadership to read through these sections alongside the Book of Acts and the Pastoral Epistles. Pray that the Holy Spirit would grant you wisdom to craft a document that does not merely serve as a legal shield, but as a faithful map for your life together in Christ. Seek the counsel of mature believers or sister churches who have walked this path before, for there is safety in an abundance of counselors (Proverbs 11:14).
What is topical preaching? Should a pastor preach topically?

Topical preaching is a method of instruction where the preacher selects a specific theme—such as forgiveness, the Trinity, or marriage—and then surveys various passages throughout the Bible to explain what God has revealed on that subject. This approach focuses on the theological "what" of a doctrine across the whole of Scripture rather than following the verse-by-verse flow of a single biblical book.

Whether a pastor should preach topically is a matter of pastoral wisdom and homiletical preference, as the Scriptures do not mandate one specific style of delivery. The primary requirement for any minister is that he must "preach the word" in 2 Timothy 4:2 and remain faithful to the "whole counsel of God" mentioned in Acts 20:27. Within the history of the church, there are two primary perspectives on this matter:

1. The Priority of Expository Preaching: Many pastors believe the steady diet of the church should be expository, meaning the preacher moves sequentially through a book of the Bible. The strength of this method is that it forces the preacher to deal with every verse in its original context, preventing the "exegesis"—or the drawing out of the true meaning—from being replaced by the preacher’s own opinions.

2. The Utility of Topical Preaching: Other faithful ministers utilize topical preaching to address specific needs within the congregation or to provide a systematic study of a core doctrine. This can be deeply helpful for grounding believers in what the entire Bible says about a complex issue. Even in the Old Testament, we see that when the Law was read, the leaders "gave the sense, so that the people understood the reading" in Nehemiah 8:8, which often involved explaining how different truths connected.

The danger of topical preaching arises if a speaker falls into "eisegesis," which is the practice of reading one's own ideas into the text. If a preacher pulls verses out of their historical and grammatical context to support a personal point, he risks misrepresenting the heart of God. A topical sermon is only as good as its commitment to the original intent of the passages it cites.

As a practical step for your own spiritual growth, I encourage you to keep your Bible open during every sermon. Whether your pastor is preaching through a book or on a topic, check the verses immediately preceding and following the ones he quotes. This discipline will help you ensure that the Word is being handled rightly and will deepen your own understanding of God’s truth. You might also consider asking God in prayer to give you a hunger for the "pure spiritual milk" of His Word, so that by it you may grow up into salvation as described in 1 Peter 2:2.
What are tribulation saints?

Tribulation saints are individuals who come to saving faith in Jesus Christ during the Great Tribulation, a period of intense global judgment and distress that precedes the final return of Christ. They are specifically identified in the Scriptures as those who have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb, persevering through extreme persecution.

The Bible provides a vivid description of these believers in the Book of Revelation. One of the elders asks, "These who are clothed in white robes, who are they, and where have they come from?" The answer given is, "These are the ones who have come out of the great tribulation; they have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" (Revelation 7:13–14). These saints represent God’s enduring mercy, showing that even in a season of divine wrath against sin, the Gospel remains the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.

Within historic Christianity, there are different perspectives on the identity of these saints based on how one understands the timing of the end times:

1. Many believers, particularly those holding to a dispensational view, see the tribulation saints as a distinct group of people who are saved after the Church has been caught up to meet the Lord in the air. In this view, these individuals respond to the Gospel after the departure of the Church and often face martyrdom for refusing the mark of the beast (Revelation 20:4).

2. Other faithful Christians, including many in the Reformed and historic premillennial traditions, believe that these saints are not a separate category but represent the Church itself as it passes through the final refining fire of history. They emphasize that Christ promised His people would face tribulation and that God preserves His elect through the trial rather than removing them from it (John 16:33; Matthew 24:21–22).

Regardless of the specific interpretive framework, the existence of tribulation saints highlights the core truth of the Gospel: salvation is a work of sovereign grace. It is secured not by human strength or endurance, but by the substitutionary death and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. Even in the face of death, these saints overcome by the blood of the Lamb and the word of their testimony (Revelation 12:11).

If you find yourself unsettled by the thought of future judgments, remember that God’s holiness requires him to judge sin, but His love provided a way of escape. We are all separated from God by our sin, yet Christ took the wrath we deserved upon Himself on the cross. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to spend time meditating on the sovereignty and goodness of God in the Book of Psalms, specifically Psalm 46, which reminds us that God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. What is it about the end times that weighs most heavily on your heart today?
Who are the Black Hebrews / Black Israelites?

The Black Hebrew Israelites are a diverse movement of groups who believe that people of color, particularly those of African descent in the Americas, are the literal physical descendants of the ancient biblical Israelites. This movement emerged in the late nineteenth century and includes a wide spectrum of beliefs, ranging from groups that practice a form of Judaism to those who identify as Christians but emphasize Hebrew roots.

You may wonder why this identity is so deeply cherished by many, and it often stems from a sincere desire to find dignity, historical belonging, and a sense of purpose in the face of the painful history of the transatlantic slave trade. Many in this movement point to Deuteronomy 28, arguing that the curses described there, such as being taken in ships and sold as slaves, are a historical marker identifying their ancestors as the true Israel.

While we can empathize with the search for identity, we must hold firmly to the truth of the New Covenant established by Jesus Christ. In many branches of this movement, there is a heavy emphasis on keeping the Mosaic Law as a requirement for standing before God. However, the Bible teaches the doctrine of justification by faith alone, where we are declared righteous by God's grace through faith in Christ, not by our works or our physical lineage. The Apostle Paul, himself a physical descendant of Abraham, wrote clearly that there is neither Jew nor Gentile, slave nor free, for all are one in Christ Jesus (Galatians 3:28).

Our true identity is not found in our earthly genealogy or our physical ancestors, but in our spiritual union with Jesus Christ. He is the true Israel, and all who are in Him by faith are heirs to the promises of God. Some radical groups within this movement deny the deity of Christ or the doctrine of the Trinity, which are essential truths of the Christian faith. The Bible teaches that Jesus is truly God and truly man, the Word who became flesh to dwell among us (John 1:1; John 1:14).

The Gospel is the good news that God is holy and we are all sinners separated from Him by our rebellion. Because of His great love, Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace, and it is offered to every tribe, tongue, and nation (Revelation 5:9). 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the book of Galatians. It was written specifically to address the error of adding requirements like law-keeping or physical markers to the simple Gospel of grace (Galatians 1:6-9; Galatians 2:16). Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the knowledge of Christ alongside other believers.
What does the Bible say about church-hopping?

The Bible describes the local church not as a building we visit or a service we consume, but as a living body to which we belong. While the term church-hopping is modern, the Scriptures speak directly to the necessity of being rooted in a specific covenant community for your spiritual protection and the health of the body.

The New Testament assumes that every believer is part of a local, identifiable assembly where they are known and where they serve. To move perpetually from one congregation to another without commitment hinders your growth and the mission of the church.

**The Authority of Elders**
Scripture commands believers to obey their leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls as those who will give an account. This is found in Hebrews 13:17. If a person is church-hopping, they have no shepherds who are truly responsible for their soul, and they are not in a position to be held accountable. True spiritual maturity involves placing oneself under the care of godly elders who can provide correction and guidance.

**The Function of the Body**
God has designed the church like a human body where every part is essential. In 1 Corinthians 12:12-27, the Apostle Paul explains that the eye cannot say to the hand, "I have no need of you." When you hop from church to church, the local body loses the benefit of the spiritual gifts God has given you for the common good. You are a "member" of Christ's body, and members are meant to be attached to the organism to function properly.

**Mutual Encouragement and Perseverance**
The writer of Hebrews warns against the habit of neglecting to meet together, as some are in the habit of doing, but instead encouraging one another, as we see in Hebrews 10:24-25. Church-hopping often prevents the deep, "one another" relationships where we can stir up one another to love and good works. Corporate sanctification, which is our growth in holiness together, requires the steady presence of brothers and sisters who know your struggles and can walk with you through them.

**A Pastoral Perspective**
It is true that there are seasons where a person must leave a church, such as when a church abandons the Gospel, drifts into unbiblical teaching, or when a move is necessary for work or family. However, if the motivation is a search for the "perfect" church or a desire to avoid the messiness of commitment, remember that the church is a hospital for sinners, not a museum for saints. As Charles Spurgeon once noted, if you ever find a perfect church, do not join it, for you would surely spoil it.

Our union with the local church is a reflection of our union with Jesus Christ. He loved the church and gave Himself up for her, as stated in Ephesians 5:25. If you are not currently committed to a local body, you are missing the primary means of grace God uses to shape you into the image of His Son.

**A Step for Reflection**
I encourage you to examine your heart: are you looking for a church that serves your preferences, or a place where you can serve and be spiritually fed? If you are currently unattached, pray and ask God to lead you to a biblically faithful church where the Word is rightly preached and the ordinances of baptism and the Lord's Supper are rightly administered. Once you find such a place, speak to the elders about formal membership so that you can be fully integrated into the life of that family.

The foundation of all our fellowship is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We were once alienated from God by our sin, but Christ, the righteous Son of God, died in our place and rose again to reconcile us to God and to one another. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that makes commitment feel difficult for you right now?
Dance in worship - what does the Bible say?

The Bible presents dance as a physical expression of intense joy, gratitude, and celebration directed toward God. In the Old Testament, we see notable instances where the people of God used dance to mark significant moments of deliverance and worship.

Miriam led the women of Israel in dancing and song after the Lord brought them through the Red Sea in Exodus 15:20. King David famously danced before the Lord with all his might when the Ark of the Covenant was brought into Jerusalem in 2 Samuel 6:14. Furthermore, the Book of Psalms explicitly encourages the use of dance in praise, saying, "Let them praise His name with dancing; let them sing praises to Him with tambourine and lyre" in Psalm 149:3 and "Praise Him with tambourine and dancing" in Psalm 150:4.

While these examples show dance as a valid expression of devotion, the question of its role in the formal gathering of the local church is a tertiary matter where faithful Christians often differ in their convictions. There are two primary perspectives within historic Christianity:

First, some believers view the commands in the Psalms as an enduring invitation for the Church to include dance in corporate worship. They see it as a holistic way to honor God with the body as well as the spirit, provided it is done with modesty and a focus on God's glory.

Second, other believers, particularly within the Reformed tradition, adhere to the Regulative Principle of Worship. This principle suggests that the elements of corporate worship should be limited to what is explicitly commanded or modeled for the New Testament church. Since the New Testament epistles do not prescribe dance for the gathered assembly but emphasize order and the ministry of the Word, these believers usually reserve dance for private celebration or cultural expression rather than the Sunday service.

In such matters, we must apply the principle of Christian liberty found in Romans 14. Whether one dances or refrains from dancing, the aim must be to honor the Lord. Scripture reminds us in 1 Corinthians 14:40 that "all things should be done decently and in order."

If you are considering how to express your joy in the Lord, I encourage you to reflect on the posture of your heart. Worship is ultimately an inward reality that manifests in outward fruit. You might find it helpful to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to align your expressions of praise with a deep reverence for His holiness and a love for the peace and unity of your local church body.
What is secular humanism?

Secular humanism is a comprehensive worldview that discards belief in the supernatural and the divine, instead placing human reason, ethics, and social justice at the center of existence. It asserts that human beings are capable of being ethical and moral without religion, relying solely on naturalism and human experience to understand the world.

A sincere concern behind this worldview is often a deep desire to uphold human dignity and find a basis for justice and morality without the perceived constraints of religious history. However, from the perspective of Scripture, secular humanism is an exercise in radical autonomy, where the creature attempts to function independently of the Creator. While it seeks to champion human rights, it simultaneously rejects the only objective foundation for those rights: the truth that every person is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26; Genesis 1:27).

Theologically, this movement reflects a fallen human anthropology. By exalting human wisdom as the supreme authority, it falls into the trap described by the Apostle Paul, where people exchange the truth about God for a lie and worship and serve created things rather than the Creator (Romans 1:25). Without a transcendent standard rooted in the character of a holy and sovereign God, ethics becomes a matter of shifting human consensus rather than absolute truth. The Bible warns believers to see that no one takes them captive through hollow and deceptive philosophy, which depends on human tradition and the elemental spiritual forces of this world rather than on Christ (Colossians 2:8).

True human flourishing is not found in our independence from God, but in our reconciliation to Him. We were made by Him and for Him, yet our sin has separated us from the life and light we were meant to enjoy. The Gospel is the good news that God did not leave us in our self-styled darkness. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the Epistle to the Romans and prayerfully consider how the Word of God addresses the human desire for autonomy and the reality of our need for a Savior.
Does the Bible predict the destruction of Damascus?

Yes, the Bible explicitly predicts the destruction of Damascus in several prophetic books, primarily as a judgment for its pride and its opposition to the purposes of God. The most prominent prophecy is found in the book of Isaiah, which declares, "An oracle against Damascus: 'Behold, Damascus will no longer be a city, but will become a heap of ruins'" (Isaiah 17:1).

In the eighth century B.C., Damascus served as the capital of the Aramean kingdom and formed a military alliance with the northern kingdom of Israel against the kingdom of Judah. God sent the prophet Isaiah to warn that this city, though ancient and powerful, would be brought low. This word was fulfilled in a significant historical sense when the Assyrian Empire, under Tiglath-Pileser III, conquered Damascus in 732 B.C., as recorded in 2 Kings 16:9.

Other prophets also spoke of this divine judgment. Jeremiah stated that "Damascus has grown feeble; she has turned to flee, and panic has gripped her" (Jeremiah 49:24). Likewise, the prophet Amos declared that God would "break the gate bar of Damascus" and send fire upon its strongholds because of its cruelty (Amos 1:3; Amos 1:4; Amos 1:5).

Because Damascus remains a populated city today, many Bible students believe these prophecies may have a dual fulfillment. While the Assyrian conquest was a literal historical event, the language of Isaiah 17:1 describes a state of ruin that some interpret as a final, total destruction yet to occur in the sovereign timing of God.

These oracles serve as a vivid reminder of the holiness of God and His supreme authority over every nation and ruler. While the prospect of such judgment is sobering, it highlights the reality that every kingdom of man is temporary, whereas the kingdom of God is eternal. 

The weight of divine judgment is something we all face because of our sin, but the heart of the Bible is the message of God’s redeeming love. God is holy and just, but He is also merciful. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice on behalf of all who believe. Christ was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers forgiveness to all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him for your salvation today?

To see how God’s hand moves through history, I encourage you to read the book of Isaiah, noticing how His warnings of judgment are often followed by beautiful promises of restoration for those who seek Him. Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can study these truths with other believers.
What does it mean to watch and pray?

To watch and pray is to maintain a spiritual alertness while actively depending on God's strength to sustain you. It is the dual heartbeat of the Christian life, combining the vigilance of a sentry with the humble petition of a child. Jesus gave this command to His disciples in their hour of greatest testing, saying, "Watch and pray so that you will not enter into temptation. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak" (Matthew 26:41).

Watching means being sober-minded and aware of the spiritual reality surrounding us. It involves guarding your heart against the subtle entry of sin and remaining expectant for the return of the Lord. The Apostle Peter instructs believers to "Be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour" (1 Peter 5:8). To watch is to recognize that we are in a spiritual conflict and to stay awake to the promptings of the Holy Spirit rather than drifting into a spiritual slumber.

Praying is the vital link that connects our awareness to God's power. If we watch without praying, we may become anxious or self-reliant, trusting in our own limited sight. If we pray without watching, we may become careless, asking for help while walking blindly into danger. Prayer is the means by which we receive grace for our sanctification, which is the process of being made holy and more like Christ. As the Apostle Paul exhorted the church, "Devote yourselves to prayer, being watchful and thankful" (Colossians 4:2).

This life of vigilance is only possible because of what Jesus Christ has done. While the disciples slept in the garden, Christ stayed awake to face the cross for us. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave, conquering death and the devil. It is His victory that gives us the hope and power to stand. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend a few minutes this evening in a spiritual audit. Ask the Lord to reveal one specific area where you have become indifferent or distracted, and then pray specifically for His strength to help you remain alert in that area tomorrow.
How is eternity in Hell a fair punishment for sin?

The question of eternal punishment is a heavy one that touches our deepest sense of fairness and justice. You may wonder how a few decades of human life can result in an everlasting consequence, and it is natural to struggle with the weight of such a solemn truth.

The Bible teaches that the fairness of a punishment is determined not only by the nature of the act, but by the infinite dignity and holiness of the One against whom the act is committed. Because God is an eternal, infinite, and perfectly holy Being, any sin against Him is an offense of infinite proportions. As the Scriptures declare, God is light and in Him there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5).

There are three primary reasons why the Bible presents eternal punishment as a just response to sin:

First, the holiness of God. God’s character is the absolute standard of goodness. He is so pure that He cannot even look upon evil with approval (Habakkuk 1:13). Because His glory is of infinite value, rebelling against Him—which is what sin is—carries a weight of guilt that matches His status as the Creator and Sustainer of the universe. In this light, sin is seen as "high treason" against the Sovereign of all things.

Second, the nature of human rebellion. The Bible describes those in hell not as people who want to be with God but are being kept out, but as those who continue in their rebellion and hatred of God. Even in the final judgment, there is no indication that the lost desire to repent or love God; rather, they remain fixed in their rejection of Him. Sin is not just a series of mistakes; it is a condition of the heart that refuses to submit to its Maker.

Third, the price paid for redemption. The infinite nature of our debt is most clearly seen at the cross. If the punishment for sin were merely temporary, a finite sacrifice might have sufficed. However, for justice to be satisfied and for sinners to be reconciled, it required the death of the Lord Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God. His sacrifice was of infinite value because He is the infinite God in human flesh (1 Peter 3:18; Romans 5:8).

God does not delight in the death of the wicked (Ezekiel 33:11). In His great love, He has provided a way for us to be spared from this judgment. He took the punishment we deserved upon Himself so that we might receive His righteousness. This is the heart of the Gospel: that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day to defeat sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord (Romans 6:23). Justice is satisfied either in the eternal punishment of the sinner or in the eternal sacrifice of the Savior.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How is Satan god of this world (2 Corinthians 4:4)?

Satan is referred to as the god of this world because he exercises a pervasive, though temporary and limited, influence over the thoughts, desires, and systems of people who are in rebellion against God. This title does not imply that he possesses divine attributes or independent sovereignty, for the Lord alone is the absolute King and Creator of all things (Psalm 24:1; Colossians 1:16-17).

The Apostle Paul explains that this god of this age works primarily through deception and spiritual blindness. He veils the Gospel so that those who are perishing cannot see the light of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God (2 Corinthians 4:4). This influence is visible in the world's philosophies, moral standards, and pursuits that intentionally exclude the lordship of Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:2; 1 John 5:19).

We must understand that Satan is a defeated foe whose dominion is entirely under the sovereign decree of God. While he blinds the minds of the unbelieving, he has no power to prevent the Holy Spirit from performing the miracle of regeneration, which is the work of God commanding light to shine out of darkness in the human heart (2 Corinthians 4:6). His "godhood" is merely a borrowed and dying shadow permitted for a season within God’s greater redemptive plan.

If you find yourself feeling overwhelmed by the darkness of the world or the power of temptation, remember that He who is in you is greater than he who is in the world (1 John 4:4). One actionable step is to begin each day by reading a portion of the Gospels, specifically focusing on the authority of Jesus over the powers of darkness, to realign your mind with the truth of His victory.

You may wonder how a person can escape this spiritual blindness and the influence of the enemy. The Gospel is the only power that can set a captive free. God is holy and perfectly just, yet we have all sinned and turned to our own ways (Isaiah 53:6; Romans 3:23). Out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for sinners (1 Peter 2:24; Romans 5:8). He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin, death, and the devil (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Hebrews 2:14).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is faith intellectual assent?

Biblical faith includes intellectual assent, but it is not limited to it. Saving faith is a heart level trust and personal reliance on the person and work of Jesus Christ for salvation, moving beyond mere recognition of facts to a total resting of the soul upon God.

In the study of theology, we often distinguish three essential elements of faith. First is knowledge (notitia), which is the understanding of the facts of the Gospel. Second is assent (assensus), which is the intellectual agreement that these facts are true. Third is trust (fiducia), which is the personal appropriation of those truths where one casts themselves entirely upon Christ.

While intellectual assent is necessary, the Bible warns that it is insufficient for salvation on its own. James 2:19 states, "You believe that there is one God. Good! Even the demons believe that, and shudder." The demons have correct theology and intellectual agreement regarding God's existence and power, yet they lack the submissive, loving trust that characterizes a child of God. True faith involves the whole person, the mind, the emotions, and the will.

Consider the difference between believing a boat is sturdy enough to cross a river and actually stepping into the boat to make the journey. Intellectual assent agrees the boat is seaworthy, but saving faith is the act of stepping in and trusting the boat to carry you. Romans 10:9-10 explains, "If you confess with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved. For with your heart a person believes, resulting in righteousness, and with your mouth he confesses, resulting in salvation."

This faith is the only way to be reconciled to our holy Creator. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His just judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own.

You may wonder if your own faith is "enough" or if you have merely agreed to a set of ideas. True faith proves its reality by a changed life and a desire to follow Jesus, though we still struggle with sin in this life. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, specifically focusing on the many times Jesus calls people to "believe in" Him. As you read, talk to God in prayer and ask Him to move your heart from simple agreement to a deep, personal reliance on His promises. Seek out a local, Bible teaching church where you can grow in your understanding of what it means to live by faith.
What does it mean to be saved by grace?

To be saved by grace means that your rescue from sin and its consequences is a free gift from God, granted entirely apart from your own works, merit, or moral effort. It is the unmerited favor of a holy God reaching down to those who are spiritually bankrupt and unable to save themselves.

The heart of the Gospel is that we are all by nature "dead in our trespasses and sins" (Ephesians 2:1). Because God is perfectly holy and just, our rebellion against Him carries a debt we can never pay. If salvation were based on our own "goodness," we would all be hopelessly lost, for "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23).

However, God, being rich in mercy, provided the way of salvation through His Son, Jesus Christ. To be saved by grace is to have your guilt removed and Christ's righteousness credited to you (justification) solely because of what He has done. Jesus lived the perfect life we failed to live and died the substitutionary death we deserved, satisfying the justice of God on our behalf. As the Scripture declares, "For by grace you have been saved through faith; and this is not of yourselves, it is the gift of God; not of works, so that no one may boast" (Ephesians 2:8–9).

This grace is not a license to continue in sin, but is the very power that transforms the heart. It produces a new desire to love and obey God, not to earn His favor, but in joyful gratitude for the favor already given. "For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all men. It instructs us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live sensible, upright, and godly lives in the present age" (Titus 2:11–12).

You may wonder if you have done too much wrong to be accepted by God, or if you must first "clean yourself up" to be worthy of His love. The wonder of the Gospel is that grace is specifically for the unworthy. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is it that makes it difficult for you to let go of your own efforts and trust entirely in His finished work?

A practical next step in understanding this grace is to read the Gospel of John, chapters 1 through 3, and to speak to God in prayer, confessing your need for the Savior and asking Him to help you trust in His promise of mercy.
What is the kalam cosmological argument for the existence of God?

The Kalam Cosmological Argument is a logical syllogism used to demonstrate that the universe requires a First Cause, which Christians identify as God. It follows three simple steps: everything that begins to exist has a cause, the universe began to exist, and therefore the universe must have a cause for its existence.

This argument points us toward the biblical truth of creation ex nihilo, meaning creation out of nothing. Scripture begins with the definitive statement that "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1). Logic and reason suggest that since the universe is not eternal but had a starting point, it could not have brought itself into being. There must be a Cause that is itself uncaused, timeless, spaceless, and immensely powerful to bring all of physical reality into existence. As the author of Hebrews writes, "By faith we understand that the universe was formed at God’s command, so that what is seen was not made out of what was visible" (Hebrews 11:3).

You may wonder how an invisible, eternal God relates to a physical world that seems so reliant on natural laws. It is a sincere concern to ask how the finite can ever truly know the Infinite. While philosophy can point us to a "First Cause," the Bible introduces us to a personal Creator who sustains all things. "For by Him all things were created, things in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible... He is before all things, and in Him all things hold together" (Colossians 1:16-17).

This Creator is not a distant force but a holy and personal God who made you with purpose. However, our sin creates a barrier between us and His perfect holiness. The wonderful news of the Gospel is that God did not leave us in our separation. He became man in the person of Jesus Christ, living a perfect life, dying on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rising bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Creator and Savior today?

As a practical next step in exploring these truths, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It beautifully connects the "Word" who was there in the beginning with the Savior who walked among us to bring us home to God.
Why is the virgin birth so important?

The virgin birth is the essential miraculous sign that Jesus Christ is both truly God and truly man, conceived by the Holy Spirit in the womb of the Virgin Mary without a human father. This historical fact is foundational because it preserves the sinless nature of Christ and confirms His identity as the promised Messiah who alone can reconcile sinners to a holy God.

The significance of the virgin birth can be understood through several biblical truths:

First, it is the fulfillment of specific prophecy. God promised centuries before the birth of Christ that a virgin would conceive and bear a son whose name would be Immanuel, which means God with us. This sign was intended to set the Messiah apart from every other human being born into the world as recorded in Isaiah 7:14 and Matthew 1:22-23.

Second, the virgin birth is necessary for the incarnation and the hypostatic union, which is the joining of a fully divine nature and a fully human nature in the one person of Jesus Christ. If Jesus had been born through the natural union of a human father and mother, He would have been a mere man. However, by the power of the Holy Spirit, the Eternal Word became flesh without losing His divinity as seen in John 1:14 and Luke 1:35.

Third, this miraculous birth distinguishes Jesus from the rest of humanity regarding the inheritance of sin. Every person descended from Adam by natural generation is born with a corrupted nature and under the legal condemnation of sin. By being born of a woman but conceived by the Holy Spirit, Jesus entered the world as the New Adam, holy and undefiled, yet truly human so that He could represent us as our Mediator. This is reflected in Romans 5:12-19 and Hebrews 7:26.

Without the virgin birth, we would have no sinless Savior, and without a sinless Savior, there could be no valid atonement for our transgressions. Our salvation depends entirely on the fact that Christ, being truly God, could bear the infinite weight of God's wrath, and being truly man, could die in our place.

As you reflect on this wonder, I encourage you to marvel at the humility of God, who stooped so low to rescue us. A practical next step for your spiritual growth is to spend time this week reading through the first chapters of the Gospel of Luke, asking God to deepen your worship of the Savior who became one of us to save us. You might also find it helpful to speak with your elders or a mature believer about how the humanity of Jesus allows Him to sympathize with your own weaknesses and struggles.
How can I overcome disappointment with life?

It is deeply painful to feel that life has not turned out the way you hoped or expected, and it is vital to remember that God is near to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit (Psalm 34:18). Disappointment often arises when our earthly expectations collide with the reality of a fallen world, but for the believer, these moments are invitations to anchor our hope in the unchanging character of God rather than shifting circumstances.

To overcome disappointment, we must first look to the doctrine of Divine Providence. This is the biblical truth that God is the sovereign ruler of all things, purposefully guiding every event in history and every detail of your life for His glory and your ultimate good (Romans 8:28). When our plans fail, it is not because God has lost control or ceased to be kind, but because His wisdom is far greater than our own. We see this most clearly in the life of Joseph, who suffered years of betrayal and imprisonment, yet could eventually say that what others meant for evil, God meant for good (Genesis 50:20).

We must also recognize that our greatest disappointment often stems from a misplaced hope. We frequently look to career, relationships, or health to provide a satisfaction that only Christ can give. The Bible teaches that this world is not our final home and that we are currently groaning along with all of creation as we wait for the full redemption of our bodies (Romans 8:23). If you find your heart heavy today, consider whether you have been asking this world to be your heaven. 

True and lasting peace is found only when we are reconciled to God. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him, which leads to a deep, restless sense of lack. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into our world. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our efforts but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of seeking ultimate satisfaction in things that fade, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that feels most difficult to hand over to His lordship today?

As a practical step in your journey through this season, I encourage you to practice the biblical language of lament. Do not hide your pain from God, but bring it directly to Him in prayer. Open the book of Psalms and read Psalm 13 or Psalm 42 aloud, making those inspired words your own. Tell Him honestly where it hurts, but end your prayer by intentionally rehearsing the truths of His faithfulness, just as the prophet Jeremiah did when he said that the steadfast love of the Lord never ceases and His mercies are new every morning (Lamentations 3:22–23). 

Finally, do not walk this path alone. Seek the fellowship of a local, Bible teaching church where you can receive the means of grace through the preaching of the Word and the encouragement of other believers. God often uses the community of the saints to remind us that while our earthly dreams may change, His kingdom is unshakeable (Hebrews 12:28).
What is the Gemara?

The Gemara is the component of the Talmud that comprises extensive rabbinical analysis and commentary on the Mishnah, which is the earlier written version of Jewish oral traditions. Together, the Mishnah and the Gemara form the Talmud, serving as a primary source of Jewish law, ethics, and theology.

While the Mishnah provides the core legal rulings, the Gemara functions as a detailed record of the discussions, arguments, and interpretations that Jewish scholars produced over several centuries. It was written primarily in Aramaic and was completed around 500 AD in Babylon (the Babylonian Talmud) and earlier in the land of Israel (the Jerusalem Talmud).

As a fellow believer, it is helpful to understand these texts to gain insight into the historical context of the New Testament, yet we must always hold to the **sufficiency of Scripture**. In the days of His earthly ministry, our Lord Jesus often spoke with great love and clarity about the distinction between God's Word and human tradition. He cautioned in Mark 7:8, "You have let go of the commands of God and are holding on to human traditions." While the Gemara contains much historical wisdom, it does not carry the divine authority of the inspired, inerrant Word of God.

We believe that the Bible alone is our final authority for faith and practice. The Law was given to point us to our need for a Savior, and Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of that Law. For those seeking to know God truly, we find that salvation is not found in the keeping of tradition, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Jesus said in John 14:6, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

If you are exploring these traditions in search of spiritual peace, I invite you to look to Jesus, who provides the rest that no legal system can offer. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died for our sins and rose again to give us new life.

A wonderful next step in your study would be to read the book of Hebrews in the New Testament. It beautifully explains how Jesus is greater than all previous traditions and systems, serving as our perfect High Priest. As you read, you might pray and ask God to open your eyes to the wonders of His grace, as described in Hebrews 1:1-3 and Hebrews 4:14-16.
How can I stop being a people-pleaser?

To stop being a people-pleaser, you must first recognize that this habit is a manifestation of what the Bible calls the fear of man, which is a dangerous snare to the soul (Proverbs 29:25). It is the act of placing the shifting opinions of people on the throne that belongs only to God, seeking their validation rather than His (Galatians 1:10).

You may feel a deep, understandable desire for harmony and a fear that saying no will lead to rejection or conflict. This often stems from a heart that is looking to others to provide a sense of worth or security that only the Lord can give. While being helpful and kind is a Christian virtue, people-pleasing is a form of relational idolatry that enslaves you to the expectations of others, often leading to resentment and a divided heart.

The remedy for this struggle is found in the Gospel. We often please others because we are searching for a "righteousness" of our own, hoping that if enough people approve of us, we will finally be enough. However, Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and we are all sinners who fall short of His glory (Romans 3:23). No amount of human approval can fix our separation from Him. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

When you trust in Christ alone, you are justified by grace through faith, not by your works or your ability to keep everyone happy (Ephesians 2:8-9). In Christ, you are already fully known, fully loved, and perfectly accepted by the King of Kings. When the approval of God becomes your primary reality, the opinions of men lose their power to control you. You are then free to serve others out of genuine love rather than a desperate need for their favor.

A practical step in your growth and sanctification is to practice the discipline of "godly boundaries" by using Galatians 1:10 as a daily prayer and reflection. Before committing to a request that causes you internal compromise, ask yourself, "Am I seeking the approval of man or of God?"
