Commit to a regular rhythm of reading the Psalms to see how the biblical writers brought their fears of others to God, and seek accountability from a mature believer in your local church who can encourage you to stand firm in your identity in Christ.

You can turn to God today, repent of the fear of man, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for complete forgiveness and a new life. What holds you back from resting in His approval today?
What is the golden chain of salvation?

The "golden chain of salvation" is the unbreakable sequence of God’s sovereign actions in redeeming His people, ensuring that everyone He chooses is eventually brought to eternal glory. This biblical truth is primarily drawn from Romans 8:29-30, where the Apostle Paul lists the specific stages of God’s work in the life of a believer.

This sequence is often called the *Ordo Salutis*, or the order of salvation, and it demonstrates that God is the author and finisher of our faith from beginning to end. The chain includes five essential links:

1.  **Foreknowledge:** This is not merely God knowing facts in advance, but God setting His special, covenantal love upon His people before the foundation of the world (Romans 8:29; Ephesians 1:4-5).
2.  **Predestination:** God’s sovereign decree to set His people apart and conform them to the image of His Son, Jesus Christ (Romans 8:29).
3.  **Calling:** This is the effectual call of the Holy Spirit, who opens the heart of the sinner to respond to the Gospel with faith and repentance (Romans 8:30; 1 Corinthians 1:9).
4.  **Justification:** A legal act where God declares the sinner righteous, not based on their own works, but through the imputation of Christ’s righteousness received by faith alone (Romans 8:30; Romans 5:1).
5.  **Glorification:** The final state of the believer, where we are made perfect in body and soul to dwell with God forever (Romans 8:30; Philippians 3:20-21).

The profound comfort of this doctrine is that not a single link in the chain can be broken. Every person whom God foreknew, He will certainly glorify. As the Scripture says, "He who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion until the day of Christ Jesus" (Philippians 1:6).

If you find yourself questioning your security or feeling the weight of your own failures, remember that your standing before God depends on His unwavering grip on you, not your fluctuating grip on Him. You are invited to rest in this finished work and the faithfulness of your Savior.

You may wonder how you can know this security for yourself. The Bible teaches that while God is holy and just, we have all turned away from Him in sin. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should Mark 16:9-20 be in the Bible?

Mark 16:9–20 is traditionally included in the Bible as a faithful record of the post-resurrection ministry of Jesus Christ. While modern translations often place these verses in brackets or include a marginal note regarding their absence in the earliest Greek manuscripts, the Church has historically received and preached them as part of the inspired canon of Scripture.

The debate over these verses centers on the science of textual criticism, which compares ancient copies of the Bible to determine the original wording. Faithful Christians generally hold to one of two primary views on this matter. 

The first view emphasizes the providential preservation of Scripture. Proponents of this view argue that God has sovereignly overseen the transmission of His Word so that no part of the inspired text would be lost to His people. They point out that these verses are found in the vast majority of existing Greek manuscripts and were quoted as Scripture by early church fathers such as Irenaeus and Justin Martyr as early as the second century. For these believers, the long standing witness of the Church confirms that these verses belong in the Bible.

The second view suggests that while the verses are historically accurate and do not contradict any core doctrine, they may have been a very early addition to the Gospel of Mark to provide a smoother conclusion. This perspective is based on the fact that the two oldest and most complete Greek manuscripts, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, end the Gospel at Mark 16:8. Those who hold this view believe that acknowledging the manuscript evidence honors the integrity of the biblical text without undermining the authority of the truths the passage contains.

Ultimately, the content of Mark 16:9–20—including the bodily resurrection of Jesus, His appearance to the disciples, the command to preach the Gospel to all nations, and His ascension into heaven—is firmly established in other parts of the New Testament. You can find these same historical facts and commands in Matthew 28:16–20, Luke 24:36–53, and Acts 1:1–11.

Whether one views these verses as the original ending of Mark’s pen or a faithful summary added by the early church under the guidance of the Holy Spirit, they serve to point us to the victory of Christ over the grave. If you find yourself unsettled by these technical questions, I encourage you to read the Gospel accounts in Matthew and Luke alongside Mark. You will find a beautiful harmony that confirms the reality of our Savior’s resurrection. 

I suggest you take some time today to read Mark 16:1–20 and then read Acts 1:1–11, asking the Lord to strengthen your confidence in the reliability and sufficiency of His Word.
What is the difference between a sect and a cult?

A sect is a subgroup within a larger religious tradition that maintains the essential, core doctrines of that faith while differing on secondary practices or interpretations, whereas a cult is a group that claims to be part of a tradition but denies its foundational, non negotiable truths. Historically, the word "sect" was used to describe the early Christians (Acts 24:5), but today it often refers to denominations that remain within the bounds of "orthodoxy" while disagreeing on matters such as baptism or church government.

A cult is defined by its theological deviation from the historic Christian faith. Most cults are characterized by the denial of the "Trinity" (Matthew 28:19), the denial of the "Deity of Christ" (John 1:1), or the denial of "justification by faith" alone (Galatians 1:8; Ephesians 2:8–9). These groups often center around a charismatic leader who claims exclusive authority or new revelation that contradicts the "Authority of Scripture" (2 Timothy 3:16). While a sect may simply be a different branch of the same tree, a cult is a different tree entirely, bearing fruit that leads away from the true Gospel.

The Apostle John, who wrote with great tenderness for the sheep and great boldness against error, warned us to "test the spirits to see whether they are from God, because many false prophets have gone out into the world" (1 John 4:1). True love does not ignore false teaching; it warns against it because the soul is at stake. If a group teaches a Jesus who is not truly God, or a salvation that must be earned by human works, it is not leading you to life, but to a heavy yoke of bondage.

If you are evaluating a specific group, a vital next step is to compare their core teachings directly with the Word of God. Ask whether they affirm that Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God who became man, died for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave.

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is perfectly holy and just, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was raised to life on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works we can do (Titus 3:5).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is "name it claim it" teaching biblical?

The "name it and claim it" teaching, often associated with the Word of Faith movement or the Prosperity Gospel, is not biblical because it shifts the focus of faith from the sovereign will of God to the desires and declarations of man. Scripture teaches that faith is a humble trust in the character and promises of God, not a creative force or a spiritual tool used to manipulate one's circumstances for personal gain.

This teaching often relies on a misunderstanding of the power of words, suggesting that believers can manifest physical health or financial wealth by speaking them into existence. However, the Bible upholds the doctrine of divine sovereignty, which establishes that God is the supreme ruler who works all things according to the counsel of His own will (Ephesians 1:11). When we pray, we are invited to bring our requests to a loving Father, but we must always do so with the heart of Christ, who prayed, "Yet not as I will, but as You will" (Matthew 26:39).

Furthermore, the "name it and claim it" ideology fails to account for the biblical reality of suffering and the refining work of God through trials. The Apostle Paul, a man of immense faith, pleaded with the Lord to remove a "thorn in the flesh," yet God did not grant the request, saying instead, "My grace is sufficient for you, for My power is perfected in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:8-9). True biblical faith persists even when the answer is "no" or "wait," resting in the providence of a God who knows what is best for His children.

The core of the Gospel is not the promise of earthly riches, but the gift of reconciliation with God through the sacrifice of Jesus Christ. We are warned that those who desire to get rich fall into temptation and a snare (1 Timothy 6:9-10). Our greatest treasure is Christ Himself, who died for our sins and rose again to give us eternal life, not merely a comfortable temporal life.

If you have found yourself discouraged because "claiming" a blessing did not result in its arrival, please know that your standing with God is not measured by your bank account or your physical health, but by the finished work of Jesus. I encourage you to make the Lord's Prayer your daily guide, specifically focusing on the phrase, "Your kingdom come, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven" (Matthew 6:10). This aligns your heart with His purposes and brings a peace that passes understanding.

You might also find comfort in studying the lives of the faithful in Hebrews 11, many of whom faced great hardship and did not receive the earthly things promised, yet were commended for their faith (Hebrews 11:36-40). 

If you are seeking a relationship with God, remember that salvation is a free gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. 

What has been your experience with these teachings, and how can I help you navigate what the Bible says about God's promises?

James 4:13-15; 1 John 5:14-15; Romans 8:28; Philippians 4:11-13
What does the Bible say about cryonics or cryogenics?

The Bible does not explicitly mention cryonics or cryogenics, as these are modern technological developments, but it speaks with absolute clarity regarding the sovereignty of God over life and death, the nature of the human soul, and the glorious hope of the bodily resurrection. To understand this through a biblical lens, we must look at how Scripture defines our existence and our end.

First, we must recognize the absolute sovereignty of God over the span of human life. The Scriptures teach that the timing of our birth and the moment of our death are firmly in the hands of our Creator. King David wrote that all the days ordained for him were written in God's book before one of them came to be in Psalm 139:16. Similarly, Job 14:5 declares that a man's days are determined and that God has set limits he cannot exceed. Cryonics, which seeks to pause death in hopes of a future cure, can sometimes reflect a heart that struggles to submit to God's providential timing.

Second, the Bible defines death as a fixed point of transition rather than a reversible biological state. Hebrews 9:27 states that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment. From a biblical perspective, death involves the separation of the soul from the body. For the believer, to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, as noted in 2 Corinthians 5:8 and Philippians 1:23. If the soul has departed to be with Christ or to face judgment, the preservation of the physical "shell" through cryogenics does not account for the spiritual reality of the person's immediate state after death.

Third, there is a profound difference between the scientific concept of reanimation and the biblical doctrine of the bodily resurrection. Cryonics hopes to eventually revive a mortal, decaying body to live a few more years in a fallen world. In contrast, the Christian hope is the resurrection of a glorified, immortal body. 1 Corinthians 15:42-44 explains that the body is sown in corruption but raised in incorruption, sown in weakness but raised in power. Our hope is not found in the hands of future scientists, but in the power of the risen Christ who has already conquered the grave.

You may wonder if seeking to extend life through technology is a lack of faith. It is vital to distinguish between wise medical stewardship, which seeks to heal the living, and the pursuit of cryonics, which often stems from a fear of the "last enemy," death. While we thank God for medical advances, we must be careful not to make an idol of physical preservation. Our resources and our trust belong to the One who holds the keys to death and Hades.

If the thought of death causes you anxiety or leads you to look toward technology for a sense of permanence, I encourage you to look instead to the person of Jesus Christ. We all face the reality of our sin and the separation from God it causes, but the Gospel offers a remedy that no laboratory can provide. God is holy and just, yet in His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death once and for all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. Rather than placing hope in a frozen chamber, you can find rest in the promise that "Death has been swallowed up in victory" in 1 Corinthians 15:54.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through 1 Corinthians 15 this week and spend time in prayer, asking the Lord to deepen your confidence in His sovereign care for your future. If you are not already part of a local, Bible-believing church, finding a community of believers to walk with can provide immense comfort and perspective on these deep questions of life and eternity. What is it about the idea of cryonics that most captures your attention or concern?
How can I obtain favor from the Lord (Proverbs 8:35)?

To obtain favor from the Lord according to Proverbs 8:35, one must find and embrace divine wisdom. While this proverb speaks of wisdom as a personified path to life, the New Testament reveals that Jesus Christ is the ultimate embodiment of the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24; 1 Corinthians 1:30). Therefore, to find wisdom and obtain favor is to find and trust in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

True favor is not a wage earned by human effort or moral performance, as the Scripture teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Instead, favor is a gift of grace received through faith. When a person is united to Christ, they are justified by faith alone and receive the favor that Christ Himself deserves, being accepted in the Beloved (Ephesians 1:6; Romans 5:1).

You may wonder if you have done enough to please a holy God or if His favor is even possible given your own failures. It is a common human concern to feel that we must earn our way into God's good graces. However, the Gospel sets us free from that burden. God is holy and sovereign, and our sins have separated us from Him. Yet, because of His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a sinless life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, ascending to the right hand of the Father as Lord of all.

This favor and the life mentioned in Proverbs are found only through Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?

As a practical step in seeking this wisdom, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Gospel of John. As you read, pray that God would open your eyes to see the glory of Christ. Additionally, seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word preached and find fellowship with other believers who are growing in the grace and knowledge of the Lord.
Why is solo Christo important?

Solo Christo, or Christ Alone, is the essential truth that Jesus Christ is the only mediator between a holy God and sinful humanity, and that salvation is found in no other person, work, or merit. This doctrine is vital because it protects the sufficiency of Christ's finished work and ensures that all glory for our redemption goes to God rather than to human effort or religious systems.

To understand why this is so important, we must first look at the character of God and the condition of man. God is infinitely holy, righteous, and just, while every human being has fallen short of His glory through sin (Romans 3:23). This sin creates a chasm that no amount of good works, religious rituals, or personal sincerity can bridge. If salvation were dependent even in part on our own performance, we would be left in utter despair, for our best works are like filthy rags before a perfect God (Isaiah 64:6).

Christ Alone is the answer to this human dilemma. Because Jesus is truly God and truly man (the hypostatic union), He alone could live the perfect life we failed to live and die the substitutionary death we deserved to die. On the cross, He bore the wrath of God for sin, satisfying divine justice. His bodily resurrection from the grave three days later proved His victory over sin and death, vindicating Him as the Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Scripture is uncompromising on this exclusivity. Jesus Himself said, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me" (John 14:6). The apostles echoed this, declaring, "Salvation exists in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved" (Acts 4:12). To add anything to the work of Christ, whether it be human merit, the intercession of saints, or religious ceremonies, is to suggest that His sacrifice was insufficient. 

If you are currently carrying the heavy burden of trying to make yourself "good enough" for God, I invite you to find rest in the finished work of Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, specifically focusing on how Jesus describes His own mission to save those who trust in Him. What is it that makes it difficult for you to rely on Christ alone rather than your own efforts? Reflect on the truth that "there is one God and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5). May you find great peace in knowing that His grace is enough.
Does Genesis chapter 1 literally mean 24 hour days?

The plain reading of Genesis chapter 1 indicates that the days of creation are literal, twenty-four-hour periods. This conclusion is drawn from the specific language and structure the Holy Spirit inspired Moses to use when recording the historical account of the world’s beginning.

The text defines each day with the repeated phrase, "And there was evening, and there was morning," which describes the natural cycle of a single solar day (Genesis 1:5; Genesis 1:8; Genesis 1:13; Genesis 1:19; Genesis 1:23; Genesis 1:31). Furthermore, when the Hebrew word for day, *yom*, is used alongside a number or the mention of evening and morning in the Old Testament, it consistently refers to a literal day. This historical reality is reinforced in the Law, where God bases the human six-day work week and the seventh-day Sabbath on the pattern He established during the creation week (Exodus 20:11).

From a theological perspective, a literal *hermeneutic* (the method of interpretation) honors the authority of Scripture as the final word on history. It presents God as the sovereign Creator who brought the universe into existence *ex nihilo*, which means out of nothing, simply by the power of His word.

Within the family of God, this is often considered a tertiary issue where faithful believers may differ in their interpretive emphasis. While the literal twenty-four-hour view is the most direct reading of the text, some Christians hold to the "Day-Age" view, suggesting these days represent long epochs of time, or the "Framework Hypothesis," which views the days as a literary structure to categorize God’s creative acts. These positions often stem from a desire to reconcile the biblical text with modern scientific theories or specific linguistic nuances in the Hebrew language.

However, we must remain united on the essential truth that God is the sole, purposeful Creator of all things. There is no room in the Christian faith for a view that denies God's direct hand in creation or the historical fall of man in the garden. As you reflect on the majesty of God’s work, let it lead you to a deeper trust in His power to sustain your life today.

A practical next step for you would be to read through the first two chapters of Genesis this week, focusing on the character of God displayed in His order and provision. As you do, talk to Him in prayer, thanking Him for the specific ways He has provided for you and acknowledging His total authority over all He has made.
What is the power of the Holy Spirit?

The power of the Holy Spirit is the sovereign, life-giving efficacy of the third Person of the Trinity, by which God executes His divine will in creation, revelation, and the transformation of human souls. It is not a mere impersonal force or energy, but the active, personal presence of the living God who brings order from chaos and spiritual life from the deadness of sin.

In the historical account of creation, the Spirit hovered over the waters to bring forth life (Genesis 1:2), and that same divine power was displayed most gloriously when He raised Jesus Christ from the dead (Romans 8:11). For the believer, this power is primarily experienced through **regeneration**, which is the supernatural work where the Spirit sovereignly grants a new heart, enabling a person to see and enter the kingdom of God (John 3:5; Titus 3:5).

The Holy Spirit also provides the necessary strength for **sanctification**, the ongoing process by which a believer is set apart from sin and made more like Jesus Christ in character and deed (Galatians 5:22–23; 2 Thessalonians 2:13). Furthermore, He empowers the church to be a bold witness for the Gospel, as Jesus promised that His followers would receive power when the Holy Spirit came upon them to take the message of salvation to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8).

You may feel the weight of your own moral failings or a sense of spiritual helplessness. It is a profound truth that no human effort can bridge the gap between a holy God and sinful man. We have all sinned and fall short of God's glory (Romans 3:23), but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. The power of the Holy Spirit is what opens our eyes to see our need for a Savior and points us to the finished work of Christ.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, specifically chapters 14, 15, and 16, where Jesus describes the work of the Holy Spirit. As you read, ask the Lord directly to help you understand His truth and to show you the beauty of Christ (John 16:13–14).
How is the word to be read and heard, that it may become effectual to salvation?

For the Word of God to become effectual to salvation, we must attend to it with diligence, preparation, and prayer, receiving it with faith and love, and hiding it in our hearts so that we may practice it in our lives. The Holy Spirit uses the Scriptures as the primary means of grace to enlighten the mind and renew the will, bringing a person into a right relationship with God.

First, we must approach the Scriptures with a heart prepared by prayer, asking the Lord to open our eyes to see the wonderful things in His law (Psalm 119:18). We do not read the Bible as we read a common book, but as the very breath of God, requiring our full attention and a humble, teachable spirit (James 1:21). This diligence involves not only reading the text but meditating upon its meaning and searching out its truths as one searches for hidden treasure.

Second, the Word must be mixed with faith in those who hear it (Hebrews 4:2). Without faith, the most glorious truths of the Gospel remain veiled. We must believe that God is who He says He is and that His promises are sure. Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of Christ (Romans 10:17). This faith is not a mere intellectual agreement but a deep, personal trust in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

Third, we must receive the truth with love, treasuring it in our innermost being. When we love the Word, we naturally desire to obey it. We are called to be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving ourselves (James 1:22). True salvation results in a transformed life, where we store up His word in our hearts that we might not sin against Him (Psalm 119:11).

The central message of the Word is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are naturally separated from Him and deserve His righteous judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Gospel of John, perhaps one chapter each morning. Before you read, ask the Holy Spirit to give you understanding and to show you the glory of Christ in the pages of His Word.
What is millennialism?

Millennialism is the theological study of the thousand year reign of Jesus Christ mentioned in the book of Revelation. It seeks to understand the nature, timing, and location of this reign in relation to Christ's second coming and the final judgment.

While all orthodox Christians agree that Jesus Christ will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead, the specific details of the millennium are considered a tertiary matter. This means that faithful, Bible believing Christians may disagree on these points while remaining in close fellowship. These differences often arise from how one interprets the highly symbolic language of apocalyptic literature and how one relates the Old Testament promises to the New Testament church.

There are three primary historical views regarding the millennium:

Premillennialism teaches that Jesus Christ will return to earth before the thousand years begin. In this view, Christ will establish a literal, visible kingdom on earth, reigning in person before the final resurrection and the creation of the new heavens and new earth. Historic premillennialism was common among early church fathers, while dispensational premillennialism, which often includes a distinct future for ethnic Israel and a rapture of the church, gained prominence in the nineteenth century. Revelation 20:1-6.

Amillennialism, a term meaning no literal millennium, holds that the thousand years is a symbolic figure representing the current church age. In this view, Christ is reigning now from His throne in heaven over the hearts of His people and through the ministry of His church. The millennium began at Christ's first coming and will conclude with His second coming. Ephesians 1:20-22; Colossians 1:13.

Postmillennialism suggests that Christ will return after the millennium. This view emphasizes the power of the Gospel to transform society, anticipating a golden age where the majority of the world is converted to Christ and righteousness prevails on earth. Following this period of great spiritual prosperity, Christ returns to judge the world and usher in the eternal state. Matthew 28:18-20; Psalm 22:27.

Regardless of which view one holds, the central hope for every believer is the glorious appearing of our Great God and Savior, Jesus Christ. Titus 2:13. The golden thread running through each of these perspectives is the absolute sovereignty of God and the ultimate victory of the Lamb who was slain. Revelation 5:12-13.

As you study these views, a helpful next step is to read Revelation 20 slowly and prayerfully, asking the Holy Spirit to fix your eyes on the King more than the calendar. You might also find it beneficial to discuss these views with an elder in your local church to see how your specific church community approaches these passages.

This great hope of Christ's return is only a comfort to those who have been reconciled to God. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God's holiness and deserve His just judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about war?

The Bible presents war as a tragic and inevitable reality of a fallen world, originating from the sinful desires of the human heart. James 4:1-2. While Scripture records war as a historical fact, it consistently points toward God’s sovereign control over history and His ultimate plan to bring about a kingdom of perfect peace through Jesus Christ.

From the earliest chapters of Genesis, we see the effects of original sin leading to violence and strife. In the Old Testament, God sometimes sovereignly commanded or utilized war as an instrument of divine judgment against nations that had abandoned all morality, such as the Canaanites. Deuteronomy 9:4-5. However, God also makes it clear that He does not delight in the death of the wicked and calls His people to be a light to the nations. Ezekiel 33:11.

In the New Testament, the focus shifts toward the spiritual nature of the believer's conflict and the role of civil government. Romans 13:4 teaches that the governing authority is God’s servant, an agent of wrath to bring punishment on the wrongdoer, which is why many believers affirm the necessity of the state to maintain order and protect the innocent, sometimes through the use of force. Yet, Jesus also commanded His followers to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them. Matthew 5:44.

Historically, faithful Christians have held different views regarding a believer's participation in war. One major view is Just War Theory, which suggests that war may be a mournful necessity if it is fought for a just cause, by a lawful authority, and as a last resort to restrain evil. Another historic view is Pacifism, which holds that Christ's call to non-resistance in the Sermon on the Mount precludes Christians from taking up arms under any circumstances. Matthew 5:38-39. Both perspectives seek to honor the Lordship of Christ in a world marred by total depravity.

Our greatest battle is not against other human beings, but against sin and the spiritual forces of evil. Ephesians 6:12. We all stand in a state of rebellion against a holy and just God, deserving of His judgment. The wonderful hope of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ, the Prince of Peace, took the judgment we deserved upon Himself. He lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers reconciliation with God to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes it difficult for you to lay down your own rebellion and trust in Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the Sermon on the Mount in Matthew 5-7 this week. As you read, pray and ask God to help you understand how to live as a peacemaker in your own community, reflecting the character of Christ to those around you. Matthew 5:9.
Are there errors in the Qur'an (Koran)?

You may wonder how one can discern between different claims of divine revelation, especially when two texts claim to be the Word of the same God yet offer fundamentally different accounts of history and salvation. From the perspective of the Bible, which is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, the Qur'an contains significant errors because it contradicts the historical facts and spiritual truths that God has revealed in Scripture.

The most critical contradictions concern the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Qur'an denies that Jesus is the Son of God and rejects the doctrine of the Trinity, suggesting that God has no partners and was not begotten, as seen in Surah 112:3 and 5:72. However, the Bible clearly teaches the deity of Christ and the triune nature of God. Jesus said, "I and the Father are one," in John 10:30, and the Great Commission commands baptism in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit in Matthew 28:19.

Furthermore, the Qur'an claims in Surah 4:157 that Jesus was not actually crucified or killed, but that it only appeared so to the onlookers. This contradicts not only the eyewitness accounts of the Apostles but also the established historical fact of the crucifixion. The death of Jesus is the very heart of the Gospel. Scripture tells us that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that He was buried, and that He was raised on the third day in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Without His physical death and bodily resurrection, there is no payment for sin and no hope for eternal life, as stated in Romans 4:25 and 1 Peter 1:3.

Finally, the Qur'an presents a path to God based on human effort and the weighing of deeds, whereas the Bible reveals that salvation is a gift of grace that cannot be earned. We are taught that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God in Romans 3:23, and that for by grace you have been saved through faith; and this is not of yourselves, it is the gift of God; not of works, so that no one may boast in Ephesians 2:8-9.

These are not merely different perspectives but represent a fundamental departure from the truth of who God is and how He saves. God is holy, and our sin has created a separation that we cannot bridge by our own merit. Because of His great love, God the Son took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died in the place of sinners to satisfy the justice of God. He rose again, proving His victory over death.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the spiritual significance to a déjà vu experience?

Déjà vu is the subjective sensation that a current event or experience has happened exactly this way before. While the Bible does not explicitly name or describe this neurological phenomenon, we can understand it spiritually through the lens of God's design of the human mind and His absolute sovereignty over time.

You may wonder if these startling moments are a form of spiritual premonition or a sign of a "past life." It is important to ground our understanding in the truth that our subjective feelings are not a source of divine revelation. The Bible is our final and sufficient authority for understanding reality (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Specifically, Scripture refutes the idea of reincarnation, stating clearly that "it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment" (Hebrews 9:27). 

From a theological perspective, déjà vu can be seen as a reminder of our human finitude. We are creatures bound by time and limited by the complexities of our own minds, which can sometimes produce echoes of memory or recognition that we cannot fully explain. In contrast, God is the sovereign Creator who exists outside of time. He sees the end from the beginning and has ordained every moment of our lives. As it is written in Psalm 139:16, "Your eyes saw my unformed body; all the days ordained for me were written in Your book before one of them came to be."

When you experience such a feeling, do not be unsettled or seek hidden meanings in the sensation itself. Instead, let the moment of "familiarity" serve as a prompt to acknowledge the providence of God. Providence is the truth that God is continually upholding, directing, and governing all creatures and events. Even the most fleeting or mysterious sensations of the mind are under His care.

A helpful practical step is to turn that moment of surprise into a moment of prayer. You might simply say, "Lord, I do not understand how my mind works, but I thank You that You are here in this moment and that my life is held in Your hands." This shifts your focus from a confusing internal feeling to the objective reality of God’s presence.

We are all like a vapor that appears for a little while and then vanishes (James 4:14), but we serve a God who is the same yesterday, today, and forever. If you ever feel lost in the mysteries of your own experience, look to Jesus Christ. Through His death and resurrection, He has secured a future for His people that is not a mere repetition of the past, but a glorious new creation. You can rest in the certainty of His Word above any passing feeling.
What are the four beasts in Daniel chapter 7?

In Daniel 7, the prophet sees a vision of four great beasts arising from the sea, which represent four successive earthly kingdoms that would dominate the biblical world. According to the plain reading of Scripture and historic Christian interpretation, these beasts are identified as the empires of Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, and Rome.

The first beast was like a lion with the wings of an eagle, symbolizing the majesty and swift conquests of the Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar. Daniel 7:4. As the vision progresses, the lion's wings are plucked and it is given the heart of a man, reflecting the humbling and restoration of the king of Babylon.

The second beast was like a bear, raised up on one of its sides with three ribs in its mouth, representing the Medo-Persian Empire. Daniel 7:5. The lopsided posture signifies the dominance of the Persians over the Medes within their alliance, while the ribs symbolize the major nations they devoured to establish their power.

The third beast appeared like a leopard with four wings and four heads, which represents the Grecian Empire. Daniel 7:6. The leopard is known for its speed, mirrored by the unprecedented rapidity of Alexander the Great's conquests. After his death, his kingdom was divided among his four generals, symbolized by the four heads.

The fourth beast was terrifying, exceedingly strong, and possessed large iron teeth that crushed everything in its path, representing the Roman Empire. Daniel 7:7. This beast was distinct from all the others, possessing ten horns and eventually a little horn that spoke boastfully against the Most High. Daniel 7:8; Daniel 7:25.

While these beasts are fearsome and describe the brutal nature of human government apart from God, the climax of the vision is not the beasts, but the Ancient of Days sitting in judgment. The Lord Jesus Christ, described as the Son of Man, is given an everlasting dominion that will never pass away. Daniel 7:13-14. This reminds us that despite the chaotic rise and fall of nations, God remains sovereign over history. He is the one who removes kings and sets up kings. Daniel 2:21.

As you reflect on these truths, take comfort in knowing that the kingdom of Christ is the only one that stands forever. You might find it helpful to read the entire seventh chapter of Daniel today, paying special attention to the victory of the Son of Man, and then spend a moment in prayer thanking God that His purposes cannot be thwarted by any earthly power. If you find yourself troubled by the state of the world, remember that Christ has already won the victory over sin and death through His resurrection, and He is coming again to make all things new.
What did Paul mean when he said, “I die daily” in 1 Corinthians 15:31?

In 1 Corinthians 15:31, the Apostle Paul is referring to the constant physical danger and the imminent threat of martyrdom he faced every day for the sake of the Gospel. He uses this vivid expression to emphasize that his life was perpetually at risk because of his unwavering witness for Jesus Christ.

This statement is situated within Paul's masterful defense of the bodily resurrection of the dead. He argues that if Christ has not been raised, then his daily endurance of life-threatening "perils every hour" would be utterly foolish (1 Corinthians 15:30). Paul’s willingness to face death daily was fueled by his absolute certainty in the historical fact of the resurrection, knowing that since Christ has been raised as the firstfruits, those who belong to Him will also be raised to eternal life (1 Corinthians 15:20; 1 Corinthians 15:23).

While the primary context is Paul’s physical suffering, there is a profound spiritual application for every believer regarding the doctrine of sanctification. To "die daily" also suggests a life of continual surrender, where we crucify our sinful desires and selfish ambitions to live for the glory of God (Luke 9:23; Galatians 2:20). It is the daily decision to submit to the Lordship of Christ, counting ourselves dead to sin but alive to God (Romans 6:11).

If you find yourself struggling with fear or the weight of earthly trials, take heart in the reality that the tomb is empty. As a practical step this week, I encourage you to spend time in prayer each morning, specifically asking the Holy Spirit to help you "yield your members as instruments of righteousness" (Romans 6:13). Consider reading 1 Corinthians 15 in its entirety to strengthen your hope in the coming resurrection.

This hope is only found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We must recognize that God is perfectly holy, yet we have all sinned and are separated from Him. In His great mercy, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human flesh, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice on our behalf. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating death forever. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

What holds you back from fully trusting in Him today?
What does the Bible say about motivation?

The Bible teaches that motivation is primarily a matter of the heart, emphasizing that God weighs our internal intentions as much as our outward actions. True biblical motivation is rooted in the desire to honor God in every aspect of life, moving beyond mere self interest or the praise of others. Proverbs 16:2 states, "All a man’s ways seem right to him, but the LORD weighs the motives."

The Scriptures call believers to a singular focus known as *Soli Deo Gloria*, which means living for the glory of God alone. When our primary motivation is to please the Lord, every task, no matter how small, becomes an act of worship. 1 Corinthians 10:31 instructs, "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God." This shift in perspective protects us from the exhaustion of "people pleasing" and anchors our worth in God's approval rather than human applause. Colossians 3:23-24 adds, "Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters, since you know that you will receive an inheritance from the Lord as a reward. It is the Lord Christ you are serving."

The reality is that our hearts are often fragmented, and we frequently find ourselves motivated by pride, fear, or selfishness. This struggle reveals our deep need for the Gospel. We cannot manufacture pure motives on our own because our nature is fallen. However, Jesus Christ lived with a perfectly pure heart, always doing what pleased the Father. Through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection, He paid the debt for our sins and provides the Holy Spirit to renew our desires. This is the beauty of sanctification, where God works within us to align our will with His.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a transformed heart. What is it that currently drives your daily decisions, and how might trusting in Christ change that focus?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to pause before your next major task today and ask the Lord in prayer to help you perform it for His honor rather than for your own credit or out of mere habit. Seeking the counsel of a mature believer in your local church can also provide accountability as you learn to discern the inclinations of your heart.
What does it mean to put off the old man (Ephesians 4:22)?

To put off the old man means to decisively reject and abandon the sinful nature, habits, and lifestyle that characterized your life before you were brought into a relationship with God through Jesus Christ. It is a spiritual stripping away of the "filthy rags" of our fallen identity, much like one would discard tattered, soiled clothing that no longer fits who they are.

This instruction is a call to the daily work of progressive sanctification. While the Bible teaches that our old self was legally crucified with Christ at the moment of our salvation, as stated in Romans 6:6, we are still commanded to practically "put off" the remnants of that old nature in our daily conduct. This process is often called the mortification of sin, which is the act of putting to death the deeds of the body through the power of the Holy Spirit. The old man is described as being "corrupt through deceitful desires," meaning that the impulses of our former life are based on lies that promise satisfaction but only lead to spiritual decay, as noted in Ephesians 4:22.

Scripture provides a clear picture of this transition. In Colossians 3:9, we are told, "Do not lie to one another, seeing that you have put off the old self with its practices." This change is not merely a matter of self improvement or moral effort; it is a fundamental shift in identity. The old man is governed by the flesh and its pride, while the "new man" is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its Creator, according to Colossians 3:10.

To practice this, you must first recognize that you cannot conquer the old man by your own willpower. It begins with a mind that is being "renewed in the spirit," as mentioned in Ephesians 4:23. A practical step you can take this week is to identify one specific "old man" habit, such as a tendency toward harsh speech or a heart of discontent, and when you feel that impulse rising, consciously stop and ask the Holy Spirit to help you "put it off." Immediately seek to "put on" the opposite grace, such as a word of kindness or a prayer of gratitude.

This transformation is only possible because of the Gospel. We were all once separated from God by our sin, under His righteous judgment. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. If you have not done so, you can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and a new life. What is it that might be hindering you from fully trusting Him today?
What is a freethinker?

A freethinker is someone who forms their opinions and beliefs based on reason, logic, and empirical evidence rather than by relying on tradition, authority, or religious revelation. In contemporary usage, the term is frequently adopted by those who reject the supernatural claims of the Bible and the historic doctrines of the Christian faith in favor of a secular or naturalistic worldview.

You may deeply value intellectual honesty and the desire to avoid being misled by unexamined traditions or empty superstitions. This pursuit of truth and the careful use of the mind are honorable, as the ability to reason is a gift from God, who created us in His own image. However, the Bible teaches that human reason is not a neutral or independent tool. Because of the fall of man, our minds are subject to the noetic effects of sin, meaning our thinking is often clouded by a desire for autonomy from our Creator (Romans 1:21; Ephesians 4:17-18).

True intellectual freedom is not found in independence from God, but in submission to the Truth that sets us free (John 8:32). While the freethinker seeks a foundation for knowledge within themselves, Scripture declares that "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge" (Proverbs 1:7). To think rightly about the world, we must acknowledge the One who created it and provides the very laws of logic we use. We are called to "destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ" (2 Corinthians 10:5).

Ultimately, our greatest need is not just better logic, but a restored relationship with God. We have all turned to our own way, seeking to be our own authorities, which is the heart of sin. Yet, God demonstrated His love by sending Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by our intellectual achievements or works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. A practical next step for your journey would be to read the Gospel of John, observing how Jesus speaks with both profound reason and divine authority. What holds you back from trusting Him as the foundation of your life and thought?
What is an overseer in the church?

An overseer is a man called by God to the office of spiritual leadership and supervision within a local church, primarily tasked with teaching the Word and shepherding the congregation. The New Testament uses the term overseer, translated from the Greek *episkopos*, interchangeably with the terms elder (*presbuteros*) and pastor (*poimen*) to describe the same office of leadership (Acts 20:17; Acts 20:28; Titus 1:5; Titus 1:7).

The Bible provides clear and stringent qualifications for those who desire this "noble task" (1 Timothy 3:1). An overseer must be above reproach, the husband of one wife, sober-minded, self-controlled, respectable, hospitable, and, crucially, able to teach (1 Timothy 3:2-7). He must not be a recent convert, lest he become conceited, and he must manage his own household well, for if a man cannot care for his own family, he cannot care for God’s church (1 Timothy 3:4-6).

The work of an overseer is a labor of love and a weight of holy responsibility. These men are called to shepherd the flock of God, exercising oversight not under compulsion or for shameful gain, but willingly and eagerly as God would have them (1 Peter 5:2). Their ministry involves protecting the congregation from false teachers, nourishing the believers with sound doctrine, and modeling a Christ-like life for all to see. Many historic Christian traditions, including the Reformed Baptist confession, recognize that the New Testament typically describes a plurality of elders in each local church to provide mutual accountability and shared wisdom (Acts 14:23; Philippians 1:1).

Because overseers watch over your souls as those who must give an account to God, the Scriptures call the church to obey and submit to them so that their work may be a joy rather than a burden (Hebrews 13:17). 

As a practical step in your walk with Christ, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray specifically for the overseers in your local church. Ask the Lord to grant them wisdom, protect their families, and keep them faithful to the truth of the Gospel. You might even reach out to one of them to ask how you can specifically support their ministry this month.
Someone gave me a prophetic word/prophesied over me. What is the interpretation?

The Bible teaches us that the Word of God is our final and sufficient authority for all matters of life and godliness, and any message claimed to be from the Lord must be tested with great care. While I cannot provide an interpretation of a personal message given to you by another individual, I can offer you the biblical principles by which you may discern whether such a word is truly from God.

In the history of the church, there are different understandings of how God speaks today. Many faithful Christians, often called cessationists, believe that the miraculous gift of prophecy served a foundational purpose for the early church and ceased once the New Testament was completed. They hold that God now speaks with absolute sufficiency through the Holy Scripture alone (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Hebrews 1:1-2). Others, known as continuationists, believe that God may still grant spiritual impressions or "words" to believers today, though they emphasize that these are never equal to the Bible and must be strictly subordinate to it.

Regardless of these different views, the primary rule for every believer is to test everything and hold fast to what is good (1 Thessalonians 5:20-21). If a "prophetic word" contradicts the clear teaching of the Bible, it is not from God. If it leads you away from the local church, encourages pride, or creates a sense of frantic urgency that bypasses sober-minded wisdom, it should be treated with extreme caution. God is not the author of confusion but of peace (1 Corinthians 14:33).

True discernment is a fruit of knowing the Voice of the Good Shepherd as He has already spoken in His Word. If you are seeking to understand a specific message, I encourage you to take the following steps in your walk with Christ:

First, compare the message to the written Word of God. The Bible is the only infallible guide we possess, and it contains everything necessary for your salvation and growth in holiness. Second, seek the counsel of your local church elders or mature, godly believers who know you well. God often provides clarity through the community of faith and the wisdom of those who are steeped in the Scriptures (Proverbs 11:14). Third, do not let a "word" from a person overshadow the glorious "Word" of the Gospel. 

Our greatest need is not a new revelation about our circumstances, but a deeper trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ. He lived the perfect life we could not live, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to secure our justification (Romans 4:25; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). If you have not yet placed your full weight on Him, I invite you to turn from your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who is the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6).

Take this word you received and lay it before the Lord in prayer, asking Him to give you a heart that loves His written Word above all else. What is the main concern or feeling you have regarding this prophecy you were given?
What is/isn’t a Christian married couple allowed to do, sexually?

The Bible teaches that sexual intimacy within the covenant of marriage is a holy, beautiful, and God ordained gift intended for the procreation of children, the deepening of relational unity, and the mutual delight of the husband and wife. Scripture declares that marriage should be honored by all and the marriage bed kept pure, for God will judge the adulterer and all the sexually immoral, as seen in Hebrews 13:4.

Within the safety of the marriage covenant, a Christian couple enjoys a wide margin of Christian liberty, yet this freedom is always governed by the higher laws of love, holiness, and mutual consent.

**What is Encouraged and Commanded**
The primary command regarding marital intimacy is that it be characterized by selflessness and regular engagement. The Apostle Paul instructs that the husband should fulfill his marital duty to his wife, and likewise the wife to her husband, in 1 Corinthians 7:3. This means that sexual intimacy is not a tool for manipulation or a reward for good behavior, but a debt of love owed to one another.

Furthermore, the Bible celebrates the physical joy of marriage. The Book of Proverbs encourages a husband to be intoxicated always by the love and physical charms of his wife in Proverbs 5:18-19. This demonstrates that God is not a cosmic killjoy but the very author of the pleasure found in the "one flesh" union described in Genesis 2:24.

**What is Forbidden**
While there is great freedom, there are clear biblical boundaries that must never be crossed:
1. **The Introduction of Third Parties:** Any inclusion of a third person, whether physically or through the use of pornography, is a violation of the marriage covenant. Jesus warned that anyone who looks at a woman lustfully has already committed adultery with her in his heart, in Matthew 5:28. The marriage bed must remain exclusive.
2. **Coercion and Selfishness:** While 1 Corinthians 7:4 notes that a spouse has authority over the other's body, this must be interpreted through the lens of Christlike sacrificial love. A husband is called to love his wife as Christ loved the church, in Ephesians 5:25. Any act that is forced, painful, or degrading to a spouse violates the command to honor one another.
3. **Acts that Defile the Conscience:** If a particular practice causes one spouse to feel a sense of guilt or shame, or if it violates their conscience, it should not be pursued. Scripture reminds us that everything that does not proceed from faith is sin, in Romans 14:23.

**Principles for Christian Liberty**
Regarding specific sexual acts not explicitly mentioned in Scripture, such as oral or anal sex, faithful Christians have historically held that these fall under the realm of private conscience and mutual agreement. As you navigate these choices, ask yourselves three questions: Is it motivated by love? Is it truly consensual and pleasing to both? Does it preserve the dignity of my spouse as an image bearer of God?

As a practical step, I encourage you to set aside time to speak openly and tenderly with your spouse about your desires and boundaries. Seek to serve the other’s joy above your own, and pray together that your physical union would always be a pointer to the greater spiritual union between Christ and His Church.

If you are struggling with guilt from the past or find that sexual sin has hindered your intimacy, remember that there is profound hope in the Gospel. We are all sinners in need of grace, but through the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ, there is full forgiveness and the power for sanctification. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive a new heart and a restored life. What is it that might be keeping you from fully resting in His grace today?
Why is God going to give us a white stone with a new name (Revelation 2:17)?

The white stone mentioned in Revelation 2:17 is a beautiful symbol of the believer’s total acquittal from sin and their intimate, permanent welcome into the kingdom of God. It serves as a personal token of Christ’s victory and His individual love for every person who overcomes by faith.

In the ancient world, white stones carried several significant meanings that help us understand this promise. In a legal context, when a jury sat in judgment, a black stone signified a vote of "guilty," while a white stone signified "not guilty" or acquittal. For the Christian, this represents the glorious truth of justification. Though we were once guilty before a holy God, because of the finished work of Jesus Christ, we are given the "white stone" of righteousness. There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus, as stated in Romans 8:1.

Furthermore, white stones were often used as tokens of admission. A victor in the athletic games might receive a stone inscribed with his name, which served as his "ticket" into a special banquet. In this sense, the stone represents our invitation to the marriage supper of the Lamb and our right to enter the New Jerusalem. It is a sign of our adoption into the family of God and our belonging in His eternal presence.

The new name written on the stone, known only to the one who receives it, speaks of a profound and personal intimacy between you and your Savior. In Scripture, a name change often signifies a transformation of character and a new beginning, much like when Abram became Abraham or Simon became Peter. God promises in Isaiah 62:2 that His people will be called by a new name that the mouth of the LORD will bestow. This name represents your unique identity in Christ, a secret bond of love and fellowship that the world cannot understand or take away.

If you find yourself struggling with feelings of shame or the weight of your past, remember that in Christ, the "black stone" of judgment has been cast aside forever. Your identity is no longer defined by your failures, but by the name Christ has given you.

As a practical step this week, I encourage you to spend time in prayer, specifically thanking God for the new identity He has given you. Instead of listening to the accusations of the enemy or the labels the world places on you, choose to meditate on what God says about you in His Word. You are chosen, redeemed, and deeply loved.

If you have not yet placed your trust in Jesus, know that He is the only one who can turn a heart of stone into a heart of flesh and give you this promise of eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive His forgiveness and this new name. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the meaning of the strange fire in Leviticus 10:1?

The strange fire in Leviticus 10:1 refers to an unauthorized offering of incense presented to God by Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron the High Priest. This fire was called strange because it was something the Lord had not commanded, signifying an attempt to worship God through human innovation rather than divine instruction.

As priests, Nadab and Abihu were responsible for following the precise regulations God had established for the Tabernacle. Just before this event, God had displayed His glory by sending His own fire to consume the legitimate sacrifices on the altar (Leviticus 9:24). By offering their own fire, these men ignored the sacred source and the specific timing God required. Their actions showed a lack of reverence for the holiness of God, treating the worship of the Almighty as something they could manipulate according to their own will.

This historical event underscores the doctrine of God's holiness. God told Aaron that among those who approach Him, He must be treated as holy and displayed as glorious (Leviticus 10:3). The immediate judgment of Nadab and Abihu was a clear, historical testimony that God is sovereign and will not be approached on human terms. It serves as a warning against "will-worship" or the idea that our sincerity is more important than our obedience to God's Word.

The gravity of this judgment also points us toward the necessity of a perfect Mediator. Because God is holy and His wrath against sin is real, we cannot approach Him based on our own merits or our own "fire" (Hebrews 12:28-29). We need a High Priest who is truly holy, and that is Jesus Christ. He is the one who offered a perfect, authorized sacrifice through His own blood, satisfying the requirements of God's justice once for all (Hebrews 9:11-12; Hebrews 10:12-14).

You may wonder how a God so holy could ever accept someone who is imperfect. The beauty of the Gospel is that while God’s holiness requires judgment for sin, His love provided the sacrifice. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took the judgment we deserved upon Himself. He died for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?

I encourage you to read through the book of Hebrews, specifically chapters 9 and 10, to see how Christ fulfills the roles that the earthly priests could only foreshadow. As a practical next step, spend time in prayer this week thanking God that because of Jesus, we can now approach His throne of grace with confidence (Hebrews 4:14-16).
Is it possible to sell your soul to the devil?

The short answer is no, you cannot literally sell your soul to the devil, because your soul does not belong to you to sell. Every human soul belongs to Almighty God by right of creation, as He declares in Ezekiel 18:4, "Behold, all souls are mine."

While popular culture and folklore often depict people signing contracts with the powers of darkness, the Bible presents a different reality. Satan is a defeated foe and a liar who has no legal authority to "purchase" what God has created. However, the Scriptures teach that because of our shared rebellion in Adam, every human being is born "sold under sin," as described in Romans 7:14. We do not need to make a deal with the devil to be lost, for we are already naturally estranged from God and in bondage to our own sinful desires.

You may wonder if a person can wander so far into darkness or occult practices that they are beyond the reach of God's mercy. It is a common fear that one's sins are too heavy or their past too dark for grace to cover. But the truth of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ came specifically to "redeem" those under the law, which means to buy them back from the marketplace of sin. This redemption was not paid to the devil, but was a satisfaction of God's own holy justice.

The Gospel is the good news that God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, fulfilling the law we broke. He then died a substitutionary, atoning death on the cross, taking upon Himself the wrath that our sins deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin, death, and the power of the devil (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Colossians 2:15). Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or "contracts" of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

No matter what you have done or what lies you have believed, the blood of Jesus is sufficient to cleanse any stain. He is the sovereign Lord who holds the keys of death and Hades (Revelation 1:18). If you feel the weight of your sin or the fear of spiritual darkness, know that Christ is a perfect and powerful Savior for all who come to Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the authority and love of Jesus firsthand, and reach out to a faithful, Bible-teaching church in your community where you can find support and discipleship. What is it that makes you concerned about this question today?
What does the Bible say about obedience?

The Bible teaches that obedience is the natural and necessary fruit of a heart that has been transformed by God’s grace. It is the outward expression of an inward love for Christ and a sincere trust in His sovereign authority over all of life.

Scripture makes a vital distinction between legalism, which seeks to earn God's favor through works, and Gospel obedience, which flows from a heart already secure in His love. We are not justified, or made right with God, by our ability to keep the Law, for "no one will be justified in His sight by works of the law" (Romans 3:20). Instead, we are saved by grace alone through faith alone. However, this saving faith always results in a life marked by "the obedience of faith" (Romans 1:5). This process of being made more like Christ is called sanctification.

Our Lord Jesus emphasized the motive behind our actions when He said, "If you love Me, you will keep My commandments" (John 14:15). To the believer, God's commands are not a heavy burden but a path of delight and safety. True obedience is "better than sacrifice" (1 Samuel 15:22) because it demonstrates that our hearts are truly surrendered to His will rather than our own. When we walk in obedience, we experience the sweetness of fellowship with the Father, as Jesus promised: "If anyone loves Me, he will keep My word. My Father will love him, and We will come to him and make Our home with him" (John 14:23).

You may wonder how anyone can truly satisfy God’s standard when we all fall so short. The truth is that because of our sin, none of us has obeyed God perfectly, and this disobedience carries the weight of His just judgment. But the Gospel is the beautiful news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, lived a life of perfect, sinless obedience on our behalf. He then took the punishment for our disobedience by dying on the cross and rising from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a new heart.

In response to such great mercy, we are called to "walk in the good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do" (Ephesians 2:10). A practical next step in your walk with God is to identify one specific area of your life where you have been hesitant to submit to His Word. Bring this before Him in prayer, asking for the Holy Spirit’s help to align your will with His, and seek the accountability of a mature believer in your local church to help you walk faithfully. What is one area of your life where you find it most difficult to trust and obey Him?
What does it mean that God is our refuge and strength and an ever-present help in trouble (Psalm 46:1)?

To say God is our refuge and strength means that He is our immediate place of safety and our source of internal power when we face life's most overwhelming storms. This verse assures us that God is not a distant observer of our pain but a proven and accessible ally who stands ready to support His people in the midst of distress (Psalm 46:1).

In the ancient world, a refuge was often a high tower or a fortified city where a person could flee for protection from an advancing enemy. When you feel exposed to the trials of life or the "fiery darts of the wicked one" (Ephesians 6:16), God invites you to find your security in His unchanging character rather than your shifting circumstances. He is a stronghold that cannot be shaken (Proverbs 18:10).

Furthermore, He is our strength, providing the spiritual and emotional fortitude necessary to endure what we cannot change. This is not a strength we produce within ourselves through human effort, but a divine sufficiency that is made perfect in our weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9). The term "ever-present help" carries the meaning of being "abundantly found" or "well-proved." He is closer to the believer than the very trouble that surrounds them. Even when the earth gives way or the mountains are moved into the heart of the sea, the Lord of hosts remains sovereign and near to His children (Psalm 46:2-7).

This security is ultimately realized through the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we were once separated from God and without a true refuge. Yet, God in His mercy sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross, rising bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat sin and death. In Christ, we find the ultimate refuge from judgment and the power to live a new life by grace. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and eternal peace.

When the winds of anxiety begin to rise, it is helpful to "be still, and know that I am God" (Psalm 46:10). An actionable step for you today is to set aside a few minutes this evening to read the entirety of Psalm 46. As you read, identify each attribute of God mentioned and then bring your specific burdens to Him in prayer, asking Him to be the refuge you need in this moment. What is the greatest concern weighing on your heart today that you need to entrust to His strength?
Should a Christian woman wear a bikini?

The question of whether a Christian woman should wear a bikini is a matter of Christian liberty and personal conscience, guided by the biblical principles of modesty, self-control, and love for one’s neighbor. Scripture does not provide a specific dress code for every setting, but it calls believers to represent the character of Christ in all things, including how we clothe ourselves.

In the historic Christian faith, this is considered a tertiary issue where faithful believers may reach different conclusions based on their understanding of how to apply biblical principles to modern culture.

One view held by many Christians is that the bikini is inherently designed to draw attention to the body in a way that may not align with the biblical call to modesty. They would point to 1 Timothy 2:9, which encourages women to adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control. From this perspective, choosing swimwear that provides more coverage is seen as a way to guard one's own heart against vanity and to act in love toward brothers in Christ by not providing an unnecessary temptation to lust, as warned in Romans 14:13.

Another view emphasizes Christian liberty and the importance of the heart’s intent. Those who hold this view believe that in certain contexts, such as a private family setting or a culture where such attire is standard and not intended to be provocative, a bikini can be worn without violating biblical principles. They would argue that modesty is more about the posture of the heart than a specific measurement of fabric, though they would still affirm that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit and should be treated with dignity, according to 1 Corinthians 6:19-20.

Ultimately, every choice we make should be an act of stewardship over the bodies God has given us. When deciding on swimwear, it is helpful to ask yourself whether your choice reflects a desire to be noticed by the world or a desire to honor the Lord who redeemed you. We must remember that we are called to look out for the interests of others and to seek the glory of God in everything we do, as noted in 1 Corinthians 10:31.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time in prayer, asking the Lord to give you a clear conscience and wisdom. You might also seek the counsel of a mature, godly woman in your local church who can offer personal guidance and help you think through how to balance your freedom in Christ with the call to be a light in the world.
Is Christian rap music appropriate?

The Bible does not provide a list of approved musical genres, making the appropriateness of Christian rap a matter of Christian liberty and personal conscience. Because Scripture focuses more on the content of the heart and the truth of the lyrics than on the specific style of the melody, faithful Christians often arrive at different conclusions on this topic.

Many believers see Christian rap as a vibrant and powerful tool for the Gospel. They argue that music is a medium created by God that can be sanctified for His service, regardless of the cultural rhythm used. If the lyrics are rich in biblical truth and the artist’s life reflects the fruit of the Spirit, the music can reach people who might otherwise never hear the Word of God. These Christians often point to the call to praise God with a variety of sounds and instruments, as seen in Psalm 150:1–6, and the command to do everything for the glory of God, as noted in 1 Corinthians 10:31.

Other believers express a more cautious view, concerned that the cultural associations of rap (which often include themes of rebellion or worldly pride) may be difficult to separate from the message of the Cross. They may feel that the aggressive nature of the style lacks the reverence and sobriety required for the Christian life. These believers often emphasize the importance of being not conformed to this world but transformed by the renewal of the mind, as stated in Romans 12:2.

In matters of tertiary doctrine such as this, we must walk in charity and grace toward one another. The Apostle Paul teaches that each person should be fully convinced in their own mind and that we should avoid judging a brother or sister on matters where the Bible is not explicit, according to Romans 14:5; Romans 14:13.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to evaluate any music you listen to by its "fruit" in your life.
