However, the Bible teaches that the Word was God and became flesh to save us, John 1:1; John 1:14. Our Lord Jesus claimed divine identity when He said, "Before Abraham was born, I am," John 8:58. Without His deity, His sacrifice would lack the infinite value necessary to reconcile us to a holy God, Colossians 1:19-20.

You may wonder why such precise theological definitions are necessary for a simple faith in God. The reason is that a "Jesus" who is not God is a Jesus who cannot save. If we strip Christ of His divinity, we lose the Gospel itself. The Gospel is the good news that the holy God, who is just and must punish sin, sent His eternal Son to take on human nature. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the wrath of God, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death, 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own moral efforts or reason, Ephesians 2:8-9. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, who died and rose for you to provide forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in the full deity and sufficiency of Jesus today?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John and note every instance where Jesus claims divine authority or attributes. It is also helpful to study the Nicene Creed, which was written by the early church to defend the true deity of Christ against similar errors. Seek to ground your heart in a local, Bible believing church where the true Gospel is preached with clarity and joy.
Can an unmarried man be a deacon or elder?

Yes, a single man can biblically serve as an elder or a deacon. While the New Testament lists the qualifications for these offices in 1 Timothy 3:1–13 and Titus 1:5–9, the requirement that a candidate be the "husband of one wife" is understood by the vast majority of faithful Bible scholars and pastors as a mandate for moral purity rather than a mandate for marriage.

The phrase "husband of one wife" in 1 Timothy 3:2 and Titus 1:6 literally translates from the Greek as a "one-woman man." This is a character qualification requiring that if a man is married, he must be a faithful, monogamous husband who is transitionally and heart-fully devoted to his wife. It excludes polygamists, those involved in sexual immorality, or those who are unfaithful. It does not, however, exclude those who are single or those who have been widowed.

If marriage were an absolute requirement for church office, the Apostle Paul would have been disqualified from the very offices he was defining. Furthermore, Paul argues in 1 Corinthians 7:32–35 that the unmarried man is actually "anxious about the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord," and can offer "undivided devotion" to the ministry. Jesus Christ, the Chief Shepherd and Head of the Church, was Himself unmarried. To suggest that marriage is a prerequisite for leadership would mean that the most perfect Example of leadership would be ineligible for office in His own church.

In the historic Reformed Baptist tradition, as well as broader evangelicalism, there is a high regard for the family as a training ground for leadership. 1 Timothy 3:4–5 notes that an elder must "manage his own household well," for if he cannot lead his family, how can he care for God's church? However, a single man can demonstrate this ability through the stewardship of his own life, his finances, and his conduct within the church family.

As you consider this question, perhaps in relation to your own life or your local church, I encourage you to focus on the totality of the biblical qualifications. If you are a single man sensing a call to ministry, continue to cultivate "undivided devotion" to Christ. Speak with your local church elders about their specific convictions and how they might help you discern your giftedness and character according to the Word of God.
Is it better, spiritually, to be poor than to be rich?

Scripture teaches that spiritual maturity is not determined by the amount of one's earthly possessions, but by the state of one's heart and its devotion to God. While neither wealth nor poverty possesses an inherent moral quality, both conditions present unique spiritual opportunities and significant dangers.

The Lord Jesus cautioned that wealth can be a profound hindrance to entering the kingdom of heaven because it often fosters a false sense of self-sufficiency. He famously remarked that it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God in Matthew 19:23-24. The danger is not the money itself, but the love of it, which is described as a root of all kinds of evil in 1 Timothy 6:10. Wealth can easily become an idol, competing with God for the throne of our hearts.

Conversely, poverty is not a guarantee of godliness. While God expresses a deep concern for the poor and often uses their lack to foster a greater reliance on Him, poverty can also lead to the temptation to bitterness or the sin of theft to satisfy basic needs. The wisdom of Agur in Proverbs 30:8-9 provides a balanced perspective, asking God to give him neither poverty nor riches, lest he be full and deny God or be poor and steal, profaning the name of the Lord.

The true spiritual standard is found in being poor in spirit, which Jesus identified as the first mark of those who belong to the kingdom of heaven in Matthew 5:3. To be poor in spirit is to acknowledge one's total spiritual bankruptcy before a holy God, regardless of the balance in one's bank account. Whether you have much or little, the call of the Christian is to practice contentment and faithful stewardship, recognizing that everything we possess belongs to God.

If you find yourself fixated on your financial status, whether in pride over what you have or in anxiety over what you lack, remember that the greatest treasure is found in Christ alone. We are all naturally separated from God by our sin, and no amount of money can buy our way back to Him, nor can any amount of suffering or poverty earn His favor. God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rise again to secure eternal life for all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through Philippians 4:11-13 this week. Reflect on how the Apostle Paul learned the secret of being content in every situation through the strength of Christ. You might also find it helpful to speak with a pastor at a local church about how to honor God with your resources, whatever they may be. What is it about your current financial situation that most challenges your trust in God?
Will the sun really be turned to darkness and the moon to blood (Joel 2:31)?

The Bible presents the darkening of the sun and the turning of the moon to blood as literal, cosmic signs that mark the approach of the great and awesome Day of the LORD. These celestial wonders serve as a divine herald, signaling that the Creator is about to intervene directly and decisively in human history for judgment and salvation.

When the prophet Joel spoke these words, he was describing a period of unprecedented upheaval that precedes the final return of Jesus Christ. This prophecy is so central to the biblical narrative that it is quoted by the apostle Peter on the Day of Pentecost in Acts 2:20 and is seen again in the vision given to the apostle John in Revelation 6:12. In the biblical worldview, the physical creation is intimately tied to the spiritual reality of God's kingdom. Just as the sun darkened when the Savior hung upon the cross to bear our sins in Matthew 27:45, the heavens will again reflect the weight of God's glory and His holy opposition to evil when He comes to wrap up the age.

You may wonder if such descriptions are merely poetic or symbolic of political unrest. While Scripture often uses vivid imagery, these passages point to a physical reality where the very foundations of the world are shaken by the presence of its Maker. The darkening of the sun and the moon's blood like appearance remind us that the lights of this world are temporary, but the glory of God is eternal. This teaching on the end times, known as eschatology, is not intended to satisfy our curiosity or cause unnecessary fear, but to produce in us a holy urgency and a steadfast hope.

The Day of the LORD is a day of darkness for those who remain in rebellion against God, but it is a day of ultimate light for those who are hidden in Christ. Because God is holy and His wrath against sin is a righteous reality, we must take these warnings to heart. However, God is also rich in mercy and has provided a way for us to stand before Him with joy. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all creation.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the book of Joel in one sitting. It is a short but powerful book that will help you see how God uses even the most terrifying signs to call His people to "rend your hearts and not your garments" and return to Him in Joel 2:13. If you have a local church, consider asking your pastor to help you understand how these prophecies point us toward a deeper love for the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ.
How can I overcome spiritual apathy?

Spiritual apathy is a cooling of the heart’s affection for God characterized by an indifference toward His Word, His people, and His glory. It is often the result of "drifting away" from the truths we have heard, as described in Hebrews 2:1.

You may wonder if God has grown tired of your indifference or if you have somehow exhausted His grace through your lack of zeal. It is a heavy burden to feel as though your heart has turned to stone, but the very fact that you are concerned about your apathy is often a sign that the Holy Spirit is at work, prompting you to return to your first love.

The remedy for a cold heart is not to look within at your own flickering spark, but to look upward at the blazing Sun of Righteousness, Jesus Christ. We do not overcome apathy by trying to manufacture feelings through our own willpower. Instead, we are renewed through the process of progressive sanctification as we utilize the means of grace God has provided.

To move from apathy to fervor, consider these biblical steps:

First, acknowledge that apathy is a sin of the heart that requires repentance. To be "lukewarm" is to live as if the cross of Christ is a small thing. In Revelation 3:19, Jesus says, "Those I love, I rebuke and discipline. Therefore be earnest and repent." Confess your coldness to God and ask Him to restore to you the joy of your salvation, as prayed in Psalm 51:12.

Second, feast upon the Gospel. Apathy often stems from a forgetfulness of what Christ has done. We love because He first loved us, according to 1 John 4:19. Remind yourself that God is holy and just, and that our sin separated us from Him. Yet, in His great mercy, Jesus Christ took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the wrath of God on our behalf. He rose bodily from the grave and is now the exalted Lord. When you realize the depth of the danger you were in and the height of the love that rescued you, your heart will begin to warm.

Third, engage in the disciplines of grace even when you do not feel like it. Do not wait for a "feeling" to pray or read Scripture. We often act our way into feeling rather than feeling our way into acting. Romans 12:11 exhorts us, "Never be lacking in zeal, but keep your spiritual fervor, serving the Lord."

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to set aside fifteen minutes today to read Psalm 63 aloud in a quiet place. After reading, speak directly to God and tell Him honestly that your heart feels cold. Ask Him to do what only He can do, which is to take your heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh, as promised in Ezekiel 36:26.

The door to God's mercy is never locked to a repentant soul. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you believe is the primary distraction currently drawing your heart away from the Lord?
How can I experience healing and recovery after an abortion?

I hear the heaviness in your heart, and I want you to know that there is a path toward peace and restoration through the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ. You may feel a weight of guilt that seems too heavy to carry, or a sense of loss that feels like an ocean, but God is a refuge for the brokenhearted.

True healing begins with the recognition that while our sins are deep, the mercy of God is deeper still. In the Bible, we see that no person is beyond the reach of God’s redeeming love when they come to Him with a contrite spirit. Healing is found not by hiding what has happened or trying to compensate for it through our own efforts, but by bringing it into the light of God's presence.

The Gospel is the only medicine that can truly heal the conscience. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, and that all of us have turned away from His perfect will, falling short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a sacrificial, substitutionary death on the cross to pay the full penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Through Christ, there is full and free forgiveness. The Apostle John tells us that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9). This cleansing is not partial; it is total. Because of the finished work of Jesus, God does not look at the believer through the lens of their past failures, but through the righteousness of His Son.

To experience this recovery, I encourage you to take these steps:

First, rest in the promise of God's Word. When the enemy of your soul brings accusations to your mind, answer them with the truth of Scripture. Remind yourself that there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1).

Second, do not walk this path alone. I encourage you to find a faithful, Bible-teaching local church where you can sit under the preaching of the Word and find fellowship with other believers. You may also find it immensely helpful to speak with a pastor or a certified biblical counselor who can provide specific, Scripture-based guidance as you navigate the process of grieving and healing.

You can turn to God even now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive His forgiveness and a brand new life. He is the Great Physician who binds up the wounds of the broken (Psalm 147:3). 

What is the greatest concern currently weighing on your mind as you think about God's forgiveness?
What does God mean when He says, “Seek me and live” (Amos 5:4)?

When God says, "Seek me and live," He is calling His people to abandon empty religious rituals and false dependencies in order to find their true existence and safety in a covenant relationship with Him. In the historical context of Amos 5:4, this was a gracious command to the Northern Kingdom of Israel to turn back to the Lord so they might be spared from the impending national judgment and destruction.

To "seek" the Lord involves a deliberate turning of the heart, which the Bible describes as **repentance**. It is the act of forsaking the "broken cisterns" of this world, such as wealth, self-righteousness, or false gods, and turning to the only One who can sustain the soul. In Amos 5:4 through Amos 5:6, God warns that seeking help from worldly religious centers like Bethel or Gilgal would lead to nothing, for only in the Lord Himself is there life.

This command serves as a beautiful shadow of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. While we have all sought life in things that lead to death, God has provided the way to "seek and live" through His Son. Our sin has separated us from the Author of life, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. Because of His victory, the promise of Amos is fulfilled eternally for all who believe: those who seek Christ will find life that never ends.

You may wonder if you have wandered too far or waited too long to turn toward God, but the very fact that He issues this invitation shows His immense patience and mercy. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in seeking Him today, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, chapter 1, and chapter 3, asking God to show you the beauty of Christ. Consider also reaching out to a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find fellowship with others who are seeking Him. What is it that feels like the greatest obstacle to you seeking Him today?

Amos 5:4; Amos 5:6; John 14:6; Romans 10:9; Acts 17:30; John 1:1-4; John 3:16-17
If aborted babies go to heaven, why is abortion wrong?

You may wonder how an act can be considered evil if it results in an innocent soul entering the presence of God. This question touches on the very nature of God's authority and our responsibility as His creatures.

Abortion is wrong because God is the sovereign Creator and Sustainer of all life, and He alone possesses the right to determine the beginning and end of a human journey. Scripture teaches that human beings are created in the image of God, known as the Imago Dei, from the moment of conception. In Psalm 139:13, the psalmist declares, "For You created my inmost being; You knit me together in my mother’s womb." Because God is the one doing the knitting, to intentionally end that life is to destroy a masterpiece of His own making.

The Bible provides a clear moral law regarding the taking of life in Exodus 20:13, which states, "You shall not murder." Murder is the intentional taking of an innocent human life. While we may trust in the mercy of God to care for those who die in infancy, the eternal destination of the victim does not change the nature of the act committed by the person. We are strictly warned in Romans 3:8 against the idea of doing evil so that good may result. A sinful action is not justified by a secondary outcome that God, in His infinite grace, might bring about.

To take the life of the unborn is to usurp the throne of God and claim a prerogative that belongs to Him alone. It is a rejection of His providential care and a violation of the stewardship He has given us over the most vulnerable.

If you are struggling with the weight of a past abortion or the reality of sin in your life, please know there is profound hope. God is holy and just, and our rebellion separates us from Him, but He is also rich in mercy. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, full forgiveness is available to all who turn to Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John to see the heart of Christ for the broken, and reach out to a faithful local church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find a community of believers to walk alongside you.
Should communion be open or closed?

The question of who should partake of the Lord’s Supper is a matter of church order and biblical stewardship. While the Bible does not use the specific terms open or closed, it provides clear principles regarding the holiness of the table and the requirements for those who participate.

Historically, Christian churches have practiced three primary approaches to communion. Open communion is the practice of inviting all present who profess faith in Jesus Christ to participate, regardless of their denominational affiliation or baptismal status. Close communion typically invites all baptized believers who are members in good standing of a church with similar doctrine. Closed communion restricts participation strictly to the members of that specific local congregation.

The Scriptures emphasize that the Lord’s Supper is a meal for the visible church, those who have publicly identified with Christ. In the book of Acts, we see a consistent pattern where those who received the word were baptized first and then added to the church to participate in the breaking of bread (Acts 2:41; Acts 2:42). This suggests that baptism is the biblical starting point for participating in the community of faith. Furthermore, the Apostle Paul warns that anyone who eats and drinks in an unworthy manner brings judgment upon himself (1 Corinthians 11:27; 1 Corinthians 11:29).

From a Reformed Baptist perspective, the table is often viewed as being for those who have made a credible profession of faith and have been baptized as believers. This view seeks to protect the purity of the ordinance and ensure that those participating understand the spiritual reality of the bread and the cup. However, many faithful churches practice a form of open communion, believing that any true child of God should be welcome at their Father’s table (1 Corinthians 10:16; 1 Corinthians 10:17).

If you are a follower of Jesus, the most vital requirement is a heart of self-examination. Before you reach for the elements, look inward and ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any unconfessed sin or broken relationships that need reconciliation (1 Corinthians 11:28). The table is not for the perfect, but for the penitent who recognize their absolute need for the Savior.

If you have not yet placed your trust in Jesus Christ, the bread and the cup serve as a visual sermon of the grace you can receive. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

For a practical next step, I encourage you to speak with the elders or pastors of your local church. Ask them how they understand the biblical requirements for communion and how you can best prepare your heart to receive this means of grace in a way that honors the Lord. What is your current understanding of the purpose of the Lord's Supper?
What is the Remnant Fellowship?

Remnant Fellowship is a religious organization founded in 1999 by Gwen Shamblin Lara in Brentwood, Tennessee. While it identifies as a Christian ministry, it is characterized by teachings and practices that stand in direct opposition to historical, orthodox Christianity, particularly regarding the nature of God and the way of salvation.

The group grew out of the Weigh Down Workshop, a weight loss program that emphasized portion control as a form of spiritual obedience. Over time, the teaching shifted from weight management to a rigid system of total submission to leadership and a rejection of essential Christian doctrines. Most notably, Remnant Fellowship denies the doctrine of the Trinity, which is the biblical truth that there is one God who eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14. By teaching that Jesus Christ is a separate, lesser being who is not co-equal and co-eternal with the Father, they deny the full deity of Christ. John 1:1; Colossians 2:9.

Furthermore, the group teaches a form of legalism that suggests salvation is maintained through one's own efforts and perfect obedience to their specific interpretations of Scripture. This contradicts the biblical doctrine of justification by faith alone, where we are declared righteous before God solely because of the finished work of Jesus Christ on our behalf. Ephesians 2:8–9; Titus 3:5. In a healthy church, holiness is the joyful response of a heart transformed by grace, not a burden carried to earn God's favor or avoid the shunning of a community.

It is understandable that many are drawn to such groups out of a sincere desire for discipline, community, or a deeper sense of purpose. However, true peace is found only in the authentic Gospel of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and we are all separated from Him by our sin. Romans 3:23. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. 1 Corinthians 15:3–4. He is now exalted as Lord over all. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. If you are seeking a place to grow in your faith, I encourage you to find a local, Bible-believing church that affirms the Trinity and preaches the Gospel of grace. What is it that you are looking for most in a church community right now?
Who wrote the book of 2 John? Who was the author of 2 John?

The Apostle John, the son of Zebedee and the "beloved disciple," is the author of the book of 2 John. Although he refers to himself simply as "the elder" in the opening greeting, the early church and the striking similarities in language and themes to his Gospel and first epistle confirm his identity as the writer (2 John 1:1).

In using the title of "elder," John likely refers not only to his advanced age at the time of writing but also to his recognized pastoral oversight and apostolic authority within the early church. He writes this brief but potent letter to "the elect lady and her children," a term that most likely refers to a specific local congregation and its members (2 John 1:1). His primary concern is to exhort believers to walk in the truth and to guard themselves against deceivers who deny the incarnation of Jesus Christ (2 John 1:7; 2 John 1:9).

John beautifully illustrates that for the Christian, truth and love are inseparable. He teaches that we cannot truly love others unless we are grounded in the truth of the Gospel, and we cannot claim to know the truth if we do not walk in love toward one another (2 John 1:5-6). This provides a steadying reminder that our faith is not merely an intellectual agreement with facts, but a lived reality of obedience to God's commands.

As you reflect on this letter, a practical next step is to spend time in prayer asking the Holy Spirit to sharpen your discernment. You might specifically ask for the wisdom to distinguish between worldly sentiments and the biblical love that is rooted in the person and work of Jesus Christ.
What is false guilt, and how can I avoid it?

It is a heavy burden to carry a heart that feels condemned when God has pronounced you free, and I want you to know that there is great tenderness in the heart of Christ for those who are weary and heavy laden. 

False guilt is a subjective feeling of condemnation for actions that do not violate God's revealed Law, or a persistent sense of shame for sins that have already been confessed and forgiven through the blood of Jesus Christ. While true conviction is a gift from the Holy Spirit that leads us to repentance and life, false guilt is a weight that leads only to despair and keeps our eyes fixed on ourselves rather than on the Savior.

To understand and avoid this burden, consider these biblical principles:

**Distinguish between Conscience and Scripture**
Our consciences are given by God, but because of the Fall, they are not always perfectly calibrated. A person may have a "weak conscience" that feels guilty over things God has not forbidden, such as certain customs, traditions, or personal preferences, as described in 1 Corinthians 8:7 and Romans 14:1-4. The remedy is to educate your conscience with the Word of God. If the Bible does not call an action sin, you must not allow your feelings to act as a judge over you.

**Trust in Objective Justification**
The doctrine of justification is the glorious truth that God declares a sinner righteous because of the work of Christ alone, received through faith alone. If you have repented and trusted in Jesus, His sacrifice was "sufficient" to pay for every one of your sins, past, present, and future, as taught in Hebrews 10:12 and Hebrews 10:14. To continue to feel guilty for a sin that God says He has "remembered no more" is to functionally doubt the power and finished work of the Cross. As Romans 8:1 declares, "Therefore, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus."

**Recognize the Source of the Voice**
The Holy Spirit convicts us of specific sins to lead us toward restoration and the "kindness of God" that leads to repentance, according to Romans 2:4. Conversely, the enemy is called "the accuser of our brothers" in Revelation 12:10. His accusations are often vague, crushing, and intended to make you hide from God. When you feel a sense of guilt, ask yourself: Is this leading me to the Cross for cleansing, or is it driving me away from God in shame?

**Practical Steps for Relief**
To avoid the trap of false guilt, I encourage you to take the following actionable step: 

Create a "Truth Table" in a journal. On one side, write down the specific thought or feeling of guilt you are experiencing. On the other side, write out a specific verse of Scripture that addresses it. For example, if you feel God is still angry with you for a past mistake, write out 1 John 1:9: "If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness." 

I recommend you spend time in prayer today, not asking for more forgiveness for what is already covered, but asking God to align your feelings with the reality of His Word. You might find it helpful to pray through Psalm 103, specifically focusing on verse 12, which says, "As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us." May you find rest in the finished work of your Savior.
Are baptism and communion means of grace?

Baptism and the Lord's Supper, often called communion, are indeed means of grace, serving as divinely appointed instruments through which God confirms His promises to our hearts and strengthens our faith. These outward ordinances do not confer salvation automatically by the mere performance of the act, but they are channels used by the Holy Spirit to nourish the believer who receives them in faith.

In the waters of baptism, we see a beautiful picture of our union with Jesus Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection. It is an outward sign of the inward reality of the washing away of sins and our new life in Him, as described in Acts 2:38; Acts 22:16; Romans 6:3-4. While baptism is not the cause of our justification, it is a command of our Lord that identifies us with His body, the church, and serves as a formal "putting on" of Christ, as noted in Matthew 28:19; Galatians 3:27.

The Lord's Supper is a holy feast where we spiritually feed upon Christ. As we partake of the bread and the cup, we do more than simply remember a past event; we experience a true spiritual participation in the body and blood of Christ through the power of the Holy Spirit, according to 1 Corinthians 10:16; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26. This meal is a means of sanctification, intended to refresh our souls, proclaim the Lord's death until He returns, and strengthen the bond of love within the local church family.

It is important to remember that these means of grace are effective only when paired with faith. Just as a pipe is not the water itself but the vessel that carries it, these ordinances are the "pipes" through which God pours His spiritual encouragement into the lives of His children.

If you are a follower of Christ, I encourage you to approach the Lord's Table with a heart of humble self-examination and joyful expectation. Before your next opportunity to participate in communion, spend time in prayer, asking the Holy Spirit to reveal any unconfessed sin and to give you a deeper hunger for the presence of Christ. If you have trusted in Christ but have not yet been baptized, I urge you to speak with the elders of a faithful local church about taking that step of obedience to our Lord's command.
What is the spirit of the law?

The spirit of the law is the heart-intention and divine purpose behind God's commands, which is to reflect His holy character through supreme love for God and sacrificial love for others. While the letter of the law often refers to the outward, literal requirement of a command, the spirit of the law addresses the inward motive and the weightier matters of justice, mercy, and faithfulness (Matthew 23:23).

Jesus Christ beautifully clarified this when He summarized the entire Law and the Prophets into two great commandments: to love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and mind, and to love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:37-40). To follow the letter without the spirit is to fall into legalism, a cold and hollow performance that honors God with the lips while the heart is far from Him. Conversely, the spirit of the law does not abolish the command but fulfills it by ensuring the outward action flows from a heart of love and devotion (Romans 13:10).

The Apostle Paul reminds us that the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life (2 Corinthians 3:6). This means that the law, when viewed merely as a list of rules to be kept by human effort, only serves to condemn us because we cannot keep it perfectly. It acts as a mirror to show us our sin and our desperate need for a Savior. However, for those in Christ, the Holy Spirit writes the law on our hearts, enabling us to walk in obedience not out of fearful obligation, but out of grateful love.

You may wonder why a loving God would provide such strict commands if He knew we could not keep them perfectly. The law was given to lead us to Christ. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has created a separation that no amount of rule-keeping can bridge. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human nature and lived a life of perfect obedience, fulfilling the spirit and the letter of the law in our place. He then suffered the penalty for our law-breaking by dying on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. We are justified, or made right with God, by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works of the law.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to read through the Sermon on the Mount in Matthew 5-7. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to reveal the motives of your heart and to cultivate in you a love that seeks to honor God's intentions in every action. Seek to pray daily, asking God to align your desires with His holy will.
Should pastors be paid a salary?

The Bible teaches that those who labor in the preaching and teaching of the Word are entitled to financial support from the church they serve. This is not merely a matter of human tradition but is a principle established by God to ensure that the ministry of the Gospel is maintained with diligence and focus.

The Apostle Paul makes this clear when he writes that the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the Gospel should get their living by the Gospel in 1 Corinthians 9:14. Furthermore, Scripture instructs the church to recognize the value of their leaders' labor, stating that the elders who direct the affairs of the church well are worthy of double honor, especially those whose work is preaching and teaching in 1 Timothy 5:17. To reinforce this, Paul cites the Old Testament law and the words of Jesus, noting that the laborer is worthy of his wages in 1 Timothy 5:18 and Luke 10:7.

Theological and practical reasons for this support include:

1.  **Freedom for Ministry:** When a congregation provides a sufficient salary, they free their pastor from the necessity of secular employment, allowing him to give himself fully to prayer, the study of Scripture, and the care of souls as seen in Acts 6:4.
2.  **Mutual Blessing:** Galatians 6:6 encourages believers by stating that nevertheless, the one who receives instruction in the word should share all good things with their instructor. This creates a beautiful cycle of spiritual feeding and physical provision within the body of Christ.
3.  **Biblical Justice:** Just as a soldier does not serve at his own expense and a farmer eats the fruit of his vineyard, a minister of the Gospel should be supported by those who benefit from his spiritual labor as argued in 1 Corinthians 9:7.

While there are instances where a minister may choose to support himself through secular work to avoid being a burden or to meet a specific missional need, such as Paul’s "tentmaking" in Acts 18:3, the biblical norm is for the local church to provide for its pastors.

As a practical step, if you are a member of a local church, you might consider how you can participate in this biblical mandate through regular, joyful giving. You may also want to encourage your church leaders by ensuring they are cared for not just financially, but also through your prayers and personal appreciation. Our Great Shepherd, Jesus Christ, has given gifts to the church in the form of pastors and teachers, and caring for them is one way we honor the One who gave His very life to redeem us.
What does it mean that man shall not live by bread alone (Deuteronomy 8:3)?

To live "not by bread alone" means that while physical food sustains our biological bodies, our true existence and spiritual vitality depend entirely on the sovereign will and revealed Word of God. This statement establishes that humanity is more than a physical entity; we are spiritual beings whose very life is upheld by the decree of our Creator.

In the original context of Deuteronomy 8:3, Moses reminded the Israelites that God led them through the wilderness and fed them with manna to teach them total dependence upon Him. By stripping away their normal means of survival, God demonstrated that He is the ultimate source of all provision. This principle was perfectly modeled by Jesus Christ when He was tempted in the wilderness. Though He was physically famished, He refused to act outside of the Father's will, quoting this verse to show that obeying God is more vital than satisfying physical hunger (Matthew 4:4).

This highlights the biblical doctrine of divine providence, which teaches that God does not merely leave the world to run on its own, but actively upholds all things by the word of His power (Hebrews 1:3). We often fall into the trap of believing that our security lies in our material possessions or physical health, but these things are temporary. True, eternal life is found only in the person of Jesus Christ, who is the Living Word. He is the "bread of life" who came down from heaven to give life to the world (John 6:33-35).

If you are feeling spiritually empty or weary, recognize that your soul was designed to be nourished by God's truth. You might find it helpful to begin a rhythm of daily Scripture reading, perhaps starting in the Gospel of John, to feast on the words of Christ before you begin your daily work. 

If you find yourself searching for a satisfaction that this world’s "bread" cannot provide, know that Jesus died and rose again to reconcile you to God and offer you life that never ends. You can turn to Him now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Sustainer today?
What does the Bible say about parenting?

The Bible presents parenting as a sacred stewardship where children are received as a gift from God to be raised for His glory and purposes. It is the primary means by which the knowledge of God is passed from one generation to the next, emphasizing the training of the heart rather than mere outward behavioral modification.

Scripture teaches that children are a heritage from the Lord (Psalm 127:3). This means parents do not own their children but are temporary guardians commissioned to point them toward their true Creator. Because every child is born with a sinful nature inherited from Adam, parenting is a work of patient evangelism and discipleship.

The foundational command for parents is found in the Old Testament, where God instructs His people to love Him with all their heart and to teach His words diligently to their children. This instruction is to happen naturally throughout the day, whether sitting in the house, walking by the way, lying down, or rising up (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). This implies that biblical parenting is an immersive lifestyle of modeling and teaching rather than a series of isolated lectures.

In the New Testament, the Apostle Paul provides a clear framework for the home. He instructs fathers not to provoke their children to anger but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4). This requires a delicate balance of firm authority and tender compassion. Discipline is necessary because "folly is bound up in the heart of a child" (Proverbs 22:15), yet it must always be administered in love, reflecting how our Heavenly Father disciplines those He loves (Hebrews 12:6).

A vital theological concept in parenting is the "nurture and admonition of the Lord," which involves both formative and corrective discipline. Formative discipline is the proactive teaching of God's Word and the Gospel, while corrective discipline involves addressing sin and leading the child back to a right path. Parents are warned not to embitter or discourage their children, as this can lead to a hardened heart (Colossians 3:21).

Ultimately, the goal of parenting is to lead children to the feet of Jesus Christ. While parents cannot regenerate a child's heart, they can faithfully plant the seeds of the Gospel, praying that God would grant them the grace of salvation. We rest in the promise that God is faithful and that His Word does not return void.

A practical and actionable step you can take today is to establish a rhythm of family worship. This does not need to be complex; simply spend ten minutes after a meal reading a short passage of Scripture together, discussing what it reveals about God's character, and praying for one another. This consistent habit demonstrates to your children that the Word of God is the ultimate authority and the greatest treasure in your home.
Who was Jeremiah in the Bible?

Jeremiah was a priest from the town of Anathoth who served as a prophet to the kingdom of Judah during the final forty years before the Babylonian captivity. He is often called the weeping prophet because of his deep grief over the sins of his nation and the heavy burden of delivering God's message of impending judgment (Jeremiah 1:1; Jeremiah 9:1).

His life was a profound demonstration of the sovereignty of God, who declared that He had set Jeremiah apart for this task before he was even formed in the womb (Jeremiah 1:5). Throughout his ministry, Jeremiah faced intense persecution, including beatings and imprisonment, yet he remained steadfast in his prophetic office. He is most significantly remembered for announcing the New Covenant, in which God promised to write His law on the hearts of His people and to remember their sins no more (Jeremiah 31:31-34). This points us directly to the Lord Jesus Christ, who fulfilled this promise by His sacrificial death and resurrection, securing our justification by grace through faith.

Jeremiah's example teaches us that true success is found in unwavering obedience to God, regardless of the world's response. Just as the Lord promised to make Jeremiah a fortified city against his enemies, He provides us with the strength to stand for the truth today (Jeremiah 1:18-19). If you are currently struggling with feelings of inadequacy or facing opposition in your walk with Christ, I encourage you to read Jeremiah chapter 1 this week. As you do, bring your heart before God in prayer and ask Him to grant you the courage to be a faithful witness for the Gospel in your own community.
Why did Jesus allow the demons to enter the herd of pigs?

Jesus allowed the demons to enter the herd of pigs to demonstrate His absolute sovereignty over the demonic realm and to illustrate that the restoration of a single human soul is of infinitely greater value than any earthly possession. By permitting this, He provided a visible testimony of the destructive nature of evil, for the demons sought only to kill and destroy the herd just as they had been destroying the man (Mark 5:13; Luke 8:33).

In this event, we see the authority of Christ, who is the King of kings. The destruction of the pigs served as a vivid sign of the malice of the spiritual forces of wickedness, while highlighting the mercy of God toward the suffering. While the townspeople mourned their financial loss, Jesus celebrated the deliverance of a man who had been bound in chains and living among the tombs (Matthew 8:28; Luke 8:27).

This miracle points us to the greater work of Jesus on the cross. You may wonder why God allows such darkness to exist at all, but we see here that even the demons must ask His permission. Jesus came to bind the "strong man" and plunder his house, ultimately defeating sin and death through His own sacrificial death and bodily resurrection (Matthew 12:29; Colossians 2:15).

Just as Jesus delivered the man from the legion of demons, He offers you deliverance from the power of sin and the weight of guilt. God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him, but Christ lived a perfect life and died as a substitute for sinners so that we might be forgiven. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose again to give you new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?

If you are walking through a season of spiritual struggle or feel overwhelmed by the brokenness of this world, I encourage you to spend time this week reading through the Gospel of Mark, specifically focusing on the authority of Jesus. Bring your fears directly to Him in prayer, knowing that He is both powerful to help and tender toward your needs (Hebrews 4:15-16; Mark 5:19).
What are the Analects of Confucius?

The Analects of Confucius is a foundational collection of sayings, anecdotes, and dialogues attributed to the Chinese philosopher Kong Fuzi, known in the West as Confucius, and his followers. Compiled during the period following his death, this text serves as the primary source for Confucian thought, emphasizing the cultivation of personal virtue, the importance of filial piety, and the necessity of ritual to maintain social and political harmony.

In these writings, we see the operation of what Christians call common grace, which is the favor of God shown to all people that allows them to perceive elements of truth and order. The Analects reflect a deep human recognition of the moral law that God has written upon every heart (Romans 2:14-15). The desire for a society governed by righteousness and respect is a reflection of the fact that we were created for a world of perfect justice and peace.

However, there is a vital distinction between the "Superior Man" described in the Analects and the new creation described in the Bible. Confucius taught that humanity could be perfected through self-discipline, education, and the performance of social duties. In contrast, the Scriptures teach that our problem is not merely a lack of social order, but a condition of sin and a fallen nature that separates us from a holy God (Isaiah 59:2). We are unable to achieve true righteousness or reconcile ourselves to God through our own moral striving (Ephesians 2:8-9).

True restoration and the power to live a godly life come not from following a moral code, but through the person of Jesus Christ. The Gospel reveals that God, in His mercy, sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die as a substitute for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and offering us the gift of justification by grace alone through faith alone (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). While the Analects seek to improve the behavior of the natural man, the Holy Spirit works to give us a completely new heart (Ezekiel 36:26).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. As a practical step in seeking the source of all wisdom, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, which describes how the true Light has come into the world to give life to all who believe (John 1:9-12).
Can faith change God's plan?

Faith does not change God’s eternal plan, because God is sovereign and unchanging in His nature, His knowledge, and His purposes. Scripture affirms that God declares the end from the beginning and that His counsel shall stand, for He performs all His pleasure (Isaiah 46:10).

The biblical doctrine of immutability teaches us that God does not change (Malachi 3:6). If our faith could alter God’s decree, it would imply that God’s original plan was imperfect or that He learned something new from us, which is impossible for an omniscient Creator. Instead, God works all things according to the counsel of His own will (Ephesians 1:11).

You may wonder if this truth makes faith or prayer feel unnecessary, but the reality is quite the opposite and far more beautiful. God, in His infinite wisdom, has not only ordained the ends (the outcomes) but also the means (the process). Faith and prayer are the very means God has appointed to accomplish His sovereign purposes. When we pray in faith, we are not bending God’s will to match ours, but rather, through the work of the Holy Spirit, our hearts are being aligned with His. God answers prayer because He sovereignly decreed that He would act in response to the faithful petitions of His people (James 5:16).

This truth provides immense comfort. We do not want a God whose plans can be shifted by our limited and often misguided desires. We want a Father whose plans are rooted in perfect holiness, love, and wisdom. His plan for the world reached its pinnacle in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Long before the foundation of the world, God planned to redeem a people for Himself.

The Gospel is the historical fact that we are all sinners, separated from a holy God by our rebellion. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in His perfect and sovereign plan for your salvation?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Psalms, such as Psalm 33 or Psalm 139. As you pray, practice "praying the Scriptures" back to God, asking Him to align your desires with the truths you find in His Word. This helps cultivate a faith that rests in His wisdom rather than one that strives to change His perfect counsel.
What is Pneumatology?

Pneumatology is the study of the person and work of the Holy Spirit, the third person of the Holy Trinity. It examines His eternal nature as God and His specific activities in history, such as His role in creation, the inspiration of Scripture, and the salvation of God's people.

The Holy Spirit is not an impersonal force or an abstract energy, but a divine person who possesses intellect, emotion, and will. He is the *Parakletos*, our Helper and Advocate, who was sent by the Father and the Son to dwell within every true believer (John 14:16–17; John 15:26). In the mystery of the Trinity, He is co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son, sharing the same divine essence (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14).

His work is essential for our spiritual life and the health of the church. He convicts the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment (John 16:8), and brings about the miracle of regeneration, which is the "new birth" required to enter the kingdom of God (Titus 3:5). Furthermore, He carries out the ongoing work of sanctification, where He produces His fruit in our lives and conforms us into the image of Christ (Galatians 5:22–23; 2 Corinthians 3:18). Without His gracious illumination, we would remain blind to the beauty of the Gospel, for the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God (1 Corinthians 2:14).

As you seek to grow in your understanding of the Spirit, a wonderful next step would be to spend time reading the Gospel of John, specifically chapters 14 through 16. As you read, I encourage you to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to guide you into all truth, remembering that His primary delight is to glorify the Lord Jesus Christ.
What does the Bible say about children's church?

The Bible does not contain a specific command to organize a children's church program, but it provides a clear mandate for the instruction and spiritual care of children within the church family. Scripture emphasizes that children are a blessing from the Lord and are to be included in the life and worship of God’s people.

Our Lord Jesus demonstrated the priority of children when He told His disciples, "Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these" in Matthew 19:14. This highlights that children are not merely the church of tomorrow, but are precious members of the community today who need to be brought to the feet of Christ.

The primary responsibility for the spiritual formation of children rests upon parents. Moses commanded the Israelites to teach God’s words to their children diligently, speaking of them when sitting in the house, walking by the way, lying down, and rising up in Deuteronomy 6:6-9. Similarly, the Apostle Paul instructs fathers to bring their children up "in the training and instruction of the Lord" in Ephesians 6:4.

Regarding how this instruction happens within the local church, faithful Christians hold different views on the best method, and this is considered a matter of Christian liberty.

One view, often called family integrated worship, suggests that children should remain with their parents during the entire corporate worship service. This approach emphasizes the unity of the family and allows children to observe their parents and the wider congregation engaging in the means of grace, such as prayer, singing, and the preaching of the Word. Proponents often point to Joel 2:16, where the entire assembly, including infants, was gathered before the Lord.

Another view suggests that providing age appropriate instruction, such as children's church, can be a helpful tool for discipleship. This approach seeks to explain the truths of Scripture at a level children can more easily grasp, following the principle of teaching "line upon line, precept upon precept." In this view, children's church is seen as a supplement to, rather than a replacement for, the parents' role or the child's eventually joining the full assembly.

Regardless of which model a church follows, the goal remains the same: the glory of God and the salvation of the next generation. It is a holy and joyful task to ensure that our little ones hear the Gospel of grace. We must remember that salvation is not a matter of mere information, but of a heart being made new by the Holy Spirit.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to establish a rhythm of family worship in your home. This can be as simple as reading a short passage of Scripture, singing a hymn together, and praying for one another. If you are seeking guidance on how your local church handles this, speak with your elders or pastors to understand their vision for how the children of your congregation are being nurtured in the faith.
Why is the humanity of Jesus important?

The humanity of Jesus is essential because our salvation required a Mediator who could represent us perfectly, suffer in our place, and sympathize with our weaknesses. If Jesus were not truly man, He could not have served as our substitute or paid the debt that humanity owed to God.

Scripture reveals that Jesus Christ exists in what theologians call the hypostatic union, meaning He is fully God and fully man in one person forever. This truth is not a mere academic point but the very foundation of our hope for several reasons.

First, Jesus had to be human to act as our representative. Just as the first man, Adam, brought sin and death into the world through his disobedience, the "Second Adam," Jesus Christ, brought righteousness and life through His perfect obedience. By living a sinless life in a human body, He fulfilled the requirements of God's law on our behalf (Romans 5:18; Romans 5:19).

Second, Jesus had to be human to offer a substitutionary atonement. The penalty for human sin is death, and since God in His divine nature cannot die, the Son of God took on human flesh so that He could lay down His life. He became a propitiation for our sins, meaning His sacrifice satisfied the righteous wrath of God against our rebellion (Hebrews 2:14; Hebrews 2:17). Without His physical body and shed blood, there would be no forgiveness for us (Hebrews 9:22).

Third, His humanity allows Him to be a sympathetic High Priest. Because He experienced hunger, weariness, grief, and even the full weight of temptation, He understands our struggles intimately. He is not a distant or cold deity, but a loving Savior who invites us to draw near to His throne of grace to find mercy and help in our time of need (Hebrews 4:15; Hebrews 4:16).

If you are currently walking through a season of trial or physical suffering, remember that your Savior knows the pain of a broken world firsthand. He does not look at your weakness with contempt, but with the tender compassion of a brother who has walked the path before you. You can take your burdens to Him in prayer today, knowing He truly understands.

This great mystery of the God-man is the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. Yet, out of His immense love, Jesus Christ took on our nature, lived the perfect life we could not live, and died the death we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the new birth?

The new birth, often called regeneration, is the sovereign and instantaneous work of the Holy Spirit where He grants new spiritual life to a person who was previously dead in their sins. It is a fundamental transformation of the heart that enables a sinner to see the kingdom of God and respond to the Gospel with saving faith and repentance.

The Lord Jesus taught the necessity of this miracle when He told Nicodemus, "Truly, truly, I tell you, no one can see the kingdom of God unless he is born again" (John 3:3). This is not a physical change or a mere moral reformation, but a spiritual resurrection. Just as a child does nothing to cause their own physical birth, the new birth is a work of God's grace alone. The Bible describes this as God taking away a heart of stone and giving a heart of flesh (Ezekiel 36:26).

Before this work of the Spirit, we are spiritually blind and unable to please God because our natures are corrupted by sin (Romans 8:7; Ephesians 2:1). In the new birth, the Holy Spirit uses the Word of God to awaken our souls, granting us a new nature that loves righteousness and hates sin (1 Peter 1:23; 1 John 3:9). This internal change is the fountain from which all Christian growth and holiness flow, often referred to as the process of sanctification.

If you are wondering about your own standing before God, consider the message of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, but we have all rebelled against Him, earning His righteous judgment. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin.

Salvation is not earned by our efforts or our goodness, but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, specifically chapters 1 through 3, and ask God in prayer to open your eyes to the beauty of Christ. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word is preached faithfully, so you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
What does the Bible say about education?

Education is the intentional training of the heart and mind to recognize God's truth and live according to His holy purposes. The Bible teaches that the fear of the LORD is the very beginning of knowledge, meaning that true education must start with a humble recognition of God as the Creator and Sustainer of all things (Proverbs 1:7).

Scripture presents education not merely as the acquisition of facts, but as a path toward wisdom and spiritual maturity. All truth is ultimately God’s truth, whether it is found in the pages of Holy Scripture or observed in the natural world through what theologians call general revelation (Psalm 19:1). Since Jesus Christ is the one in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, a truly biblical education seeks to see every subject through the lens of His lordship (Colossians 2:3).

The Bible places the primary responsibility for the education and discipleship of children upon the parents. Fathers and mothers are commanded to teach God’s Word diligently to their children, weaving spiritual instruction into the natural rhythms of daily life (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). This instruction is not intended to be cold or legalistic, but is a form of nurturing meant to bring children up in the training and admonition of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4).

Furthermore, education is a lifelong process of sanctification. As believers, we are called to be transformed by the renewing of our minds so that we may discern and approve the perfect will of God (Romans 12:2). The ultimate textbook for this growth is the Bible itself, which is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

To apply this practically, I encourage you to view your daily learning or work as an act of worship. Whether you are studying a school subject or reading a theological book, ask God to give you an understanding heart that sees His handiwork in every detail. You might consider choosing one specific attribute of God, such as His faithfulness or His justice, and looking for evidence of that attribute in what you are learning this week.
What is the Edenic covenant?

The Edenic covenant, often identified by theologians as the Covenant of Works, is the formal arrangement God established with Adam in the Garden of Eden before the entrance of sin. In this covenant, God promised eternal life and blessedness to Adam and his posterity on the condition of Adam’s perfect and personal obedience, specifically signified by the command not to eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil (Genesis 2:15-17).

This relationship is vital to understand because Adam did not stand in the garden as a private individual, but as the federal head or legal representative of all humanity. When Adam broke the covenant through disobedience, the penalty of death was not only applied to him but was also imputed to all his descendants (Romans 5:12; 1 Corinthians 15:22). This explains why every human being is born with a sinful nature and remains under the righteous judgment of God apart from divine intervention. Some scholars also point to Hosea 6:7 as a scriptural confirmation that Adam’s relationship with God was indeed a covenantal one, stating that like Adam, Israel had transgressed the covenant.

While the Edenic covenant reveals the holiness of God and the high standard of His law, its ultimate purpose in the grand narrative of Scripture is to point us toward our need for a Savior. We see in the Gospel that where the first Adam failed, Jesus Christ, the Last Adam, succeeded perfectly. Through His active obedience to God’s law and His passive obedience in suffering the penalty for sin, Christ fulfills the requirements that Adam could not (Romans 5:18-19).

If you feel the weight of your own imperfection or the brokenness of the world around you, I encourage you to look away from your own efforts and toward the perfect work of Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive the gift of eternal life that Adam lost. As a practical step in your discipleship, I suggest reading through Romans 5:12-21 this week and reflecting on how the grace of Christ is far greater than the sin of Adam.
What is Wahhabism? What is Wahhabi Islam?

Wahhabism is a conservative reform movement within Sunni Islam that originated in the eighteenth century in present day Saudi Arabia. It was founded by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who advocated for a strict, literal interpretation of Islamic texts and the removal of what he considered to be "innovations" or idolatry.

This movement emphasizes the absolute oneness of God, a concept known as tawhid, and rejects practices such as visiting shrines or seeking the intercession of saints. In modern times, it is often associated with the official state ideology of Saudi Arabia and has influenced many other conservative Islamic movements globally. While the movement seeks to honor the sovereignty of God, it remains a system of legalism and works righteousness that lacks the assurance of peace with God found in the Gospel.

You may wonder how a person can ever be sure they have done enough to please a holy and sovereign God. It is a heavy burden to live under a system where your standing with the Almighty depends entirely on your own rigorous performance and avoidance of error. The Bible teaches that God is indeed holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3), and that He is the only true God (Isaiah 44:6). However, the Scriptures also reveal that all human beings have fallen short of His glory and are separated from Him by sin (Romans 3:23).

The good news of the Christian faith is that what we could not do for ourselves through religious effort, God has done for us through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus is the eternal Word who became flesh, truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). He lived a life of perfect obedience, was tempted in every way as we are yet remained without sin (Hebrews 4:15), and then died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God (Romans 5:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21). His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

We are not reconciled to God by our own works or strict adherence to a law, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This is the doctrine of justification, where God declares a sinner righteous because of the work of Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today? If you are looking for a next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character of Jesus for yourself, and to talk to God in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to your heart.
What is the Watchtower Bible and Tract Society?

The Watchtower Bible and Tract Society is the primary legal and administrative organization used by Jehovah's Witnesses to direct their global activities and publish their literature. It was established in the late nineteenth century, beginning with the work of Charles Taze Russell, and it serves as the central authority for the doctrines and practices of the movement.

From a biblical and historical Christian perspective, the teachings of the Watchtower Society depart from the essential foundations of the faith in several critical ways. Most notably, the organization denies the doctrine of the Trinity, teaching instead that God is a single person named Jehovah and that the Holy Spirit is merely an impersonal force. Furthermore, they deny the full deity of Christ, asserting that Jesus is a created being, specifically Michael the Archangel, rather than the eternal Son of God who is one in essence with the Father (John 1:1; Colossians 1:15-17; John 20:28).

The Watchtower also teaches that Jesus did not rise bodily from the grave but was instead raised as a spirit creature. This contradicts the biblical account of Christ's physical resurrection, which is the very foundation of our hope (Luke 24:39; 1 Corinthians 15:14-17). Additionally, their view of salvation often emphasizes organizational loyalty and personal works, which contrasts with the biblical truth of justification by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You may wonder at the deep sincerity and discipline shown by those within this organization. It is often a genuine desire for order and a sense of purpose that draws people to their community. However, sincerity cannot replace the truth of who Jesus Christ is.

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God the Son took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is not a reward for our service or obedience to an organization, but a free gift of grace received through faith in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in the sufficiency of His work today?

To grow in your understanding of these truths, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John directly from the Scriptures and seek the fellowship of a local church that honors the Bible as the final authority and proclaims the deity of Jesus Christ. 1 John 5:20; Titus 2:13.
What sort of interfaith ministries are appropriate?
What is presuppositional apologetics?

Presuppositional apologetics is an approach to defending the faith which asserts that the Bible is the necessary starting point for all human knowledge and reasoning. It teaches that because God is the source of all truth, we must begin with His revelation rather than attempting to find a neutral ground with those who deny Him.

This method is deeply rooted in the conviction that the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge, as stated in Proverbs 1:7. It recognizes the noetic effects of the fall, which is the theological term describing how sin has darkened the human mind and corrupted our ability to reason rightly about God apart from His grace. According to Romans 1:18; Romans 1:19; Romans 1:20; Romans 1:21, all people possess an inherent knowledge of God through creation, yet they suppress this truth in unrighteousness. Therefore, the presuppositionalist does not try to prove God’s existence as if He were the conclusion of a human argument, but rather demonstrates that God is the very foundation that makes logic, science, and morality possible in the first place.

In practice, this means showing that a non-Christian worldview is ultimately inconsistent because it borrows from Christian truth while simultaneously denying it. For example, a person may claim there is no God while still relying on fixed laws of logic or objective moral standards. The believer points out that these things can only be justified in a universe created and sustained by the God of Scripture. In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, as declared in Colossians 2:3. To attempt to reason without Him is like trying to see while closing your eyes to the very light that makes sight possible.

The ultimate goal of this defense is not to win an intellectual debate, but to point people to their desperate need for the Savior. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness and new life that only He can provide.

If you are speaking with someone who doubts the Gospel, a practical next step is to ask them how their own worldview accounts for the existence of universal, unchanging laws of logic. As you do this, remember to speak with the gentleness and respect commanded in 1 Peter 3:15. You might also find it helpful to study this within the fellowship of your local church, asking an elder for guidance on how to hold the line of truth while showing the tender love of Christ to those who are lost. If you feel overwhelmed by a difficult question, remember to pray and ask the Lord for the wisdom He promises to give generously to all who ask, as written in James 1:5.
What does the Bible say about chanting? Should a Christian chant?

The Bible does not use the specific word "chanting" in the way modern secular or Eastern spiritualities do, but it speaks clearly about the nature of our communication with God. In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus explicitly warns His followers against "vain repetitions" or "babbling like the pagans," who think they will be heard because of their many words (Matthew 6:7).

True Christian worship and prayer are characterized by the engagement of both the spirit and the mind. While some historical traditions use "chanting" as a rhythmic way to sing the Psalms or Scripture, this is distinct from the repetitive use of mantras intended to empty the mind or induce a trance-like state. The Apostle Paul emphasizes that we are to pray with the spirit and also with the understanding (1 Corinthians 14:15). 

Whether a Christian should "chant" depends entirely on the definition of the act. If by chanting one means the repetitive recitation of a word or sound to achieve a different state of consciousness, this is contrary to the biblical model of prayer. Biblical meditation is not the emptying of the mind, but the filling of the mind with the truth of God's Word (Joshua 1:8). We are invited into a personal, cognitive relationship with a God who hears us because of the finished work of Jesus Christ, not because of the frequency or rhythm of our speech.

If you are seeking peace or a closer connection to God, I encourage you to look to the sufficiency of the Lord Jesus. We do not find favor with God through spiritual techniques or linguistic formulas, but through the grace provided by Christ’s death and resurrection. The Gospel tells us that we are sinners separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died in our place to pay the debt for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and new life. 

As a practical next step, I suggest you begin a daily rhythm of reading the Psalms aloud. Instead of repeating a single phrase, allow the rich, diverse language of Scripture to shape your prayers. This engages your heart and mind in the "means of grace" God has provided for your growth and comfort. Have you ever experienced the peace that comes from simply resting in the promises of Scripture rather than trying to achieve a feeling through repetition?
Who is Jesus — Day 4 — Jesus the Prophet

Jesus is the final and supreme Prophet promised by God to reveal His perfect will and the way of salvation to all people. While earlier prophets spoke the words of God, Jesus is the Word of God made flesh, serving as the ultimate Mediator between God and man (John 1:1; John 1:14; 1 Timothy 2:5).

In His prophetic office, Jesus does not merely bring a message; He is the message. In earlier times, God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son (Hebrews 1:1-2). Because He is truly God and truly man, His teaching carries an authority that no other messenger could claim, for He only speaks what the Father has commanded Him (John 12:49-50).

You may find yourself searching for clarity and truth amidst a world of confusing and contradictory voices. It is a natural human longing to want to hear a certain word from the Creator. Jesus meets this need by revealing the heart of the Father and the reality of our condition.

The message this Great Prophet brings is the Gospel. Though we are separated from a holy God by our sin, Jesus took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile us to God. His bodily resurrection confirms His victory over death and the absolute truth of His words. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in the words of the One who spoke the world into existence?
