As a practical step in following this Prophet, I encourage you to read one chapter of the Gospel of John each day this week. As you do, ask the Holy Spirit to help you hear the voice of Jesus and understand His will through His written Word.
Will there be a second chance for salvation after death?

The Bible teaches that there is no second chance for salvation after death; instead, this earthly life is the period God has graciously appointed for us to repent and believe. Scripture is clear that "it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment" (Hebrews 9:27).

This reality can feel heavy, and you may wonder how a merciful God could limit the window of opportunity to our few years on earth. It is a natural concern to desire more time for ourselves or for those we love. However, the Lord, in His perfect wisdom and justice, has set the bounds of our habitation and calls us to respond to Him while it is still "today" (Hebrews 3:15). In the account of the rich man and Lazarus, Jesus describes a "great chasm" that is fixed after death, making it impossible for anyone to cross from one state to the other (Luke 16:26).

Because our physical death marks the end of our opportunity to trust in Christ, the Gospel is presented with a sense of loving urgency. God is holy and sovereign, and our sins have separated us from Him, deserving His righteous judgment. Yet, because of His immense love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, triumphed over death, and is now exalted as Lord of all.

Salvation is not earned by our efforts or "goodness" but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today? I encourage you to set aside time today to speak directly to God in prayer, confessing your need for Him and asking Him to reveal the truth of His Word to your heart. A wonderful next step would be to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the person and work of Jesus clearly for yourself.
What is biblical homemaking?

Biblical homemaking is the intentional and joyful stewardship of the domestic sphere to create an environment where God is honored, the family is nurtured, and the Gospel is lived out in daily life. It is far more than a list of chores, it is a sacred ministry of cultivation and care where the home becomes a sanctuary of grace and a center for Christian hospitality.

The heart of this calling is found in the Word of God, which describes the home as a primary place for spiritual formation and service. In Titus 2:4-5, the Scriptures encourage women to be "workers at home," a term that implies being a guardian or manager of the household. This is echoed in Proverbs 31:27, where the virtuous woman is commended because she "watches over the affairs of her household and does not eat the bread of idleness."

This work is an exercise in stewardship, recognizing that our time, our relationships, and our physical dwellings belong to the Lord. When a home is managed with order and love, it reflects the character of God, who is a God of peace and not of confusion. Through the process of sanctification, even the most mundane tasks like preparing a meal or cleaning a room are transformed into acts of worship when they are done heartily as unto the Lord and not unto men (Colossians 3:23).

Biblical homemaking also extends outward through the practice of hospitality. The home is meant to be a place where "all are welcomed as Christ" and where believers "show hospitality to one another without grumbling" (1 Peter 4:9). It serves as a light in a dark world, providing a tangible picture of the kindness and welcome of God.

For those seeking to grow in this area, a practical first step is to establish a consistent rhythm of "family worship" or personal devotion within the home. Setting aside a specific time each day to read the Scriptures and pray together anchors the household in the truth of God and invites His peace to rule in every heart.

If you find yourself overwhelmed by the demands of the home, remember that your identity is not found in the perfection of your house, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect life, died for our sins, and rose again to give us new life. We serve Him not to earn His favor, but because we have already received it by grace through faith. You can turn to Him today, resting in His strength as you care for those He has placed in your charge. What is the greatest challenge you face in seeking to honor God in your home?
How should Christians view statues of Jesus?

Christians should view statues of Jesus through the lens of the Second Commandment, which addresses the way God’s people are to worship Him. The primary biblical concern is that God is Spirit and cannot be fully or rightly captured by human hands or material things.

The Bible provides a clear boundary in the Second Commandment, which states, "You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth. You shall not bow down to them or serve them" (Exodus 20:4–5). Because God is infinite and holy, any physical representation of Him necessarily falls short of His glory and can easily lead to the sin of idolatry.

There are different historic Christian perspectives on how this applies to statues of Jesus:

First, many in the Reformed Baptist and broader Protestant traditions hold that because Jesus is God, any visual representation of Him is a violation of the Second Commandment. They argue that since we do not know what Jesus looked like, any statue is merely a product of human imagination. More importantly, because Jesus is the "express image of His person" (Hebrews 1:3), His glory cannot be contained in wood or stone. To look at a statue and call it "Jesus" risks worshiping a mental or physical image rather than the living Christ who is revealed in Scripture.

Second, other traditions, such as Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox, use statues or icons as "windows to the divine" or aids for memory and devotion. They distinguish between "veneration," which is a sign of respect, and "worship," which is reserved for God alone. They point to the Incarnation, where the invisible God became a visible man (John 1:14), as a justification for representing Him artistically.

However, the Bible emphasizes that "God is Spirit, and those who worship Him must worship in spirit and truth" (John 4:24). True spiritual growth comes not from sight, but from the Word of God, for "faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of Christ" (Romans 10:17). We are called to walk by faith, not by sight (2 Corinthians 5:7).

As an actionable step, I encourage you to focus your devotional life on the "means of grace" that God has provided, specifically the diligent reading and study of the Bible. Rather than looking to a physical object, look to the Scriptures to see the character, power, and love of your Savior.

My dear friend, the most important thing is not a statue of Christ, but knowing the living Christ Himself. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). But in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord. Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
I am in the process of getting a divorce. Can I start dating, or do I have to wait until the divorce is final?

According to the Scriptures, you should not begin dating until your divorce is legally and finally settled. As long as you are still legally married, you remain in a covenant relationship that God regards as binding until it is officially dissolved.

The Bible presents marriage as a sacred covenant, a "one flesh" union established by God that is intended to last a lifetime, as seen in Genesis 2:24 and Matthew 19:6. To seek a romantic connection with another person while your marriage still exists is to step outside the boundaries of that covenant. Even if the relationship with your spouse has emotionally ended, the pursuit of a new romantic interest during this time is a form of spiritual and relational unfaithfulness. 

I understand that this is likely a season of profound loneliness and perhaps a deep desire to be known and loved after much pain. It is natural to crave the comfort of a new companion when a previous union is fracturing. However, the path of wisdom and obedience to Christ calls for a season of "sanctification" and reflection rather than a search for a new partner. Rushing into dating often serves as a distraction from the healing and repentance that God may want to work in your heart during this difficult transition. 

Your focus right now should be on your relationship with the Lord and the responsibilities you have within your current circumstances. The Apostle Paul reminds us in 1 Corinthians 7:11 that if a person departs, they should remain unmarried or be reconciled. While there are specific biblical grounds where remarriage is permitted after a divorce is finalized—such as cases of sexual immorality or abandonment by an unbeliever—those provisions only apply after the marriage is truly over.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to meet with the elders or pastors of your local church. They can provide the personal oversight, biblical counsel, and accountability you need to navigate this season in a way that honors God. Spend this time in intentional prayer and in the Word, asking God to strengthen your soul and guide your steps. 

For further reflection on the nature of marriage and the heart of God, please consider these passages:

Malachi 2:16
Matthew 5:31–32
1 Corinthians 7:10–15
Hebrews 13:4
What is the importance of the empty tomb?

The empty tomb is the historical and physical proof that Jesus Christ conquered death, confirming that His sacrifice for sin was accepted by God the Father. It serves as the indispensable foundation of the Christian faith, for if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is your faith (1 Corinthians 15:14).

The significance of the empty tomb is tied to the doctrine of justification. It acts as the divine receipt, proving that the debt for our sins was paid in full. As the Scripture declares, Jesus was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification (Romans 4:25). By rising from the dead, Jesus was vindicated and declared with power to be the Son of God (Romans 1:4). The empty tomb distinguishes Jesus from every other religious leader in history, as He alone has defeated the power of the grave.

For the believer, the empty tomb provides a living hope. It guarantees our own future resurrection and the renewal of all things. Because He lives, you can face the reality of death not with despair, but with the confidence that the grave is not the end. When you face seasons of grief or the weight of your own mortality, let the angel's words comfort your soul: "He is not here; He has risen, just as He said" (Matthew 28:6). As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through the resurrection account in John 20 and ask God to deepen your joy in the reality of the risen Christ.

Perhaps you wonder how a tomb in Jerusalem from two thousand years ago changes your life today. The reality of the empty tomb means that Jesus is not a dead martyr to be admired, but a living Lord to be followed. We have all sinned and fallen short of God's holiness, earning the penalty of spiritual and physical death. Yet God, in His great love, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die an atoning death on the cross in our place, and rise again to give us new life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins. What holds you back from trusting in the living Savior today?
What is traducianism?

Traducianism is the theological position that the human soul is transmitted from parents to their children through the process of natural generation, just as the physical body is. This view suggests that when a child is conceived, both the material body and the immaterial soul are derived from the parents rather than the soul being a brand new, immediate creation by God at that specific moment.

The term comes from the Latin word *tradux*, which refers to a shoot or sprout of a vine, illustrating the idea of life being propagated from an existing source. This doctrine is often used to explain the transmission of original sin, which is the fallen state and guilt we inherit from Adam. By viewing the soul as being derived from one's parents, theologians find a clear path for how a sinful nature is passed down through the generations. Scripture notes this reality of our fallen condition from the very start of our existence. Psalm 51:5; Romans 5:12.

Within historic Christianity, there are two primary orthodox views regarding the origin of the soul. While Traducianists emphasize the continuity of the human race in Adam, those who hold to Creationism believe that God creates each individual soul directly and joins it to the body at conception. Both positions seek to be faithful to the Bible, as the Scriptures do not provide an exhaustive explanation of the soul's beginning, though they clearly affirm God as the ultimate source of all life. Numbers 16:22; Hebrews 12:9.

Understanding how we inherit our nature reminds us of our profound need for a Savior. Because we are born with a nature that is naturally inclined away from God, we cannot save ourselves through our own efforts or good works. Salvation is found only in Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, who took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. As a practical next step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to read through the third chapter of the Gospel of John and reflect on what it means to be born again by the Spirit of God. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
I am a Buddhist, why should I consider becoming a Christian?

You may wonder how a person can ever truly escape the cycle of suffering and find a peace that does not depend on your own constant efforts to reach enlightenment. This desire for peace and an end to suffering is a deep and valid human concern, but the Bible offers a different and more hopeful answer than the one you may have known.

The most compelling reason to consider becoming a Christian is that it offers a restored relationship with a personal, loving Creator who entered into our suffering to rescue us. While many traditions focus on personal effort to overcome desire or karma, Christianity teaches that our deepest problem is sin, which is a rebellion against our holy and sovereign God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin (Isaiah 6:3; Habakkuk 1:13).

However, God is also merciful and loving. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life that we cannot live. Jesus then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment we deserved for our sins upon Himself to satisfy the justice of God (1 Peter 3:18). On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and showing that He is the Lord of all (Romans 1:4).

In Christianity, you do not have to work your way toward a state of nothingness or moral perfection. Instead, you are justified, which means being declared legally righteous in God's sight, as a free gift of His grace through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This brings a peace that surpasses understanding because it rests entirely on the finished work of Jesus rather than your own performance.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, notice how Jesus speaks to those who are weary and burdened, and consider asking God to reveal His truth to you as you look at the life of His Son.
How should a Christian view the intellect?

A Christian should view the intellect as a precious, God-given faculty designed to know, love, and glorify the Creator, though it must always function in humble submission to the authority of Scripture. The mind is a vital part of the Imago Dei, the image of God, which enables humanity to think God's thoughts after him and to exercise wise stewardship over creation (Genesis 1:27; Genesis 1:28).

While the intellect is a magnificent gift, we must recognize the noetic effects of sin, which refers to how the Fall has corrupted human reasoning. Because of sin, the natural mind is darkened, futile in its thinking, and inherently hostile toward the truths of God (Ephesians 4:17-18; Romans 8:7). Therefore, the intellect is not a neutral tool or a final judge of truth. It is a fallen instrument that requires the supernatural work of the Holy Spirit to be redirected toward its proper end.

In the life of a believer, the intellect is not suppressed but is instead redeemed and mobilized for the sake of the Gospel. We are commanded to love the Lord our God with all of our mind (Matthew 22:37). This involves the diligent process of sanctification, where we are transformed by the renewal of our minds so that we may discern the will of God (Romans 12:2). True intellectual maturity for a Christian is found in bringing every thought captive to the obedience of Christ (2 Corinthians 10:5). We do not check our brains at the door of the church; rather, we bring our sharpened minds to the foot of the Cross, recognizing that all treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden in Christ alone (Colossians 2:3).

To grow in this area, you should commit to a disciplined study of sound doctrine. One actionable step is to begin reading a systematic theology or a historical confession of faith alongside your daily Bible reading. This will help you categorize biblical truths and protect your mind from the empty philosophies of this world (Colossians 2:8). Ask the Lord in your private prayers to grant you a spirit of wisdom and revelation so that the eyes of your heart and mind may be enlightened to know him better.
What does the Bible say about being zealous / having zeal?

Biblical zeal is a fervent, burning desire of the soul for the glory of God and the advancement of His truth. It is the holy heat of the affections that drives a believer to love what God loves and to hate what God hates with an intensity that moves the heart to action.

The foundation of all righteous zeal is God Himself, who is described as being "clothed with zeal as a cloak" in Isaiah 59:17. This divine passion ensures that His purposes will be accomplished, as seen in the promise of the eternal kingdom of Christ where "the zeal of the LORD of Hosts will accomplish this" in Isaiah 9:7. When the Lord Jesus entered the temple and drove out the money changers, His disciples remembered the words of the Psalmist, "Zeal for your house will consume me," which is recorded in John 2:17. True zeal is never a cold or mechanical duty, but a reflection of the very heart of Christ.

However, Scripture warns that zeal can be dangerous if it is not grounded in the truth of God's Word. Paul spoke of those who had "a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge" in Romans 10:2. Zeal without the light of sound doctrine is like a fire out of the hearth, it can destroy rather than warm. Our passion must be directed by the Holy Spirit and informed by the Bible so that we do not become "zealous for the traditions of my fathers" in a way that opposes the Gospel, as Paul once was before his conversion as described in Galatians 1:14.

The purpose of our redemption is that we might be a people who are "zealous for good works," as stated in Titus 2:14. This fervor is a vital part of progressive sanctification, where the Spirit matures our desires so that we are no longer lukewarm. To the church in Laodicea, the Lord gave the firm but loving command to "be zealous and repent" in Revelation 3:19. If you find your heart growing cold, the remedy is not to manufacture human emotion, but to look again at the cross where Jesus Christ gave Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice for your sins.

As a practical step this week, I encourage you to read through Psalm 119 and ask the Lord to revive your heart according to His Word. Pray specifically that He would align your desires with His own, and ask Him to show you one area in your local church where you can serve with a renewed and joyful energy.

Have you found that your desire for God has been fading lately, or is there a particular truth that currently ignites your heart for Him?
What is the grace of God?

The grace of God is His unmerited favor and divine assistance given to those who deserve only His righteous judgment. It is the sovereign and free gift of God by which He saves, sanctifies, and sustains His people through the person and work of Jesus Christ (Romans 3:24; Ephesians 2:8–9).

To understand grace, we must see it against the backdrop of our own spiritual bankruptcy. Scripture teaches that we were once dead in our trespasses and sins, unable to change our own hearts or earn God’s approval (Ephesians 2:1–3). Grace is not a reward for the diligent, but a rescue for the helpless. While mercy is God withholding the punishment we deserve, grace is God giving us the infinite blessing of His fellowship and the gift of eternal life which we could never earn. This is most clearly seen in our justification, where God declares a sinner righteous because the righteousness of Christ is credited to them by faith alone (Titus 3:5–7).

This grace is not a license to continue in sin, but is the very power that trains us to renounce ungodliness and live self controlled, upright lives (Titus 2:11–12). It is the steady hand of a Father who keeps His children from falling and ensures they will persevere until the end. As the Apostle John wrote, from the fullness of Christ we have all received grace upon grace (John 1:16).

You may wonder how a perfect and just God could ever look upon someone with favor despite their flaws. The answer lies in the Gospel: God is holy and must punish sin, but because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take that punishment upon Himself. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to secure victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith, not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to spend time this week reading the Gospel of John, specifically looking for how Jesus displays kindness to the broken and the undeserving. Let those accounts remind you that His grace is sufficient for every one of your needs (2 Corinthians 12:9).
How young can you be to ask Jesus to be your Savior? Can a 3 or 4 year old truly believe?

Scripture does not set a minimum age for salvation because faith is a gift of God's grace rather than a result of human intellectual maturity. While a child of three or four is still developing cognitively, the Holy Spirit is entirely capable of performing the work of regeneration, which is the sovereign act of God giving a person a new heart and a new nature (John 3:5; John 3:8; Titus 3:5).

Jesus Christ specifically welcomed children, saying, "Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 19:14). He even taught His disciples that unless they turned and became like little children, they would never enter the kingdom of heaven (Matthew 18:3). This suggests that the simple, dependent trust of a child is often closer to the heart of true faith than the complex intellectualism of adults.

For a child to truly believe, they do not need to understand complex systematic theology, but they do need a basic, heart-level grasp of the Gospel. This involves recognizing that they have done wrong things that displease God (sin), believing that Jesus is the Son of God who died on the cross for their sins and rose again, and wanting to love and follow Him. 

It is important to remember that salvation is a work of God. He is holy and just, yet in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death in our place. Christ rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified (declared righteous) by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

When observing a young child, look for signs of genuine spiritual life rather than just the ability to repeat a prayer. These signs include a sincere sorrow for sin, a natural affection for Jesus, and a simple desire to obey Him. You can help a child by consistently sharing the stories of Jesus and modeling a life of repentance and faith yourself.

If you are reflecting on your own standing before God while considering this for a child, know that the same door is open to you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him with the simple faith of a child?

As a practical step, continue to read the Gospels with the child, such as the Gospel of Mark, and ask simple questions to see what they understand about who Jesus is and why He died. Encourage them to talk to God in their own words, expressing their need for Him and their love for Him. Seek the wisdom of your local church elders as you discern the work of the Holy Spirit in the life of a young child.
Is it wrong for a Christian husband and wife to attend separate churches?

While the Bible does not explicitly forbid a husband and wife from attending separate churches, doing so is generally discouraged because it tends to fragment the spiritual unity and shared mission intended for a Christian marriage. Scripture describes marriage as a one flesh union, a profound mystery that reflects the relationship between Jesus Christ and His Church, as seen in Genesis 2:24 and Ephesians 5:31–32.

Attending the same local church allows a couple to sit under the same preaching of the Word, submit to the same elder leadership, and serve the same community together. This shared life is vital for maintaining covenantal unity within the home. When a couple attends separate congregations, they miss the opportunity to discuss the same sermons, pray for the same local needs, and grow together under a single body of believers. Furthermore, it complicates the husband’s biblical responsibility to lead his wife in spiritual matters and to nourish her through the Word, as described in Ephesians 5:25–26.

If the separation is due to secondary doctrinal differences, such as views on baptism or worship style, it is often better for one or both spouses to graciously concede personal preferences for the sake of the harmony of the home. However, if one spouse is attending a church that denies core doctrines of the faith, the other spouse has a duty to remain faithful to the truth of Scripture, as the Bible is our ultimate authority.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to set aside time this week to prayerfully discuss your reasons for attending separate churches. Ask whether these reasons are rooted in essential convictions or personal comforts. Seek to find a faithful, gospel preaching church where both of you can joyfully submit to God’s Word and grow in grace together, for "the two will become one flesh" (Genesis 2:24) applies to your spiritual journey as much as your physical life. You might also find it helpful to read Hebrews 10:24–25 together and consider how you can best stir one another up to love and good works within a single local body.
What is the Druze religion?

The Druze religion is an esoteric, monotheistic faith that originated in the eleventh century as an offshoot of Isma'ili Shia Islam. It is characterized by its belief in the absolute unity of God, the transmigration of souls (reincarnation), and a secretive body of teachings known as the Epistles of Wisdom.

This faith is often described as a form of religious syncretism, as it incorporates elements of Islam, Gnosticism, Neoplatonism, and various other philosophies. A central and defining tenet of the Druze faith is the belief that Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah, a Fatimid caliph, was a divine manifestation of God. This belief is a departure from the biblical revelation of the Trinity, where the one true God eternally exists in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19).

The Druze community is strictly closed; they do not accept converts, nor do they permit their members to marry outside the faith. Their social structure is divided between the *uqqal*, the "wise" or initiated who have access to their sacred texts, and the *juhhal*, the "ignorant" or uninitiated majority. While they emphasize moral uprightness and honesty, their hope is rooted in a cycle of rebirth to achieve spiritual refinement. This stands in direct contradiction to the historical fact of the Gospel, which teaches that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment (Hebrews 9:27).

You may wonder how a community so focused on preserving "hidden wisdom" views the clarity of the Christian message. While we can respect their deep loyalty to their community and their search for truth, the Bible teaches that the fullness of God's wisdom is not hidden in secret scrolls but is openly revealed in the person and work of Jesus Christ (Colossians 2:2-3).

True peace and reconciliation with God do not come through successive lifetimes or human effort, but through the finished work of Christ. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we cannot save ourselves. In His great mercy, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature through the Virgin Birth and lived a sinless life. He offered Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice, dying on the cross to satisfy God's justice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, ascended to heaven, and is now the exalted Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Philippians 2:9-11).

Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or cycles of living (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in His complete work today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see how Jesus reveals the Father and offers eternal life to all who believe in Him. You might also consider praying for the Druze people, asking that God would shine the light of the Gospel into their hearts.
What is an invocation prayer?

An invocation prayer is a formal petition offered at the beginning of a Christian worship service or a significant gathering to invite the presence of God and ask for His blessing, guidance, and help. It serves as a public acknowledgment that we are entirely dependent upon the Lord and that our gathering is centered on His glory rather than our own interests.

This practice is rooted in the biblical pattern of "calling upon the name of the Lord," which began in the earliest days of humanity (Genesis 4:26). Theologically, an invocation recognizes the **sovereignty** of God over the assembly and the necessity of the Holy Spirit's aid for any spiritual work to be fruitful. Because God is holy and we are fallen, we invoke His name with reverence, knowing we have no right to approach Him except through the **mediation** of Jesus Christ, our Great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14-16).

In a congregational setting, the invocation sets a solemn and joyful tone, refocusing the hearts of the people away from the distractions of the world and toward the majesty of the Creator. It is a humble declaration that without the Lord, we can do nothing (John 15:5).

As a practical step in your own walk with Christ, I encourage you to begin your personal Bible study or family worship with a brief invocation. You might simply ask the Lord to "Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things in your law" (Psalm 119:18).

You may wonder how a person can dare to call upon a holy God when we are aware of our own failings and guilt. The Gospel is the beautiful answer to that concern. While our sins deserve the just wrath of God, the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sake. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the spiritual gift of prophecy?

The spiritual gift of prophecy is the God-given ability to proclaim His truth to others for the purpose of strengthening, encouraging, and comforting the church. In its most basic biblical sense, it involves communicating a message from God to His people so that they may grow in faith and holiness (1 Corinthians 14:3).

Within the history of the church, there are different understandings of how this gift operates today. Many believers, particularly in the Reformed tradition, hold to a view called cessationism. This perspective teaches that prophecy as "special revelation" (new, direct words from God) was a foundational gift given to the early church before the Bible was completed (Ephesians 2:20). From this viewpoint, the gift of prophecy today is primarily the Spirit-empowered proclamation or "forthtelling" of the already-revealed Word of God found in the Scriptures (2 Peter 1:19-21).

Other faithful Christians hold to a view called continuationism, believing that the Holy Spirit may still give spontaneous impressions or revelations to individuals for the edification of a local body. However, regardless of which position one holds, all orthodox Christians agree on the absolute sufficiency and authority of the Bible. Any word or teaching claimed to be from God must be tested against the Holy Scriptures, which serve as our final and infallible rule of faith and practice (Acts 17:11; 1 Thessalonians 5:20-21).

The ultimate goal of all spiritual gifts is the exaltation of Jesus Christ and the building up of His bride, the Church. Prophecy is not intended to bring attention to the speaker, but to bring the heart of the listener into closer alignment with the heart of God.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to devote yourself to the regular study of the Bible in the fellowship of your local church. As you grow in your knowledge of God's written Word, you will be better equipped to discern His voice and to speak words of biblical encouragement that truly "build up" those around you (Hebrews 3:13). Whether through formal teaching or a private word of exhortation, let your speech always be seasoned with grace and grounded in the truth of the Gospel.
What does the Bible say about purification?

In the Bible, purification is the process of being made clean and holy, moving from a state of ritual or moral defilement into a state of fitness for God’s presence. It begins with the external rituals of the Old Testament and finds its perfect fulfillment in the internal, spiritual cleansing provided by Jesus Christ (Hebrews 9:13-14).

In the Old Covenant, God provided laws for purification to teach His people about His absolute holiness. Contact with death, disease, or various "unclean" things required specific washings and sacrifices to restore a person to the community and the Tabernacle (Leviticus 12:1-8; Numbers 19:11-22). These ceremonies were vivid, physical illustrations that sin and its effects create a barrier between a holy God and a fallen humanity. They pointed toward our desperate need for a cleansing that water alone could never provide.

The heart of the biblical message is that our deepest defilement is not skin-deep; it is a spiritual stain on the soul caused by sin. Because God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, He cannot overlook the rebellion of the human heart. We are all naturally separated from Him by our transgressions. However, God, in His infinite mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the ultimate means of our purification.

Jesus lived the perfectly pure life we could not live and then offered Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice on the cross. His death paid the penalty for our sins, and His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over death and the power to wash us white as snow. When a person is born again, they undergo the "washing of regeneration," where the Holy Spirit cleanses the heart and dwells within the believer (Titus 3:5).

This purification has two aspects for the Christian. First, there is a definitive cleansing called justification, where God declares you righteous and pure in His sight the moment you trust in Christ (1 Corinthians 6:11). Second, there is a progressive purification called sanctification, where the Holy Spirit works within you to refine your character and turn you away from sinful habits (1 John 3:3).

You may wonder if you can ever truly be clean after the things you have done or thought. The Gospel provides a glorious hope: the blood of Jesus Christ cleanses us from all sin (1 John 1:7). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone. What is it that might be holding you back from resting in His finished work today?

As a practical step in walking in this purity, I encourage you to begin each day by reading a chapter of the New Testament, perhaps starting in the Gospel of John. Ask the Lord in prayer to use His Word to "sanctify you by the truth" (John 17:17) and to help you walk in the light of His presence. If you stumble, do not hide in shame, but go immediately to Him in confession, for He is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9).
Does the Bible say that an increase in technology is a sign of the end times?

The Bible does not explicitly use the modern word "technology," but it does point to an increase in human travel and knowledge as the end of the age approaches. The prophet Daniel was told to seal his vision until the time of the end, when many would roam to and fro and knowledge would increase (Daniel 12:4).

In the study of eschatology, which is the doctrine of the last things, faithful Christians hold various views on this passage. Some interpret this increase in knowledge as a reference to the rapid scientific and technological advancements of the modern era. Others believe it refers to a deeper spiritual understanding of God’s prophetic Word as history nears its conclusion. Regardless of the specific interpretation, we recognize that human innovation is an expression of common grace, the favor God shows to all people that allows for discoveries that can be used for His glory.

The Scriptures place a greater emphasis on the global reach of the Gospel and the moral state of the world than on specific mechanical inventions. Jesus stated that the gospel of the kingdom will be preached in all the world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come (Matthew 24:14). In our day, technology has become a primary tool for fulfilling this commission. Conversely, the Bible warns that the last days will be marked by a decline in godliness, where people are lovers of self, boastful, and proud (2 Timothy 3:1-2). While technology may change the way we live, it cannot change the fallen nature of the human heart.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through Matthew 24 and 25 this week. Focus your heart not on speculating about the timing of Christ’s return, but on the call to be a faithful and wise servant who is ready for His arrival.

You may wonder if the rapid pace of change in our world is a sign that God’s judgment is approaching. It is true that while human knowledge grows, our fundamental problem remains sin and separation from our holy Creator. The Gospel is the only answer to this crisis. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day and is now exalted as Lord. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and the hope of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Savior?
What does the Bible say about caring for our old parents?

The Bible teaches that honoring and providing for elderly parents is a fundamental command of God and a primary way believers demonstrate the sincerity of their faith. This duty is rooted in the moral law of the Ten Commandments, which calls us to honor our father and mother so that we may live long in the land the Lord is giving us (Exodus 20:12; Ephesians 6:2; Ephesians 6:3).

This honor is not merely a sentiment for childhood, but a lifelong commitment of practical service and provision. The Apostle Paul emphasizes that caring for one's own family is an essential expression of familial piety, or godliness in action (1 Timothy 5:4). He speaks with great solemnity on this matter, stating that if anyone fails to provide for his relatives, and especially for those in his own household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever (1 Timothy 5:8).

Our Lord Jesus Christ also upheld the weight of this responsibility. He sharply rebuked the religious leaders of His day for creating traditions that allowed people to avoid supporting their aging parents under the guise of religious devotion (Matthew 15:3-9). Furthermore, even in the midst of His sacrificial suffering on the cross, Jesus demonstrated this care by ensuring His mother, Mary, would be looked after by the apostle John (John 19:26; John 19:27).

Caring for parents often requires deep patience and sacrifice, yet it is a beautiful reflection of the Gospel. Just as God has been patient and provident toward us in our weakness, we are called to show that same tender mercy to those who once cared for us. This service is a way of "repaying" our parents for their years of labor and is pleasing in the sight of God.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to set aside a specific time this week to assess your parents' current needs, whether they are physical, financial, or spiritual. Reach out to them directly to ask how you can best support them, and consider involving your local church elders or siblings in a conversation to create a sustainable plan for their long term care. Seek the Lord in prayer, asking Him to grant you a servant's heart and the wisdom to balance your various responsibilities with grace.
Does salvation affect more than just the afterlife?

Salvation is the comprehensive work of God to rescue the whole person, affecting our past, our present, and our eternal future. It is far more than a "fire insurance" policy for the afterlife; it is the beginning of a restored relationship with the Creator that transforms a person's life here and now.

In the Bible, salvation is often described in three tenses. First, there is a past reality called **justification**, where the believer is legally declared righteous before God and freed from the penalty of sin the moment they trust in Christ (Romans 5:1). Second, there is a present process called **sanctification**, where the Holy Spirit works within the believer to break the power of sin and conform them into the image of Jesus (Romans 6:22). Finally, there is the future hope of **glorification**, when the believer will be physically resurrected and entirely freed from the very presence of sin (Romans 8:30).

Because salvation involves being made a "new creation" (2 Corinthians 5:17), it fundamentally alters your identity, your desires, and your purpose today. It brings peace of conscience, a new capacity to love others, and the power to live a life that pleases God. As Titus 2:11-12 explains, the grace of God that brings salvation also "trains us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age."

This life-altering salvation is rooted in the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all rebelled against Him through sin, earning His righteous judgment. In His great love, God the Son became man in Jesus Christ. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You may wonder if you must "clean yourself up" before coming to God, but the Gospel invites you to come as you are to the One who can make you new. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a transformed life.

What is currently holding you back from trusting Him with your life today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the book of Titus, specifically Titus 2:11-14, and reflect on how God's grace is intended to train and change your daily conduct. Seek out a local, Bible-preaching church where you can grow in these truths alongside other believers.
What is the difference between Judaism and Islam?

Judaism is the religious expression of the covenant God made with the descendants of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, centered on the Torah and the history of Israel. Islam is a monotheistic faith that emerged in the seventh century through the teachings of Muhammad, centered on the Quran as its final authority.

The primary differences between these two religions involve their history, their scriptures, and their understanding of the way to God. Judaism finds its foundation in the Tanakh (the Old Testament) and focuses on the life of the Jewish people under the Law of Moses. Practitioners are still waiting for the arrival of the Messiah. Islam, however, teaches that the Quran is the final and perfect revelation from God, superseding the previous revelations of the Bible. It emphasizes submission to the will of Allah through the Five Pillars, which include the confession of faith, prayer, fasting, almsgiving, and a pilgrimage to Mecca.

While both religions emphasize the oneness of God and reject the doctrine of the Trinity, they differ most significantly in their view of Jesus Christ. Judaism generally regards Jesus as a historical figure or a teacher who did not fulfill the messianic requirements. Islam honors Jesus as a great prophet, born of a virgin, but explicitly denies His divinity, His status as the Son of God, and His sacrificial death on the cross for the sins of the world.

You may wonder how two faiths that both claim a connection to the patriarch Abraham can arrive at such different conclusions regarding the nature of God and the path to salvation. Acknowledge that the desire to worship the one true God and to live a life of devotion is a profound human pursuit. However, the Bible teaches that the only way to the Father is through the Son (John 14:6).

Scripture reveals that God is perfectly holy and just, and because all people have sinned, we are separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). No amount of law-keeping or religious submission can remove the guilt of our sin. In His mercy, God the Son became man in the person of Jesus Christ, being conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the Virgin Mary. He lived a sinless life and offered Himself as a perfect sacrifice, dying on the cross to satisfy the justice of God on behalf of sinners. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 4:25).

Salvation is a gift of God, provided by grace alone and received through faith alone in Jesus Christ, not by any works we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus is the only Mediator between God and men (1 Timothy 2:5). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What does it mean to trust in the person and work of Jesus Christ?

Trusting in the person and work of Jesus Christ means placing your entire confidence in who He is as the eternal God-man and what He accomplished through His life, death, and resurrection to reconcile you to God. It is far more than mere mental agreement with historical facts, it is a heartfelt reliance on Christ alone as your only hope for justification and eternal life.

To trust in His person is to believe that Jesus is truly God and truly man, a reality often called the hypostatic union. As the eternal Son of God, He took on human nature to dwell among us, remaining sinless and perfectly reflecting the Father's glory (John 1:1; John 1:14; Hebrews 1:3). Because He is fully man, He can represent us as our High Priest, and because He is fully God, His sacrifice carries infinite value to satisfy the justice of an infinite God.

To trust in His work is to rest in His finished achievements rather than your own moral efforts. This includes His active obedience, where He lived the perfect life of righteousness that we failed to live, and His passive obedience, where He submitted to the cross to bear the penalty for our sins. This is the heart of penal substitutionary atonement, Jesus took the punishment we deserved so that we might receive the blessing He earned (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). His bodily resurrection is the absolute proof that God accepted His sacrifice and that death has been defeated (1 Corinthians 15:17-20).

The biblical reality is that God is perfectly holy, and our sin has created a chasm that no human effort can bridge (Isaiah 6:3; Romans 3:23). We are naturally separated from God and under His righteous judgment. However, the Gospel declares that salvation is a gift of grace. Christ’s historical death, burial, and resurrection provide the only way for sinners to be declared righteous. You do not befriend God through your works, you are reconciled to Him through the blood of His Son (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 5:1).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your journey, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Gospel of John. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal the beauty of Jesus to you through His Word, and consider speaking with a pastor at a local, Bible-believing church who can walk alongside you in this faith.
Who was Plato? How did Platonism / Platonic thought influence society in Bible times?

Plato (c. 427–347 BC) was a foundational Greek philosopher, a student of Socrates, and the teacher of Aristotle. He is best known for his "Theory of Forms," which suggests that the physical world we perceive is merely a shadow of a higher, eternal, and perfect reality. His work, preserved in famous dialogues like *The Republic*, shaped the intellectual and cultural atmosphere of the Mediterranean world for centuries, including the era of the New Testament.

In Bible times, Platonic thought influenced society through a perspective known as dualism, which created a sharp divide between the spiritual and the material. This mindset led many to believe that the soul was good and eternal, while the physical body was a "prison" to be escaped. This cultural backdrop presented several challenges and opportunities for the early Church:

1. **The Nature of Christ:** Because Platonism often viewed matter as inferior or even corrupt, the idea of the Incarnation—God becoming a physical human being—was a "stumbling block" to many (1 Corinthians 1:23). However, the Apostle John addressed this head-on. He used the term *Logos* (the Word), a concept familiar to those influenced by Greek philosophy, to identify Jesus as the true source of all reason and creation, emphasizing that this Word truly "became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:1; John 1:14).

2. **The Resurrection of the Body:** The Platonic influence made the doctrine of the bodily resurrection difficult for many to accept. When the Apostle Paul preached in Athens, he was mocked by philosophers because they saw the body as something to be discarded, not redeemed (Acts 17:32). Christians, however, upheld the historical fact of Christ's resurrection as the guarantee that our own bodies will be raised and glorified (1 Corinthians 15:20–22).

3. **Gnosticism:** Later, distorted versions of Platonic dualism contributed to the heresy of Gnosticism, which claimed that the physical world was created by a lesser, evil deity. The New Testament writers, such as John and Paul, countered this by affirming that God created all things through Christ and that the physical world is part of His good and intentional design (Colossians 1:16–17).

It is vital for us to remember that our faith is not one of escape, but of redemption. God does not hate the world He made; He loved it enough to send His Son to save it (John 3:16). While Platonism looked for a shadow-world of ideas, we look for the return of a real, physical Savior who will make all things new.

As a practical step for your own growth, I encourage you to spend some time reading the first chapter of the Gospel of John this week. Consider how John bridges the gap between the deep longings of the human mind for "Truth" and the historical reality of Jesus Christ.

What aspects of the resurrection of the body do you find most encouraging or perhaps most difficult to grasp in our modern age?
Is a virtual/online communion service biblical?

The Lord’s Supper is a holy ordinance intended for the physically gathered local church, serving as a visible sign of our spiritual union with Christ and our corporate unity with one another. While technology allows us to hear the Word preached from a distance, the biblical pattern for Communion consistently emphasizes the physical assembly of believers.

The Apostle Paul, in his instructions to the church in Corinth, repeatedly uses the phrase "when you come together" in the context of the Lord’s Supper (1 Corinthians 11:17; 1 Corinthians 11:18; 1 Corinthians 11:20; 1 Corinthians 11:33; 1 Corinthians 11:34). This suggests that the "togetherness" is not merely a shared time or a digital connection, but a physical proximity where the church acts as a single body. The theological term for this is *koinonia*, often translated as "fellowship" or "participation," which involves a shared life and a mutual partaking of the elements at a common table (1 Corinthians 10:16).

Furthermore, the symbolism of the ordinance relies heavily on the presence of the community. Paul writes, "Because there is one loaf, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one loaf" (1 Corinthians 10:17). A virtual setting, where individuals provide their own elements in isolation, can obscure this profound picture of the "many" being "one" in the local assembly. Additionally, the Lord’s Supper is a meal under the oversight of church elders, who are tasked with guarding the table and ensuring it is observed with reverence and proper self examination (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). This pastoral care and accountability are difficult to maintain in a purely virtual environment.

There are differing views on this matter, especially in times of crisis or for those who are homebound. Some believers hold that a "spiritual presence" or a "virtual gathering" is a permissible exception when physical assembly is impossible, viewing the heart’s intent as more vital than the physical location. However, the historic and majority biblical view is that because the Supper is a corporate act of the visible church, it should be reserved for the physical gathering.

If you find yourself unable to attend a physical service due to illness or other trials, do not let your heart be troubled as though you are cut off from Christ’s grace. His Spirit is with you wherever you are. You might consider contacting the elders of your local church to see if they can visit you to pray and administer the elements in person. This preserves the biblical pattern of fellowship and pastoral oversight while meeting your spiritual need for the comforts of the Gospel found in the bread and the cup.
Why do some churches thrive while others die?

The vitality of a local church depends fundamentally on its union with Jesus Christ, who is the Head of the body and the source of all spiritual life. While many pragmatic factors exist, a church truly thrives when it honors the Word of God and relies upon the power of the Holy Spirit, whereas a church begins to die when it drifts from the Gospel or loses its devotion to Christ.

Our Lord Jesus, who walks among the golden lampstands, warns that a church can have a reputation for being alive while actually being dead (Revelation 3:1). A thriving church is not merely one with a large budget or a crowded sanctuary, but one where the Word of Christ dwells richly (Colossians 3:16). When a congregation neglects the "means of grace" (the faithful preaching of Scripture, fervent prayer, and the right administration of baptism and the Lord’s Supper), it loses its spiritual nourishment. Without this food, the flock grows weak and eventually scatters.

The reasons for a church’s decline often involve a slow, subtle departure from the "faith that was once for all entrusted to the saints" (Jude 1:3). When a church begins to prioritize cultural relevance or human tradition over the authority of Scripture, it exchanges the living water for broken cisterns that cannot hold water (Jeremiah 2:13). Furthermore, a lack of love and unity can grieve the Holy Spirit. Jesus taught that the world would know we are His disciples by our love for one another (John 13:35). A church consumed by internal strife or legalism eventually devours itself (Galatians 5:15).

However, we must also recognize the "sovereignty" of God in the life of a local assembly. Sometimes, in His providence, a church may conclude its ministry because its specific mission in a location is finished, or as a form of judgment when a people refuse to repent of persistent sin (Revelation 2:5). Yet, even in the face of decline, there is always hope in the Gospel for renewal and "sanctification." Christ is a tender Shepherd who is able to revive the weary and restore the wandering.

If you are concerned about the health of your own local church, I encourage you to commit to a season of focused prayer for your elders and fellow members. Ask God to grant your congregation a fresh hunger for His Word and a deeper love for the person of Jesus Christ. Your next actionable step might be to host a small gathering for prayer and Scripture reading, seeking to encourage one another to "stir up one another to love and good works" (Hebrews 10:24-25).

A thriving church is one that remains centered on the historical death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ as the only hope for sinners. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from your sin and believe in Jesus Christ today. He is the one who died to reconcile us to God and rose again to give us eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is your current understanding of the Gospel, and how does that shape your view of the church?
How much authority should a pastor have over a church?

A pastor's authority is a delegated, ministerial authority from Jesus Christ, who is the only Head of the Church, and it is strictly limited by the Word of God for the purpose of teaching, protecting, and overseeing the flock. This authority is not personal or absolute but is a stewardship given to serve the body of Christ and ensure that the Gospel is purely proclaimed and the people of God are cared for in love.

The Scriptures teach that pastors (also called elders or overseers) are to shepherd the flock of God willingly and eagerly, not lording it over those entrusted to them, but being examples to the flock 1 Peter 5:1–4. The congregation is commanded to obey and submit to their leaders because these men keep watch over their souls as those who must give an account Hebrews 13:17. This authority is exercised primarily through the preaching of the Word and the application of biblical principles to the life of the church Acts 20:28; 1 Timothy 5:17.

Because church government is a secondary doctrine, faithful Christians hold several historic views regarding how this authority is structured:

1.  **The Episcopal View:** In this framework, authority resides in a hierarchy of individual bishops who oversee multiple churches. This view emphasizes historical succession and a top down structure of accountability.
2.  **The Presbyterian View:** This model places authority in a plurality of elders at the local level (the session) and in regional and national gatherings of elders (presbyteries and synods). It emphasizes representative government and mutual accountability across the broader church.
3.  **The Congregational View:** This view maintains that the highest authority under Christ resides in the local congregation itself. While elders lead and oversee, the final decisions on major matters like membership and the calling of pastors rest with the whole body Matthew 18:15–17; Acts 6:3–5.

Within the Reformed Baptist tradition, we believe that the most biblical model is a form of elder led congregationalism. In this view, the elders provide spiritual leadership and oversight, but they do so with the consent and affirmation of the congregation. The pastor’s authority is only as great as his faithfulness to the Bible; if a pastor departs from the truth of Scripture, he loses his claim to that authority.

It is vital to remember that all church authority exists to point us toward the supreme authority of Jesus Christ. He is the one who took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death, and now reigns as Lord of all. Salvation is found in Him alone, by grace alone, through faith alone. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step in your own discipleship, I encourage you to read through the qualifications for leaders in 1 Timothy 3:1–7 and Titus 1:5–9. Use these passages as a guide to pray specifically for your pastors, asking that God would keep them humble, protect their integrity, and grant them wisdom as they seek to lead you toward Christ. What are some of the qualities you see in your current leaders that most encourage your faith?
What is Luciferianism?

Luciferianism is a belief system, either atheistic or theistic, that honors Lucifer as a bringer of enlightenment, independence, and human progress. It often views the biblical God as a restrictive or tyrannical figure and characterizes Lucifer as the true "light-bearer" who helps humanity achieve self-deification and personal autonomy.

From a biblical perspective, this worldview is rooted in a fundamental deception. The name Lucifer, meaning "morning star" or "shining one," appears in the Latin Vulgate translation of Isaiah 14:12, where the prophet describes the fall of a prideful king, a passage historically understood by the church to describe the spiritual fall of Satan himself. Scripture records this fall in Isaiah 14:12, saying, "How you have fallen from heaven, O morning star, son of the dawn! You have been cut down to the ground, O conqueror of nations." While Luciferianism presents itself as a path to wisdom and light, the Apostle Paul warns us that this is a facade, stating that "Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light" in 2 Corinthians 11:14.

The core error of Luciferianism is the exaltation of the creature over the Creator. It is a modern expression of the very first temptation in the Garden of Eden, where the serpent promised that "you will be like God" in Genesis 3:5. This pursuit of autonomy is a heavy burden, for humans were never meant to carry the weight of being their own gods. Such a path leads not to freedom, but to spiritual separation and death.

You may wonder if true freedom can really be found in submission to a Creator rather than in following your own path. It is important to know that God is not a tyrant, but a loving Father who created you for His glory and your joy. True enlightenment and life are found only in Jesus Christ, who declared in John 8:12, "I am the light of the world. Whoever follows Me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life."

I invite you to turn from the pursuit of self-exaltation and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for your sins and rose from the dead to reconcile you to God. Only in Him can you find the forgiveness and purpose that the world's philosophies cannot provide. As a next step, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the Gospel of John, which describes the coming of the true Light into our world, and pray that God would open your eyes to His truth. What is it about this particular belief system that has captured your interest?
What is the satisfaction or commercial theory of the atonement?

The satisfaction theory of the atonement is a theological understanding of Christ's work that emphasizes His death as a necessary payment to restore the honor of God, which was insulted by human sin. Developed most notably by Anselm of Canterbury in the eleventh century, this view argues that because God is the sovereign of the universe, sin is not merely a mistake but a violation of His infinite honor, creating a debt that humanity is obligated to pay but utterly unable to satisfy.

In this framework, the gravity of sin is measured by the one against whom it is committed. Since God is infinite, the debt incurred by sin is also infinite. Therefore, only one who is both truly God and truly man (the *Cur Deus Homo* or "Why God Became Man") could offer a satisfaction of sufficient value. Jesus Christ, through His voluntary death, offered a gift of such infinite worth to the Father that it surpassed the debt of human sin, allowing God to be merciful without compromising His own character.

While this theory is sometimes called the "commercial theory" because it uses the language of debt and merit, it is more accurately seen as a "legal-honor" framework. It correctly identifies that sin is a direct offense against God himself and that we are powerless to fix what we have broken. As the Scriptures say, "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). We find that "He had to be made like His brothers in every way, so that He might become a merciful and faithful high priest in service to God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people" (Hebrews 2:17).

Though the satisfaction theory laid the groundwork for the later Reformation focus on penal substitutionary atonement, it focuses more on God's wounded honor than on the judicial penalty of the law. Nevertheless, it points us toward the glorious truth of the Gospel: God's holiness is so great that sin cannot be ignored, yet His love is so vast that He provided the payment Himself. You may wonder how a holy God could ever look upon someone who has failed Him so deeply. The answer lies in the cross, where the debt that stood against us was canceled, "having nailed it to the cross" (Colossians 2:14).

Because of this work, salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone. God is holy, and our sin has separated us from Him, but Christ's perfect life and substitutionary death on the cross have fully satisfied the requirements of divine justice. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step in your spiritual walk, I encourage you to read the book of Hebrews this week. Focus on how Christ is presented as the perfect sacrifice who does for us what we could never do for ourselves. Does the reality of Christ paying your infinite debt change how you view your daily struggles and God's love for you?
Who are the Shakers?

The Shakers, formally known as the United Society of Believers in Christ’s Second Appearing, were a religious sect that emerged in the eighteenth century, characterized by their communal lifestyle, strict pacifism, and ecstatic, "shaking" worship. While they are often remembered today for their simple furniture and industrious spirit, their movement was built upon theological foundations that depart from historic, biblical Christianity.

The group was led by Mother Ann Lee, who moved the small congregation from England to America in 1774. The Shakers believed that Ann Lee was the female incarnation of the Christ spirit, representing the second appearing of Christ on earth. This belief stands in direct opposition to the biblical truth that Jesus Christ is the unique and final incarnation of God, who will return personally and bodily in glory to judge the living and the dead, as described in Acts 1:11 and Hebrews 9:28.

One of the most defining marks of the Shakers was their requirement of absolute celibacy. They believed that the "fall of man" in the Garden of Eden was primarily sexual in nature, and therefore, true holiness required the total abandonment of marriage and procreation. While the Shakers sought a life of purity, this mandate contradicts the Word of God, which honors marriage as a gift. The Bible teaches that marriage is a holy institution and that the marriage bed is undefiled, as seen in Hebrews 13:4 and 1 Timothy 4:1-3.

In their pursuit of a sinless life, the Shakers practiced a form of communal living where all property was shared, following a strict "order" in all things. However, their doctrine regarding the nature of God was also problematic, as they viewed God as having a dual male and female nature rather than the biblical revelation of the Trinity, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, as established in Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14.

It is a sobering reminder for us that sincerity and hard work, while admirable, cannot replace the foundation of sound doctrine. The Shakers attempted to build a kingdom of heaven on earth through human effort and legalistic rules, but the Gospel teaches us that our righteousness is found in Christ alone. We are justified not by our works or our ability to maintain a perfect community, but by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, as stated in Ephesians 2:8-9 and Romans 3:24.

If you find yourself striving to earn God's favor through your own strength or strict rules, I encourage you to rest in the finished work of Jesus. He has already accomplished everything necessary for your salvation through His death and resurrection. As a practical step, I invite you to spend time this week reading the book of Galatians, which beautifully defends the freedom we have in Christ against the weight of legalism. Trust in Him today, for His yoke is easy and His burden is light, according to Matthew 11:30.
What is the favor of God, and how can I get it?

The favor of God is His unmerited kindness, grace, and divine approval directed toward His people through Jesus Christ. It is not a wage earned by human effort or moral performance, but a gift flowing from God’s sovereign love and good pleasure. To have God’s favor is to be reconciled to Him, enjoying His presence, protection, and the spiritual blessings that come from being His child.

You may wonder how a person could ever stand before a holy God and receive His smile rather than His judgment. This is a vital question because the Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, while every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory, as written in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God and cannot gain His favor through our own good works or religious rituals.

However, the wonderful news of the Gospel is that what we could never do for ourselves, God has done for us. God the Father, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, fulfilling all of God’s requirements. He then willingly went to the cross to suffer the punishment we deserved for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and proving He is Lord of all.

We receive the favor of God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When a person trusts in Jesus, God credits the perfect righteousness of Christ to them, a reality theologians call imputed righteousness. In Christ, God no longer sees your sins, but instead sees the perfection of His Son. As Ephesians 2:8-9 states, "For it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this not from yourselves; it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast."

True favor is found only in a relationship with God through His Son. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

To grow in your understanding of this favor, I encourage you to take the following practical steps:

1. Read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It beautifully illustrates the love of God and the deity of Jesus Christ.
2. Begin a rhythm of daily prayer, simply speaking to God from your heart, thanking Him for His grace and asking Him to lead you.
3. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Gospel is preached, so you can learn and grow alongside other believers.

Scripture assures us in Romans 5:1-2 that "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have gained access by faith into this grace in which we now stand." This standing in grace is the ultimate definition of God’s favor. It is a secure, eternal position given to all who believe. Trust in Him, for He is faithful and His mercy endures forever.
What does it mean that Jesus is the Savior?

To say that Jesus is the Savior means that He is the only person appointed by God to rescue humanity from the guilt, penalty, and power of sin. This rescue is a historical and spiritual reality accomplished through His perfect life, substitutionary death, and bodily resurrection.

You may wonder how a holy and just God could ever reconcile with people who have broken His laws. It is a profound concern, for the Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and cannot dwell with evil (Habakkuk 1:13). Because every person has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23), we are naturally separated from Him and face the just consequence of our rebellion, which is eternal death (Romans 6:23).

However, in His great love and mercy, God provided the way for our restoration. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came into the world through the Virgin Birth to do what we could not. He lived a life of perfect, sinless obedience to God's law. In His great work of atonement, Jesus willingly went to the cross to serve as our substitute. He took our sin upon Himself and endured the righteous wrath of God that we deserved, dying in our place (1 Peter 2:24). 

He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His absolute victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all, and He intercedes for those who come to God through Him (Hebrews 7:25). This salvation is not earned by our good works or religious efforts; it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). To be saved is to be justified, which means being declared righteous in God's sight because the righteousness of Jesus is credited to us (2 Corinthians 5:21).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Savior today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31). I also suggest finding a local, Bible teaching church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find fellowship with other believers.
What is baby dedication? Is child / baby dedication biblical?

Baby dedication is a voluntary public ceremony in which parents present their child before the congregation and solemnly pledge to raise them in the nurture and admonition of the Lord. It is an act of stewardship where parents acknowledge that their child is a gift from God and commit themselves to modeling a Christ centered life, teaching the Scriptures, and praying for the child's future conversion.

While the specific ceremony of "baby dedication" is not a formal command or a sacrament like baptism or the Lord’s Supper, it is a practice rooted in biblical principles. We see historical precedents for presenting children to God, such as Hannah bringing her son Samuel to the temple in 1 Samuel 1:27-28, and Mary and Joseph presenting the infant Jesus in the temple according to the Law of Moses in Luke 2:22. These examples show a heart of gratitude and a desire to set the child apart for God's purposes.

The biblical foundation for dedication rests primarily on the responsibility of parents to disciple their children. Scripture instructs parents to teach God’s Word diligently to their children at all times in Deuteronomy 6:6-7 and commands fathers to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord in Ephesians 6:4.
