The gift of tongues, whether as human languages previously unknown to the speaker or as a spiritual utterance, was a specific sign given to the Church by the Holy Spirit after Christ's ascension and exaltation (Acts 2:4-11). This gift served to empower the apostles and the early believers to spread the Gospel across linguistic barriers and to serve as a sign of God's judgment and grace (1 Corinthians 14:21-22). Jesus did not need to speak in tongues to authenticate His message, for His life, His clear teaching, and His resurrection from the dead served as the ultimate proof of His divinity and Messiahship (John 20:30-31).

If you are seeking to understand the nature of God's communication with us, I encourage you to set aside time this week to read through the Gospel of John. Pay close attention to how Jesus speaks with such profound clarity and authority, for in Him, God has spoken His final and most beautiful word to us (Hebrews 1:1-2). 

You might consider asking God in your own words to help you hear His voice through the written Word and to give you a heart that is quick to obey what He has plainly revealed. Have you found yourself more drawn to the miraculous signs in the Bible lately, or to the specific teachings of Jesus?
What does the Bible say about dealing with regrets?

The Bible teaches that regret is a heavy burden intended to drive us toward the mercy of God rather than into the darkness of despair. Scripture makes a vital distinction between godly sorrow, which brings repentance leading to salvation, and worldly sorrow, which produces only death (2 Corinthians 7:10).

When we are haunted by the "if onlys" of life, we must look to the finished work of Jesus Christ. If your regret stems from past sin, know that for the believer, Christ has made full propitiation for those sins on the cross (1 John 2:2). Propitiation means that His sacrifice turned away God's righteous wrath by satisfying the demands of justice. By His substitutionary death, He took the penalty we deserved, and through His resurrection, He secured our justification (Romans 4:25). Justification is the legal act where God declares a sinner righteous because of the merit of Christ. Because of this, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). Even when our hearts condemn us, God is greater than our hearts (1 John 3:20).

Furthermore, God is the sovereign Lord who redeems our time and our failures. He promised His people that He would restore the years the locusts had eaten (Joel 2:25). While we cannot change the past, God is able to use even our failures to produce humility and a deeper reliance on His grace. Like the Apostle Paul, we are encouraged to forget what lies behind and reach forward to what lies ahead, pressing toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus (Philippians 3:13; Philippians 3:14).

If you find yourself paralyzed by the weight of your past, an important actionable step is to take those specific regrets to God in prayer. Do not merely dwell on them in your own mind, but name them before Him, ask for His perspective from the Word, and then purposefully choose to serve Him in your current circumstances. Trust that He is currently working all things together for the good of those who love Him and are called according to His purpose (Romans 8:28).

You may wonder if your past mistakes or the weight of your regret have permanently disqualified you from God’s favor. The truth of the Gospel is that while God is holy and our sin truly separates us from Him, Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh and lived a perfect life on our behalf. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the full price for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or our ability to fix our past mistakes. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Monergism vs. synergism - which view is correct?

Monergism is the theological conviction that God alone works to bring about the rebirth of a sinner, while synergism is the view that God and man cooperate in the process of salvation. The debate centers on whether the Holy Spirit sovereignly gives life to the spiritually dead heart so that a person believes, or if God offers grace that the human will must then accept or reject.

In the monergistic view, which is the historic position of the Reformed tradition, regeneration precedes faith. This means that because we are naturally dead in our sins and unable to seek God on our own, God must first perform a miracle in our hearts to enable us to believe. The Bible describes this as God taking away a heart of stone and giving a heart of flesh in Ezekiel 36:26. This view emphasizes that salvation is a work of God from start to finish, ensuring that He receives all the glory. As the Apostle Paul writes, it does not depend on human desire or effort, but on God's mercy in Romans 9:16. Other supporting passages include John 1:13, where children of God are born not of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God, and Ephesians 2:1-5, which describes God making us alive while we were yet dead in our trespasses.

Synergism, on the other hand, is a view held by many faithful Christians, including those in the Wesleyan, Arminian, and Eastern Orthodox traditions. This perspective suggests that God provides a form of grace to all people, often called prevenient grace, which restores the human ability to choose Him. In this framework, God makes the offer of salvation, but the final determination rests upon the individual's choice to cooperate with that grace. Proponents often point to the many "whosoever" invitations in Scripture, such as John 3:16 and Revelation 22:17, arguing that these commands to believe imply a present human ability to respond.

While both views seek to honor the Bible, the monergistic position provides a more consistent explanation for the total depravity of the human heart and the absolute necessity of sovereign grace. If we are truly dead in sin, a dead man cannot cooperate with his resurrection. It is the Lord Jesus who calls the dead to life, just as He called Lazarus from the tomb. This gives the believer great comfort, knowing that their salvation rests in the unshakeable hands of a sovereign God rather than their own fickle will.

Regardless of where one stands on this deep mystery, the heart of the Christian faith remains the same. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory. Because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever sensed the Lord drawing you to Himself in this way?

As you reflect on these truths, I encourage you to read through the first two chapters of the book of Ephesians this week. Pay close attention to how the Apostle Paul describes the state of the human heart and the source of our salvation. May you find great peace in the kindness of our Savior.
Did Old Testament believers have eternal security?

Yes, believers in the Old Testament enjoyed the same eternal security that believers possess today because their salvation was anchored in the unchanging nature and sovereign promise of God. The Bible teaches that salvation has always been a work of divine grace received through faith alone, as seen when Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness (Genesis 15:6; Romans 4:3).

The theological term for this is the preservation of the saints. This doctrine emphasizes that our security rests not on our own fickle strength, but on the faithfulness of God to His covenant. In the Old Testament, the Lord explicitly promised His people, "For the LORD loves justice and will not forsake His saints; they are preserved forever" (Psalm 37:28). He further assured them of His keeping power, saying, "I will make an everlasting covenant with them: I will never stop doing good to them, and I will put the fear of Me in their hearts, so that they will never turn away from Me" (Jeremiah 32:40).

This security is seen vividly in the life of David. Though he fell into terrible sin, God’s covenant with him remained sure because it was based on God’s own character and oath rather than David's perfection (Psalm 89:30-34). David’s eventual repentance and restoration to fellowship were a testimony to the fact that God does not abandon those He has redeemed (Psalm 138:8). While Old Testament saints looked forward to the promised Messiah and we look back at His finished work, the source of security remains the same: the power of God to keep those who belong to Him.

If you feel the weight of your own failures today, take comfort in knowing that the same God who kept Abraham, David, and Ruth is the God who keeps those who trust in Him now. True security is found by looking away from your own performance and looking entirely to the perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. He took the judgment for sin that we deserved so that we might be reconciled to God forever. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and an unshakable eternal life.

What holds you back from resting fully in the promise that God will never let you go?
What does it mean that the church is the bride of Christ?

The church being the bride of Christ is a profound biblical metaphor representing the intimate, sacrificial, and covenantal union between Jesus Christ and His redeemed people. It signifies that the church is the object of Christ's special love, the focus of His redemptive work, and the companion of His eternal joy.

This relationship is most clearly described by the Apostle Paul, who explains that Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her to make her holy, cleansing her by the washing with water through the word, as seen in Ephesians 5:25-27. This imagery teaches us several vital truths about our life in Him.

First, it speaks of Christ's sacrificial love. Just as a husband is called to love his wife, Jesus demonstrated the ultimate love by laying down His life for His people. This is the heart of the atonement, where Christ took our place to reconcile us to God. Through His penal substitutionary atonement, He paid the debt of our sin so that we might be presented to Him in purity.

Second, it describes our progressive sanctification. Christ is currently at work in His church, refining and purifying us through His Word and Spirit. He is preparing us for a day when we will be presented to Him without stain, wrinkle, or any other blemish, as stated in Ephesians 5:27. This means that as believers, we are called to live lives of devotion and holiness, keeping ourselves from the world’s pollutions out of love for our Bridegroom.

Third, it points to our future hope. The Bible concludes with the vision of the Marriage Supper of the Lamb, where the union between Christ and His church is fully and visibly consummated. Revelation 19:7-9 tells us to rejoice and be glad because the marriage of the Lamb has come, and His bride has made herself ready. This is the great culmination of history, where we will dwell with Him in perfect fellowship forever.

To truly understand this mystery, we must recognize our own need for a Savior. God is holy and just, yet we have all turned away in sin and rebellion. But God, in His infinite mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering sin and the grave. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your journey of faith, I encourage you to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Gospel is faithfully preached. Just as a bride flourishes in the care of her husband, a believer flourishes within the fellowship and accountability of the local body of Christ. Spend time this week reading through the book of Ephesians, asking the Lord to show you the depth of His love for His people.
How can I believe in the goodness of God when there is so much evil in the world?

You may find it difficult to reconcile the idea of a loving Creator with the reality of cancer, war, and heartbreak that we see every day. This struggle is understandable because we were created for a world of perfect harmony, and our souls rightly recoil at the presence of death and injustice.

The Bible teaches that God is light and in Him is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). He created a world that was very good, but through the rebellion of humanity, sin and death entered our experience (Genesis 1:31; Romans 5:12). Much of the evil we see is the result of human depravity, yet God in His providence remains sovereign even over the darkest moments. He is not the author of evil, but He is the master of it, often using what was intended for harm to bring about a greater redemptive purpose (Genesis 50:20).

We see God’s goodness most clearly not in the absence of suffering, but in His presence within it. In the incarnation, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, entered our broken world. He was a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief (Isaiah 53:3). On the cross, Jesus endured the greatest evil ever committed, the execution of the innocent Son of God, to achieve the greatest good, which is our atonement and reconciliation to God. He did not watch our suffering from a distance; He took it upon Himself.

God’s goodness is ultimately proven by His grace toward those who do not deserve it. Though we have all turned away from Him, God shows His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Because He lives, we have the promise that He will return personally to judge all evil and wipe away every tear from the eyes of His people (Revelation 21:4).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness and the hope of a restored world. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you navigate these hard questions, I encourage you to read through the Psalms, particularly Psalm 13 and Psalm 22. These are psalms of lament where God’s people cry out in their pain while still choosing to trust in His steadfast love. Bringing your honest questions directly to God in prayer is a vital step in walking with Him through the valley. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can process these deep truths in a community of faith.
What does "communion of the saints" mean in the ancient creeds?

The communion of the saints refers to the spiritual union and fellowship that all true believers share with Jesus Christ and, as a consequence of that union, with one another. It describes a reality where every person redeemed by the blood of Christ is knit together into one body, sharing in the same Spirit, the same hope, and the same divine life.

This doctrine, found in the Apostles' Creed, has two primary dimensions. First, there is a vertical communion. By faith, we are united to Christ, our Head, and through Him, we have fellowship with the Father and the Holy Spirit. We share in His righteousness, His sufferings, and the promise of His glory. Second, there is a horizontal communion. Because we are all members of the same body, we are called to a life of mutual love, service, and spiritual accountability. This fellowship transcends time and space, encompassing both the saints currently living on earth and those who have already passed into the presence of the Lord.

In the New Testament, the Greek word for this fellowship is koinonia, which implies a "sharing in common." As believers, we share common gifts, common struggles, and a common mission. We are commanded to bear one another's burdens and to use our varied spiritual gifts for the building up of the whole church. The Bible teaches that this is not merely a social club but a mystical union created by God Himself.

1 John 1:3
1 Corinthians 12:12-13
1 Corinthians 12:26-27
Ephesians 4:4-6
Hebrews 12:22-24

This communion is only possible because of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We were once alienated from God and one another due to our sin, but God, who is rich in mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. Through His burial and bodily resurrection, He conquered death and reconciled us to God. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When we repent of our sins and trust in Him, we are not only forgiven but adopted into this eternal family.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive this new life and be joined to His people.

As a practical step in experiencing this communion, I encourage you to commit yourself to a local, Bible-believing church. It is within the context of a local congregation that the communion of the saints becomes visible as we pray for one another, study the Word together, and partake in the Lord's Supper. Seek out a place where the Gospel is preached faithfully and where you can both give and receive the love of Christ.
How can I gain a passion for winning souls?

A passion for winning souls is born from a deep, personal encounter with the majesty of God and a clear-eyed view of the eternal peril of those without Christ. It is the natural overflow of a heart that has been captivated by the glory of Jesus Christ and the magnitude of His mercy toward sinners.

To cultivate this holy fire, you must first spend much time in the presence of the Lord, contemplating the wonder of your own salvation. When we lose our wonder at the cross, we lose our zeal for the lost. We must remember that we were once "alienated from God and were enemies in our minds because of our evil behavior," but have now been reconciled through Christ's physical body through death (Colossians 1:21-22). A heart that is truly staggered by the doctrine of justification by grace alone through faith alone will naturally desire that others participate in that same joy.

Second, you must look at the world through the lens of eternity. The Lord Jesus looked upon the crowds and was "moved with compassion for them, because they were weary and scattered, like sheep without a shepherd" (Matthew 9:36). We must recognize that every person we meet is an eternal being who will spend forever either in the presence of God's glory or under the weight of His righteous judgment. True love does not remain silent while neighbors wander toward destruction. We serve as ambassadors for Christ, as though God were making His appeal through us (2 Corinthians 5:20).

Third, realize that the power of evangelism does not rest in your eloquence or personality, but in the Gospel itself, which is "the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes" (Romans 1:16). You are simply a beggar telling other beggars where to find bread. This removes the fear of man and replaces it with a settled confidence in the sovereign grace of God.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin a "Prayer List for the Lost." Write down the names of five people you know who do not yet follow Christ. Commit to praying for them daily, asking the Father to draw them to His Son (John 6:44) and to grant you a specific opportunity to speak of His grace this week.

The Gospel we share is this: God is perfectly holy and just, and we have all rebelled against Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Is there a particular fear or hesitation that makes you feel reluctant to share this beautiful news with others?
What is reprobation? What does it mean to be reprobate?

Reprobation is the judicial act of God whereby He sovereignly decides to leave some individuals in their fallen state, passing them over in His decree of election and justly sentencing them to punishment for their sins. To be reprobate is to be rejected by God, abandoned to the natural hardness of one's own heart, and ultimately found disqualified concerning the faith.

In the Scripture, this doctrine serves as the solemn counterpart to God's gracious election. It reveals that God is not only a merciful Savior but also a holy and sovereign Judge. Those whom God passes over are left to their own rebellious will, which leads only to death. This is often described as judicial hardening, where God gives the sinner over to their own depravity as a form of judgment. We see this depicted in the life of Pharaoh and in the descriptions of those who persistently reject the light of God's truth. Romans 9:18; Romans 9:21; Romans 1:28; 1 Peter 2:8.

My dear friend, you may feel a deep sense of trembling when considering such heavy truths, perhaps wondering if your own heart is beyond the reach of God's mercy. It is a mercy of God that you even care to ask, for those who are truly hardened in a reprobate state seldom worry about their standing before a holy God. The very fact that you desire to know the truth suggests that God may be stirring your soul to look toward Him.

We must remember that while God is sovereign in His decrees, He is also perfect in His goodness and justice. No one is ever punished for being "not elect," but rather, every person who perishes does so because of their own voluntary and persistent sin against a holy Creator. God is under no obligation to save any of us, yet in His infinite love, He has provided a way of escape for all who will come to Him.

The heart of the Christian faith is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. However, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life of obedience, die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt of our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is not something we earn by our own effort or merit; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He has promised that whoever comes to Him He will never cast out. John 6:37. As an actionable step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John and ask the Holy Spirit to reveal the beauty of Christ to you, then seek out a faithful, Bible teaching church where you can hear the Word preached and find fellowship with other believers.

What holds you back today from resting entirely in the finished work of Jesus Christ?
What does the Bible say about how to get baptized / how to baptize?

Baptism is the solemn immersion of a believer in water, performed in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. It serves as a public declaration of a person's faith in the crucified, buried, and risen Savior, signifying their death to sin and resurrection to a new life in Christ Jesus.

The New Testament provides a clear pattern for how baptism is to be administered and received.

**The Proper Subjects of Baptism**
Scripture teaches that baptism is for those who have first repented of their sins and placed their trust in Jesus Christ alone for salvation. In the book of Acts, we see that "those who accepted his message were baptized" (Acts 2:41). There is no biblical record of baptism being administered to anyone apart from a profession of faith. Before one is baptized, they must understand that they are a sinner separated from a holy God, and that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone, not by any works or even the act of baptism itself (Ephesians 2:8-9).

**The Proper Mode of Baptism**
The word used in the original Greek text is *baptizo*, which means to immerse, submerge, or dip. The biblical descriptions of baptism consistently point to immersion. For instance, when Jesus was baptized, He "came up out of the water" (Mark 1:10). Likewise, when Philip baptized the Ethiopian eunuch, "both Philip and the eunuch went down into the water" (Acts 8:38). This physical act of being lowered into the water and raised out of it beautifully pictures the believer’s union with Christ in His death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4; Colossians 2:12).

**The Proper Formula for Baptism**
Jesus gave the definitive instruction for how to baptize in the Great Commission. He commanded His followers to "go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 28:19). This highlights the doctrine of the Trinity, acknowledging that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are one God, eternally existing in three persons, all active in the work of our redemption.

If you have recognized your need for forgiveness and have turned from your sin to trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ, baptism is your first act of public obedience to Him. It is not the means of your salvation, but it is the beautiful, outward testimony of the inward change God has already wrought in your soul.

You may wonder if you are "good enough" to be baptized, but baptism is not for the perfect; it is for the forgiven. It is a humble admission that you are entirely dependent on the grace of God. If you have not yet trusted in Christ, I invite you to turn to Him now. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

A practical next step for you is to contact the elders or pastors of a local, biblically faithful church. Share your testimony of faith with them and express your desire to follow the Lord's command in baptism. They can provide personal guidance and walk with you as you prepare for this joyful milestone in your walk with Christ.

What is currently on your heart as you consider taking this step of obedience?
What is prevenient grace?

Prevenient grace is the grace of God that precedes any human effort, decision, or merit in the process of salvation, literally meaning the grace that "comes before." It is the divine initiative that seeks out the lost before they ever think to seek after God.

In the history of the church, faithful believers have understood the mechanics of this grace in different ways. Many in the Arminian and Wesleyan traditions believe that God grants a universal prevenient grace to every human being. They teach that this grace restores a measure of free will to all people, sovereignly counteracting the effects of original sin just enough to allow an individual to either accept or reject the Gospel invitation (Titus 2:11; John 1:9).

From a Reformed Baptist perspective, we also believe that God is the first mover in salvation, for no one can come to the Son unless the Father draws him (John 6:44). However, we generally understand this "coming before" as the effectual calling or regeneration of the believer. Because the Scriptures describe us as being "dead in trespasses and sins" (Ephesians 2:1), we believe that God does more than merely offer an opportunity; He sovereignly grants new life to the spiritually dead. This work of the Holy Spirit removes our heart of stone and gives us a heart of flesh, sovereignly enabling us to see the beauty of Christ and respond with a willing and joyful faith (Ezekiel 36:26).

The common ground we share with all who love the Lord is the humble recognition that we love Him because He first loved us (1 John 4:19). Salvation is not a human achievement or a reward for our foresight, but a gift of sheer mercy from a God who pursues His wandering sheep.

If you find your heart stirred today with a desire for God or a conviction of sin, take comfort in knowing that this is not your own doing; it is the kindness of God leading you to repentance. I encourage you to set aside time today to thank the Lord for His initiative in your life. Turn to Him in prayer, acknowledging that you cannot save yourself, and trust entirely in the finished work of Jesus Christ, who died and rose again to reconcile you to the Father.
What does it mean that Jesus died for our sins?

To say that Jesus died for our sins means that He acted as our legal substitute, taking the full punishment we deserved for our rebellion against God so that we could be forgiven and reconciled to Him. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin; every transgression must receive a righteous penalty, which Scripture describes as death (Romans 6:23).

This beautiful and solemn truth is built upon several historical and theological realities:

First, we must understand the character of God and the condition of man. God is the sovereign and holy Creator who demands perfect obedience. However, all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Our sin creates a chasm of separation and incurs the debt of God's righteous wrath.

Second, we see the person of Jesus Christ. He is truly God and truly man, the Word made flesh (John 1:1; John 1:14). Because He was born of a virgin and conceived by the Holy Spirit, He did not inherit a sinful nature. He lived a life of perfect, sinless obedience to the Father, fulfilling the requirements of the law that we have broken.

Third, we see the work of the cross, often called **penal substitutionary atonement**. On the cross, a holy exchange took place. Jesus, who knew no sin, was made to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:21). He suffered as our substitute, enduring the physical agony of crucifixion and the spiritual weight of bearing our guilt. His death was a **propitiation**, meaning it satisfied the justice of God and turned away His wrath from all who believe (1 John 4:10).

Fourth, the work of Jesus was confirmed by His bodily resurrection. On the third day, He rose from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and demonstrating that the Father had accepted His sacrifice as sufficient (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all things.

Through this finished work, we receive **justification**, which is a legal declaration where God counts us as righteous, not based on our own works, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Romans 3:24-25).

You may wonder how a holy God could love people who have so often turned their backs on Him. The cross is the ultimate answer, where God’s perfect justice and His infinite love meet. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a next step in your journey, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, chapter 3, and spend time talking to God in prayer, confessing your sins to Him and asking Him to help you trust in the work of Jesus for your salvation. It is also vital to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can grow in the fellowship of other believers.
What is Calvinism, and is it biblical?

Calvinism, often called the Doctrines of Grace, is a system of biblical interpretation that emphasizes the absolute sovereignty of God in all things, especially in the salvation of sinners. At its heart, Calvinism seeks to protect the biblical truth that salvation is entirely a work of God's grace from beginning to end, ensuring that all glory goes to Him alone.

To understand if Calvinism is biblical, we look to how it describes the relationship between a holy God and sinful humanity. Many people summarize these teachings using the acronym TULIP, which stands for five key points:

1. Total Depravity: Because of the fall of Adam, every part of human nature, including the mind, will, and emotions, is corrupted by sin. Left to ourselves, we are spiritually dead and unable to seek God or please Him (Romans 3:10-12; Ephesians 2:1-3).
2. Unconditional Election: Before the foundation of the world, God chose specific individuals for salvation, not based on any foreseen merit or faith in them, but solely according to His sovereign good pleasure (Ephesians 1:4-5; Romans 9:15-16).
3. Particular Redemption: This is the belief that Christ's death on the cross was intended to certainly and effectively save His people. While His sacrifice is of infinite value and sufficient for all, it was specifically designed to secure the redemption of those the Father gave to the Son (John 10:11; John 10:14-15; Acts 20:28).
4. Irresistible Grace: When God calls a person to Himself through the Gospel, the Holy Spirit performs a miracle of regeneration, giving the sinner a new heart and a new will. This grace is not forced, but it is effective, making the unwilling soul willing to come to Christ (John 6:37; John 6:44).
5. Perseverance of the Saints: Those whom God has truly called and justified will never finally fall away. Because salvation depends on God's power rather than human effort, the believer is kept secure until the end (John 10:27-29; Philippians 1:6; Romans 8:29-30).

You may wonder if such a view makes human choices meaningless or makes God appear distant. It is a sincere concern to want to uphold the reality of our responsibility and the sincerity of God's love. However, Calvinism maintains that God works through our wills rather than against them, and His choice to save anyone at all is a display of unimaginable mercy.

While Calvinism is a historic and faithful framework held by many within the Reformed tradition, it is considered a secondary doctrine. Faithful Christians who hold the Bible as their ultimate authority sometimes differ on how to harmonize God's sovereignty with human responsibility. Some emphasize that God provides grace to all people to enable a choice (often called Arminianism), while others see the Bible teaching that God must first give life before a person can believe (monergism).

Ultimately, the Bible teaches that we are separated from God by our sin, and the punishment for that sin is death. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. As you reflect on these truths, what is it about the idea of God’s absolute control that most impacts your view of Him?

For a practical next step in exploring these truths, I encourage you to read through the first two chapters of the book of Ephesians. As you read, notice who is performing the actions of saving, choosing, and redeeming. It may also be helpful to speak with a pastor at a local Bible-teaching church who can walk through these passages with you in person.
Who was Ishtar, and is there any connection between Ishtar and Easter?

Ishtar was the ancient Mesopotamian goddess of fertility, love, and war, primarily worshipped by the Akkadians, Babylonians, and Assyrians. In the Holy Scriptures, she is closely associated with the Canaanite goddess Ashtoreth, whose worship frequently enticed the Israelites away from the one true God, leading to severe spiritual consequences (1 Kings 11:5; Judges 2:13).

Regarding the connection between Ishtar and Easter, there is no historical or linguistic evidence to link the two. The name Ishtar is Semitic in origin, while the English word Easter likely derives from the Old English Eostre, the name of a Germanic month or goddess associated with the dawn. While some people point to the phonetic similarity between the names, they belong to entirely different language families. Most importantly, the Christian celebration of the Resurrection was originally, and remains in most languages today, called Pascha, derived from the Hebrew word for Passover, as Christ is our true Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7).

The worship of deities like Ishtar often involved rituals that God's Word explicitly condemns. For example, the prophet Jeremiah rebuked the people of Judah for making cakes for the Queen of Heaven, a title often attributed to Ishtar or her equivalents (Jeremiah 7:18; Jeremiah 44:17-19). These practices were empty shadows that could never provide the peace or forgiveness that only comes through the Creator.

The celebration of the Resurrection of Jesus Christ is not based on ancient myths or seasonal folklore, but on the historical fact of the empty tomb. You may wonder if the traditions of men have obscured the truth of God, yet the reality remains that Jesus Christ truly took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and suffered a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or rituals we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9). Because God is holy and we have all sinned, we are naturally separated from Him, but Christ's sacrifice is sufficient to reconcile you to the Father. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in the finished work of Jesus today?

As a practical next step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It clearly presents the identity of Jesus and His purpose in coming to save the world (John 20:31). Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word preached and find fellowship with other believers.
Who are the Alawites, and what do they believe?

The Alawites are a religious minority group primarily located in Syria, as well as parts of Lebanon and Turkey, who follow a secretive and syncretistic faith that originated in the ninth century. Their beliefs are often described as syncretistic because they combine elements of Shia Islam, Gnosticism, Neoplatonism, and ancient Middle Eastern religions.

At the core of Alawite doctrine is a belief in a divine triad, which they believe consists of Ali ibn Abi Talib (the cousin and son-in-law of Muhammad), Muhammad himself, and Salman al-Farisi (a companion of Muhammad). They hold the view that Ali is an incarnation of the divine. This is a significant departure from orthodox Islam and a complete denial of the biblical revelation of God. Furthermore, Alawites believe in the transmigration of souls, or reincarnation. They teach that human souls were originally stars in the celestial world who were cast out due to disobedience and must undergo a series of earthly cycles before they can return to their heavenly state.

From the standpoint of biblical truth, we must speak with clarity and love. The Alawite view of God is not the God of the Bible. Scripture teaches that there is only one God, eternally existing in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). Unlike the Alawite triad, the biblical Trinity reveals God as holy, sovereign, and unique, with the Lord Jesus Christ being the only true incarnation of God (John 1:1; John 1:14). Additionally, the concept of reincarnation is contrary to the Word of God, which declares that man is destined to die once, and after that to face judgment (Hebrews 9:27).

One might understand the human desire to find belonging and spiritual security within a secretive, close-knit community, particularly in a region marked by centuries of instability. However, true security and identity are found only in a right relationship with the Creator through His Son.

The Gospel reveals that God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His righteous judgment. Yet, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3, 1 Corinthians 15:4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or cycles of reincarnation (Ephesians 2:8, Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your journey of understanding, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray for the Alawite people. Ask the Lord to send believers into their communities to share the light of the Gospel, so that they may find the true peace that comes only through Jesus Christ (Romans 10:14, Romans 10:15). In your own study, meditate on the Gospel of John to see the beauty of the true Word made flesh (John 1:1, John 1:18).
Why is sola fide important?

Sola Fide, or faith alone, is the biblical truth that a person is justified—declared righteous in the sight of God—solely through faith in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ. This doctrine is the very heart of the Gospel, as it ensures that salvation is an entirely gracious act of God and that no human effort can add to the perfect sacrifice of Christ.

This truth is essential because it preserves the purity of the Gospel and the glory of God. If our standing before a holy God depended even in part on our own moral achievement or religious performance, we would remain under a heavy burden of guilt, for no human can meet the perfect standard of God's law. Romans 3:28 states, "For we maintain that a man is justified by faith apart from works of the law." When we trust in Jesus, His perfect righteousness is imputed, or credited, to our account, while our sins are placed upon Him. Galatians 2:16 further affirms that "a man is not justified by works of the law, but by faith in Jesus Christ."

Sola Fide is important because it provides the only true ground for peace with God. Because our justification is based on the finished work of Jesus on the cross and His bodily resurrection, our security rests in His unchangeable faithfulness rather than our own fluctuating feelings or failures. Ephesians 2:8-9 declares, "For it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this not from yourselves; it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast." This doctrine allows the weary soul to find rest, knowing that Christ has done everything necessary for our reconciliation.

You may feel that you must clean yourself up or achieve a certain level of goodness before God will accept you, but the Gospel is for those who realize they cannot save themselves. God is holy and His wrath against sin is real, yet in His love, He sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the substitutionary death we deserved. Jesus rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
Does the Bible say anything about clairvoyance?

The Bible addresses clairvoyance, often categorized under divination, fortunetelling, or seeking after mediums, as a practice that is strictly forbidden for those who follow the living God. Scripture presents these activities not as harmless entertainment or neutral "gifts," but as a rejection of God's sovereign authority and a dangerous turning toward deceptive spiritual powers.

In the Old Testament, God provided clear boundaries for His people to protect them from the spiritual darkness of the surrounding nations. Deuteronomy 18:10-12 states, "There shall not be found among you anyone who burns his son or his daughter as an offering, anyone who practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD." This prohibition is rooted in the fact that God is the only legitimate source of truth and revelation regarding the future and the spiritual realm.

The New Testament continues this stance, showing that such "abilities" are often linked to spiritual bondage. In Acts 16:16-18, the Apostle Paul encountered a slave girl who had a "spirit of divination" and brought her owners much gain by fortunetelling. Paul, recognizing the source was not from God, commanded the spirit to leave her in the name of Jesus Christ, and she was immediately delivered. This event demonstrates that "hidden knowledge" gained through clairvoyance often has a demonic origin rather than a divine one.

It is entirely understandable why someone might be drawn to clairvoyance. In a world filled with uncertainty, grief, and a longing for direction, the desire to see behind the veil of the unknown is a deeply human impulse. We often crave the comfort of knowing what lies ahead or hearing from those we have lost. However, the Bible teaches that true peace and security are found only in the character and promises of God, not in the predictions of men or spirits.

Because God is holy and supreme, He requires our exclusive trust. When we seek hidden knowledge through occult means, we are essentially saying that God's Word is not enough and His providence is not trustworthy. Isaiah 8:19 asks poignantly, "And when they say to you, 'Inquire of the mediums and the necromancers who chirp and mutter,' should not a people inquire of their God? Should they inquire of the dead on behalf of the living?"

The ultimate answer to our search for truth and future hope is found in the person of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that we are all separated from God by our sin, which leads us to seek fulfillment in things that cannot satisfy. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a sacrificial death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through Christ alone, we are reconciled to the Father and given the Holy Spirit, who guides us into all truth.

Instead of seeking a clairvoyant, I encourage you to turn to the Savior who holds the future in His hands. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. He promises to never leave you nor forsake you, as noted in Hebrews 13:5.

As a practical next step, I suggest reading through the Gospel of John to see the clarity and light that Jesus brings to those who follow Him. If you find yourself anxious about tomorrow, take those specific fears to God in prayer, asking Him to increase your faith in His good and perfect will. What is it about the future that concerns you most today?
What is the Shahada in Islam?

The Shahada is the Islamic profession of faith, which declares, "There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah." It serves as the first of the five pillars of Islam, and its sincere public recitation is the formal requirement for an individual to enter the Muslim faith.

You may wonder how various religions define the gate through which one must pass to find peace with the Creator. While the Shahada emphasizes a particular view of God's oneness and the authority of a prophet, the Bible reveals that our deepest need is not merely a new creed, but a new birth through a Savior.

Scripture teaches that there is one God, eternally existing in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). God is perfectly holy, and because we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are naturally separated from Him (Romans 3:23). No human effort, ritual, or profession can remove the stain of our guilt or reconcile us to a holy God. 

However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (1 Peter 2:24). He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is the only mediator between God and men (1 Timothy 2:5). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the dispensation of Grace?

The dispensation of grace refers to the current period of redemptive history in which God primarily deals with humanity through the Gospel of Jesus Christ, emphasizing that salvation is a free gift received by faith rather than through the works of the Law. This era began with the coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost and continues until the return of Christ, representing a time of God's patient favor toward a fallen world (John 1:17; Romans 6:14).

In the New Testament, the Apostle Paul describes this as the "stewardship of God’s grace" which was revealed to him for the benefit of the Church (Ephesians 3:2). This period is characterized by the indwelling of the Holy Spirit in every believer and the mystery of the Church, where both Jews and Gentiles are brought together as one body through the finished work of Jesus Christ (Ephesians 3:6; Colossians 1:26-27).

Within historic Christianity, there are different ways of framing this concept:

1. Many believers hold to a framework called Dispensationalism, which sees this "dispensation of grace" as a distinct administration in God's timeline. In this view, God has separate programs for national Israel and the Church, and this current age is a specific period where the Church is the primary focus of His work on earth.

2. Other believers, particularly those from a Reformed or Covenantal perspective, view this age as the administration of the New Covenant. They see it as the fulfillment of promises made throughout the Old Testament, emphasizing the continuity of God's one people throughout history (Jeremiah 31:31-34; Hebrews 8:6-13).

While these secondary frameworks differ on the details of how God’s plan unfolds, the core doctrine remains the same: salvation is found only in Christ. God is holy and just, and we have all fallen short of His glory through our sin. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's wrath against sin. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over death, and He is now exalted as Lord of all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step in your study, I encourage you to read through the Book of Galatians. It beautifully explains the distinction between the Law and the Gospel, helping you ground your heart in the freedom that comes from God's grace. Additionally, I suggest you speak with a pastor at a local, Bible-believing church to discuss how this grace is lived out in a community of believers.
What does the Bible say about repentance? What does it mean to repent?

Repentance is a sovereignly granted grace where a person undergoes a sincere change of mind, heart, and direction, turning away from sin and toward God through faith in Jesus Christ. In the original Greek of the New Testament, the word is metanoia, which signifies a change of mind that results in a transformed life.

The Bible teaches that repentance is not merely a feeling of regret or a fear of consequences, but a deep recognition of the holiness of God and the offensiveness of our own sin. The Apostle Paul explains that godly sorrow produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly sorrow only produces death, as seen in 2 Corinthians 7:10. True repentance involves acknowledging our rebellion against God, grieving over it, and choosing to follow Christ as Lord. This turning is seen in Acts 3:19, which says, Repent, then, and turn back, so that your sins may be wiped away.

It is vital to understand that repentance and faith are two sides of the same coin. We cannot truly turn toward Christ without turning away from our sin. This is a gift from God, as it is His kindness that leads us to repentance, according to Romans 2:4. While we are justified by grace alone through faith alone, the evidence of a true saving faith is a life that begins the process of progressive sanctification, where we daily die to ourselves and live for God.

You may feel that your sins are too great or that you must first fix yourself before you can come to God. However, the Gospel is the good news that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us, as stated in Romans 5:8. God is holy and must judge sin, yet in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He now sits at the right hand of the Father as Lord over all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to set aside time today to read Psalm 51. Use this Scripture as a guide for your own heart to speak honestly with God about your sins and to ask Him for the strength to walk in His ways. Seek out a local, Bible teaching church where you can find fellowship and guidance from mature believers who can pray with you.
Is there an argument for the existence of God?

You may wonder if faith in God is a leap into the dark or a step into the light of clear evidence. The Bible teaches that God has not left Himself without witness, but has provided ample testimony of His existence through the world He made and the hearts He created.

The primary argument for God's existence is found in what theologians call general revelation. This is the truth that God reveals Himself to all people, at all times, through the created order. The Apostle Paul writes that God's invisible attributes, namely, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made (Romans 1:19–20). When we look at the vast complexity of the universe, from the rotation of the planets to the intricate coding of DNA, we see the handiwork of a Divine Architect. Just as a building requires a builder and a painting requires a painter, the universe requires a First Cause who is Himself uncaused (Genesis 1:1; Hebrews 3:4).

Furthermore, the Bible points to the human conscience as evidence of a moral Lawgiver. Every person possesses an internal sense of right and wrong, a "law written on their hearts," which bears witness to a God who is perfectly holy and just (Romans 2:14–15). Our deep longing for justice, beauty, and purpose is not an accident of biology, but a reflection of the fact that we are made in the image of God (Genesis 1:27).

While nature and conscience tell us that God exists, the most definitive argument is the person of Jesus Christ. God did not remain distant; He took on human flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). Jesus Christ lived a sinless life, performed miracles that demonstrated His power over creation, and ultimately died on a cross to pay the penalty for human sin. Most significantly, His bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day serves as the historical proof of His divinity and the truth of His claims (1 Corinthians 15:3–8).

Because God is holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are naturally separated from Him (Romans 3:23). However, the Gospel is the good news that salvation is a free gift of grace. Christ’s sacrificial death is sufficient to reconcile you to God. By turning from sin and placing your trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for your sins and rose again, you can receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life (John 3:16; Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

To explore this further, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, ask God to show you the truth about who Jesus is and what He has done for you. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is Christmas a pagan holiday?

Christmas is a holiday observed by the church to commemorate the birth of Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh for our salvation. While the date of December 25 was historically associated with various Roman winter festivals, the holiday’s central purpose is to celebrate the biblical truth of the Incarnation, the moment the Creator entered His creation as a man.

You may wonder if participating in a holiday with such complex historical roots compromises your devotion to God. It is a sincere desire to want your worship to be pure and pleasing to Him.

Historically, faithful Christians have viewed this matter in different ways, as it is a tertiary issue where there is room for Christian liberty. One perspective, often held within the stricter Reformed traditions, suggests that because the Bible does not explicitly command the observance of Christmas, and because many traditions have secular or ancient non-Christian origins, it is best to abstain. This is rooted in a desire to follow the Word of God alone as the rule for worship.

Another perspective is that Christians have the freedom to set aside a specific time to focus on the glorious news that the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, as recorded in John 1:14. Those who hold this view believe that the historical timing of the date does not contaminate the worship of the one true God for a historical, biblical event. They see the season as a unique opportunity to proclaim the Gospel to a world that is already hearing the name of Jesus.

The Apostle Paul provides clear guidance for such differences in Romans 14:5; Romans 14:6, where he explains that one person considers one day more sacred than another, while another considers every day alike. He instructs that each person should be fully convinced in their own mind, and whatever they do, they should do it in honor of the Lord.

The essential truth for all believers is that Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man. We are separated from a holy God by our sin, but in His mercy, God sent His Son to be born of a virgin, to live a perfect life, and to die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, ascending to heaven where He reigns as Lord. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to study the accounts of Christ’s birth in Matthew 1 and Luke 2 and ask the Lord for wisdom in prayer. If you feel a conviction to abstain from the holiday, do so with charity toward those who celebrate. If you choose to celebrate, ensure that your focus remains on the person and work of Christ rather than on worldly traditions. What holds you back from finding peace in this decision?
What is a Calvinist?

A Calvinist is a Christian who adheres to the theological system known as Reformed theology, which emphasizes the absolute sovereignty of God in all things, especially in the salvation of man. This tradition, named after the French Reformer John Calvin, centers on the biblical conviction that God is the author and finisher of our faith from beginning to end.

At the heart of Calvinism is the concept of monergism, the belief that God alone performs the work of regeneration in the human heart. This is often summarized by the Five Points of Calvinism, frequently remembered by the acronym TULIP:

1.  Total Depravity: Because of the fall of Adam, every human being is born spiritually dead and corrupted by sin in every part of their nature, leaving them unable and unwilling to choose God on their own (Romans 3:10–12; Ephesians 2:1–3).
2.  Unconditional Election: Before the foundation of the world, God chose a people for Himself based solely on His sovereign grace and good pleasure, not because of any foreseen faith or merit in the individuals (Ephesians 1:4–5; Romans 9:11–16).
3.  Limited Atonement: Also called Particular Redemption, this teaches that Christ’s sacrificial death was intended to effectively secure the salvation of those the Father had given Him (John 10:11; John 10:14–15; John 17:9).
4.  Irresistible Grace: When God calls His elect to Himself through the Gospel, the Holy Spirit works within them to overcome their resistance, granting them a new heart and a willing faith (John 6:37; John 6:44).
5.  Perseverance of the Saints: Those whom God has truly called and justified will never finally fall away from grace but will be kept by His power until the end (John 10:27–29; Romans 8:29–30; Philippians 1:6).

For the Calvinist, these truths are not merely academic but lead to a life of deep humility and worship. When we realize that we did nothing to earn our salvation and that God chose to love us despite our rebellion, the only appropriate response is Soli Deo Gloria, meaning "to God alone be the glory." It provides a steadying comfort to know that our security rests in the unchanging hands of a sovereign Savior rather than our own frail efforts.

The Gospel is the announcement that while we were helpless in our sin, Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a life of perfect obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the wrath of God. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day and is now exalted as Lord. This salvation is a free gift for all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, paying close attention to how Jesus describes His relationship with His sheep and the Father’s authority over all things. Reflect on how much peace comes from knowing that the King of the universe is also a Shepherd who never loses one of His own.
What is Reformed epistemology apologetics?

Reformed epistemology is an approach to defending the Christian faith which asserts that belief in God is "properly basic," meaning it is rational to believe in Him without requiring a sequence of logical proofs or external evidences. This perspective, championed by philosophers such as Alvin Plantinga, suggests that the human mind is designed with a natural capacity to recognize the existence of God when functioning correctly in its intended environment.

The theological foundation for this view is often traced back to the "sensus divinitatis," or a "sense of the divine," which is an innate awareness of God implanted in every person. This concept aligns with the biblical teaching that God has made His existence and attributes plain to all humanity through the created order. The Apostle Paul writes that "what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them," so that people are "without excuse" (Romans 1:19-20). Because God is the author of both the world and the human mind, the believer can have confidence that their faith is a reasonable response to the reality God has revealed, rather than a blind leap into the dark.

In this framework, the primary reason people deny God is not a lack of evidence, but the "noetic effects of sin," which is the way the Fall has darkened and corrupted human reasoning and desires (Ephesians 4:18). While traditional apologetics might focus on building a case from the ground up using historical or scientific data, Reformed epistemology focuses on removing the intellectual obstacles that prevent a person from acknowledging what their conscience and the world around them already declare (Psalm 19:1-2).

Practically, this means you do not need to be a scholar or a scientist to have a perfectly rational and certain faith. Just as you are rational to believe the sun has risen because you see its light, you are rational to believe in God because He has made Himself known to your heart and mind through His Word and His works. This should encourage you to stand firm in your convictions, knowing that your faith is grounded in the truth of God Himself.

Ultimately, while we are created to know God, our sin has separated us from Him, clouding our vision and hardening our hearts. True knowledge of God is found in the person of Jesus Christ, who is the "image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15). Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to reconcile us to God by grace alone through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For further study on how God reveals Himself, you may find it helpful to read through the first chapter of the book of Romans or the nineteenth Psalm. If you are struggling with doubts, consider speaking with a pastor or an elder at a local gospel preaching church who can pray with you and provide biblical guidance.
Can a person be saved through general revelation?

While general revelation provides a clear testimony of God's existence and His power, it is not sufficient to bring a person to salvation. General revelation, which is God's self disclosure through the created order and the human conscience, serves to leave all people without excuse before God but does not reveal the specific way of redemption through Jesus Christ.

The Apostle Paul explains in Romans 1:19-20 that what may be known about God is plain to everyone because God has made it plain through what He has created, so that men are without excuse. However, while creation declares the glory of God (Psalm 19:1), it does not declare the Gospel of God. Salvation requires special revelation, which is the Word of God made flesh in Jesus Christ and recorded in the Holy Scriptures.

Scripture is explicit that faith comes by hearing the specific message of Christ. Romans 10:14-17 asks how people can believe in the One of whom they have not heard, concluding that faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word about Christ. There is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). To be justified, or declared righteous before God, a person must trust in the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ alone.

You may wonder how a loving God deals with those who have never heard this message. We must trust that the Judge of all the earth will do what is right (Genesis 18:25), yet we are clearly told that all have sinned and fall short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This reality underscores the absolute urgency of the Christian mission to proclaim the Good News to every nation.

The heart of that message is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all rebelled against Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the specific revelation of Jesus Christ. If you are not already part of one, please seek out a local church that faithfully preaches the Word of God, where you can grow in your understanding of these truths alongside other believers.
What is Christianization?

Christianization is the process by which individuals, societies, or entire cultures are converted to Christianity or come under the influence of Christian values, symbols, and institutions. Historically, it describes the spread of the Gospel and the establishment of the Church throughout the world as commanded by Jesus Christ in the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18–20).

While the term often refers to the outward adoption of Christian rituals, laws, and calendars by a nation, the Bible teaches that true transformation begins with the regeneration of the individual heart by the Holy Spirit. This is the supernatural work of God where a person is made a new creation in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17). When many people in a community are reconciled to God, the fruits of the Spirit, such as love, joy, peace, and justice, begin to influence the laws, art, and social structures of that society (Galatians 5:22–23). We see this pattern in the early church, where the preaching of the Word was said to have turned the world upside down as people abandoned idols to serve the living and true God (Acts 17:6; 1 Thessalonians 1:9).

It is important to distinguish between cultural Christianity, which may only have an appearance of godliness, and a life truly submitted to the Lordship of Jesus Christ. As believers, our call is to be salt and light in our communities, reflecting the character of our Father in every sphere of life, from our work to our families (Matthew 5:13–16).

You may wonder if this process is merely about changing external behaviors or following a new set of rules. However, the heart of the message is that we are all separated from a holy God by our sin and cannot save ourselves through our own efforts. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

To grow in your understanding of how Christ transforms a life, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John and begin speaking to God in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to you. Seeking fellowship in a local, Bible-believing church is also a vital step to see how the Gospel is lived out in a community of faith.
What are the satanic verses?

The "Satanic Verses" refer to an alleged historical incident in early Islamic tradition where it is claimed that the prophet Muhammad briefly mistook a temptation from Satan for a divine revelation. According to these accounts, the verses originally suggested that three pagan goddesses of Mecca could act as intermediaries to God, though the statement was later retracted and replaced with a firm rejection of those deities.

You may wonder if it is possible for a message from God to be corrupted by spiritual enemies or if we can truly know which words are divine. This concern touches on the very foundation of our hope, for if God's Word is not certain, our faith has no firm ground.

In contrast to these accounts, the Bible teaches that the Holy Scriptures are the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. We hold to the doctrine of inspiration, which means that God so superintended the human authors of the Bible that, using their own individual personalities and styles, they composed and recorded without error His message to mankind. The Apostle Peter explains that "no prophecy of Scripture comes from one’s own interpretation, for no prophecy was ever borne by the will of man; rather, men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit" in 2 Peter 1:20-21.

Because God is sovereign, holy, and immutable, He does not allow His redemptive truth to be subverted or polluted by the enemy. He is "not a man, that He should lie, or a son of man, that He should change His mind" in Numbers 23:19. When God speaks, His Word stands forever, and it is the light by which we navigate this dark world.

While other religious traditions may struggle with the reliability of their origins, the Bible invites you to behold the perfect consistency of God's character and His plan for salvation. This plan centers on the person of Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. Yet, in His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him and the certainty of His Word today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, notice how Jesus speaks with absolute authority and total consistency, reflecting the unchanging nature of the God who loves you. You might also consider speaking with a local pastor about how we can be certain that the Bible is indeed the Word of God.
Is the "Way of the Master" evangelism method biblical?

The "Way of the Master" evangelism method is biblically sound because it utilizes the Law of God to produce the knowledge of sin, preparing the heart for the message of God’s grace. This approach follows the pattern established by the biblical authors, who used the Ten Commandments to show that every mouth is stopped and the whole world is held accountable to God (Romans 3:19-20).

Theologically, this method emphasizes the pedagogical use of the Law. This term refers to the Law acting as a tutor or schoolmaster that leads a person to Christ by demonstrating their desperate need for a Savior (Galatians 3:24). By walking someone through the moral requirements of God, the evangelist seeks to strip away self-righteousness, making the news of Christ's substitutionary atonement truly "good news."

While the framework is biblical, its application must always be seasoned with the gentleness and respect commanded in 1 Peter 3:15. It is not merely about winning an argument or following a script, but about a shepherd-like concern for a lost soul. The goal is to bring a person to a place of genuine repentance and faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.

If you are looking for a practical next step, I encourage you to study the Ten Commandments in Exodus 20 and ask the Holy Spirit to show you how they reflect God’s holy character. Practice explaining the Gospel to a trusted friend, focusing on how Christ fulfilled the Law on our behalf.

The core of our message is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because God is a just judge, our sin deserves His righteous wrath. However, God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take our place. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from placing your full trust in Him today?
Is there a God?

Yes, there is one eternal, sovereign, and holy God who created all things by His power and for His glory. The Bible teaches that God has made His existence known to all people through the majesty of the created world, a reality called general revelation, and most clearly through His Word and His Son, Jesus Christ (Psalm 19:1; Romans 1:19–20; Hebrews 1:1–2).

You may wonder why, if God exists, He does not make Himself more obvious to our physical senses or why He allows the world to remain in such a broken state. The Scriptures explain that while God created everything good, humanity rebelled against Him. This sin caused a deep separation between a holy God and His creation, bringing death and suffering into the world.

Yet, God in His mercy did not remain distant. He entered our world in the person of Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This is the Gospel: that we are justified before God by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

Because of what Jesus has done, there is a way for you to be reconciled to your Creator. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth and His love to your heart.
Does God exist?

Yes, God exists and has clearly revealed Himself to all people through the majesty of creation and, most perfectly, through the person of His Son, Jesus Christ. You may wonder how we can be certain of a Being we cannot see with our physical eyes, especially in a world that often demands material evidence for every truth.

The Bible teaches that God’s existence is evident to every human heart through what is called general revelation. The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork (Psalm 19:1).
